AI.method: 30 participants in a realistic search-and-rescue HRI context; multimodal data (eye movements, pupil diameter, biosignals, user interactions, robot data); predefined events requiring timely operator assistance; ground-truth SA latency for perceptual and comprehension types; evaluation of ML models using generic eye-tracking features with and without contextual data; leave-one-group-out cross-validation.
AI.result: Eye-tracking features alone achieved recall 88.91% and F1 67.63% for perceptual SA latency detection; incorporating contextual features improved recall to 91.51% and F1 to 80.38%.
AI.conclusion: First public dataset for systematic evaluation of SA in HRI missions; demonstrates potential of generic eye-tracking features for continuous perceptual SA latency detection in remote human-robot teams.

title: Consumer-to-Clinical Language Shifts in Ambient AI Draft Notes and Clinician-Finalized Documentation: A Multi-level Analysis
summary: Ambient AI generates draft clinical notes from patient-clinician conversations, often using lay or consumer-oriented phrasing to support patient understanding instead of standardized clinical terminology. How clinicians revise these drafts for professional documentation conventions remains unclear. We quantified clinician editing for consumer-to- clinical normalization using a dictionary-confirmed transformation framework. We analyzed 71,173 AI-draft and finalized-note section pairs from 34,726 encounters. Confirmed transformations were defined as replacing a consumer expression with its dictionary-mapped clinical equivalent in the same section. Editing significantly reduced terminology density across all sections (p < 0.001). The Assessment and Plan accounted for the largest transformation volume (59.3%). Our analysis identified 7,576 transformation events across 4,114 note sections (5.8%), representing 1.2% consumer-term deletions. Transformation intensity varied across individual clinicians (p < 0.001). Overall, clinician post-editing demonstrates consistent shifts from conversational phrasing toward standardized, section- appropriate clinical terminology, supporting section-aware ambient AI design.

AI.tldr: Clinicians heavily post-edit AI-drafted clinical notes to convert consumer terms into dictionary-mapped clinical terms; most changes occur in the Assessment/Plan; 5.8% of sections edited; 1.2% consumer-term deletions; editing reduces terminology density; wide variation across clinicians; implies ambient AI should be section-aware.
AI.motivation: To understand how clinicians revise lay-language AI drafts to meet professional documentation standards, addressing a gap in how such edits occur and vary by clinician.
AI.method: Analyzed 71,173 AI-draft/finalized-note section pairs from 34,726 encounters using a dictionary-confirmed transformation framework that flags consumer-to-clinical term replacements within the same section; measured terminology density and transformation events; assessed statistical significance and inter-clinician variation.
AI.result: Confirmed transformations decreased terminology density across sections (p<0.001). The Assessment/Plan contained 59.3% of transformations. There were 7,576 transformation events across 4,114 note sections (5.8%), amounting to 1.2% consumer-term deletions. Transformation intensity varied across clinicians (p<0.001).
AI.conclusion: Post-editing by clinicians shifts conversational phrasing toward standardized section-appropriate clinical terminology; findings support designing ambient AI that is aware of section structure and terminology needs.

title: Semantic Segmentation and Depth Estimation for Real-Time Lunar Surface Mapping Using 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: Navigation and mapping on the lunar surface require robust perception under challenging conditions, including poorly textured environments, high-contrast lighting, and limited computational resources. This paper presents a real-time mapping framework that integrates dense perception models with a 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) representation. We first benchmark several models on synthetic datasets generated with the LuPNT simulator, selecting a stereo dense depth estimation model based on Gated Recurrent Units for its balance of speed and accuracy in depth estimation, and a convolutional neural network for its superior performance in detecting semantic segments. Using ground truth poses to decouple the local scene understanding from the global state estimation, our pipeline reconstructs a 120-meter traverse with a geometric height accuracy of approximately 3 cm, outperforming a traditional point cloud baseline without LiDAR. The resulting 3DGS map enables novel view synthesis and serves as a foundation for a full SLAM system, where its capacity for joint map and pose optimization would offer significant advantages. Our results demonstrate that combining semantic segmentation and dense depth estimation with learned map representations is an effective approach for creating detailed, large-scale maps to support future lunar surface missions.

AI.tldr: Real-time lunar mapping using a 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) map that fuses dense depth, semantic segmentation, and learned representations to produce accurate, large-scale maps (120 m traverse, ~3 cm height accuracy) and enable novel view synthesis and future SLAM.
AI.motivation: Navigate and map the lunar surface under challenging lighting, texture, and compute constraints by leveraging dense perception and a learned, flexible 3D map representation to improve map quality and SLAM potential.
AI.method: Benchmark stereo dense depth estimation using a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) based model for speed-accuracy trade-off, and a convolutional neural network for semantic segmentation on synthetic LuPNT data. Use ground-truth poses to decouple local scene understanding from global pose estimation, reconstruct a 120 m traverse with 3D Gaussian Splatting, and compare to a LiDAR-less point cloud baseline. Demonstrate novel view synthesis and discuss integration into a full SLAM pipeline with joint map-pose optimization.
AI.result: Achieves geometric height accuracy of about 3 cm over a 120 m traverse, outperforming a traditional point cloud baseline without LiDAR. The 3DGS map enables novel view synthesis and provides a solid foundation for a full SLAM system, where joint map and pose optimization could offer additional advantages. Demonstrates that combining semantic segmentation and dense depth with learned map representations yields detailed, large-scale maps suitable for future lunar missions.
AI.conclusion: Integrating semantic segmentation and dense depth estimation with learned, large-scale map representations (3DGS) is an effective approach for robust, high-detail lunar mapping and supports future mission needs by enabling accurate geometry, view synthesis, and potential SLAM enhancements.

title: Enhancing Reinforcement Learning Fine-Tuning with an Online Refiner
summary: Constraints are essential for stabilizing reinforcement learning fine-tuning (RFT) and preventing degenerate outputs, yet they inherently conflict with the optimization objective because stronger constraints limit the ability of a fine-tuned model to discover better solutions. We propose \textit{dynamic constraints} that resolve this tension by adapting to the evolving capabilities of the fine-tuned model based on the insight that constraints should only intervene when degenerate outputs occur. We implement this by using a reference model as an \textit{online refiner} that takes the response from the fine-tuned model and generates a minimally corrected version which preserves correct content verbatim while fixing errors. A supervised fine-tuning loss then trains the fine-tuned model to produce the refined output. This mechanism yields a constraint that automatically strengthens or relaxes based on output quality. Experiments on dialogue and code generation show that dynamic constraints outperform both KL regularization and unconstrained baselines, achieving substantially higher task rewards while maintaining training stability.

AI.tldr: Adaptive dynamic constraints for RL fine-tuning using an online refiner to minimally edit outputs, yielding higher rewards and stability than KL or unconstrained baselines.
AI.motivation: Constraints stabilize outputs but trade off optimization; dynamic constraints adapt to the model's evolving capabilities by intervening only when outputs degenerate.
AI.method: Employ a reference model as an online refiner that post-edits the fine-tuned model's response to be correct verbatim while fixing errors; train the fine-tuned model with a supervised loss on the refined outputs; the strength of constraints is dynamic and responds to output quality.
AI.result: Dynamic constraints outperform KL regularization and unconstrained baselines on dialogue and code generation, achieving substantially higher task rewards with maintained training stability.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, self-adjusting constraints balance safety and exploration in RL fine-tuning, enabling better performance across domains by activating constraints only when needed.

title: Multi-material Direct Ink Writing and Embroidery for Stretchable Wearable Sensors
summary: The development of wearable sensing systems for sports performance tracking, rehabilitation, and injury prevention has driven growing demand for smart garments that combine comfort, durability, and accurate motion detection. This paper presents a textile-compatible fabrication workflow that integrates multi-material direct ink writing with automated embroidery to create stretchable strain sensors directly embedded into garments. The process combines sequential multi-material printing of a silicone-carbon grease-silicone stack with automated embroidery that provides both mechanical fixation and electrical interfacing in a single step. The resulting hybrid sensor demonstrates stretchability up to 120% strain while maintaining electrical continuity, with approximately linear behaviour up to 60% strain (R^2 = 0.99), a gauge factor of 31.4, and hysteresis of 22.9%. Repeated loading-unloading tests over 80 cycles show baseline and peak drift of 0.135% and 0.236% per cycle, respectively, indicating moderate cycle-to-cycle stability. Mechanical testing further confirms that the silicone-fabric interface remains intact under large deformation, with failure occurring in the textile rather than at the stitched boundary. As a preliminary proof of concept, the sensor was integrated into wearable elbow and knee sleeves for joint angle monitoring, showing a clear correlation between normalised resistance change and bending angle. By addressing both mechanical fixation and electrical interfacing through embroidery-based integration, this approach provides a reproducible and scalable pathway for incorporating printed stretchable electronics into textile systems for motion capture and soft robotic applications.

AI.tldr: A textile-integrated stretchable strain sensor via multi-material direct ink writing and embroidery, yielding a scalable, reproducible pathway for garment-based motion capture.
AI.motivation: Growing demand for comfortable, durable smart garments capable of accurate motion sensing for sports, rehabilitation, and injury prevention.
AI.method: Sequential multi-material printing of a silicone-carbon grease-silicone stack combined with automated embroidery to fixate and interface electrically, embedded directly into fabric to create a stretchable sensor; tested under large strain with mechanical integrity at the silicone-fabric interface.
AI.result: Sensor supports up to 120% strain with electrical continuity; linear behavior up to 60% strain (R^2=0.99); gauge factor 31.4; hysteresis 22.9%; 80-cycle stability with baseline and peak drift ~0.135% and 0.236% per cycle; failure at textile boundary rather than stitched interface; integrated into elbow/knee sleeves showing correspondence between resistance changes and joint angle.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a reproducible, scalable fabrication route for embedding printed stretchable electronics in textiles, enabling textile-based motion capture and soft robotic applications.

title: FaithSteer-BENCH: A Deployment-Aligned Stress-Testing Benchmark for Inference-Time Steering
summary: Inference-time steering is widely regarded as a lightweight and parameter-free mechanism for controlling large language model (LLM) behavior, and prior work has often suggested that simple activation-level interventions can reliably induce targeted behavioral changes. However, such conclusions are typically drawn under relatively relaxed evaluation settings that overlook deployment constraints, capability trade-offs, and real-world robustness. We therefore introduce \textbf{FaithSteer-BENCH}, a stress-testing benchmark that evaluates steering methods at a fixed deployment-style operating point through three gate-wise criteria: controllability, utility preservation, and robustness. Across multiple models and representative steering approaches, we uncover several systematic failure modes that are largely obscured under standard evaluation, including illusory controllability, measurable cognitive tax on unrelated capabilities, and substantial brittleness under mild instruction-level perturbations, role prompts, encoding transformations, and data scarcity. Gate-wise benchmark results show that existing methods do not necessarily provide reliable controllability in deployment-oriented practical settings. In addition, mechanism-level diagnostics indicate that many steering methods induce prompt-conditional alignment rather than stable latent directional shifts, further explaining their fragility under stress. FaithSteer-BENCH therefore provides a unified benchmark and a clearer analytical lens for future method design, reliability evaluation, and deployment-oriented research in steering.

AI.tldr: FaithSteer-BENCH is a stress-testing benchmark for inference-time LLM steering, evaluated at a fixed deployment-style point across gate-wise criteria; it uncovers failures like illusory controllability, cognitive tax, and fragility under perturbations, and shows that current methods may lack reliable controllability in deployment settings.
AI.motivation: To address shortcomings of prior evaluation that ignore deployment constraints, capability trade-offs, and real-world robustness in inference-time steering.
AI.method: Assess multiple models and steering approaches at a fixed deployment-like operating point using gate-wise criteria: controllability, utility preservation, robustness; apply perturbations (instruction-level changes, role prompts, encoding transformations, data scarcity); include mechanism-level diagnostics to examine latent vs prompt-conditional effects.
AI.result: Systematic failure modes identified: illusory controllability, measurable cognitive tax on unrelated capabilities, substantial brittleness under perturbations; evidence that steering methods do not guarantee reliable controllability; many methods produce prompt-conditional rather than stable latent shifts.
AI.conclusion: FaithSteer-BENCH provides a unified framework for evaluating steering reliability in deployment contexts and offers a clearer lens for future method design and deployment-oriented research.

title: LRConv-NeRV: Low Rank Convolution for Efficient Neural Video Compression
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Neural Representations for Videos (NeRV) encode entire video sequences within neural network parameters, offering an alternative paradigm to conventional video codecs. However, the convolutional decoder of NeRV remains computationally expensive and memory intensive, limiting its deployment in resource-constrained environments. This paper proposes LRConv-NeRV, an efficient NeRV variant that replaces selected dense 3x3 convolutional layers with structured low-rank separable convolutions, trained end-to-end within the decoder architecture. By progressively applying low-rank factorization from the largest to earlier decoder stages, LRConv-NeRV enables controllable trade-offs between reconstruction quality and efficiency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that applying LRConv only to the final decoder stage reduces decoder complexity by 68%, from 201.9 to 64.9 GFLOPs, and model size by 9.3%, while incurring negligible quality loss and achieving approximately 9.2% bitrate reduction. Under INT8 post-training quantization, LRConv-NeRV preserves reconstruction quality close to the dense NeRV baseline, whereas more aggressive factorization of early decoder stages leads to disproportionate quality degradation. Compared to existing work under layer-aligned settings, LRConv-NeRV achieves a more favorable efficiency versus quality trade-off, offering substantial GFLOPs and parameter reductions while maintaining higher PSNR/MS-SSIM and improved temporal stability. Temporal flicker analysis using LPIPS further shows that the proposed solution preserves temporal coherence close to the NeRV baseline, results establish LRConv-NeRV as a potential architectural alternative for efficient neural video decoding under low-precision and resource-constrained settings.

AI.tldr: LRConv-NeRV reduces decoder complexity of neural video representations by substituting selected dense 3x3 convolutions with structured low-rank separable convolutions, enabling controllable efficiency–quality trade-offs with minor quality loss or bitrate gains, especially at the final decoder stage and under INT8 quantization.
AI.motivation: NeRV encodes entire video sequences in neural network parameters, but its convolutional decoder is computationally expensive and memory-intensive, hindering deployment on resource-constrained devices. There is a need to improve efficiency without sacrificing reconstruction quality or temporal stability.
AI.method: Progressively apply structured low-rank factorization to selected dense 3×3 convolutions in the decoder, starting from the largest (later) decoder stages and moving to earlier ones. Train end-to-end within the decoder architecture. Evaluate on standard metrics (GFLOPs, parameter count, PSNR/MS-SSIM, LPIPS for temporal flicker, and bitrate) and assess robustness under INT8 post-training quantization. Compare to layer-aligned baselines.
AI.result: Applying LRConv only to the final decoder stage yields a 68% reduction in decoder GFLOPs (from 201.9 to 64.9 GFLOPs) and a 9.3% reduction in model size, with negligible quality loss and about 9.2% bitrate reduction. Under INT8 PTQ, reconstruction quality remains close to the dense NeRV baseline; aggressive factorization of early stages degrades quality. Compared to existing layer-aligned methods, LRConv-NeRV offers a more favorable efficiency–quality trade-off, preserving PSNR/MS-SSIM and temporal stability close to NeRV, and showing better temporal coherence per LPIPS analysis.
AI.conclusion: LRConv-NeRV demonstrates that structured low-rank convolutional factorization within the NeRV decoder is a viable path toward efficient neural video decoding in resource-constrained and low-precision settings, maintaining competitive quality and bitrate improvements while significantly reducing compute and parameters. Future work could explore broader factorization schemes, hardware-aware optimizations, and extensions to other neural video representations.

title: ARTEMIS: A Neuro Symbolic Framework for Economically Constrained Market Dynamics
summary: Deep learning models in quantitative finance often operate as black boxes, lacking interpretability and failing to incorporate fundamental economic principles such as no-arbitrage constraints. This paper introduces ARTEMIS (Arbitrage-free Representation Through Economic Models and Interpretable Symbolics), a novel neuro-symbolic framework combining a continuous-time Laplace Neural Operator encoder, a neural stochastic differential equation regularised by physics-informed losses, and a differentiable symbolic bottleneck that distils interpretable trading rules. The model enforces economic plausibility via two novel regularisation terms: a Feynman-Kac PDE residual penalising local no-arbitrage violations, and a market price of risk penalty bounding the instantaneous Sharpe ratio. We evaluate ARTEMIS against six strong baselines on four datasets: Jane Street, Optiver, Time-IMM, and DSLOB (a synthetic crash regime). Results demonstrate ARTEMIS achieves state-of-the-art directional accuracy, outperforming all baselines on DSLOB (64.96%) and Time-IMM (96.0%). A comprehensive ablation study confirms each component's contribution: removing the PDE loss reduces directional accuracy from 64.89% to 50.32%. Underperformance on Optiver is attributed to its long sequence length and volatility-focused target. By providing interpretable, economically grounded predictions, ARTEMIS bridges the gap between deep learning's power and the transparency demanded in quantitative finance.

AI.tldr: ARTEMIS is a neuro-symbolic, arbitrage-free model for quantitative finance that enforces no-arbitrage via PDE residuals and controls risk via market price of risk, delivering interpretable trading rules and state-of-the-art directional accuracy on multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: Address black-box DL models in finance and incorporate fundamental economic principles (no-arbitrage, risk-reward) into a trainable, interpretable framework.
AI.method: Components: continuous-time Laplace Neural Operator encoder; neural SDE regularized with physics-informed losses; differentiable symbolic bottleneck extracting trading rules; two regularizers: Feynman-Kac PDE residual and market price of risk penalty.
AI.result: Outperforms six baselines on four datasets; DSLOB 64.96% directional accuracy; Time-IMM 96.0%; ablation: PDE loss absence drops accuracy from 64.89% to 50.32%; Optiver underperforms due to long sequence length and volatility-focused target.
AI.conclusion: ARTEMIS provides interpretable, economically grounded predictions, bridging DL power and required transparency in quantitative finance.

title: Contact Status Recognition and Slip Detection with a Bio-inspired Tactile Hand
summary: Stable and reliable grasp is critical to robotic manipulations especially for fragile and glazed objects, where the grasp force requires precise control as too large force possibly damages the objects while small force leads to slip and fall-off. Although it is assumed the objects to manipulate is grasped firmly in advance, slip detection and timely prevention are necessary for a robot in unstructured and universal environments. In this work, we addressed this issue by utilizing multimodal tactile feedback from a five-fingered bio-inspired hand. Motivated by human hands, the tactile sensing elements were distributed and embedded into the soft skin of robotic hand, forming 24 tactile channels in total. Different from the threshold method that was widely employed in most existing works, we converted the slip detection problem to contact status recognition in combination with binning technique first and then detected the slip onset time according to the recognition results. After the 24-channel tactile signals passed through discrete wavelet transform, 17 features were extracted from different time and frequency bands. With the optimal 120 features employed for status recognition, the test accuracy reached 96.39% across three different sliding speeds and six kinds of materials. When applied to four new unseen materials, a high accuracy of 91.95% was still achieved, which further validated the generalization of our proposed method. Finally, the performance of slip detection is verified based on the trained model of contact status recognition.

AI.tldr: A multimodal, 24-channel tactile sensing system on a five-finger hand detects slip by reframing slip detection as contact-status recognition and using wavelet-based features, achieving high accuracy and good generalization to unseen materials.
AI.motivation: Ensure reliable grasp of fragile/glazed objects by timely slip detection in unstructured environments; address limitations of threshold-based slip detection; leverage contact-status recognition with binning.
AI.method: Five-finger bio-inspired hand with 24 tactile channels embedded in soft skin; convert slip detection to contact-status recognition using binning; apply discrete wavelet transform to signals; extract 17 features; select 120 optimal features for status recognition; train and test across three sliding speeds and six materials; evaluate on four unseen materials.
AI.result: Test accuracy 96.39% across existing materials/speeds; 91.95% accuracy on unseen materials; slip onset time inferred from contact-status recognition; demonstrates robustness and generalization of the approach.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effective use of multimodal tactile feedback and wavelet-based features for robust slip detection via contact-status recognition, with strong generalization to new materials and potential for real-time grasp stabilization.

title: MemArchitect: A Policy Driven Memory Governance Layer
comment: This is an on going research work and will be updated periodically
summary:
Persistent Large Language Model (LLM) agents expose a critical governance gap in memory management. Standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) frameworks treat memory as passive storage, lacking mechanisms to resolve contradictions, enforce privacy, or prevent outdated information ("zombie memories") from contaminating the context window.
  We introduce MemArchitect, a governance layer that decouples memory lifecycle management from model weights. MemArchitect enforces explicit, rule-based policies, including memory decay, conflict resolution, and privacy controls.
  We demonstrate that governed memory consistently outperforms unmanaged memory in agentic settings, highlighting the necessity of structured memory governance for reliable and safe autonomous systems.

AI.tldr: MemArchitect introduces a governance layer that decouples memory lifecycle from model weights, applying rule-based policies to memory (decay, conflict resolution, privacy) to prevent contradictions and outdated data from contaminating context—leading to improved agent reliability.
AI.motivation: Addresses a governance gap in persistent LLM agents where standard RAG treats memory as passive storage, causing contradictions, privacy issues, and zombie memories that degrade performance and safety.
AI.method: Proposes MemArchitect as an independent layer between the memory store and the model, implementing explicit memory governance policies (decay, conflict resolution, privacy controls) within existing retrieval-augmented generation frameworks.
AI.result: Governed memory consistently outperforms unmanaged memory in agentic settings, demonstrating the value of structured memory governance for reliability and safety in autonomous systems.
AI.conclusion: Structured memory governance is necessary for reliable, safe autonomous LLM-based systems; decoupling memory management from model weights enables explicit policy enforcement and better overall performance.

title: CycleCap: Improving VLMs Captioning Performance via Self-Supervised Cycle Consistency Fine-Tuning
summary: Visual-Language Models (VLMs) have achieved remarkable progress in image captioning, visual question answering, and visual reasoning. Yet they remain prone to vision-language misalignment, often producing overly generic or hallucinated descriptions. Existing approaches address this via instruction tuning-requiring costly, large-scale annotated datasets or via complex test-time frameworks for caption refinement. In this work, we revisit image-text alignment through the lens of cycle consistency: given an image and a caption generated by an image-to-text model, the backward mapping through a text-to-image model should reconstruct an image that closely matches the original. In our setup, a VLM serves as the image-to-text component, while a pre-trained text-to-image model closes the loop by reconstructing the image from the generated caption. Building on this, we introduce CycleCap, a fine-tuning scheme to improve image captioning using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a reward based on the similarity between the original and reconstructed images, computed on-the-fly. Unlike previous work that uses cycle consistency loss for preference dataset construction, our method leverages cycle consistency directly as a self-supervised training signal. This enables the use of raw images alone, eliminating the need for curated image-text datasets, while steering the VLM to produce more accurate and grounded text descriptions. Applied to four VLMs ranging from 1B to 7B parameters, CycleCap yields consistent improvements across captioning and hallucination benchmarks, surpassing state-of-the-art methods that rely on supervised cycle consistency training.

AI.tldr: CycleCap uses cycle-consistent supervision with a VLM generating captions and a text-to-image model reconstructing images to provide a self-supervised reward signal, improving grounding and reducing hallucinations without curated image-text data.
AI.motivation: Vision-language models still suffer from misalignment and hallucinations; existing approaches rely on costly instruction tuning or complex test-time refinement. A self-supervised, cycle-consistent objective could provide a data-efficient path to align captions with images.
AI.method: Fine-tune image-to-text VLMs via CycleCap using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). The reward is the similarity between the original image and the image reconstructed from the caption produced by the VLM, computed on-the-fly. The approach forms a closed loop: image -> caption (via VLM) -> reconstructed image (via a pretrained text-to-image model). This uses raw images only, avoiding curated image-text datasets.
AI.result: CycleCap yields consistent improvements on captioning and hallucination benchmarks across four VLMs ranging from 1B to 7B parameters, outperforming state-of-the-art methods that rely on supervised cycle-consistency training.
AI.conclusion: Self-supervised cycle-consistency provides a robust training signal for grounding and reducing hallucinations in VLMs, enabling data-efficient fine-tuning without curated image-text datasets, and generalizes across model scales.

title: BoundAD: Boundary-Aware Negative Generation for Time Series Anomaly Detection
summary: Contrastive learning methods for time series anomaly detection (TSAD) heavily depend on the quality of negative sample construction. However, existing strategies based on random perturbations or pseudo-anomaly injection often struggle to simultaneously preserve temporal semantic consistency and provide effective decision-boundary supervision. Most existing methods rely on prior anomaly injection, while overlooking the potential of generating hard negatives near the data manifold boundary directly from normal samples themselves. To address this issue, we propose a reconstruction-driven boundary negative generation framework that automatically constructs hard negatives through the reconstruction process of normal samples. Specifically, the method first employs a reconstruction network to capture normal temporal patterns, and then introduces a reinforcement learning strategy to adaptively adjust the optimization update magnitude according to the current reconstruction state. In this way, boundary-shifted samples close to the normal data manifold can be induced along the reconstruction trajectory and further used for subsequent contrastive representation learning. Unlike existing methods that depend on explicit anomaly injection, the proposed framework does not require predefined anomaly patterns, but instead mines more challenging boundary negatives from the model's own learning dynamics. Experimental results show that the proposed method effectively improves anomaly representation learning and achieves competitive detection performance on the current dataset.

AI.tldr: Reconstruction-driven boundary negative generation for TSAD improves contrastive learning by mining hard negatives from normal data via reconstruction trajectory and RL, achieving competitive detection without explicit anomaly injection.
AI.motivation: Existing negative sampling strategies (random perturbations, pseudo-anomalies) struggle to preserve temporal semantics and provide proper boundary supervision; many methods rely on predefined anomalies and ignore mining hard negatives near the data manifold.
AI.method: Train a reconstruction network to model normal temporal patterns; apply reinforcement learning to adaptively adjust optimization update magnitude based on reconstruction state; generate boundary-shifted negatives along reconstruction trajectory; use these negatives for contrastive learning.
AI.result: Experiments show improved anomaly representation learning and competitive detection performance on the datasets.
AI.conclusion: The framework can mine harder negatives from the model's own learning dynamics without predefined anomaly patterns, enhancing TSAD performance.

title: Graph-of-Constraints Model Predictive Control for Reactive Multi-agent Task and Motion Planning
comment: 8 main content pages, 4 main content figures, camera ready version submitted to IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA 2026)
summary: Sequences of interdependent geometric constraints are central to many multi-agent Task and Motion Planning (TAMP) problems. However, existing methods for handling such constraint sequences struggle with partially ordered tasks and dynamic agent assignments. They typically assume static assignments and cannot adapt when disturbances alter task allocations. To overcome these limitations, we introduce Graph-of-Constraints Model Predictive Control (GoC-MPC), a generalized sequence-of-constraints framework integrated with MPC. GoC-MPC naturally supports partially ordered tasks, dynamic agent coordination, and disturbance recovery. By defining constraints over tracked 3D keypoints, our method robustly solves diverse multi-agent manipulation tasks-coordinating agents and adapting online from visual observations alone, without relying on training data or environment models. Experiments demonstrate that GoC-MPC achieves higher success rates, significantly faster TAMP computation, and shorter overall paths compared to recent baselines, establishing it as an efficient and robust solution for multi-agent manipulation under real-world disturbances. Our supplementary video and code can be found at https://sites.google.com/view/goc-mpc/home .

AI.tldr: GoC-MPC generalizes a sequence-of-constraints MPC framework to handle partially ordered tasks, dynamic agent coordination, and disturbance recovery in multi-agent manipulation by defining constraints over tracked 3D keypoints, enabling online adaptation from visual observations without training or environment models; it yields higher success, faster planning, and shorter paths than baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing constraint-sequence methods assume static task allocations and struggle with partial ordering and disturbances; there is a need for a framework that adapts online to disturbances and dynamic agent assignments in multi-agent TAMP.
AI.method: Graph-of-Constraints Model Predictive Control (GoC-MPC) integrated with MPC. Constraints are defined over tracked 3D keypoints to coordinate multiple agents, support partial task ordering, dynamic coordination, and disturbance recovery. The system operates online using visual observations without requiring training data or explicit environment models.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows GoC-MPC achieves higher success rates, significantly faster TAMP computation, and shorter overall paths compared with recent baselines, demonstrating robustness to disturbances and improved efficiency in multi-agent manipulation.
AI.conclusion: GoC-MPC is an efficient and robust solution for multi-agent manipulation under real-world disturbances, capable of handling partially ordered tasks and dynamic agent coordination with online adaptation from vision, without relying on training data or environment models.

title: Understanding the Theoretical Foundations of Deep Neural Networks through Differential Equations
summary: Deep neural networks (DNNs) have achieved remarkable empirical success, yet the absence of a principled theoretical foundation continues to hinder their systematic development. In this survey, we present differential equations as a theoretical foundation for understanding, analyzing, and improving DNNs. We organize the discussion around three guiding questions: i) how differential equations offer a principled understanding of DNN architectures, ii) how tools from differential equations can be used to improve DNN performance in a principled way, and iii) what real-world applications benefit from grounding DNNs in differential equations. We adopt a two-fold perspective spanning the model level, which interprets the whole DNN as a differential equation, and the layer level, which models individual DNN components as differential equations. From these two perspectives, we review how this framework connects model design, theoretical analysis, and performance improvement. We further discuss real-world applications, as well as key challenges and opportunities for future research.

AI.tldr: Differential equations provide a principled, unifying framework to understand, analyze, and improve DNNs, using model-level (treating networks as ODEs) and layer-level (components as differential equations) perspectives; the survey links architecture, theory, and applications and discusses challenges and future directions.
AI.motivation: DNNs lack a rigorous theoretical foundation; differential equations offer a natural mathematical language to model continuous-time dynamics and structured transformations underlying DNNs; enables principled design and analysis.
AI.method: A two-fold perspective: model-level (whole DNN as a differential equation) and layer-level (DNN components modeled as differential equations). Systematic review of theoretical connections, architectural implications, performance improvements via DE tools, and real-world applications; discussion of challenges and opportunities.
AI.result: Synthesizes how the DE framework clarifies architectural choices, informs analysis and improvement, and identifies domains where grounding DNNs in differential equations yields tangible benefits; provides a map of challenges and opportunities for future work.
AI.conclusion: Differential equations offer a coherent foundation for understanding and advancing DNNs, with a roadmap for theory-guided design, analysis, and application across domains; future research should address remaining open questions and real-world deployment.

title: Fast and Generalizable NeRF Architecture Selection for Satellite Scene Reconstruction
summary: Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) have emerged as a powerful approach for photorealistic 3D reconstruction from multi-view images. However, deploying NeRF for satellite imagery remains challenging. Each scene requires individual training, and optimizing architectures via Neural Architecture Search (NAS) demands hours to days of GPU time. While existing approaches focus on architectural improvements, our SHAP analysis reveals that multi-view consistency, rather than model architecture, determines reconstruction quality. Based on this insight, we develop PreSCAN, a predictive framework that estimates NeRF quality prior to training using lightweight geometric and photometric descriptors. PreSCAN selects suitable architectures in < 30 seconds with < 1 dB prediction error, achieving 1000$\times$ speedup over NAS. We further demonstrate PreSCAN's deployment utility on edge platforms (Jetson Orin), where combining its predictions with offline cost profiling reduces inference power by 26% and latency by 43% with minimal quality loss. Experiments on DFC2019 datasets confirm that PreSCAN generalizes across diverse satellite scenes without retraining.

AI.tldr: PreSCAN predicts NeRF training quality for satellite imagery, enabling fast architecture selection and edge deployment with minimal retraining.
AI.motivation: NeRF-based satellite 3D reconstruction requires per-scene training and expensive NAS; need robust generalization and practical deployment.
AI.method: Introduce PreSCAN, a predictive framework using lightweight geometric and photometric descriptors to estimate NeRF quality before training; uses these predictions to select architectures; evaluations include NAS-free selection and edge deployment.
AI.result: Architecture selection in under 30 seconds with <1 dB prediction error; 1000x NAS speedup; on Jetson Orin, combining predictions with offline profiling reduces inference power by 26% and latency by 43% with minimal quality loss; generalizes across diverse satellite scenes on DFC2019 without retraining.
AI.conclusion: Multi-view consistency, not architecture, primarily governs NeRF quality for satellite imagery; PreSCAN enables fast, generalizable architecture selection and practical deployment.

title: Tula: Optimizing Time, Cost, and Generalization in Distributed Large-Batch Training
summary: Distributed training increases the number of batches processed per iteration either by scaling-out (adding more nodes) or scaling-up (increasing the batch-size). However, the largest configuration does not necessarily yield the best performance. Horizontal scaling introduces additional communication overhead, while vertical scaling is constrained by computation cost and device memory limits. Thus, simply increasing the batch-size leads to diminishing returns: training time and cost decrease initially but eventually plateaus, creating a knee-point in the time/cost versus batch-size pareto curve. The optimal batch-size therefore depends on the underlying model, data and available compute resources. Large batches also suffer from worse model quality due to the well-known generalization gap. In this paper, we present Tula, an online service that automatically optimizes time, cost, and convergence quality for large-batch training of convolutional models. It combines parallel-systems modeling with statistical performance prediction to identify the optimal batch-size. Tula predicts training time and cost within 7.5-14% error across multiple models, and achieves up to 20x overall speedup and improves test accuracy by 9% on average over standard large-batch training on various vision tasks, thus successfully mitigating the generalization gap and accelerating training at the same time.

AI.tldr: Tula: an online service that auto-optimizes batch size for large-batch CNN training by combining a parallel-system model with statistical performance prediction, delivering substantial speedups and accuracy gains.
AI.motivation: Distributed training with larger batch sizes faces diminishing returns due to communication overhead, memory limits, and a generalization gap. There is a need to automatically optimize time, cost, and convergence quality.
AI.method: Online service that fuses parallel-systems modeling with data-driven performance prediction to identify the optimal batch size for convolutional models across vision tasks.
AI.result: Accurately predicts training time and cost within 7.5–14% across models; up to 20x overall speedup; average test accuracy improvement of 9% compared to standard large-batch training.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that modeling-assisted automatic batch-size optimization can effectively balance time, cost, and convergence quality in large-batch training of vision models.

title: Efficient and Versatile Quadrupedal Skating: Optimal Co-design via Reinforcement Learning and Bayesian Optimization
summary: In this paper, we present a hardware-control co-design approach that enables efficient and versatile roller skating on quadrupedal robots equipped with passive wheels. Passive-wheel skating reduces leg inertia and improves energy efficiency, particularly at high speeds. However, the absence of direct wheel actuation tightly couples mechanical design and control. To unlock the full potential of this modality, we formulate a bilevel optimization framework: an upper-level Bayesian Optimization searches the mechanical design space, while a lower-level Reinforcement Learning trains a motor control policy for each candidate design. The resulting design-policy pairs not only outperform human-engineered baselines, but also exhibit versatile behaviors such as hockey stop (rapid braking by turning sideways to maximize friction) and self-aligning motion (automatic reorientation to improve energy efficiency in the direction of travel), offering the first system-level study of dynamic skating motion on quadrupedal robots.

AI.tldr: Bilevel hardware–control co-design for quadrupedal robots with passive wheels enables efficient, dynamic roller skating, using Bayesian optimization for design and reinforcement learning for control; design-policy pairs outperform baselines and enable hockey stop and self-aligning motion.
AI.motivation: Passive wheels reduce leg inertia and improve energy efficiency at high speeds, but their absence of direct wheel actuation creates tight coupling between mechanical design and control. A co-design framework is needed to unlock roller skating capabilities on quadrupeds.
AI.method: A bilevel optimization: an upper-level Bayesian Optimization searches the mechanical design space; for each candidate design, a lower-level Reinforcement Learning policy is trained to control the motors, producing a design–policy pair.
AI.result: The resulting design–policy pairs outperform human-engineered baselines and exhibit versatile behaviors such as hockey stop and self-aligning motion, representing the first system-level study of dynamic skating motion on quadrupedal robots.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility and benefits of hardware–control co-design for passive-wheel skating on quadrupeds, enabling energy-efficient high-speed locomotion and opening avenues for further exploration of dynamic skating behaviors.

title: Large-Scale Analysis of Political Propaganda on Moltbook
summary: We present an NLP-based study of political propaganda on Moltbook, a Reddit-style platform for AI agents. To enable large-scale analysis, we develop LLM-based classifiers to detect political propaganda, validated against expert annotation (Cohen's $κ$= 0.64-0.74). Using a dataset of 673,127 posts and 879,606 comments, we find that political propaganda accounts for 1% of all posts and 42% of all political content. These posts are concentrated in a small set of communities, with 70% of such posts falling into five of them. 4% of agents produced 51% of these posts. We further find that a minority of these agents repeatedly post highly similar content within and across communities. Despite this, we find limited evidence that comments amplify political propaganda.

AI.tldr: LLM-based detectors identify political propaganda on Moltbook; propaganda is rare (1% of posts) but accounts for 42% of political content, concentrated in a few communities and a small number of agents; some repetitive posting; comments have limited amplificatory effect.
AI.motivation: Quantify scale and dynamics of political propaganda in an AI-agent Reddit-like platform; develop scalable detection method validated against expert labeling.
AI.method: Train/validate LLM-based classifiers for propaganda detection; Cohen's kappa 0.64–0.74; analyze dataset of 673,127 posts and 879,606 comments; quantify distribution across posts, communities, and agents; assess content similarity and interaction effects.
AI.result: Propaganda is 1% of posts and 42% of political content; 70% of propaganda posts in five communities; 4% of agents produce 51% of propaganda posts; minority post highly similar content; comments show limited amplification.
AI.conclusion: Propaganda is highly concentrated among a small set of communities and agents; LLM-based detection is feasible; engagement via comments has limited amplifying impact; implications for moderation and platform design.

title: Unrolled Reconstruction with Integrated Super-Resolution for Accelerated 3D LGE MRI
summary: Accelerated 3D late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) MRI requires robust reconstruction methods to recover thin atrial structures from undersampled k-space data. While unrolled model-based networks effectively integrate physics-driven data consistency with learned priors, they operate at the acquired resolution and may fail to fully recover high-frequency detail. We propose a hybrid unrolled reconstruction framework in which an Enhanced Deep Super-Resolution (EDSR) network replaces the proximal operator within each iteration of the optimization loop, enabling joint super-resolution enhancement and data consistency enforcement. The model is trained end-to-end on retrospectively undersampled preclinical 3D LGE datasets and compared against compressed sensing, Model-Based Deep Learning (MoDL), and self-guided Deep Image Prior (DIP) baselines. Across acceleration factors, the proposed method consistently improves PSNR and SSIM over standard unrolled reconstruction and better preserves fine cardiac structures, leading to improved LA (left atrium) segmentation performance. These results demonstrate that integrating super-resolution priors directly within model-based reconstruction provides measurable gains in accelerated 3D LGE MRI.

AI.tldr: A hybrid unrolled reconstruction for accelerated 3D LGE MRI uses an Enhanced Deep Super-Resolution (EDSR) network as the proximal operator within each optimization iteration to jointly perform super-resolution and data consistency, trained end-to-end on retrospective undersampled 3D LGE data. It outperforms CS, MoDL, and self-guided DIP baselines in PSNR/SSIM and improves left atrium segmentation by better preserving thin cardiac structures.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitation of standard unrolled, physics-informed reconstructions that operate at acquired resolution and may fail to recover high-frequency details essential for thin atrial structures in accelerated 3D LGE MRI.
AI.method: A hybrid unrolled optimization where the proximal operator is replaced by an Enhanced Deep Super-Resolution (EDSR) network, enabling joint super-resolution and data-consistency enforcement. End-to-end training on retrospectively undersampled preclinical 3D LGE datasets, comparing against compressed sensing (CS), Model-Based Deep Learning (MoDL), and self-guided Deep Image Prior (DIP) baselines.
AI.result: The proposed method consistently improves PSNR and SSIM over standard unrolled reconstruction across acceleration factors, better preserves fine cardiac structures, and yields improved left atrium (LA) segmentation performance.
AI.conclusion: Integrating super-resolution priors directly within model-based reconstruction provides measurable gains in accelerated 3D LGE MRI, indicating that joint SR and data consistency can enhance anatomical fidelity under undersampling.

title: VC-Soup: Value-Consistency Guided Multi-Value Alignment for Large Language Models
summary: As large language models (LLMs) increasingly shape content generation, interaction, and decision-making across the Web, aligning them with human values has become a central objective in trustworthy AI. This challenge becomes even more pronounced when aligning multiple, potentially conflicting human values. Although recent approaches, such as reward reweighting, prompt-based supervised fine-tuning, and model merging, attempt to tackle multi-value alignment, they still face two major limitations: (1) training separate models for each value combination is prohibitively expensive; (2) value conflicts substantially degrade alignment performance. These limitations make it difficult to achieve favorable trade-offs across diverse human values. To address these challenges, we revisit multi-value alignment from the perspective of value consistency in data and propose VC-soup, a data filtering and parameter merging framework grounded in value-consistent learning. We first design a value consistency metric based on the cosine similarity between the reward-gap vector of each preference pair and an all-ones vector, which quantifies its cross-value coherence. We then filter out low-consistency preference pairs in each value dataset and train on the remaining data to obtain smooth, value-consistent policy models that better preserve linear mode connectivity. Finally, we linearly combine these policies and apply Pareto filtering across values to obtain solutions with balanced multi-value performance. Extensive experiments and theoretical analysis demonstrate that VC-soup effectively mitigates conflicts and consistently outperforms existing multi-value alignment methods.

AI.tldr: VC-soup is a data-centric approach for multi-value alignment that filters value-consistent preferences and merges policies to balance diverse human values, mitigating value conflicts and outperforming prior methods.
AI.motivation: Align LLMs to multiple, potentially conflicting human values without training many separate models; existing methods suffer from high cost and degraded alignment due to value conflicts.
AI.method: Introduce value consistency (VC) metric defined as cosine similarity between the reward-gap vector for each preference pair and an all-ones vector. Filter out low-consistency pairs within each value dataset, train on the remaining data to obtain value-consistent policies that preserve linear mode connectivity, then linearly combine these policies and apply Pareto filtering across values to obtain balanced solutions.
AI.result: The proposed VC-soup framework reduces conflicts between values and yields superior multi-value alignment performance compared with existing methods, supported by extensive experiments and theoretical analysis.
AI.conclusion: Value consistency-based data filtering plus linear policy merging provides a scalable, Pareto-efficient route to multi-value alignment, mitigating cross-value conflicts with improved performance over prior approaches.

title: MemoAct: Atkinson-Shiffrin-Inspired Memory-Augmented Visuomotor Policy for Robotic Manipulation
summary: Memory-augmented robotic policies are essential in handling memory-dependent tasks. However, existing approaches typically rely on simple observation window extensions, struggling to simultaneously achieve precise task state tracking and robust long-horizon retention. To overcome these challenges, inspired by the Atkinson-Shiffrin memory model, we propose MemoAct, a hierarchical memory-based policy that leverages distinct memory tiers to tackle specific bottlenecks. Specifically, lossless short-term memory ensures precise task state tracking, while compressed long-term memory enables robust long-horizon retention. To enrich the evaluation landscape, we construct MemoryRTBench based on RoboTwin 2.0, specifically tailored to assess policy capabilities in task state tracking and long-horizon retention. Extensive experiments across simulated and real-world scenarios demonstrate that MemoAct achieves superior performance compared to both existing Markovian baselines and history-aware policies. The project page is \href{https://tlf-tlf.github.io/MemoActPage/}{available}.

AI.tldr: MemoAct introduces a hierarchical, memory-based policy with lossless short-term memory and compressed long-term memory to improve task-state tracking and long-horizon retention in memory-dependent robotic tasks; evaluated on MemoryRTBench built from RoboTwin 2.0, showing superior performance over Markovian and history-aware baselines.
AI.motivation: Robotic policies often fail to simultaneously ensure precise task-state tracking and robust long-horizon retention due to relying on simple observation windows. The Atkinson–Shiffrin memory model motivates a tiered memory approach to address distinct bottlenecks.
AI.method: Propose MemoAct, a two-tier memory architecture with lossless short-term memory for accurate state tracking and compressed long-term memory for retaining information over long horizons; develop MemoryRTBench on RoboTwin 2.0 to evaluate policy capabilities in state tracking and horizon retention; conduct experiments in simulated and real-world settings, comparing against Markovian baselines and history-aware policies.
AI.result: MemoAct delivers superior performance across both simulated and real-world scenarios, outperforming existing Markovian baselines and history-aware policies.
AI.conclusion: A hierarchical memory design with distinct memory tiers effectively tackles the dual challenges of precise task-state tracking and long-horizon retention in robotic policies; the MemoryRTBench benchmark enables robust evaluation; findings support broader adoption of memory-tiered architectures with potential extensions to more complex tasks.

title: Interpretability without actionability: mechanistic methods cannot correct language model errors despite near-perfect internal representations
comment: 27 pages, 5 figures, 10 tables. Code available at https://github.com/sanjaybasu/interpretability-triage
summary: Language models encode task-relevant knowledge in internal representations that far exceeds their output performance, but whether mechanistic interpretability methods can bridge this knowledge-action gap has not been systematically tested. We compared four mechanistic interpretability methods -- concept bottleneck steering (Steerling-8B), sparse autoencoder feature steering, logit lens with activation patching, and linear probing with truthfulness separator vector steering (Qwen 2.5 7B Instruct) -- for correcting false-negative triage errors using 400 physician-adjudicated clinical vignettes (144 hazards, 256 benign). Linear probes discriminated hazardous from benign cases with 98.2% AUROC, yet the model's output sensitivity was only 45.1%, a 53-percentage-point knowledge-action gap. Concept bottleneck steering corrected 20% of missed hazards but disrupted 53% of correct detections, indistinguishable from random perturbation (p=0.84). SAE feature steering produced zero effect despite 3,695 significant features. TSV steering at high strength corrected 24% of missed hazards while disrupting 6% of correct detections, but left 76% of errors uncorrected. Current mechanistic interpretability methods cannot reliably translate internal knowledge into corrected outputs, with implications for AI safety frameworks that assume interpretability enables effective error correction.

AI.tldr: Mechanistic interpretability methods fail to reliably translate internal task knowledge into corrected outputs; large knowledge-action gap observed; some methods offer limited corrections but cause substantial collateral disruption; overall unreliable for error correction in high-stakes tasks.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether four mechanistic interpretability methods can bridge the knowledge-action gap in a high-stakes clinical triage task and assess their reliability for correcting false-negative errors.
AI.method: Evaluation of four methods (concept bottleneck steering Steerling-8B, sparse autoencoder feature steering, logit lens with activation patching, linear probing with truthfulness separator vector steering on Qwen 2.5 7B Instruct) using 400 physician-adjudicated clinical vignettes (144 hazards, 256 benign). Measured: AUROC of linear probes distinguishing hazards vs benign; output sensitivity; effect of each method on correcting missed hazards and disrupting correct detections; reported significance (e.g., p=0.84 for random perturbation in CB).
AI.result: Linear probes achieved 98.2% AUROC but only 45.1% output sensitivity, reflecting a 53-percentage-point knowledge-action gap. Concept bottleneck steering corrected 20% of missed hazards but disrupted 53% of correct detections (not distinguishing from random, p=0.84). SAE feature steering had zero effect despite 3,695 significant features. TSV steering at high strength corrected 24% of missed hazards and disrupted 6% of correct detections, leaving 76% of errors uncorrected.
AI.conclusion: Current mechanistic interpretability methods cannot reliably translate internal knowledge into corrected outputs in this setting; implications for AI safety frameworks that assume interpretability enables effective error correction; drives need for better methods to align internal representations with actionable outputs.

title: VISTA: Validation-Guided Integration of Spatial and Temporal Foundation Models with Anatomical Decoding for Rare-Pathology VCE Event Detection
summary: Capsule endoscopy event detection is challenging because diagnostically relevant findings are sparse, visually heterogeneous, and embedded in long, noisy video streams, while evaluation is performed at the event level rather than by frame accuracy alone. We therefore formulate the RARE-VISION task as a metric-aligned event detection problem instead of a purely frame-wise classification task. Our framework combines two complementary backbones, EndoFM-LV for local temporal context and DINOv3 ViT-L/16 for strong frame-level visual semantics, followed by a Diverse Head Ensemble, Validation-Guided Hierarchical Fusion, and Anatomy-Aware Temporal Event Decoding. The fusion stage uses validation-derived class-wise model weighting, backbone weighting, and probability calibration, while the decoding stage applies temporal smoothing, anatomical constraints, threshold refinement, and per-label event generation to produce stable event predictions. Validation ablations indicate that complementary backbones, validation-guided fusion, and anatomy-aware temporal decoding all contribute to event-level performance. On the official hidden test set, the proposed method achieved an overall temporal mAP@0.5 of 0.3530 and temporal mAP@0.95 of 0.3235.

AI.tldr: Treats capsule endoscopy event detection as metric-aligned event detection rather than frame-wise classification, achieving improved event-level performance via multi-backbone fusion and anatomy-aware decoding.
AI.motivation: Diagnostically relevant findings are sparse and heterogeneous in long noisy videos; evaluation at the event level is more meaningful than frame-level accuracy.
AI.method: Dual backbones: EndoFM-LV for local temporal context and DINOv3 ViT-L/16 for strong frame-level semantics; a Diverse Head Ensemble; Validation-Guided Hierarchical Fusion; and Anatomy-Aware Temporal Event Decoding. Fusion uses validation-derived weights and probability calibration; decoding enforces temporal smoothing, anatomical constraints, threshold refinement, and per-label event generation.
AI.result: On official hidden test set: temporal mAP@0.5 = 0.3530; temporal mAP@0.95 = 0.3235. Ablations indicate contributions from complementary backbones, validation-guided fusion, and anatomy-aware temporal decoding.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework advances event-level detection in capsule endoscopy by aligning evaluation with temporal events and by integrating complementary representations with validation-guided and anatomy-informed post-processing; performance gains are demonstrated via ablations and test-set results, though generalization and computational cost may be considerations.

title: LLM-Augmented Computational Phenotyping of Long Covid
summary:
Phenotypic characterization is essential for understanding heterogeneity in chronic diseases and for guiding personalized interventions. Long COVID, a complex and persistent condition, yet its clinical subphenotypes remain poorly understood. In this work, we propose an LLM-augmented computational phenotyping framework ``Grace Cycle'' that iteratively integrates hypothesis generation, evidence extraction, and feature refinement to discover clinically meaningful subgroups from longitudinal patient data. The framework identifies three distinct clinical phenotypes, Protected, Responder, and Refractory, based on 13,511 Long Covid participants. These phenotypes exhibit pronounced separation in peak symptom severity, baseline disease burden, and longitudinal dose-response patterns, with strong statistical support across multiple independent dimensions.
  This study illustrates how large language models can be integrated into a principled, statistically grounded pipeline for phenotypic screening from complex longitudinal data. Note that the proposed framework is disease-agnostic and offers a general approach for discovering clinically interpretable subphenotypes.

AI.tldr: LLM-augmented framework Grace Cycle discovers three interpretable Long COVID subphenotypes (Protected, Responder, Refractory) from 13,511 patients using an iterative hypothesis-evidence-feature loop with statistically robust separation across longitudinal data.
AI.motivation: Address heterogeneity in chronic diseases, especially long COVID, to enable personalized interventions by leveraging LLMs for hypothesis generation, evidence extraction, and feature refinement.
AI.method: Iterative loop combining hypothesis generation, evidence extraction, and feature refinement (Grace Cycle) guided by LLMs, applied to longitudinal patient data; identification of subphenotypes; statistical validation across multiple dimensions; disease-agnostic framework.
AI.result: Three distinct phenotypes (Protected, Responder, Refractory) with pronounced separation in peak symptom severity, baseline disease burden, and longitudinal dose-response patterns; strong statistical support across independent dimensions.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can be integrated into a principled, statistically grounded pipeline for phenotypic screening from complex longitudinal data; framework is disease-agnostic and generalizable to discovering clinically interpretable subphenotypes.

title: Robotic Agentic Platform for Intelligent Electric Vehicle Disassembly
summary: Electric vehicles (EV) create an urgent need for scalable battery recycling, yet disassembly of EV battery packs remains largely manual due to high design variability. We present our Robotic Agentic Platform for Intelligent Disassembly (RAPID), designed to investigate perception-driven manipulation, flexible automation, and AI-assisted robot programming in realistic recycling scenarios. The system integrates a gantry-mounted industrial manipulator, RGB-D perception, and an automated nut-running tool for fastener removal on a full-scale EV battery pack. An open-vocabulary object detection pipeline achieves 0.9757 mAP50, enabling reliable identification of screws, nuts, busbars, and other components. We experimentally evaluate (n=204) three one-shot fastener removal strategies: taught-in poses (97% success rate, 24 min duration), one-shot vision execution (57%, 29 min), and visual servoing (83%, 36 min), comparing success rate and disassembly time for the battery's top cover fasteners. To support flexible interaction, we introduce agentic AI specifications for robotic disassembly tasks, allowing LLM agents to translate high-level instructions into robot actions through structured tool interfaces and ROS services. We evaluate SmolAgents with GPT-4o-mini and Qwen 3.5 9B/4B on edge hardware. Tool-based interfaces achieve 100% task completion, while automatic ROS service discovery shows 43.3% failure rates, highlighting the need for structured robot APIs for reliable LLM-driven control. This open-source platform enables systematic investigation of human-robot collaboration, agentic robot programming, and increasingly autonomous disassembly workflows, providing a practical foundation for research toward scalable robotic battery recycling.

AI.tldr: RAPID is an open-source robotic platform integrating perception, flexible automation, and agentic AI for scalable EV battery disassembly, with a gantry manipulator, RGB-D sensing, and one-shot fastener strategies evaluated; highlights needs for structured APIs for LLM-driven robotics.
AI.motivation: Battery recycling demand meets high design variability in EV packs; perception-driven manipulation and autonomous programming are needed to scale disassembly and reduce manual labor.
AI.method: System integrates a gantry-mounted manipulator, RGB‑D perception, automated nut-running tool; open-vocabulary object detection (0.9757 mAP50) for screws, nuts, busbars; compares taught-in poses, one-shot vision, and visual servoing; introduces agentic AI specifications enabling LLMs to translate high-level tasks into robot actions via structured interfaces and ROS; evaluates SmolAgents (GPT-4o-mini, Qwen variants) on edge hardware; tool-based interfaces yield full task completion; ROS discovery shows 43.3% failures.
AI.result: Open-vocabulary detector achieves 0.9757 mAP50; one-shot fastener removal: taught-in poses 97% (24 min), one-shot vision 57% (29 min), visual servoing 83% (36 min); tool interfaces achieved 100% task completion; ROS service discovery had 43.3% failures across experiments.
AI.conclusion: Platform enables systematic study of human–robot collaboration and agentic programming for scalable disassembly workflows; emphasizes need for robust robot APIs to realize reliable LLM-driven control in recycling contexts; provides open-source foundation for research in automated battery disassembly.

title: LGESynthNet: Controlled Scar Synthesis for Improved Scar Segmentation in Cardiac LGE-MRI Imaging
summary: Segmentation of enhancement in LGE cardiac MRI is critical for diagnosing various ischemic and non-ischemic cardiomyopathies. However, creating pixel-level annotations for these images is challenging and labor-intensive, leading to limited availability of annotated data. Generative models, particularly diffusion models, offer promise for synthetic data generation, yet many rely on large training datasets and often struggle with fine-grained conditioning control, especially for small or localized features. We introduce LGESynthNet, a latent diffusion-based framework for controllable enhancement synthesis, enabling explicit control over size, location, and transmural extent. Formulated as inpainting using a ControlNet-based architecture, the model integrates: (a) a reward model for conditioning-specific supervision, (b) a captioning module for anatomically descriptive text prompts, and (c) a biomedical text encoder. Trained on just 429 images (79 patients), it produces realistic, anatomically coherent samples. A quality control filter selects outputs with high conditioning-fidelity, which when used for training augmentation, improve downstream segmentation and detection performance, by up-to 6 and 20 points respectively.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based inpainting framework (LGESynthNet) enables controllable synthesis of LGE MRI enhancements with explicit control over size, location, and transmural extent, using a small dataset to augment segmentation/detection.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity and annotation burden in LGE MRI segmentation; enable fine-grained, anatomically coherent synthetic data to improve downstream tasks.
AI.method: Latent diffusion model with a ControlNet-based inpainting architecture; conditioning via (i) reward model for conditioning supervision, (ii) captioning module for anatomically descriptive prompts, and (iii) a biomedical text encoder; trained on 429 images (79 patients); outputs filtered by a quality-control selector.
AI.result: Produces realistic, anatomically coherent samples; augmentation with high-conditioning-fidelity samples improves segmentation by up to 6 points and detection by up to 20 points.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effectiveness of controllable diffusion-based synthesis for medical image augmentation under limited data, potentially applicable to other modalities; underscores conditioning fidelity as key to improving downstream performance.

title: To See or To Please: Uncovering Visual Sycophancy and Split Beliefs in VLMs
summary: When VLMs answer correctly, do they genuinely rely on visual information or exploit language shortcuts? We introduce the Tri-Layer Diagnostic Framework, which disentangles hallucination sources via three metrics: Latent Anomaly Detection (perceptual awareness), Visual Necessity Score (visual dependency, measured via KL divergence), and Competition Score (conflict between visual grounding and instruction following). Using counterfactual interventions (blind, noise, and conflict images) across 7 VLMs and 7,000 model-sample pairs, our taxonomy reveals that 69.6% of samples exhibit Visual Sycophancy--models detect visual anomalies but hallucinate to satisfy user expectations--while zero samples show Robust Refusal, indicating alignment training has systematically suppressed truthful uncertainty acknowledgment. A scaling analysis (Qwen2.5-VL 7B to 72B) shows larger models reduce Language Shortcuts but amplify Visual Sycophancy, demonstrating scale alone cannot resolve the grounding problem. Diagnostic scores further enable a post-hoc selective prediction strategy achieving up to +9.5pp accuracy at 50% coverage with no additional training cost.

AI.tldr: A Tri-Layer Diagnostic Framework disentangles sources of VLM hallucinations via Latent Anomaly Detection, Visual Necessity Score, and Competition Score, using counterfactuals across 7 VLMs (7,000 model-sample pairs). Findings: 69.6% Visual Sycophancy; no Robust Refusal; scaling reduces Language Shortcuts but increases Visual Sycophancy; post-hoc selective prediction improves accuracy at 50% coverage without extra training.
AI.motivation: To separate perceptual (visual) from linguistic shortcuts in visual-language models and quantify grounding failures, addressing whether correct answers rely on real visual cues or language priors.
AI.method: Tri-Layer Diagnostic Framework with three metrics (latent anomaly detection, visual necessity score via KL divergence, competition score). Counterfactual interventions (blind, noise, conflict images) across 7 VLMs and 7,000 model-sample pairs. Scaling analysis from 7B to 72B. Post-hoc selective prediction evaluation (up to 50% coverage).
AI.result: Quantifies grounding failures: 69.6% Visual Sycophancy; zero Robust Refusal observed. Larger models reduce Language Shortcuts but amplify Visual Sycophancy. Diagnostic scores enable post-hoc selective prediction achieving up to +9.5pp accuracy at 50% coverage without extra training.
AI.conclusion: Alignment training suppresses truthful uncertainty acknowledgment; scaling alone cannot resolve grounding problems. The framework provides actionable diagnostics and a potential mitigation path via selective prediction.

title: Scaling Sim-to-Real Reinforcement Learning for Robot VLAs with Generative 3D Worlds
summary: The strong performance of large vision-language models (VLMs) trained with reinforcement learning (RL) has motivated similar approaches for fine-tuning vision-language-action (VLA) models in robotics. Many recent works fine-tune VLAs directly in the real world to avoid addressing the sim-to-real gap. While real-world RL circumvents sim-to-real issues, it inherently limits the generality of the resulting VLA, as scaling scene and object diversity in the physical world is prohibitively difficult. This leads to the paradoxical outcome of transforming a broadly pretrained model into an overfitted, scene-specific policy. Training in simulation can instead provide access to diverse scenes, but designing those scenes is also costly. In this work, we show that VLAs can be RL fine-tuned without sacrificing generality and with reduced labor by leveraging 3D world generative models. Using these models together with a language-driven scene designer, we generate hundreds of diverse interactive scenes containing unique objects and backgrounds, enabling scalable and highly parallel policy learning. Starting from a pretrained imitation baseline, our approach increases simulation success from 9.7% to 79.8% while achieving a 1.25$\times$ speedup in task completion time. We further demonstrate successful sim-to-real transfer enabled by the quality of the generated digital twins together with domain randomization, improving real-world success from 21.7% to 75% and achieving a 1.13$\times$ speedup. Finally, we further highlight the benefits of leveraging the effectively unlimited data from 3D world generative models through an ablation study showing that increasing scene diversity directly improves zero-shot generalization.

AI.tldr: RL-fine-tuning of vision-language-action models in robotics is improved by using 3D world generative models and a language-driven scene designer to generate diverse simulation environments, enabling scalable policy learning and better sim-to-real transfer.
AI.motivation: Real-world fine-tuning yields scene-specific policies and limited generality; simulation offers diversity but is expensive to design; need scalable, diverse, and transferable training environments.
AI.method: Use 3D world generative models plus a language-driven scene designer to generate hundreds of diverse interactive scenes with unique objects/backgrounds; start from pretrained imitation baseline; RL fine-tune; domain randomization; evaluate sim-to-real transfer with digital twins; ablation study on scene diversity and zero-shot generalization.
AI.result: Simulation success rate increases from 9.7% to 79.8%; task completion time speedup 1.25x; real-world success increases from 21.7% to 75% with sim-to-real transfer; 1.13x speedup; ablation shows increased scene diversity improves zero-shot generalization.
AI.conclusion: 3D world generative models with language-driven scene design enable scalable RL fine-tuning of VLA models with maintained generalization, improving sim-to-real transfer and zero-shot generalization while reducing manual scene design labor.

title: From Weak Cues to Real Identities: Evaluating Inference-Driven De-Anonymization in LLM Agents
summary: Anonymization is widely treated as a practical safeguard because re-identifying anonymous records was historically costly, requiring domain expertise, tailored algorithms, and manual corroboration. We study a growing privacy risk that may weaken this barrier: LLM-based agents can autonomously reconstruct real-world identities from scattered, individually non-identifying cues. By combining these sparse cues with public information, agents resolve identities without bespoke engineering. We formalize this threat as \emph{inference-driven linkage} and systematically evaluate it across three settings: classical linkage scenarios (Netflix and AOL), \emph{InferLink} (a controlled benchmark varying task intent, shared cues, and attacker knowledge), and modern text-rich artifacts. Without task-specific heuristics, agents successfully execute both fixed-pool matching and open-ended identity resolution. In the Netflix Prize setting, an agent reconstructs 79.2\% of identities, significantly outperforming a 56.0\% classical baseline. Furthermore, linkage emerges not only under explicit adversarial prompts but also as a byproduct of benign cross-source analysis in \emph{InferLink} and unstructured research narratives. These findings establish that identity inference -- not merely explicit information disclosure -- must be treated as a first-class privacy risk; evaluations must measure what identities an agent can infer.

AI.tldr: Inference-driven linkage enables LLM-based agents to reconstruct real-world identities from sparse cues, outperforming classical baselines; privacy risk extends beyond explicit disclosures.
