AI.motivation: Anonymization has historically protected identities, but inference from non-identifying cues via LLMs may enable re-identification; privacy risk should account for inferred identities.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation across three settings: classical linkage (Netflix and AOL), InferLink benchmark (varying task intent, shared cues, attacker knowledge), and modern text-rich artifacts; assess fixed-pool matching and open-ended identity resolution without task-specific heuristics.
AI.result: Agents achieve high reconstruction rates (e.g., 79.2% in Netflix vs. 56.0% baseline); linkage arises even without adversarial prompts and from benign cross-source analysis; open-ended identity inference is feasible.
AI.conclusion: Identity inference must be treated as a first-class privacy risk; evaluations should measure the identities that can be inferred, not only disclosed data; motivates for new privacy protections and evaluation metrics.

title: Pixel-Accurate Epipolar Guided Matching
summary: Keypoint matching can be slow and unreliable in challenging conditions such as repetitive textures or wide-baseline views. In such cases, known geometric relations (e.g., the fundamental matrix) can be used to restrict potential correspondences to a narrow epipolar envelope, thereby reducing the search space and improving robustness. These epipolar-guided matching approaches have proved effective in tasks such as SfM; however, most rely on coarse spatial binning, which introduces approximation errors, requires costly post-processing, and may miss valid correspondences. We address these limitations with an exact formulation that performs candidate selection directly in angular space. In our approach, each keypoint is assigned a tolerance circle which, when viewed from the epipole, defines an angular interval. Matching then becomes a 1D angular interval query, solved efficiently in logarithmic time with a segment tree. This guarantees pixel-level tolerance, supports per-keypoint control, and removes unnecessary descriptor comparisons. Extensive evaluation on ETH3D demonstrates noticeable speedups over existing approaches while recovering exact correspondence sets.

AI.tldr: An exact angular-space epipolar matching method using per-keypoint tolerance circles and a segment-tree 1D interval query to efficiently select correspondences, achieving pixel-level tolerance and speedups on ETH3D.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of coarse spatial binning in epipolar-guided matching (approximation errors, need for post-processing, missed correspondences) by enabling exact, fast candidate selection.
AI.method: Compute an angular interval for each keypoint relative to the epipole from its tolerance circle; perform 1D angular interval queries via a segment tree to find potential matches; avoids descriptor comparisons; provides per-keypoint control and exact tolerance.
AI.result: Demonstrates noticeable speedups over existing approaches and recovers exact correspondence sets on ETH3D.
AI.conclusion: Exact angular-space epipolar matching is viable, yielding precise correspondences with computational efficiency gains, reducing reliance on coarse binning and post-processing.

title: R2-Dreamer: Redundancy-Reduced World Models without Decoders or Augmentation
comment: 20 pages, 12 figures, 2 tables. Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2026. Code available at https://github.com/NM512/r2dreamer
summary: A central challenge in image-based Model-Based Reinforcement Learning (MBRL) is to learn representations that distill essential information from irrelevant visual details. While promising, reconstruction-based methods often waste capacity on large task-irrelevant regions. Decoder-free methods instead learn robust representations by leveraging Data Augmentation (DA), but reliance on such external regularizers limits versatility. We propose R2-Dreamer, a decoder-free MBRL framework with a self-supervised objective that serves as an internal regularizer, preventing representation collapse without resorting to DA. The core of our method is a redundancy-reduction objective inspired by Barlow Twins, which can be easily integrated into existing frameworks. On DeepMind Control Suite and Meta-World, R2-Dreamer is competitive with strong baselines such as DreamerV3 and TD-MPC2 while training 1.59x faster than DreamerV3, and yields substantial gains on DMC-Subtle with tiny task-relevant objects. These results suggest that an effective internal regularizer can enable versatile, high-performance decoder-free MBRL. Code is available at https://github.com/NM512/r2dreamer.

AI.tldr: R2-Dreamer: a decoder-free MBRL with a self-supervised redundancy-reduction objective (Barlow Twins-inspired) as internal regularizer, achieving competitive performance and faster training without data augmentation, on DeepMind Control Suite and Meta-World; improves on small task-relevant objects.
AI.motivation: Learn compact, task-relevant representations for image-based MBRL by avoiding large, irrelevant visual details and decoder-based reconstruction wastes; move from DA-dependent regularization to an internal self-supervised objective.
AI.method: Introduce a redundancy-reduction objective inspired by Barlow Twins, integrated into a decoder-free MBRL framework; acts as an internal regularizer to prevent representation collapse without relying on data augmentation; can be plugged into existing MBRL pipelines.
AI.result: Empirically competitive with strong baselines (DreamerV3, TD-MPC2) on DeepMind Control Suite and Meta-World; trains 1.59x faster than DreamerV3; shows notable gains on DMC-Subtle with tiny task-relevant objects.
AI.conclusion: An effective internal regularizer enables versatile, high-performance decoder-free MBRL, reducing reliance on data augmentation and improving efficiency; code available for reproduction.

title: Inductance-Based Force Self-Sensing in Fiber-Reinforced Pneumatic Twisted-and-Coiled Actuators
summary: Fiber-reinforced pneumatic twisted-and-coiled actuators (FR-PTCAs) offer high power density and compliance but their strong hysteresis and lack of intrinsic proprioception limit effective closed-loop control. This paper presents a self-sensing FR-PTCA integrated with a conductive nickel wire that enables intrinsic force estimation and indirect displacement inference via inductance feedback. Experimental characterization reveals that the inductance of the actuator exhibits a deterministic, low-hysteresis inductance-force relationship at constant pressures, in contrast to the strongly hysteretic inductance-length behavior. Leveraging this property, this paper develops a parametric self-sensing model and a nonlinear hybrid observer that integrates an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) with constrained optimization to resolve the ambiguity in the inductance-force mapping and estimate actuator states. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves force estimation accuracy comparable to that of external load cells and maintains robust performance under varying load conditions.

AI.tldr: Self-sensing FR-PTCA using a conductive nickel wire enables intrinsic force estimation via low-hysteresis inductance-force behavior at constant pressure; an EKF-based hybrid observer with constrained optimization resolves mapping ambiguity, achieving force-estimation on par with external load cells under varying loads.
AI.motivation: Hysteresis and lack of proprioception in fiber-reinforced pneumatic twisted-and-coiled actuators hinder precise closed-loop control. Intrinsic sensing via inductance feedback could provide direct force information and improve control performance.
AI.method: Integrate a conductive nickel wire into FR-PTCA. Characterize inductance as a function of force and length at fixed pressure, noting a deterministic low-hysteresis inductance-force relation. Develop a parametric self-sensing model and a nonlinear hybrid observer that fuses an Extended Kalman Filter with constrained optimization to disambiguate inductance-force mapping and estimate actuator states.
AI.result: Inductance exhibits a deterministic, low-hysteresis relationship with force at constant pressure, unlike the hysteretic inductance-length behavior. The proposed self-sensing model and EKF-based observer enable force estimation with accuracy comparable to external load cells and maintain robustness under varying loads.
AI.conclusion: An effective intrinsic force sensing framework for FR-PTCAs is demonstrated, enabling improved closed-loop control through inductance-based self-sensing and a hybrid observer that resolves force-position ambiguity under different loading conditions.

title: Inst4DGS: Instance-Decomposed 4D Gaussian Splatting with Multi-Video Label Permutation Learning
summary: We present Inst4DGS, an instance-decomposed 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS) approach with long-horizon per-Gaussian trajectories. While dynamic 4DGS has advanced rapidly, instance-decomposed 4DGS remains underexplored, largely due to the difficulty of associating inconsistent instance labels across independently segmented multi-view videos. We address this challenge by introducing per-video label-permutation latents that learn cross-video instance matches through a differentiable Sinkhorn layer, enabling direct multi-view supervision with consistent identity preservation. This explicit label alignment yields sharp decision boundaries and temporally stable identities without identity drift. To further improve efficiency, we propose instance-decomposed motion scaffolds that provide low-dimensional motion bases per object for long-horizon trajectory optimization. Experiments on Panoptic Studio and Neural3DV show that Inst4DGS jointly supports tracking and instance decomposition while achieving state-of-the-art rendering and segmentation quality. On the Panoptic Studio dataset, Inst4DGS improves PSNR from 26.10 to 28.36, and instance mIoU from 0.6310 to 0.9129, over the strongest baseline.

AI.tldr: Inst4DGS introduces instance-decomposed 4D Gaussian Splatting with cross-video identity alignment via differentiable Sinkhorn and long-horizon per-Gaussian trajectories, plus motion scaffolds for efficiency. It achieves state-of-the-art rendering and instance segmentation on Panoptic Studio and Neural3DV, with notable PSNR and instance mIoU gains.
AI.motivation: Tackle the challenge of inconsistent instance labels across independently segmented multi-view videos in dynamic 4D Gaussian Splatting, enabling direct multi-view supervision and consistent identity preservation.
AI.method: Per-video label-permutation latents are learned via a differentiable Sinkhorn layer to align instance identities across videos; explicit label alignment yields sharp decision boundaries and stable identities; instance-decomposed motion scaffolds provide low-dimensional motion bases for long-horizon trajectory optimization; long-horizon per-Gaussian trajectories.
AI.result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art rendering and segmentation. On Panoptic Studio, PSNR improves from 26.10 to 28.36; instance mIoU improves from 0.6310 to 0.9129; supports joint tracking and instance decomposition; validated on Panoptic Studio and Neural3DV datasets.
AI.conclusion: Explicit cross-view label alignment combined with motion scaffolds enables robust tracking and instance decomposition in 4D Gaussian Splatting, reducing identity drift and improving efficiency and accuracy.

title: Gradient-Informed Temporal Sampling Improves Rollout Accuracy in PDE Surrogate Training
summary: Researchers train neural simulators on uniformly sampled numerical simulation data. But under the same budget, does systematically sampled data provide the most effective information? A fundamental yet unformalized problem is how to sample training data for neural simulators so as to maximize rollout accuracy. Existing data sampling methods either tend to collapse into locally high-information-density regions, or preserve diversity but remain insufficiently model-specific, often leading to performance that is no better than uniform sampling. To address this, we propose a data sampling method tailored to neural simulators, Gradient-Informed Temporal Sampling (GITS). GITS jointly optimizes pilot-model local gradients and set-level temporal coverage, thereby effectively balancing model specificity and dynamical information. Compared with multiple sampling baselines, the data selected by GITS achieves lower rollout error across multiple PDE systems, model backbones and sample ratios. Furthermore, ablation studies demonstrate the necessity and complementarity of the two optimization objectives in GITS. In addition, we analyze the successful sampling patterns of GITS as well as the typical PDE systems and model backbones on which GITS fails.

AI.tldr: GITS: gradient-informed temporal sampling improves neural simulators' rollout accuracy by jointly optimizing local gradient information and set-level temporal coverage, outperforming baselines across PDEs and models.
AI.motivation: Unformalized problem of data sampling for neural simulators; need budget-efficient sampling that maximizes rollout accuracy; existing methods either concentrate on high-information regions or preserve diversity without model specificity.
AI.method: GITS jointly optimizes pilot-model local gradients and temporal coverage; data selection guided by gradient information and coverage objectives; balances model specificity and dynamical information.
AI.result: Outperforms multiple sampling baselines; lower rollout error across various PDE systems, model backbones, and sample ratios; ablation shows both objectives are necessary and complementary.
AI.conclusion: Two optimization objectives are essential for effective data sampling in neural simulators; analysis of sampling patterns and failure cases reveals conditions for success and limitations.

title: TiBCLaG: A Trigger-induced Bistable Compliant Laparoscopic Grasper
summary: Industrial laparoscopic graspers use multi-link rigid mechanisms manufactured to tight tolerances, resulting in high manufacturing and assembly costs. This work presents the design and proof-of-concept validation of a monolithic, fully compliant, bistable, laparoscopic grasper that eliminates the need for multiple rigid links, thereby reducing part count. The device integrates a compliant trigger and a compliant gripper end-effector, coupled via a control push-rod, to achieve stable grasping without continuous user input. The trigger mechanism is synthesized using a Two-Element Beam Constraint Model as a design framework to control the deformation and stiffness of V-beam-like elements. This technique enables elastic energy storage while preventing snap-through instability. The end-effector is designed as a compliant gripper to achieve adaptive grasping through elastic deformation. Jaws' opening-and-closing performance is demonstrated using nonlinear finite element analysis. The laparoscopic design presented here is fabricated using fused deposition 3D printing. The fabricated prototype demonstrates reliable bistable actuation, confirming the feasibility of such compliant laparoscopic grasper architectures.

AI.tldr: Monolithic, fully compliant, bistable laparoscopic grasper that eliminates rigid multi-link mechanisms, enabling stable grasping via elastic energy storage and without continuous input, validated by FEA and a 3D-printed prototype.
AI.motivation: Reduce manufacturing/assembly costs and part count in laparoscopic tools by replacing multi-link rigid mechanisms with a monolithic compliant design while enabling stable, user-input-free grasping.
AI.method: Design a monolithic compliant structure with a compliant trigger and gripper end-effector connected by a control push-rod. Use a Two-Element Beam Constraint Model to tailor deformation and stiffness of V-beam-like elements, enabling energy storage without snap-through. Validate via nonlinear finite element analysis and fabricate a proof-of-concept using fused-deposition 3D printing.
AI.result: Demonstrated reliable bistable actuation and opening/closing performance of the monolithic compliant grasper. FEA confirms functional range, and a fabricated prototype proves feasibility and energy-storage capability.
AI.conclusion: A feasible monolithic, bistable, fully compliant laparoscopic grasper that reduces part count and manufacturing complexity, with potential for simplified sterilization and assembly; future work should optimize performance, durability, and in vivo applicability.

title: From Topic to Transition Structure: Unsupervised Concept Discovery at Corpus Scale via Predictive Associative Memory
summary: Embedding models group text by semantic content, what text is about. We show that temporal co-occurrence within texts discovers a different kind of structure: recurrent transition-structure concepts or what text does. We train a 29.4M-parameter contrastive model on 373 million co-occurrence pairs from 9,766 Project Gutenberg texts (24.96 million passages), mapping pre-trained embeddings into an association space where passages with similar transition structure cluster together. Under capacity constraint (42.75% accuracy), the model must compress across recurring patterns rather than memorise individual co-occurrences. Clustering at six granularities (k=50 to k=2,000) produces a multi-resolution concept map; from broad modes like "direct confrontation" and "lyrical meditation" to precise registers and scene templates like "sailor dialect" and "courtroom cross-examination." At k=100, clusters average 4,508 books each (of 9,766), confirming corpus-wide patterns. Direct comparison with embedding-similarity clustering shows that raw embeddings group by topic while association-space clusters group by function, register, and literary tradition. Unseen novels are assigned to existing clusters without retraining; the association model concentrates each novel into a selective subset of coherent clusters, while raw embedding assignment saturates nearly all clusters. Validation controls address positional, length, and book-concentration confounds. The method extends Predictive Associative Memory (PAM, arXiv:2602.11322) from episodic recall to concept formation: where PAM recalls specific associations, multi-epoch contrastive training under compression extracts structural patterns that transfer to unseen texts, the same framework producing qualitatively different behaviour in a different regime.

AI.tldr: Temporal co-occurrence yields association-space clusters that capture function, register, and literary tradition rather than topical content; produces multi-resolution concept maps of Gutenberg literature; enables unseen novels to map without retraining; extends Predictive Associative Memory (PAM) to concept formation under compression.
AI.motivation: Explore whether text contains recurrent transition-structure concepts shaped by literary form, not just semantic topics, and whether a constrained model can compress and transfer these structural patterns across a large corpus.
AI.method: Train a 29.4M-parameter contrastive model on 373 million co-occurrence pairs extracted from 9,766 Project Gutenberg texts (24.96 million passages). Map pre-trained embeddings into an association space. Perform clustering at six granularities (k=50 to k=2000) to build a multi-resolution concept map. Evaluate on unseen novels, validate against positional/length/book-concentration confounds, and compare to embedding-similarity clustering. Frame results in Predictive Associative Memory (PAM) terms, extending from episodic recall to concept formation under compression.
AI.result: Association-space clusters organize by function, register, and literary tradition rather than by topic. At k=100, clusters average 4,508 books each (among 9,766). Unseen novels map to a selective subset of clusters without retraining; raw embeddings saturate most clusters. Model achieves 42.75% accuracy under a capacity constraint, indicating compression of recurring patterns rather than memorization. The six clustering granularities reveal broad modes (e.g., direct confrontation, lyrical meditation) down to specific templates (sailor dialect, courtroom cross-examination). Validation controls address positional, length, and book-concentration confounds. The work extends PAM from episodic recall to concept formation, showing transferable, structure-focused representations across unseen texts.
AI.conclusion: Temporal co-occurrence-based association modeling yields qualitatively different structure from standard embeddings, producing a scalable, multi-resolution map of literary organization by function and style. This supports a memory-inspired pathway to concept formation and generalization across unseen texts, contrasting topic-focused clustering.

title: Mind the Rarities: Can Rare Skin Diseases Be Reliably Diagnosed via Diagnostic Reasoning?
summary: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) demonstrate strong performance in dermatology; however, evaluating diagnostic reasoning for rare conditions remains largely unexplored. Existing benchmarks focus on common diseases and assess only final accuracy, overlooking the clinical reasoning process, which is critical for complex cases. We address this gap by constructing DermCase, a long-context benchmark derived from peer-reviewed case reports. Our dataset contains 26,030 multi-modal image-text pairs and 6,354 clinically challenging cases, each annotated with comprehensive clinical information and step-by-step reasoning chains. To enable reliable evaluation, we establish DermLIP-based similarity metrics that achieve stronger alignment with dermatologists for assessing differential diagnosis quality. Benchmarking 22 leading LVLMs exposes significant deficiencies across diagnosis accuracy, differential diagnosis, and clinical reasoning. Fine-tuning experiments demonstrate that instruction tuning substantially improves performance while Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) yields minimal gains. Systematic error analysis further reveals critical limitations in current models' reasoning capabilities.

AI.tldr: DermCase is a long-context, case-based dermatology benchmark for LVLMs with 26k image-text pairs and 6.3k cases; introduces DermLIP metrics and shows instruction tuning helps more than DPO; models still struggle with diagnosis, differential diagnosis, and reasoning.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of evaluation for diagnostic reasoning in LVLMs, especially for rare dermatological conditions, beyond final accuracy on common diseases.
AI.method: Construct DermCase from peer-reviewed case reports, yielding 26,030 multimodal image-text pairs and 6,354 clinically challenging cases with comprehensive clinical data and step-by-step reasoning chains; develop DermLIP similarity metrics aligned with dermatologists; benchmark 22 LVLMs; perform fine-tuning experiments (instruction tuning vs. Direct Preference Optimization, DPO); conduct systematic error analysis.
AI.result: Leading LVLMs exhibit deficiencies in diagnosis accuracy, differential diagnosis, and clinical reasoning; instruction tuning substantially improves performance, while DPO yields minimal gains; error analysis highlights key gaps in current models’ reasoning capabilities.
AI.conclusion: DermCase and DermLIP enable reliable, scalable evaluation of dermatology-oriented LVLMs; targeted instruction tuning can improve performance, but robust reasoning for rare conditions remains a challenge; the benchmark exposes critical weaknesses deserving further methodological development.

title: AGRI-Fidelity: Evaluating the Reliability of Listenable Explanations for Poultry Disease Detection
summary: Existing XAI metrics measure faithfulness for a single model, ignoring model multiplicity where near-optimal classifiers rely on different or spurious acoustic cues. In noisy farm environments, stationary artifacts such as ventilation noise can produce explanations that are faithful yet unreliable, as masking-based metrics fail to penalize redundant shortcuts. We propose AGRI-Fidelity, a reliability-oriented evaluation framework for listenable explanations in poultry disease detection without spatial ground truth. The method combines cross-model consensus with cyclic temporal permutation to construct null distributions and compute a False Discovery Rate (FDR), suppressing stationary artifacts while preserving time-localized bioacoustic markers. Across real and controlled datasets, AGRI-Fidelity effectively provides reliability-aware discrimination for all data points versus masking-based metrics.

AI.tldr: Proposes AGRI-Fidelity, a reliability-oriented XAI evaluation for listenable explanations in poultry disease detection; uses cross-model consensus and cyclic temporal permutation to compute FDR, mitigating stationary artifacts and outperforming masking-based faithfulness metrics across real and controlled datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing XAI metrics evaluate faithfulness for a single model and ignore model multiplicity; in noisy farm acoustics, stationary artifacts can yield explanations that are faithful but unreliable. There is a need for reliability-aware evaluation that penalizes redundant shortcuts without requiring spatial ground truth.
AI.method: 1) Build cross-model consensus across near-optimal classifiers to identify explanations common across models. 2) Apply cyclic temporal permutation to construct null distributions that preserve temporal structure while breaking label-explanation associations. 3) Compute False Discovery Rate (FDR) to quantify reliability of explanations. 4) Evaluate listenable explanations for poultry disease detection without spatial ground truth.
AI.result: Across real and controlled datasets, AGRI-Fidelity yields reliability-aware discrimination for data points, suppressing stationary artifacts while preserving time-localized bioacoustic cues, outperforming masking-based metrics.
AI.conclusion: AGRI-Fidelity provides a principled reliability-oriented framework for evaluating listenable explanations under model multiplicity and noisy conditions, enabling more trustworthy explanations in poultry health monitoring and potentially extending to other acoustic domains.

title: Benchmarking Visual Feature Representations for LiDAR-Inertial-Visual Odometry Under Challenging Conditions
summary: Accurate localization in autonomous driving is critical for successful missions including environmental mapping and survivor searches. In visually challenging environments, including low-light conditions, overexposure, illumination changes, and high parallax, the performance of conventional visual odometry methods significantly degrade undermining robust robotic navigation. Researchers have recently proposed LiDAR-inertial-visual odometry (LIVO) frameworks, that integrate LiDAR, IMU, and camera sensors, to address these challenges. This paper extends the FAST-LIVO2-based framework by introducing a hybrid approach that integrates direct photometric methods with descriptor-based feature matching. For the descriptor-based feature matching, this work proposes pairs of ORB with the Hamming distance, SuperPoint with SuperGlue, SuperPoint with LightGlue, and XFeat with the mutual nearest neighbor. The proposed configurations are benchmarked by accuracy, computational cost, and feature tracking stability, enabling a quantitative comparison of the adaptability and applicability of visual descriptors. The experimental results reveal that the proposed hybrid approach outperforms the conventional sparse-direct method. Although the sparse-direct method often fails to converge in regions where photometric inconsistency arises due to illumination changes, the proposed approach still maintains robust performance under the same conditions. Furthermore, the hybrid approach with learning-based descriptors enables robust and reliable visual state estimation across challenging environments.

AI.tldr: Hybrid photometric-descriptor LIVO extends FAST-LIVO2 by integrating direct photometric methods with descriptor-based matching, using ORB, SuperPoint, SuperGlue, LightGlue, and XFeat, outperforming the sparse-direct baseline under challenging illumination.
AI.motivation: Visual odometry in autonomous driving degrades in challenging lighting and high parallax; a hybrid approach combining direct photometry with learning-based and traditional descriptors aims to improve robustness, tracking stability, and accuracy in LIVO systems.
AI.method: Extend FAST-LIVO2 with a hybrid pipeline that fuses direct photometric tracking with descriptor-based matching. Evaluate pairs: ORB+Hamming, SuperPoint+SuperGlue, SuperPoint+LightGlue, and XFeat with mutual nearest neighbor. Benchmark on accuracy, computational cost, and feature tracking stability.
AI.result: The hybrid approach outperforms the conventional sparse-direct method, maintaining robust performance where photometric inconsistency exists due to illumination changes; learning-based descriptors further enhance robustness and reliability of visual state estimation in challenging environments.
AI.conclusion: A hybrid photometric-descriptor strategy improves LIVO robustness and applicability in adverse lighting and parallax conditions, offering a practical path for robust autonomous navigation; however, trade-offs between accuracy and computation depend on descriptor choice.

title: Prune-then-Quantize or Quantize-then-Prune? Understanding the Impact of Compression Order in Joint Model Compression
summary: What happens when multiple compression methods are combined-does the order in which they are applied matter? Joint model compression has emerged as a powerful strategy to achieve higher efficiency by combining multiple methods such as pruning and quantization. A central but underexplored factor in joint model compression is the compression order, or the sequence of different methods within the compression pipeline. Most prior studies have either sidestepped the issue by assuming orthogonality between techniques, while a few have examined them only in highly constrained cases. Consequently, the broader role of compression order in shaping model performance remains poorly understood. In this paper, we address the overlooked problem of compression order and provide both theoretical and empirical analysis. We formulate the problem of optimizing the compression order and introduce the Progressive Intensity Hypothesis, which states that weaker perturbations should precede stronger ones. We provide theoretical guarantees showing that the relative benefit of one order increases with the underlying performance gap. Extensive experiments on both language and vision models validate the hypothesis, and further show its generality to broader setups such as multi-stage compression and mixed-precision quantization.

AI.tldr: Joint model compression order matters: applying weaker perturbations before stronger ones improves efficiency; the paper introduces the Progressive Intensity Hypothesis, provides theory, and validates it empirically across NLP and vision and in multi-stage and mixed-precision settings.
AI.motivation: There is a lack of understanding of how the sequence of compression methods affects performance when combining pruning, quantization, and other techniques; existing work often assumes orthogonality or analyzes only constrained scenarios.
AI.method: Formulate an optimization problem for compression order; propose the Progressive Intensity Hypothesis (weaker perturbations first); derive theoretical guarantees showing the benefit of a given order grows with the performance gap; conduct extensive experiments on language and vision models, including multi-stage compression and mixed-precision quantization.
AI.result: The hypothesis is validated: the relative advantage of an order increases with the underlying performance gap; results generalize across tasks and setups, demonstrating the broader applicability of compression order considerations beyond single-stage pipelines.
AI.conclusion: Compression order is a critical factor in joint model compression. The Progressive Intensity Hypothesis provides a principled guideline for sequencing methods to maximize efficiency gains, with theoretical support and broad empirical validation.

title: SynQ: Accurate Zero-shot Quantization by Synthesis-aware Fine-tuning
summary: How can we accurately quantize a pre-trained model without any data? Quantization algorithms are widely used for deploying neural networks on resource-constrained edge devices. Zero-shot Quantization (ZSQ) addresses the crucial and practical scenario where training data are inaccessible for privacy or security reasons. However, three significant challenges hinder the performance of existing ZSQ methods: 1) noise in the synthetic dataset, 2) predictions based on off-target patterns, and the 3) misguidance by erroneous hard labels. In this paper, we propose SynQ (Synthesis-aware Fine-tuning for Zero-shot Quantization), a carefully designed ZSQ framework to overcome the limitations of existing methods. SynQ minimizes the noise from the generated samples by exploiting a low-pass filter. Then, SynQ trains the quantized model to improve accuracy by aligning its class activation map with the pre-trained model. Furthermore, SynQ mitigates misguidance from the pre-trained model's error by leveraging only soft labels for difficult samples. Extensive experiments show that SynQ provides the state-of-the-art accuracy, over existing ZSQ methods.

AI.tldr: SynQ is a data-free zero-shot quantization method that combats three main ZSQ challenges by: (1) applying a low-pass filter to reduce synthetic data noise, (2) aligning the quantized model's class activation map with the pretrained model, and (3) using soft labels for difficult samples. It achieves state-of-the-art accuracy against prior ZSQ approaches.
AI.motivation: Quantizing pretrained models without access to training data is crucial for privacy/security, but existing ZSQ methods struggle with synthetic data noise, reliance on off-target patterns, and guidance from incorrect hard labels. There is a need for a robust, data-free quantization framework.
AI.method: SynQ consists of three components: (i) noise reduction on synthetic data via a low-pass filter; (ii) alignment of the quantized model's class activation maps (CAMs) with those of the pretrained model to improve localization and class-wise decisions; (iii) soft-label–driven guidance for hard samples to mitigate erroneous hard-label guidance.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that SynQ achieves state-of-the-art accuracy among zero-shot quantization methods, outperforming existing ZSQ approaches.
AI.conclusion: SynQ effectively addresses the core challenges of data-free quantization by filtering synthetic data noise, guiding the quantized model through CAM alignment, and avoiding misguidance from incorrect hard labels, leading to superior performance over prior ZSQ methods.

title: MolRGen: A Training and Evaluation Setting for De Novo Molecular Generation with Reasonning Models
summary: Recent advances in reasoning-based large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated substantial improvements in complex problem-solving tasks. Motivated by these advances, several works have explored the application of reasoning LLMs to drug discovery and molecular design. However, most existing approaches either focus on evaluation or rely on training setups that require ground-truth labels, such as molecule pairs with known property modifications. Such supervision is unavailable in \textit{de novo} molecular generation, where the objective is to generate novel molecules that optimize a desirability score without prior knowledge of high-scoring candidates. To bridge this gap, we introduce MolRGen, a large-scale benchmark and dataset for training and evaluating reasoning-based LLMs on \textit{de novo} molecular generation. Our contributions are threefold. First, we propose a setting to evaluate and train models for \textit{de novo} molecular generation and property prediction. Second, we introduce a novel diversity-aware top-$k$ score that captures both the quality and diversity of generated molecules. Third, we show our setting can be used to train LLMs for molecular generation, training a 24B LLM with reinforcement learning, and we provide a detailed analysis of its performance and limitations.

AI.tldr: MolRGen is a new benchmark and dataset for training and evaluating reasoning-based LLMs on de novo molecular generation, addressing the lack of supervision in this setting; it introduces a diversity-aware top-k score and demonstrates training a 24B LLM with reinforcement learning.
AI.motivation: Existing work on reasoning-based LLMs in drug discovery suffers from reliance on evaluation or supervised data (e.g., labeled molecule pairs). In de novo molecular generation, there is no ground-truth high-scoring candidates. There is a need for benchmarks and training paradigms that enable generation and property prediction without explicit supervision.
AI.method: Propose a training/evaluation setting for de novo molecular generation with LLMs, introduce a diversity-aware top-k score combining quality and diversity of generated molecules, and demonstrate the approach by training a 24B LLM with reinforcement learning and conducting a detailed performance and limitation analysis.
AI.result: The framework enables training LLMs for molecular generation within the proposed MolRGen setting, providing empirical insights into performance, diversity, and limitations of reasoning-based LLMs in this task.
AI.conclusion: MolRGen offers a scalable benchmark and dataset to advance reasoning-based LLMs in de novo molecular design, enabling joint optimization of generation and property prediction and highlighting future directions for evaluation metrics and model capabilities.

title: REST: Receding Horizon Explorative Steiner Tree for Zero-Shot Object-Goal Navigation
summary: Zero-shot object-goal navigation (ZSON) requires navigating unknown environments to find a target object without task-specific training. Prior hierarchical training-free solutions invest in scene understanding (\textit{belief}) and high-level decision-making (\textit{policy}), yet overlook the design of \textit{option}, i.e., a subgoal candidate proposed from evolving belief and presented to policy for selection. In practice, options are reduced to isolated waypoints scored independently: single destinations hide the value gathered along the journey; an unstructured collection obscures the relationships among candidates. Our insight is that the option space should be a \textit{tree of paths}. Full paths expose en-route information gain that destination-only scoring systematically neglects; a tree of shared segments enables coarse-to-fine LLM reasoning that dismisses or pursues entire branches before examining individual leaves, compressing the combinatorial path space into an efficient hierarchy. We instantiate this insight in \textbf{REST} (Receding Horizon Explorative Steiner Tree), a training-free framework that (1) builds an explicit open-vocabulary 3D map from online RGB-D streams; (2) grows an agent-centric tree of safe and informative paths as the option space via sampling-based planning; and (3) textualizes each branch into a spatial narrative and selects the next-best path through chain-of-thought LLM reasoning. Across the Gibson, HM3D, and HSSD benchmarks, REST consistently ranks among the top methods in success rate while achieving the best or second-best path efficiency, demonstrating a favorable efficiency-success balance.

AI.tldr: Proposes REST, a training-free ZSON method that uses a tree-structured set of complete paths (not isolated waypoints) as the option space, enabling en-route information gain and hierarchical reasoning via LLMs to guide navigation; achieves strong efficiency–success trade-offs on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current approaches treat options as isolated destinations, missing how partial paths accumulate value and how hierarchical reasoning can prune search; a tree of paths can expose en-route gains and support coarse-to-fine decision making.
AI.method: Builds an explicit open-vocabulary 3D map from RGB-D streams; grows an agent-centric tree of safe, informative paths via sampling-based planning; textualizes each branch into spatial narratives and uses chain-of-thought LLM reasoning to select the next best path.
AI.result: REST ranks among top methods in success rate and achieves the best or second-best path efficiency across Gibson, HM3D, and HSSD benchmarks, indicating a favorable efficiency–accuracy balance.
AI.conclusion: A tree-structured option space with en-route information gain and LLM-guided reasoning enables effective, training-free ZSON, improving upon traditional waypoint-only planning.

title: AS2 -- Attention-Based Soft Answer Sets: An End-to-End Differentiable Neuro-Soft-Symbolic Reasoning Architecture
summary: Neuro-symbolic artificial intelligence (AI) systems typically couple a neural perception module to a discrete symbolic solver through a non-differentiable boundary, preventing constraint-satisfaction feedback from reaching the perception encoder during training. We introduce AS2 (Attention-Based Soft Answer Sets), a fully differentiable neuro-symbolic architecture that replaces the discrete solver with a soft, continuous approximation of the Answer Set Programming (ASP) immediate consequence operator $T_P$. AS2 maintains per-position probability distributions over a finite symbol domain throughout the forward pass and trains end-to-end by minimizing the fixed-point residual of a probabilistic lift of $T_P$, thereby differentiating through the constraint check without invoking an external solver at either training or inference time. The architecture is entirely free of conventional positional embeddings. Instead, it encodes problem structure through constraint-group membership embeddings that directly reflect the declarative ASP specification, making the model agnostic to arbitrary position indexing. On Visual Sudoku, AS2 achieves 99.89% cell accuracy and 100% constraint satisfaction (verified by Clingo) across 1,000 test boards, using a greedy constrained decoding procedure that requires no external solver. On MNIST Addition with $N \in \{2, 4, 8\}$ addends, AS2 achieves digit accuracy above 99.7% across all scales. These results demonstrate that a soft differentiable fixpoint operator, combined with constraint-aware attention and declarative constraint specification, can match or exceed pipeline and solver-based neuro-symbolic systems while maintaining full end-to-end differentiability.

AI.tldr: AS2: differentiable neuro-symbolic architecture that replaces a discrete ASP solver with a soft, continuous approximation of the ASP immediate consequence operator (T_P); enables end-to-end training and achieves strong results without external solvers.
AI.motivation: To overcome the non-differentiable boundary between neural perception and symbolic constraint solving in neuro-symbolic systems, enabling gradient-based training with constraint satisfaction signals.
AI.method: AS2 uses a soft, differentiable approximation to the ASP immediate consequence operator T_P. It maintains per-position probability distributions over a finite symbol domain, uses a probabilistic lift of T_P during training to compute a fixed-point residual, and optimizes end-to-end without invoking an external solver. Key design features include constraint-group membership embeddings that encode declarative ASP constraints and avoid conventional positional embeddings; inference uses greedy constrained decoding without a solver.
AI.result: On Visual Sudoku: 99.89% cell accuracy and 100% constraint satisfaction (verified by Clingo) over 1,000 test boards. On MNIST Addition with N in {2,4,8}: digit accuracy >99.7% across scales.
AI.conclusion: A soft differentiable fixpoint operator combined with constraint-aware attention and declarative constraints can match or exceed solver-based neuro-symbolic systems while preserving full end-to-end differentiability, with a model architecture that remains agnostic to explicit position indexing.

title: R&D: Balancing Reliability and Diversity in Synthetic Data Augmentation for Semantic Segmentation
summary: Collecting and annotating datasets for pixel-level semantic segmentation tasks are highly labor-intensive. Data augmentation provides a viable solution by enhancing model generalization without additional real-world data collection. Traditional augmentation techniques, such as translation, scaling, and color transformations, create geometric variations but fail to generate new structures. While generative models have been employed to extend semantic information of datasets, they often struggle to maintain consistency between the original and generated images, particularly for pixel-level tasks. In this work, we propose a novel synthetic data augmentation pipeline that integrates controllable diffusion models. Our approach balances diversity and reliability data, effectively bridging the gap between synthetic and real data. We utilize class-aware prompting and visual prior blending to improve image quality further, ensuring precise alignment with segmentation labels. By evaluating benchmark datasets such as PASCAL VOC and BDD100K, we demonstrate that our method significantly enhances semantic segmentation performance, especially in data-scarce scenarios, while improving model robustness in real-world applications. Our code is available at \href{https://github.com/chequanghuy/Enhanced-Generative-Data-Augmentation-for-Semantic-Segmentation-via-Stronger-Guidance}{https://github.com/chequanghuy/Enhanced-Generative-Data-Augmentation-for-Semantic-Segmentation-via-Stronger-Guidance}.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based controllable synthetic data augmentation for semantic segmentation that improves performance and robustness, especially in data-scarce settings, using class-aware prompts and visual prior blending, validated on VOC and BDD100K; code released.
AI.motivation: Reduce labeling burden and improve generalization for pixel-level segmentation, addressing limitations of traditional augmentations and prior generative methods that struggle with pixel-level consistency and label alignment.
AI.method: A pipeline integrating controllable diffusion models guided by class-aware prompts and visual priors to generate synthetic images paired with segmentation labels. The process emphasizes precise alignment with label maps (likely via label-conditioned generation or post-hoc alignment) and uses visual priors to boost realism; evaluation on standard benchmarks to assess gains in data-scarce scenarios.
AI.result: Significant improvements in semantic segmentation performance, particularly when data is scarce, and enhanced robustness to real-world variations; validation on PASCAL VOC and BDD100K; code released.
AI.conclusion: The approach effectively bridges the synthetic-real data gap for pixel-level tasks, offering a practical augmentation strategy that reduces data annotation needs while delivering reliable performance gains.

title: Discovering What You Can Control: Interventional Boundary Discovery for Reinforcement Learning
summary: Selecting relevant state dimensions in the presence of confounded distractors is a causal identification problem: observational statistics alone cannot reliably distinguish dimensions that correlate with actions from those that actions cause. We formalize this as discovering the agent's Causal Sphere of Influence and propose Interventional Boundary Discovery IBD, which applies Pearl's do-operator to the agent's own actions and uses two-sample testing to produce an interpretable binary mask over observation dimensions. IBD requires no learned models and composes with any downstream RL algorithm as a preprocessing step. Across 12 continuous control settings with up to 100 distractor dimensions, we find that: (1) observational feature selection can actively select confounded distractors while discarding true causal dimensions; (2) full-state RL degrades sharply once distractors outnumber relevant features by roughly 3:1 in our benchmarks; and (3)IBD closely tracks oracle performance across all distractor levels tested, with gains transferring across SAC and TD3.

AI.tldr: Interventional Boundary Discovery (IBD) uses Pearl's do-operator on the agent’s actions and two-sample tests to produce a binary mask that isolates the causal sphere of influence of the agent, serving as a model-free preprocessing step for RL in high-distractor settings.
AI.motivation: Observational statistics frequently conflate dimensions correlated with actions with those that cause actions, making feature selection unreliable in the presence of distractors. A causal identification method is needed to reliably distinguish truly causal state features from confounded distractors to improve RL performance and robustness.
AI.method: Define the agent’s Causal Sphere of Influence and apply Pearl’s do-operator to the agent’s own actions. Use two-sample testing to distinguish interventional distributions from observational ones, yielding an interpretable binary mask over observation dimensions. The approach requires no learned models and can be integrated as a preprocessing step for any downstream RL algorithm. Evaluated across 12 continuous-control tasks with up to 100 distractor dimensions, using SAC and TD3.
AI.result: IBD reliably tracks oracle performance across distractor levels, outperforming naive observational feature selection which can select confounded distractors and disrupt full-state RL when distractors outnumber relevant features (roughly 3:1). Gains transfer across SAC and TD3.
AI.conclusion: IBD offers a model-free, interpretable feature-filtering technique that identifies the agent’s causal state dimensions, remains robust to increasing distractors, and generalizes across popular RL algorithms, serving as a practical preprocessing step for improving RL in cluttered observation spaces.

title: CSSDF-Net: Safe Motion Planning Based on Neural Implicit Representations of Configuration Space Distance Field
summary: High-dimensional manipulator operation in unstructured environments requires a differentiable, scene-agnostic distance query mechanism to guide safe motion generation. Existing geometric collision checkers are typically non-differentiable, while workspace-based implicit distance models are hindered by the highly nonlinear workspace--configuration mapping and often suffer from poor convergence; moreover, self-collision and environment collision are commonly handled as separate constraints. We propose Configuration-Space Signed Distance Field-Net (CSSDF-Net), which learns a continuous signed distance field directly in configuration space to provide joint-space distance and gradient queries under a unified geometric notion of safety. To enable zero-shot generalization without environment-specific retraining, we introduce a spatial-hashing-based data generation pipeline that encodes robot-centric geometric priors and supports efficient retrieval of risk configurations for arbitrary obstacle point sets. The learned distance field is integrated into safety-constrained trajectory optimization and receding-horizon MPC, enabling both offline planning and online reactive avoidance. Experiments on a planar arm and a 7-DoF manipulator demonstrate stable gradients, effective collision avoidance in static and dynamic scenes, and practical inference latency for large-scale point-cloud queries, supporting deployment in previously unseen environments.

AI.tldr: Learned configuration-space signed distance field (CSSDF-Net) for high-DoF manipulators to provide differentiable joint-space distance and gradients; uses spatial-hashing data generation for zero-shot generalization to unseen obstacles; integrates with trajectory optimization and MPC; shows effective collision avoidance in static/dynamic scenes with real-time inference.
AI.motivation: Need differentiable, scene-agnostic distance queries in configuration space to guide safe motion in unstructured environments; existing methods are non-differentiable or hard to generalize.
AI.method: Train a CSSDF-Net to represent a continuous SDF in configuration space; use spatial-hashing-based data generation encoding robot-centric priors; unify self-collision and environment collision under a single safety metric; enable zero-shot generalization to arbitrary obstacle point clouds; integrate into safety-constrained planning and receding-horizon MPC.
AI.result: Stable gradients; effective collision avoidance in static/dynamic scenes; practical inference latency for large-scale point-cloud queries; successful deployment in unseen environments.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of jointly modeling configuration-space distances and gradients for real-time safe motion; enables offline planning and online reactive avoidance in high-DoF manipulators.

title: AlignMamba-2: Enhancing Multimodal Fusion and Sentiment Analysis with Modality-Aware Mamba
summary: In the era of large-scale pre-trained models, effectively adapting general knowledge to specific affective computing tasks remains a challenge, particularly regarding computational efficiency and multimodal heterogeneity. While Transformer-based methods have excelled at modeling inter-modal dependencies, their quadratic computational complexity limits their use with long-sequence data. Mamba-based models have emerged as a computationally efficient alternative; however, their inherent sequential scanning mechanism struggles to capture the global, non-sequential relationships that are crucial for effective cross-modal alignment. To address these limitations, we propose \textbf{AlignMamba-2}, an effective and efficient framework for multimodal fusion and sentiment analysis. Our approach introduces a dual alignment strategy that regularizes the model using both Optimal Transport distance and Maximum Mean Discrepancy, promoting geometric and statistical consistency between modalities without incurring any inference-time overhead. More importantly, we design a Modality-Aware Mamba layer, which employs a Mixture-of-Experts architecture with modality-specific and modality-shared experts to explicitly handle data heterogeneity during the fusion process. Extensive experiments on four challenging benchmarks, including dynamic time-series (on the CMU-MOSI and CMU-MOSEI datasets) and static image-related tasks (on the NYU-Depth V2 and MVSA-Single datasets), demonstrate that AlignMamba-2 establishes a new state-of-the-art in both effectiveness and efficiency across diverse pattern recognition tasks, ranging from dynamic time-series analysis to static image-text classification.

AI.tldr: AlignMamba-2 proposes a dual alignment and modality-aware Mixture-of-Experts framework to achieve efficient and effective multimodal fusion for sentiment analysis, with no inference-time overhead and state-of-the-art results on dynamic time-series and static image-text tasks.
AI.motivation: Address efficiency and cross-modal heterogeneity in multimodal sentiment analysis. Transformers have quadratic costs; Mamba is efficient but sequential and struggles with global cross-modal alignment; need global, geometry-aware regularization without adding inference overhead.
AI.method: Introduce dual alignment regularization using Optimal Transport distance and Maximum Mean Discrepancy to align modalities geometrically and statistically. Develop a Modality-Aware Mamba layer based on a Mixture-of-Experts architecture with modality-specific and modality-shared experts to handle data heterogeneity during fusion. Maintain no additional inference-time overhead. Evaluate on CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI (dynamic time-series) and NYU-Depth V2, MVSA-Single (static image-text tasks).
AI.result: Achieves new state-of-the-art performance in both effectiveness and efficiency across diverse tasks, demonstrating strong multimodal fusion and sentiment analysis performance for both dynamic time-series and static image-text datasets.
AI.conclusion: AlignMamba-2 offers an effective and efficient framework for multimodal fusion in sentiment analysis, leveraging geometry-aware and statistics-aware alignment plus a modality-aware MoE to handle heterogeneity without increasing inference cost, with robust performance across varied modalities and data types.

title: Sharpness-Aware Minimization in Logit Space Efficiently Enhances Direct Preference Optimization
summary: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) has emerged as a popular algorithm for aligning pretrained large language models with human preferences, owing to its simplicity and training stability. However, DPO suffers from the recently identified squeezing effect (also known as likelihood displacement), where the probability of preferred responses decreases unintentionally during training. To understand and mitigate this phenomenon, we develop a theoretical framework that models the coordinate-wise dynamics in logit space. Our analysis reveals that negative-gradient updates cause residuals to expand rapidly along high-curvature directions, which underlies the squeezing effect, whereas Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM) can suppress this behavior through its curvature-regularization effect. Building on this insight, we investigate logits-SAM, a computationally efficient variant that perturbs only the output layer with negligible overhead. Extensive experiments on Pythia-2.8B, Mistral-7B, and Gemma-2B-IT across multiple datasets and benchmarks demonstrate that logits-SAM consistently improves the effectiveness of DPO and integrates seamlessly with other DPO variants. Code is available at https://github.com/RitianLuo/logits-sam-dpo.

AI.tldr: DPO's squeezing (likelihood displacement) is analyzed via a logit-space, coordinate-wise dynamic framework; negative-gradient updates amplify residuals along high-curvature directions, driving squeezing; SAM curvature-regularization mitigates this; logits-SAM offers an efficient, output-layer perturbation variant that improves DPO across multiple models; code available.
AI.motivation: To understand the squeezing phenomenon in Direct Preference Optimization and to mitigate it with a principled, efficient method that preserves training stability and improves alignment performance across diverse models.
AI.method: Develop a theoretical framework in logit space to model coordinate-wise dynamics under DPO; identify the role of curvature and negative-gradient updates; relate SAM's curvature-regularization to mitigating squeezing; propose logits-SAM that perturbs only the output layer; validate with extensive experiments on multiple LLMs (Pythia-2.8B, Mistral-7B, Gemma-2B-IT) and datasets; release code.
AI.result: Negative-gradient updates cause residuals to expand along high-curvature directions, causing likelihood displacement and squeezing; SAM suppresses this by curvature regularization; logits-SAM achieves similar benefits with negligible overhead by perturbing only the output layer; across models and benchmarks, it consistently improves DPO effectiveness and integrates with other DPO variants; code provided.
AI.conclusion: Logits-SAM is an efficient, compatible remedy to the squeezing problem in DPO, grounded in a curvature-aware theoretical framework; it enhances DPO performance with minimal computational cost and broad applicability.

title: ROFT-VINS: Robust Feature Tracking-based Visual-Inertial State Estimation for Harsh Environment
summary: SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) and Odometry are important systems for estimating the position of mobile devices, such as robots and cars, utilizing one or more sensors. Particularly in camera-based SLAM or Odometry, effectively tracking visual features is important as it significantly impacts system performance. In this paper, we propose a method that leverages deep learning to robustly track visual features in monocular camera images. This method operates reliably even in textureless environments and situations with rapid lighting changes. Additionally, we evaluate the performance of our proposed method by integrating it into VINS-Fusion (Monocular-Inertial), a commonly used Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO) system.

AI.tldr: A deep-learning based monocular feature-tracking method for SLAM/Odometry, robust to textureless scenes and lighting changes, integrated into VINS-Fusion Monocular-Inertial for evaluation.
AI.motivation: Feature tracking quality is critical for SLAM/Odometry; traditional methods degrade in textureless regions and under rapid illumination changes, limiting robustness and accuracy.
AI.method: A learned feature-tracking module designed for monocular SLAM that operates within a VIO pipeline, with emphasis on robustness to textureless scenes and lighting changes; evaluated by integration with VINS-Fusion (Monocular-Inertial).
AI.result: Preliminary evaluation described as integration into VINS-Fusion to assess robustness and impact on VIO performance; no quantitative metrics provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: A deep-learning based feature tracker can enhance monocular SLAM/Odometry robustness in challenging lighting and texture conditions, and is feasible to integrate with established VIO systems like VINS-Fusion.

title: Cognitive Mismatch in Multimodal Large Language Models for Discrete Symbol Understanding
summary: While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable success in interpreting natural scenes, their ability to process discrete symbols -- the fundamental building blocks of human cognition -- remains a critical open question. Unlike continuous visual data, symbols such as mathematical formulas, chemical structures, and linguistic characters require precise, deeper interpretation. This paper introduces a comprehensive benchmark to evaluate how top-tier MLLMs navigate these "discrete semantic spaces" across five domains: language, culture, mathematics, physics, and chemistry. Our investigation uncovers a counterintuitive phenomenon: models often fail at basic symbol recognition yet succeed in complex reasoning tasks, suggesting they rely on linguistic probability rather than true visual perception. By exposing this "cognitive mismatch", we highlight a significant gap in current AI capabilities: the struggle to truly perceive and understand the symbolic languages that underpin scientific discovery and abstract thought. This work offers a roadmap for developing more rigorous, human-aligned intelligent systems.

AI.tldr: A benchmark shows that top MLLMs struggle with recognizing discrete symbols (e.g., formulas, chemical structures) but can perform higher-level symbolic reasoning, indicating reliance on linguistic probability rather than true visual perception; reveals a cognitive mismatch and a need to ground models in symbol perception across domains.
AI.motivation: To rigorously evaluate whether multimodal LLMs can perceive and interpret discrete symbolic languages that underlie science and human cognition, which are not well captured by existing continuous-visual benchmarks.
AI.method: Introduce a comprehensive benchmark covering five domains (language, culture, mathematics, physics, chemistry) to separately assess basic symbol recognition and higher-level symbolic reasoning in leading MLLMs; analyze patterns to diagnose whether models rely on perceptual understanding or statistical language priors.
AI.result: Across the benchmark, models underperform on basic symbol recognition yet achieve relative strength in complex reasoning tasks, suggesting a cognitive mismatch where linguistic probability drives performance rather than true visual-symbol perception.
AI.conclusion: Current MLLMs lack robust grounding in discrete symbol perception; the study provides a roadmap for developing architectures and training strategies that better align models with human-like symbolic understanding, enabling more rigorous scientific discovery and abstract reasoning.

title: SR-Nav: Spatial Relationships Matter for Zero-shot Object Goal Navigation
summary: Zero-shot object-goal navigation aims to find target objects in unseen environments using only egocentric observation. Recent methods leverage foundation models' comprehension and reasoning capabilities to enhance navigation performance. However, when faced with poor viewpoints or weak semantic cues, foundation models often fail to support reliable reasoning in both perception and planning, resulting in inefficient or failed navigation. We observe that inherent relationships among objects and regions encode structured scene priors, which help agents infer plausible target locations even under partial observations. Motivated by this insight, we propose Spatial Relation-aware Navigation (SR-Nav), a framework that models both observed and experience-based spatial relationships to enhance both perception and planning. Specifically, SR-Nav first constructs a Dynamic Spatial Relationship Graph (DSRG) that encodes the target-centered spatial relationships through the foundation models and updates dynamically with real-time observations. We then introduce a Relation-aware Matching Module. It utilizes relationship matching instead of naive detection, leveraging diverse relationships in the DSRG to verify and correct errors, enhancing visual perception robustness. Finally, we design a Dynamic Relationship Planning Module to reduce the planning search space by dynamically computing the optimal paths based on the DSRG from the current position, thereby guiding planning and reducing exploration redundancy. Experiments on HM3D show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in both success rate and navigation efficiency. The code will be publicly available at https://github.com/Mzyw-1314/SR-Nav

AI.tldr: SR-Nav constructs a Dynamic Spatial Relationship Graph to encode target-centered spatial relations from foundation models and live observations, using a Relation-aware Matching Module and a Dynamic Relationship Planning Module to improve perception and planning in zero-shot object-goal navigation; achieves state-of-the-art on HM3D.
AI.motivation: Foundation-model-driven navigation struggles under poor viewpoints or weak semantic cues; structured scene priors from object-region relationships can guide inference of plausible target locations under partial observations.
AI.method: Build Dynamic Spatial Relationship Graph (DSRG) encoding target-centered spatial relations; update with observations; use Relation-aware Matching Module to verify/correct detections via relationships; use Dynamic Relationship Planning Module to compute optimal paths from the current position based on the DSRG to reduce exploration.
AI.result: Experiments on HM3D show state-of-the-art performance in both success rate and navigation efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Spatial relation-aware navigation leveraging observed and experience-based spatial relations improves perception robustness and planning efficiency; code will be released.

title: Enactor: From Traffic Simulators to Surrogate World Models
summary: Traffic microsimulators are widely used to evaluate road network performance under various ``what-if" conditions. However, the behavior models controlling the actions of the actors are overly simplistic and fails to capture realistic actor-actor interactions. Deep learning-based methods have been applied to model vehicles and pedestrians as ``agents" responding to their surrounding ``environment" (including lanes, signals, and neighboring agents). Although effective in learning actor-actor interaction, these approaches fail to generate physically consistent trajectories over long time periods, and they do not explicitly address the complex dynamics that arise at traffic intersections which is a critical location in urban networks. Inspired by the World Model paradigm, we have developed an actor centric generative model using transformer-based architecture that is able to capture the actor-actor interaction, at the same time understanding the geometry to the traffic intersection to generate physically grounded trajectories that are based on learned behavior. Moreover, we test the model in a live ``simulation-in-the-loop" setting, where we generate the initial conditions of the actors using SUMO and then let the model control the dynamics of the actors. We let the simulation run for 40000 timesteps (4000 seconds), testing the performance of the model on long timerange and evaluating the trajectories on traffic engineering related metrics. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework effectively captures complex actor-actor interactions and generates long-horizon, physically consistent trajectories, while requiring significantly fewer training samples than traditional agent-centric generative approaches. Our model is able to outperform the baseline in traffic related as well as aggregate metrics where our model beats the baseline by more than 10x on the KL-Divergence.

AI.tldr: Actor-centric, transformer-based generative model (World Model-inspired) for traffic microsimulation that captures actor-actor interactions and intersection geometry to produce long-horizon, physically grounded trajectories. Validated in a simulation-in-the-loop with SUMO over 40,000 timesteps, it achieves superior traffic metrics, with >10x KL-divergence improvement over a baseline and requires fewer training samples.
AI.motivation: Current traffic microsimulators rely on simplistic behavior models; deep learning approaches that model agents in an environment struggle to maintain physical consistency over long horizons and often neglect complex intersection dynamics, a critical urban locus. There is a need for geometry-aware, long-horizon, physically grounded trajectory generation.
AI.method: Proposes an actor-centric generative model using a transformer-based architecture within a World Model paradigm to capture actor-actor interactions and intersection geometry. The model generates trajectories that respect physical constraints. Evaluation is conducted in a live simulation-in-the-loop setting: SUMO initializes actors and the model controls their dynamics for 40k timesteps (4000s). Performance is measured with traffic engineering metrics and KL-divergence against a baseline.
AI.result: The framework effectively captures complex actor-actor interactions and produces long-horizon, physically consistent trajectories. It requires significantly fewer training samples than traditional agent-centric approaches. It outperforms the baseline on traffic-related and aggregate metrics, achieving more than a 10x improvement in KL-divergence.
AI.conclusion: The actor-centric transformer-based, World Model-inspired approach offers a data-efficient path to realistic, intersection-aware traffic microsimulation, delivering physically grounded, long-horizon trajectories and strong performance gains over traditional baselines in simulation-in-the-loop settings.

title: Empathetic Motion Generation for Humanoid Educational Robots via Reasoning-Guided Vision--Language--Motion Diffusion Architecture
summary: This article suggests a reasoning-guided vision-language-motion diffusion framework (RG-VLMD) for generating instruction-aware co-speech gestures for humanoid robots in educational scenarios. The system integrates multi-modal affective estimation, pedagogical reasoning, and teaching-act-conditioned motion synthesis to enable adaptive and semantically consistent robot behavior. A gated mixture-of-experts model predicts Valence/Arousal from input text, visual, and acoustic features, which then mapped to discrete teaching-act categories through an affect-driven policy.These signals condition a diffusion-based motion generator using clip-level intent and frame-level instructional schedules via additive latent restriction with auxiliary action-group supervision. Compared to a baseline diffusion model, our proposed method produces more structured and distinctive motion patterns, as verified by motion statics and pairwise distance analysis. Generated motion sequences remain physically plausible and can be retargeted to a NAO robot for real-time execution. The results reveal that reasoning-guided instructional conditioning improves gesture controllability and pedagogical expressiveness in educational human-robot interaction.

AI.tldr: Reasoning-guided vision-language-motion diffusion (RG-VLMD) for instruction-aware co-speech gestures in educational robots; achieves more structured, expressive, and plausible gestures with real-time retargeting.
AI.motivation: To enable adaptive, semantically coherent, pedagogically expressive robot gestures in education by integrating affective estimation, pedagogical reasoning, and diffusion-based motion generation.
AI.method: Gated mixture-of-experts predicts Valence/Arousal from multimodal inputs; maps to teaching-act categories via an affect-driven policy; diffusion-based motion generator conditioned on clip-level intents and frame-level instructional schedules via additive latent restriction with auxiliary action-group supervision; retargetable to NAO.
AI.result: Compared to a baseline diffusion model, the approach yields more structured and distinctive motion patterns, supported by motion statistics and pairwise-distance analyses; motions are physically plausible and can be retargeted to a NAO robot for real-time execution; reasoning-guided instructional conditioning improves gesture controllability and pedagogical expressiveness in educational HRI.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning-guided instructional conditioning enhances gesture controllability and pedagogical expressiveness; RG-VLMD supports adaptive, instruction-aligned co-speech gestures in education-focused human-robot interaction.

title: Cross-Domain Demo-to-Code via Neurosymbolic Counterfactual Reasoning
summary: Recent advances in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have enabled video-instructed robotic programming, allowing agents to interpret video demonstrations and generate executable control code. We formulate video-instructed robotic programming as a cross-domain adaptation problem, where perceptual and physical differences between demonstration and deployment induce procedural mismatches. However, current VLMs lack the procedural understanding needed to reformulate causal dependencies and achieve task-compatible behavior under such domain shifts. We introduce NeSyCR, a neurosymbolic counterfactual reasoning framework that enables verifiable adaptation of task procedures, providing a reliable synthesis of code policies. NeSyCR abstracts video demonstrations into symbolic trajectories that capture the underlying task procedure. Given deployment observations, it derives counterfactual states that reveal cross-domain incompatibilities. By exploring the symbolic state space with verifiable checks, NeSyCR proposes procedural revisions that restore compatibility with the demonstrated procedure. NeSyCR achieves a 31.14% improvement in task success over the strongest baseline Statler, showing robust cross-domain adaptation across both simulated and real-world manipulation tasks.

AI.tldr: NeSyCR is a neurosymbolic counterfactual framework for video-instructed robotic programming that converts demonstrations into symbolic trajectories, uses deployment observations to identify cross-domain incompatibilities via counterfactuals, and proposes verifiable procedural revisions, achieving a 31.14% improvement over Statler across simulated and real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: Perceptual and physical gaps between demonstration domains and deployment environments cause procedural mismatches in video-to-code robotic programming, and current VLMs lack the procedural reasoning to adapt across domains.
AI.method: Abstracts demonstrations into symbolic trajectories; derives counterfactual states from deployment observations to reveal cross-domain incompatibilities; searches the symbolic state space with verifiable checks to propose procedural revisions aligning with the demonstrated procedure.
AI.result: Achieves 31.14% higher task success than the strongest baseline Statler, demonstrating robust cross-domain adaptation in both simulated and real-world manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: NeSyCR provides verifiable, counterfactual-driven procedural adaptation for video-instructed robotics, enabling reliable synthesis of code policies across domain shifts.

title: Learning Consistent Temporal Grounding between Related Tasks in Sports Coaching
summary: Video-LLMs often attend to irrelevant frames, which is especially detrimental for sports coaching tasks requiring precise temporal grounding. Yet obtaining frame-level supervision is challenging: expensive to collect from humans and unreliable from other models. We improve temporal grounding without additional annotations by exploiting the observation that related tasks, such as generation and verification, must attend to the same frames. We enforce this via a self-consistency objective over select visual attention maps of tightly-related tasks. Using VidDiffBench, which provides ground-truth keyframe annotations, we first validate that attention misallocation is a significant bottleneck. We then show that training with our objective yields gains of +3.0%, +14.1% accuracy and +0.9 BERTScore over supervised finetuning across three sports coaching tasks: Exact, FitnessQA, and ExpertAF, even surpassing closed-source models.

AI.tldr: Self-consistency across attention maps of related tasks improves temporal grounding in Video-LLMs without extra annotations, validated on VidDiffBench; yields improvements across three sports coaching tasks and rivals closed-source models.
AI.motivation: Temporal grounding is hindered by attention misallocation; frame-level supervision is expensive; leverage related tasks (generation, verification) to enforce consistent attention without extra labels.
AI.method: Introduce a self-consistency objective that enforces alignment of visual attention maps among closely related tasks (generation and verification). Train on data with existing VidDiffBench ground-truth keyframes to validate and guide attention alignment; no additional frame-level annotations required.
AI.result: On VidDiffBench, attention misallocation is a bottleneck; applying the objective yields improvements of +3.0 percentage points, +14.1% accuracy, and +0.9 BERTScore across three sports coaching tasks (Exact, FitnessQA, ExpertAF), surpassing closed-source models.
AI.conclusion: Self-consistency of attention maps is an effective, annotation-free strategy to improve temporal grounding in Video-LLMs for sports coaching tasks, with potential applicability to other domains.

title: Detection Is Cheap, Routing Is Learned: Why Refusal-Based Alignment Evaluation Fails
summary: Current alignment evaluation mostly measures whether models encode dangerous concepts and whether they refuse harmful requests. Both miss the layer where alignment often operates: routing from concept detection to behavioral policy. We study political censorship in Chinese-origin language models as a natural experiment, using probes, surgical ablations, and behavioral tests across nine open-weight models from five labs. Three findings follow. First, probe accuracy alone is non-diagnostic: political probes, null controls, and permutation baselines can all reach 100%, so held-out category generalization is the informative test. Second, surgical ablation reveals lab-specific routing.
