Removing the political-sensitivity direction eliminates censorship and restores accurate factual output in most models tested, while one model confabulates because its architecture entangles factual knowledge with the censorship mechanism. Cross-model transfer fails, indicating that routing geometry is model- and lab-specific. Third, refusal is no longer the dominant censorship mechanism. Within one model family, hard refusal falls to zero while narrative steering rises to the maximum, making censorship invisible to refusal-only benchmarks. These results support a three-stage descriptive framework: detect, route, generate. Models often retain the relevant knowledge; alignment changes how that knowledge is expressed. Evaluations that audit only detection or refusal therefore miss the routing mechanism that most directly determines behavior.

AI.tldr: Three-stage alignment framework (detect, route, generate) for political censorship in Chinese-origin LMs; exposes limitations of current probing; demonstrates model- and lab-specific routing of political content; censorship often operates via routing rather than refusal; calls for evaluation beyond detect/refusal.
AI.motivation: Clarify where alignment operates beyond detection and refusal; uncover the routing mechanism by which concepts are translated into censored or altered outputs; establish a framework to evaluate alignment more comprehensively.
AI.method: Probing, surgical ablations, and behavioral tests across nine open-weight models from five labs.
AI.result: Probe accuracy alone is non-diagnostic; political probes, null controls, and permutation baselines can all reach 100%, making hold-out generalization informative; surgical ablations reveal lab-specific routing of political sensitivity; cross-model transfer of routing is weak; refusal is no longer the dominant censorship mechanism as narrative steering can maximize censorship while hard refusals drop.
AI.conclusion: Alignments operate via a detect-route-generate process. Models retain relevant political knowledge but how it is expressed is altered by alignment. Evaluations that only assess detection or refusal miss the routing stage that most directly governs behavior.

title: ViTac-Tracing: Visual-Tactile Imitation Learning of Deformable Object Tracing
comment: The paper has been accepted by ICRA2026
summary: Deformable objects often appear in unstructured configurations. Tracing deformable objects helps bringing them into extended states and facilitating the downstream manipulation tasks. Due to the requirements for object-specific modeling or sim-to-real transfer, existing tracing methods either lack generalizability across different categories of deformable objects or struggle to complete tasks reliably in the real world. To address this, we propose a novel visual-tactile imitation learning method to achieve one-dimensional (1D) and two-dimensional (2D) deformable object tracing with a unified model. Our method is designed from both local and global perspectives based on visual and tactile sensing. Locally, we introduce a weighted loss that emphasizes actions maintaining contact near the center of the tactile image, improving fine-grained adjustment. Globally, we propose a tracing task loss that helps the policy to regulate task progression. On the hardware side, to compensate for the limited features extracted from visual information, we integrate tactile sensing into a low-cost teleoperation system considering both the teleoperator and the robot. Extensive ablation and comparative experiments on diverse 1D and 2D deformable objects demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, achieving an average success rate of 80% on seen objects and 65% on unseen objects.

AI.tldr: A visual-tactile imitation learning framework for unified 1D/2D deformable-object tracing, featuring a local contact-centered weighted loss and a global tracing-task loss, integrated with tactile sensing in a low-cost teleoperation system. It demonstrates improved generalization with 80% success on seen objects and 65% on unseen objects.
AI.motivation: Existing deformable-object tracing methods struggle to generalize across object categories and to operate reliably in real-world settings due to reliance on object-specific models or sim-to-real transfer gaps. A unified multimodal approach is proposed to address generalization and robustness.
AI.method: A unified visual-tactile imitation learning framework for 1D and 2D deformable-object tracing. Locally, a weighted loss emphasizes actions maintaining contact near the tactile image center to improve fine-grained adjustment. Globally, a tracing-task loss guides policy to regulate task progression. Hardware integration of tactile sensing into a low-cost teleoperation system complements visual input.
AI.result: Extensive ablation and comparative experiments across diverse 1D and 2D deformable objects show the method’s effectiveness, achieving an average success rate of 80% on seen objects and 65% on unseen objects.
AI.conclusion: The approach enhances generalization and reliability in deformable-object tracing by fusing visual and tactile information with targeted loss designs, enabling more robust manipulation in unstructured configurations; future work may further close the sim-to-real gap and broaden category coverage.

title: Interpretable Prostate Cancer Detection using a Small Cohort of MRI Images
summary: Prostate cancer is a leading cause of mortality in men, yet interpretation of T2-weighted prostate MRI remains challenging due to subtle and heterogeneous lesions. We developed an interpretable framework for automatic cancer detection using a small dataset of 162 T2-weighted images (102 cancer, 60 normal), addressing data scarcity through transfer learning and augmentation. We performed a comprehensive comparison of Vision Transformers (ViT, Swin), CNNs (ResNet18), and classical methods (Logistic Regression, SVM, HOG+SVM). Transfer-learned ResNet18 achieved the best performance (90.9% accuracy, 95.2% sensitivity, AUC 0.905) with only 11M parameters, while Vision Transformers showed lower performance despite substantially higher complexity. Notably, HOG+SVM achieved comparable accuracy (AUC 0.917), highlighting the effectiveness of handcrafted features in small datasets. Unlike state-of-the-art approaches relying on biparametric MRI (T2+DWI) and large cohorts, our method achieves competitive performance using only T2-weighted images, reducing acquisition complexity and computational cost. In a reader study of 22 cases, five radiologists achieved a mean sensitivity of 67.5% (Fleiss Kappa = 0.524), compared to 95.2% for the AI model, suggesting potential for AI-assisted screening to reduce missed cancers and improve consistency. Code and data are publicly available.

AI.tldr: Small, transfer-learning–driven study on 162 T2-weighted prostate MRI images showing ResNet18 (CNN) outperforms Vision Transformers; handcrafted HOG+SVM matches AUC; AI-assisted screening significantly more sensitive than radiologists; code/data released.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity and computational cost in automated prostate cancer detection. Compare multiple architectures (CNNs, ViTs) and classical methods on a single-modality, small dataset to evaluate feasibility of AI-assisted screening.
AI.method: Dataset of 162 T2-weighted prostate MRI slices (102 cancer, 60 normal). Apply transfer learning and augmentation. Compare ViT, Swin Transformer, ResNet18, Logistic Regression, SVM, and HOG+SVM. Evaluate accuracy, sensitivity, and AUC. Conduct a reader study with 22 cases comparing radiologists (n=5) versus AI model; report Fleiss kappa for agreement. Publicly release code and data.
AI.result: Best performance from transfer-learned ResNet18: 90.9% accuracy, 95.2% sensitivity, AUC 0.905 with ~11M parameters. ViTs underperform given higher complexity. HOG+SVM achieves AUC 0.917, with comparable accuracy, highlighting effectiveness of handcrafted features in small datasets. Radiologists achieved 67.5% sensitivity in reader study vs AI 95.2%, indicating strong potential for AI-assisted screening. Code and data are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: In small, single-modality prostate MRI datasets, traditional handcrafted features (HOG) with SVM can match or exceed deep models; transfer learning improves CNN performance on limited data, while ViTs may be inefficient in this context. T2-weighted MRI alone can yield competitive diagnostic performance, reducing acquisition cost and computation. AI-assisted screening could improve detection rates and inter-reader consistency; public release supports reproducibility.

title: On Additive Gaussian Processes for Wind Farm Power Prediction
summary: Population-based Structural Health Monitoring (PBSHM) aims to share information between similar machines or structures. This paper takes a population-level perspective, exploring the use of additive Gaussian processes to reveal variations in turbine-specific and farm-level power models over a collected wind farm dataset. The predictions illustrate patterns in wind farm power generation, which follow intuition and should enable more informed control and decision-making.

AI.tldr: Additive Gaussian processes are applied to PBSHM to model turbine- and farm-level power variations from wind farm data, yielding interpretable population-level insights and potential control benefits.
AI.motivation: PBSHM seeks to share information across similar machines to understand heterogeneity and transfer knowledge. The paper aims to reveal turbine-specific and farm-level variations in wind power models to enable more informed decisions.
AI.method: Use additive Gaussian processes to decompose wind farm power into turbine-specific and farm-level latent components, learned from a collected wind farm dataset, to capture hierarchical structure and shared information.
AI.result: Predictions display intuitive, farm- and turbine-level patterns in power generation, consistent with physical intuition, indicating the model captures meaningful heterogeneity and can inform control and decision-making.
AI.conclusion: An additive GP framework provides a principled, probabilistic approach for population-based SHM in wind farms, enabling knowledge sharing across turbines and farms and supporting improved control, maintenance, and decision-support strategies.

title: "You've got a friend in me": Co-Designing a Peer Social Robot for Young Newcomers' Language and Cultural Learning
summary: Community literacy programs supporting young newcomer children in Canada face limited staffing and scarce one-to-one time, which constrains personalized English and cultural learning support. This paper reports on a co-design study with United for Literacy tutors that informed Maple, a table-top, peer-like Socially Assistive Robot (SAR) designed as a practice partner within tutor-mediated sessions. From shadowing and co-design interviews, we derived newcomer-specific requirements and added them in an integrated prototype that uses short story-based activities, multi-modal scaffolding (speech, facial feedback, gesture), and embedded quizzes that support attention while producing tutor-actionable formative signals. We contribute system design implications for tutor-in-the-loop SARs supporting language socialization in community settings and outline directions for child-centered evaluation in authentic programs.

AI.tldr: Co-design study leading to Maple, a table-top Socially Assistive Robot (SAR) that acts as a practice partner in tutor-mediated sessions for newcomer children in Canada, featuring short story activities, multi-modal scaffolding and embedded quizzes; presents design implications and directions for child-centered evaluation in authentic programs.
AI.motivation: Limited staffing and scarce one-to-one time in community literacy programs for young newcomers in Canada constrain personalized English and cultural learning; a tutor-in-the-loop SAR could extend support and enable scalable, tutor-guided language socialization.
AI.method: Shadowing and co-design interviews with United for Literacy tutors; development of an integrated Maple prototype incorporating newcomer-specific requirements; features include short story-based activities, multi-modal scaffolding (speech, facial feedback, gesture), and embedded quizzes to sustain attention and generate tutor-actionable formative signals.
AI.result: An integrated prototype (Maple) and associated newcomer-specific requirements; the SAR serves as a practice partner within tutor-mediated sessions and yields tutor-actionable signals; the work outlines system design implications for tutor-in-the-loop SARs in community settings and proposes directions for authentic, child-centered evaluation.
AI.conclusion: The study advances design guidance for tutor-in-the-loop SARs that support language socialization in community programs and highlights the need for child-centered, authentic evaluations in real-world educational settings.

title: Correlation-Weighted Multi-Reward Optimization for Compositional Generation
summary: Text-to-image models produce images that align well with natural language prompts, but compositional generation has long been a central challenge. Models often struggle to satisfy multiple concepts within a single prompt, frequently omitting some concepts and resulting in partial success. Such failures highlight the difficulty of jointly optimizing multiple concepts during reward optimization, where competing concepts can interfere with one another. To address this limitation, we propose Correlation-Weighted Multi-Reward Optimization (\ours), a framework that leverages the correlation structure among concept rewards to adaptively weight each attribute concept in optimization. By accounting for interactions among concepts, \ours balances competing reward signals and emphasizes concepts that are partially satisfied yet inconsistently generated across samples, improving compositional generation. Specifically, we decompose multi-concept prompts into pre-defined concept groups (\eg, objects, attributes, and relations) and obtain reward signals from dedicated reward models for each concept. We then adaptively reweight these rewards, assigning higher weights to conflicting or hard-to-satisfy concepts using correlation-based difficulty estimation. By focusing optimization on the most challenging concepts within each group, \ours encourages the model to consistently satisfy all requested attributes simultaneously. We apply our approach to train state-of-the-art diffusion models, SD3.5 and FLUX.1-dev, and demonstrate consistent improvements on challenging multi-concept benchmarks, including ConceptMix, GenEval 2, and T2I-CompBench.

AI.tldr: A correlation-weighted multi-reward optimization framework for text-to-image generation improves compositionality by adaptively reweighting concept rewards based on inter-concept correlations; validated on diffusion models SD3.5 and FLUX.1-dev across ConceptMix, GenEval 2, and T2I-CompBench.
AI.motivation: Text-to-image models often fail to satisfy multiple concepts within a single prompt due to competing reward signals and the difficulty of jointly optimizing multiple objectives; interactions among concepts can hinder compositional generation.
AI.method: Propose Correlation-Weighted Multi-Reward Optimization (CWMRO). Decompose prompts into predefined concept groups (objects, attributes, relations); assign dedicated reward models per concept; estimate correlations to identify hard-to-satisfy or conflicting concepts; adaptively reweight these rewards to focus optimization on challenging concepts within each group during diffusion-model training.
AI.result: Demonstrates consistent improvements in compositional generation on challenging multi-concept benchmarks ConceptMix, GenEval 2, and T2I-CompBench when applied to state-of-the-art diffusion models SD3.5 and FLUX.1-dev.
AI.conclusion: Accounting for inter-concept correlations via adaptive reward weighting improves the model’s ability to satisfy all requested attributes concurrently, yielding more reliable and consistent compositional generation across benchmarks and models.

title: Cell-Type Prototype-Informed Neural Network for Gene Expression Estimation from Pathology Images
summary: Estimating slide- and patch-level gene expression profiles from pathology images enables rapid and low-cost molecular analysis with broad clinical impact. Despite strong results, existing approaches treat gene expression as a mere slide- or spot-level signal and do not incorporate the fact that the measured expression arises from the aggregation of underlying cell-level expression. To explicitly introduce this missing cell-resolved guidance, we propose a Cell-type Prototype-informed Neural Network (CPNN) that leverages publicly available single-cell RNA-sequencing datasets. Since single-cell measurements are noisy and not paired with histology images, we first estimate cell-type prototypes-mean expression profiles that reflect stable gene-gene co-variation patterns.CPNN then learns cell-type compositional weights directly from images and models the relationship between prototypes and observed bulk or spatial expression, providing a biologically grounded and structurally regularized prediction framework. We evaluate CPNN on three slide-level datasets and three patch-level spatial transcriptomics datasets. Across all settings, CPNN achieves the highest performance in terms of Spearman correlation. Moreover, by visualizing the inferred compositional weights, our framework provides interpretable insights into which cell types drive the predicted expression. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/naivete5656/CPNN.

AI.tldr: CPNN uses cell-type prototypes derived from scRNA-seq to predict bulk/spatial gene expression from pathology images, achieving superior Spearman correlations and interpretable cell-type contributions.
AI.motivation: To incorporate cell-level composition into image-based expression inference by leveraging cell-type prototypes learned from single-cell data, addressing the aggregation nature of expression.
AI.method: Construct cell-type prototypes from scRNA-seq as mean expression profiles reflecting gene co-variation; train a neural network that maps image-derived cell-type weights to observed expression via the prototypes, enabling inference without paired scRNA-seq and histology data.
AI.result: CPNN achieves the highest Spearman correlation across three slide-level and three patch-level datasets; it also provides interpretable visualizations of inferred cell-type weights.
AI.conclusion: A biologically grounded, regularized framework with publicly available code that enables rapid, interpretable molecular analysis from histology images.

title: Path-Constrained Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures enable efficient scaling by activating only a subset of parameters for each input. However, conventional MoE routing selects each layer's experts independently, creating N^L possible expert paths -- for N experts across L layers. This far exceeds typical training set sizes, leading to statistical inefficiency as the model may not learn meaningful structure over such a vast path space. To constrain it, we propose \pathmoe, which shares router parameters across consecutive layers. Experiments on 0.9B and 16B parameter models demonstrate consistent improvements on perplexity and downstream tasks over independent routing, while eliminating the need for auxiliary load balancing losses. Analysis reveals that tokens following the same path naturally cluster by linguistic function, with \pathmoe{} producing more concentrated groups, better cross-layer consistency, and greater robustness to routing perturbations. These results offer a new perspective for understanding MoE architectures through the lens of expert paths.

AI.tldr: Path-MoE introduces router-parameter sharing across consecutive layers in sparse MoE to constrain the combinatorial path space, yielding consistent perplexity and downstream gains and eliminating the need for auxiliary load-balancing losses.
AI.motivation: Independent routing in standard MoE creates N^L possible expert paths, causing statistical inefficiency and poor generalization on typical data volumes; sharing routers aims to reduce path-space complexity and improve cross-layer consistency.
AI.method: Propose Path-MoE, which shares router parameters across adjacent layers to induce correlated routing decisions along depth; evaluate on 0.9B and 16B parameter models, comparing against independent routing and without auxiliary load-balancing losses.
AI.result: Consistent improvements in perplexity and downstream tasks over independent routing; no need for extra load-balancing losses; tokens following the same path cluster by linguistic function; Path-MoE yields more concentrated path groups, better cross-layer consistency, and robustness to routing perturbations.
AI.conclusion: Sharing routers across consecutive layers reframes MoE analysis around expert paths, reducing statistical inefficiency and yielding more structured routing with better performance and robustness.

title: V-Dreamer: Automating Robotic Simulation and Trajectory Synthesis via Video Generation Priors
summary: Training generalist robots demands large-scale, diverse manipulation data, yet real-world collection is prohibitively expensive, and existing simulators are often constrained by fixed asset libraries and manual heuristics. To bridge this gap, we present V-Dreamer, a fully automated framework that generates open-vocabulary, simulation-ready manipulation environments and executable expert trajectories directly from natural language instructions. V-Dreamer employs a novel generative pipeline that constructs physically grounded 3D scenes using large language models and 3D generative models, validated by geometric constraints to ensure stable, collision-free layouts. Crucially, for behavior synthesis, we leverage video generation models as rich motion priors. These visual predictions are then mapped into executable robot trajectories via a robust Sim-to-Gen visual-kinematic alignment module utilizing CoTracker3 and VGGT. This pipeline supports high visual diversity and physical fidelity without manual intervention. To evaluate the generated data, we train imitation learning policies on synthesized trajectories encompassing diverse object and environment variations. Extensive evaluations on tabletop manipulation tasks using the Piper robotic arm demonstrate that our policies robustly generalize to unseen objects in simulation and achieve effective sim-to-real transfer, successfully manipulating novel real-world objects.

AI.tldr: Automated, open-vocabulary manipulation data generation from natural language enabling sim-to-real policy learning via an end-to-end generative pipeline (LLM + 3D models + video priors + sim-to-gen alignment).
AI.motivation: Bridge the data gap for training generalist robots by providing diverse, high-fidelity manipulation data without costly real-world collection or manual asset creation.
AI.method: Pipeline that (1) builds physically grounded 3D scenes from natural language using LLMs and 3D generators with geometric validation; (2) leverages video generation models as motion priors; (3) converts visual predictions into executable robot trajectories via Sim-to-Gen alignment using CoTracker3 and VGGT; (4) trains imitation policies on synthetic trajectories with varied objects/environments; (5) evaluates on tabletop tasks with a Piper arm and demonstrates sim-to-real transfer.
AI.result: Policies learned on synthesized trajectories generalize to unseen objects in simulation and effectively transfer to real-world manipulation of novel objects; high visual diversity and physical fidelity without manual intervention.
AI.conclusion: Shows that automated, open-vocabulary data generation can enable robust imitation-learning pipelines and enhance sim-to-real transfer for manipulation, reducing manual asset creation and policy data collection requirements.

title: Reasonably reasoning AI agents can avoid game-theoretic failures in zero-shot, provably
summary: AI agents are increasingly deployed in interactive economic environments characterized by repeated AI-AI interactions. Despite AI agents' advanced capabilities, empirical studies reveal that such interactions often fail to stably induce a strategic equilibrium, such as a Nash equilibrium. Post-training methods have been proposed to induce a strategic equilibrium; however, it remains impractical to uniformly apply an alignment method across diverse, independently developed AI models in strategic settings. In this paper, we provide theoretical and empirical evidence that off-the-shelf reasoning AI agents can achieve Nash-like play zero-shot, without explicit post-training. Specifically, we prove that `reasonably reasoning' agents, i.e., agents capable of forming beliefs about others' strategies from previous observation and learning to best respond to these beliefs, eventually behave along almost every realized play path in a way that is weakly close to a Nash equilibrium of the continuation game. In addition, we relax the common-knowledge payoff assumption by allowing stage payoffs to be unknown and by having each agent observe only its own privately realized stochastic payoffs, and we show that we can still achieve the same on-path Nash convergence guarantee. We then empirically validate the proposed theories by simulating five game scenarios, ranging from a repeated prisoner's dilemma game to stylized repeated marketing promotion games. Our findings suggest that AI agents naturally exhibit such reasoning patterns and therefore attain stable equilibrium behaviors intrinsically, obviating the need for universal alignment procedures in many real-world strategic interactions.

AI.tldr: Reasoning-based AI agents can reach Nash-like play in repeated AI–AI interactions without post-training, under realistic assumptions, with zero-shot convergence on most play paths; robust to unknown payoffs and private signals; validated by simulations across multiple games.
AI.motivation: Tackle the practical challenge of inducing strategic equilibrium in heterogeneous, independently developed AI models without applying a uniform post-training alignment across models.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis showing that reasonably reasoning agents (who form beliefs about others’ strategies and best-respond to them) converge to Nash-like behavior on almost all continuation-paths. The model allows stage payoffs to be unknown and agents to observe only their own private realized payoffs. Empirical validation via simulations in five game scenarios (including repeated prisoner's dilemma and marketing-promotion-like games).
AI.result: Proves zero-shot convergence to Nash-like behavior along on-path play for a broad class of reasoning agents; demonstrates robustness to unknown payoffs; empirical results align with theory across multiple strategic settings.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning-based agents intrinsically exhibit equilibrium-like behavior in repeated strategic interactions, reducing or eliminating the need for universal alignment procedures in many real-world multi-agent systems.

title: MedQ-UNI: Toward Unified Medical Image Quality Assessment and Restoration via Vision-Language Modeling
summary: Existing medical image restoration (Med-IR) methods are typically modality-specific or degradation-specific, failing to generalize across the heterogeneous degradations encountered in clinical practice. We argue this limitation stems from the isolation of Med-IR from medical image quality assessment (Med-IQA), as restoration models without explicit quality understanding struggle to adapt to diverse degradation types across modalities. To address these challenges, we propose MedQ-UNI, a unified vision-language model that follows an assess-then-restore paradigm, explicitly leveraging Med-IQA to guide Med-IR across arbitrary modalities and degradation types. MedQ-UNI adopts a multimodal autoregressive dual-expert architecture with shared attention: a quality assessment expert first identifies degradation issues through structured natural language descriptions, and a restoration expert then conditions on these descriptions to perform targeted image restoration. To support this paradigm, we construct a large-scale dataset of approximately 50K paired samples spanning three imaging modalities and five restoration tasks, each annotated with structured quality descriptions for joint Med-IQA and Med-IR training, along with a 2K-sample benchmark for evaluation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that a single MedQ-UNI model, without any task-specific adaptation, achieves state-of-the-art restoration performance across all tasks while generating superior descriptions, confirming that explicit quality understanding meaningfully improves restoration fidelity and interpretability.

AI.tldr: Unified multimodal MedQ-UNI assess-then-restore model guided by Med-IQA; single model achieves SOTA restoration across multiple modalities and degradation types without task-specific adaptation.
AI.motivation: Address generalization gaps in medical image restoration caused by modality- and degradation-specific methods; leverage Med-IQA to inform restoration and improve robustness and interpretability.
AI.method: Autoregressive dual-expert architecture with shared attention. A quality-assessment expert produces structured, natural-language degradation descriptions via Med-IQA; a restoration expert conditions on these descriptions to perform targeted restoration. A large-scale dataset (~50K paired samples) across three modalities and five restoration tasks with quality annotations, plus a 2K benchmark for evaluation.
AI.result: MedQ-UNI achieves state-of-the-art restoration across all tasks without task-specific adaptation and generates superior quality descriptions; explicit quality understanding improves restoration fidelity and interpretability.
AI.conclusion: Integrating Med-IQA into Med-IR enables a generalizable, interpretable restoration framework. The dataset and assess-then-restore paradigm can facilitate cross-modality improvements, with potential clinical impact.

title: ALIGN: Adversarial Learning for Generalizable Speech Neuroprosthesis
summary: Intracortical brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) can decode speech from neural activity with high accuracy when trained on data pooled across recording sessions. In realistic deployment, however, models must generalize to new sessions without labeled data, and performance often degrades due to cross-session nonstationarities (e.g., electrode shifts, neural turnover, and changes in user strategy). In this paper, we propose ALIGN, a session-invariant learning framework based on multi-domain adversarial neural networks for semi-supervised cross-session adaptation. ALIGN trains a feature encoder jointly with a phoneme classifier and a domain classifier operating on the latent representation. Through adversarial optimization, the encoder is encouraged to preserve task-relevant information while suppressing session-specific cues. We evaluate ALIGN on intracortical speech decoding and find that it generalizes consistently better to previously unseen sessions, improving both phoneme error rate and word error rate relative to baselines. These results indicate that adversarial domain alignment is an effective approach for mitigating session-level distribution shift and enabling robust longitudinal BCI decoding.

AI.tldr: ALIGN leverages multi-domain adversarial learning to produce a session-invariant encoder for semi-supervised cross-session intracortical speech decoding, improving generalization to unseen sessions.
AI.motivation: Cross-session nonstationarities (e.g., electrode shifts, neural turnover, strategy changes) degrade BCI performance; there is a need for label-efficient adaptation in new sessions.
AI.method: Train a feature encoder jointly with a phoneme classifier and a domain classifier on latent representations; adversarial optimization encourages preservation of task-relevant information while suppressing session-specific cues; semi-supervised, multi-domain setting across recording sessions.
AI.result: ALIGN generalizes better to unseen sessions, yielding improvements in phoneme error rate and word error rate over baselines in intracortical speech decoding.
AI.conclusion: Adversarial domain alignment mitigates session-level distribution shift and supports robust longitudinal BCI decoding.

title: A Passive Elastic-Folding Mechanism for Stackable Airdrop Sensors
comment: 8 pages, 8 figures, The 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA 2026)
summary: Air-dispersed sensor networks deployed from aerial robotic systems (e.g., UAVs) provide a low-cost approach to wide-area environmental monitoring. However, existing methods often rely on active actuators for mid-air shape or trajectory control, increasing both power consumption and system cost. Here, we introduce a passive elastic-folding hinge mechanism that transforms sensors from a flat, stackable form into a three-dimensional structure upon release. Hinges are fabricated by laminating commercial sheet materials with rigid printed circuit boards (PCBs) and programming fold angles through a single oven-heating step, enabling scalable production without specialized equipment. Our geometric model links laminate geometry, hinge mechanics, and resulting fold angle, providing a predictive design methodology for target configurations. Laboratory tests confirmed fold angles between 10 degrees and 100 degrees, with a standard deviation of 4 degrees and high repeatability. Field trials further demonstrated reliable data collection and LoRa transmission during dispersion, while the Horizontal Wind Model (HWM)-based trajectory simulations indicated strong potential for wide-area sensing exceeding 10 km.

AI.tldr: Passive elastic-folding hinges convert flat UAV-deployable sensors into 3D configurations without active actuators, enabling scalable production and long-range wide-area sensing; fold angles 10–100° with 4° std, reliable field data and LoRa transmission, >10 km potential per trajectory modeling.
AI.motivation: Reduce power consumption and cost of mid-air shape/trajectory control in air-dispersed sensor networks by replacing active actuators with passive, scalable hinges; enable mass production and robust deployment from aerial platforms.
AI.method: Fabricate hinges by laminating commercial sheet materials with rigid PCBs; program fold angles via a single oven heating step; develop a geometric model linking laminate geometry, hinge mechanics, and fold angle; validate with laboratory fold-angle tests (10–100°; σ≈4°); conduct field dispersion trials with LoRa data transmission; perform Horizontal Wind Model (HWM) trajectory simulations to assess sensing range.
AI.result: Demonstrated fold angles between 10° and 100° with low variance and high repeatability; field trials showed reliable data collection and LoRa transmission during dispersion; HWM-based simulations indicate potential wide-area sensing exceeding 10 km.
AI.conclusion: A scalable, passive fabrication approach enables 3D sensor configurations for air-dispersed networks, reducing actuation power and system cost while supporting wide-area environmental monitoring; potential enhancements include tighter control of fold precision and integration with sensing payloads for extended range.

title: CAPSUL: A Comprehensive Human Protein Benchmark for Subcellular Localization
summary: Subcellular localization is a crucial biological task for drug target identification and function annotation. Although it has been biologically realized that subcellular localization is closely associated with protein structure, no existing dataset offers comprehensive 3D structural information with detailed subcellular localization annotations, thus severely hindering the application of promising structure-based models on this task. To address this gap, we introduce a new benchmark called $\mathbf{CAPSUL}$, a $\mathbf{C}$omprehensive hum$\mathbf{A}$n $\mathbf{P}$rotein benchmark for $\mathbf{SU}$bcellular $\mathbf{L}$ocalization. It features a dataset that integrates diverse 3D structural representations with fine-grained subcellular localization annotations carefully curated by domain experts. We evaluate this benchmark using a variety of state-of-the-art sequence-based and structure-based models, showcasing the importance of involving structural features in this task. Furthermore, we explore reweighting and single-label classification strategies to facilitate future investigation on structure-based methods for this task. Lastly, we showcase the powerful interpretability of structure-based methods through a case study on the Golgi apparatus, where we discover a decisive localization pattern $α$-helix from attention mechanisms, demonstrating the potential for bridging the gap with intuitive biological interpretability and paving the way for data-driven discoveries in cell biology.

AI.tldr: CAPSUL is a comprehensive protein benchmark integrating 3D structures with detailed subcellular localization annotations, enabling evaluation of both sequence- and structure-based models and providing interpretability insights.
AI.motivation: Subcellular localization correlates with protein structure; existing datasets lack integrated 3D structure and localization annotations, hindering structure-based approaches.
AI.method: Curated CAPSUL dataset; multiple 3D representations; domain-expert annotations; evaluate state-of-the-art sequence-based and structure-based models; investigate reweighting and single-label classification; case study on Golgi using attention to extract alpha-helix localization pattern.
AI.result: Structure-informed models improve localization predictions; reweighting and single-label strategies facilitate modeling; interpretable attention reveals alpha-helix pattern linked to Golgi localization.
AI.conclusion: CAPSUL enables data-driven discoveries and bridges structure-function localization gap; fosters development of structure-based methods for subcellular localization.

title: Recolour What Matters: Region-Aware Colour Editing via Token-Level Diffusion
summary: Colour is one of the most perceptually salient yet least controllable attributes in image generation. Although recent diffusion models can modify object colours from user instructions, their results often deviate from the intended hue, especially for fine-grained and local edits. Early text-driven methods rely on discrete language descriptions that cannot accurately represent continuous chromatic variations. To overcome this limitation, we propose ColourCrafter, a unified diffusion framework that transforms colour editing from global tone transfer into a structured, region-aware generation process. Unlike traditional colour driven methods, ColourCrafter performs token-level fusion of RGB colour tokens and image tokens in latent space, selectively propagating colour information to semantically relevant regions while preserving structural fidelity. A perceptual Lab-space Loss further enhances pixel-level precision by decoupling luminance and chrominance and constraining edits within masked areas. Additionally, we build ColourfulSet, a largescale dataset of high-quality image pairs with continuous and diverse colour variations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ColourCrafter achieves state-of-the-art colour accuracy, controllability and perceptual fidelity in fine-grained colour editing. Our project is available at https://yangyuqi317.github.io/ColourCrafter.github.io/.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based, region-aware colour editing framework (ColourCrafter) that fuses RGB colour tokens with image tokens in latent space to enable precise, local colour edits, aided by a Lab-space perceptual loss and a large ColourfulSet dataset; achieves state-of-the-art colour accuracy and controllability.
AI.motivation: Current colour editing on diffusion models struggles with fine-grained, local edits and often misrepresents continuous chromatic variations; discrete textual prompts and global tone transfer lack precise control over hue, saturation, and region-specific colours.
AI.method: ColourCrafter performs token-level fusion of RGB colour tokens and image tokens in latent space to propagate colour information selectively to semantically relevant regions, preserving structure. It introduces a perceptual Lab-space loss to decouple luminance from chrominance and constrain edits within masked regions. It also introduces ColourfulSet, a large-scale dataset of image pairs with continuous colour variations.
AI.result: Experiments show ColourCrafter achieves superior colour accuracy, controllability, and perceptual fidelity for fine-grained colour edits compared to baselines, validated on diverse colour variation tasks.
AI.conclusion: ColourCrafter provides a unified, controllable, region-aware colour editing framework for diffusion models, with a dedicated dataset to support learning diverse colour variations; the approach advances precise local colour manipulation in image synthesis.

title: Safety-Guaranteed Imitation Learning from Nonlinear Model Predictive Control for Spacecraft Close Proximity Operations
summary: This paper presents a safety-guaranteed, runtime-efficient imitation learning framework for spacecraft close proximity control. We leverage Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) for safety certificates and Control Lyapunov Functions (CLFs) for stability as unified design principles across data generation, training, and deployment. First, a nonlinear Model Predictive Control (NMPC) expert enforces CBF constraints to provide safe reference trajectories. Second, we train a neural policy with a novel CBF-CLF-informed loss and DAgger-like rollouts with curriculum weighting, promoting data-efficiency and reducing future safety filter interventions. Third, at deployment a lightweight one-step CBF-CLF quadratic program minimally adjusts the learned control input to satisfy hard safety constraints while encouraging stability. We validate the approach for ESA-compliant close proximity operations, including fly-around with a spherical keep-out zone and final approach inside a conical approach corridor, using the Basilisk high-fidelity simulator with nonlinear dynamics and perturbations. Numerical experiments indicate stable convergence to decision points and strict adherence to safety under the filter, with task performance comparable to the NMPC expert while significantly reducing online computation. A runtime analysis demonstrates real-time feasibility on a commercial off-the-shelf processor, supporting onboard deployment for safety-critical on-orbit servicing.

AI.tldr: Safety-guaranteed imitation learning for spacecraft close-proximity control using CBFs and CLFs, integrating an NMPC safety expert with DAgger-like training to produce a real-time, onboard-capable policy.
AI.motivation: Need for safe, real-time autonomous close-proximity operations in space, balancing rigorous safety guarantees with data-efficient learning and limited onboard computation.
AI.method: 1) Use NMPC as a safety-enforcing expert by applying CBF constraints to generate safe reference trajectories. 2) Train a neural policy with a CBF-CLF-informed loss and perform DAgger-like rollouts with curriculum weighting to improve data efficiency and reduce future safety interventions. 3) Deploy with a one-step CBF-CLF quadratic program that minimally adjusts the learned input to satisfy hard safety constraints while promoting stability.
AI.result: Validated on ESA-compliant scenarios (fly-around with spherical keep-out; conical final approach) in Basilisk with nonlinear dynamics and perturbations. Experiments show convergence to decision points and strict safety under the filter; learning performance comparable to the NMPC expert with substantially reduced online computation. Real-time feasibility demonstrated on a commercial off-the-shelf processor, enabling onboard deployment for safety-critical on-orbit servicing.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a safety-guaranteed, data-efficient imitation-learning approach for spacecraft proximity control, achieving competitive task performance with significant runtime reductions and onboard safety assurances.

title: Interplay: Training Independent Simulators for Reference-Free Conversational Recommendation
summary: Training conversational recommender systems (CRS) requires extensive dialogue data, which is challenging to collect at scale. To address this, researchers have used simulated user-recommender conversations. Traditional simulation approaches often utilize a single large language model (LLM) that generates entire conversations with prior knowledge of the target items, leading to scripted and artificial dialogues. We propose a reference-free simulation framework that trains two independent LLMs, one as the user and one as the conversational recommender. These models interact in real-time without access to predetermined target items, but preference summaries and target attributes, enabling the recommender to genuinely infer user preferences through dialogue. This approach produces more realistic and diverse conversations that closely mirror authentic human-AI interactions. Our reference-free simulators match or exceed existing methods in quality, while offering a scalable solution for generating high-quality conversational recommendation data without constraining conversations to pre-defined target items. We conduct both quantitative and human evaluations to confirm the effectiveness of our reference-free approach.

AI.tldr: A reference-free, dual-LLM simulation framework for training conversational recommender systems, where user and recommender interact without fixed target items, guided by preference summaries; achieves realistic, diverse conversations and scalable data generation, matching or surpassing existing methods.
AI.motivation: CRS training requires large dialogue corpora; existing simulators often rely on a single LLM with predefined target items, yielding scripted dialogues. A reference-free, interactive setup with two LLMs aims to produce more natural conversations and flexibly infer user preferences without fixed targets.
AI.method: Train two independent LLMs (user and conversational recommender) that interact in real time without predetermined target items. The user provides preference summaries and attribute signals; the recommender infers preferences through dialogue. The framework is reference-free (no fixed target items) and focuses on authentic user-recommender interactions.
AI.result: The approach yields more realistic and diverse conversations. It matches or exceeds the quality of existing simulation methods and offers scalable data generation for CRS training without constraining conversations to predefined targets.
AI.conclusion: Reference-free, dual-LLM simulators are effective and scalable for generating high-quality CRS data, enabling more authentic human-AI interactions and broader exploration of user preferences during dialogue.

title: Do Vision Language Models Understand Human Engagement in Games?
summary: Inferring human engagement from gameplay video is important for game design and player-experience research, yet it remains unclear whether vision--language models (VLMs) can infer such latent psychological states from visual cues alone. Using the GameVibe Few-Shot dataset across nine first-person shooter games, we evaluate three VLMs under six prompting strategies, including zero-shot prediction, theory-guided prompts grounded in Flow, GameFlow, Self-Determination Theory, and MDA, and retrieval-augmented prompting. We consider both pointwise engagement prediction and pairwise prediction of engagement change between consecutive windows. Results show that zero-shot VLM predictions are generally weak and often fail to outperform simple per-game majority-class baselines. Memory- or retrieval-augmented prompting improves pointwise prediction in some settings, whereas pairwise prediction remains consistently difficult across strategies. Theory-guided prompting alone does not reliably help and can instead reinforce surface-level shortcuts. These findings suggest a perception--understanding gap in current VLMs: although they can recognize visible gameplay cues, they still struggle to robustly infer human engagement across games.

AI.tldr: Zero-shot VLMs struggle to infer engagement from gameplay visuals; retrieval-augmented prompts help some pointwise predictions, but pairwise engagement changes remain hard; theory-guided prompts are not reliably beneficial; overall indicates a perception-to-understanding gap in VLMs.
AI.motivation: Assess whether vision-language models can recover latent psychological states (engagement) from visual cues in gameplay, addressing a gap in using VLMs for player-experience research and game design.
AI.method: Dataset: GameVibe Few-Shot across nine FPS games. Models: three VLMs. Prompts: six strategies including zero-shot, theory-guided prompts grounded in Flow, GameFlow, Self-Determination Theory, MDA, and retrieval-augmented prompting. Tasks: pointwise engagement prediction and pairwise prediction of engagement change between consecutive windows.
AI.result: Zero-shot predictions are weak and often not better than majority baseline. Retrieval-augmented prompting improves pointwise predictions in some settings. Pairwise prediction remains difficult across strategies. Theory-guided prompting alone does not reliably help and may reinforce surface shortcuts.
AI.conclusion: There is a perception–understanding gap in current VLMs: they can recognize visible cues but struggle to robustly infer engagement across games; purely visual cues are insufficient for reliable engagement inference; incorporating retrieval or multimodal data may be necessary, and theory-grounded prompts alone are not reliably effective.

title: Learning to Reason with Curriculum I: Provable Benefits of Autocurriculum
summary: Chain-of-thought reasoning, where language models expend additional computation by producing thinking tokens prior to final responses, has driven significant advances in model capabilities. However, training these reasoning models is extremely costly in terms of both data and compute, as it involves collecting long traces of reasoning behavior from humans or synthetic generators and further post-training the model via reinforcement learning. Are these costs fundamental, or can they be reduced through better algorithmic design? We show that autocurriculum, where the model uses its own performance to decide which problems to focus training on, provably improves upon standard training recipes for both supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL). For SFT, we show that autocurriculum requires exponentially fewer reasoning demonstrations than non-adaptive fine-tuning, by focusing teacher supervision on prompts where the current model struggles. For RL fine-tuning, autocurriculum decouples the computational cost from the quality of the reference model, reducing the latter to a burn-in cost that is nearly independent of the target accuracy. These improvements arise purely from adaptive data selection, drawing on classical techniques from boosting and learning from counterexamples, and requiring no assumption on the distribution or difficulty of prompts.

AI.tldr: Autocurriculum—adaptive data selection using the model's own performance—substantially reduces the data and compute costs for training chain-of-thought reasoning in both SFT and RL, by focusing on prompts the model struggles with and decoupling RL cost from target quality.
AI.motivation: Chain-of-thought models incur high data and compute costs due to collecting long reasoning traces and post-training via RL; it is unclear if these costs are fundamental or reducible. This work probes whether algorithmic data selection can achieve cost reductions.
AI.method: Introduce autocurriculum: an adaptive data-selection mechanism driven by the model's current performance to decide training prompts. Theoretical analysis for supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL) showing improvements: in SFT, concentrating supervision on hard prompts yields exponential reduction in required demonstrations; in RL, decouples compute cost from the reference model quality, making burn-in cost nearly independent of target accuracy. The approach relies on adaptive data selection and foundational ideas from boosting and learning from counterexamples, with no distributional/difficulty assumptions about prompts.
AI.result: For SFT, autocurriculum reduces the number of necessary reasoning demonstrations exponentially compared to non-adaptive fine-tuning. For RL, the training cost becomes largely independent of the desired final performance via a burn-in reference model. Overall, adaptive data selection substantially lowers data/computation needs without distributional assumptions.
AI.conclusion: Autocurriculum provides a principled, distribution-free method to cut data and compute costs in chain-of-thought training, with practical gains for SFT and RL in reasoning-enabled LMs.

title: Lightweight Model Predictive Control for Spacecraft Rendezvous Attitude Synchronization
summary: This work introduces two lightweight model predictive control (MPC) approaches for attitude tracking with reaction wheels during spacecraft rendezvous synchronization. Both approaches are based on a novel attitude deviation formulation, which enables the use of inherently linear constraints on angular velocity. We develop a single-loop and a dual-loop MPC; the latter embeds a stabilizing feedback controller within the inner loop, yielding a linear time-invariant system. Both controllers are implemented with CasADi - including automatic code generation - evaluated across various solvers, and validated within the Basilisk astrodynamics simulation framework. The experimental results demonstrate improved tracking accuracy alongside reductions in computational effort and memory consumption. Finally, embedded delivery to an ARM Cortex-M7 - representative of commercial off-the-shelf devices used in New Space platforms - confirms the real-time feasibility of these approaches and highlights their suitability for onboard attitude control in resource-constrained spacecraft rendezvous missions.

AI.tldr: Two lightweight MPC schemes (single-loop and dual-loop) for attitude tracking with reaction wheels in spacecraft rendezvous, enabling real-time onboard control using CasADi; validated in Basilisk with ARM Cortex-M7 deployment.
AI.motivation: Address real-time, resource-constrained attitude control during spacecraft rendezvous by deriving a linearized attitude deviation formulation that yields linear velocity constraints and enables lightweight MPC.
AI.method: Develop a single-loop MPC and a dual-loop MPC where the inner loop of the dual-loop includes a stabilizing feedback controller, yielding a linear time-invariant system; implement with CasADi (automatic code generation), compare solvers, and validate in Basilisk; demonstrate onboard feasibility on an ARM Cortex-M7.
AI.result: Both approaches achieve improved tracking accuracy and reduced computational load and memory usage; real-time feasibility demonstrated on a Cortex-M7, supporting onboard attitude control for rendezvous missions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MPC formulations are viable for resource-constrained spacecraft rendezvous control, with the dual-loop structure offering a stabilized inner loop and CasADi-based lightweight implementation suitable for onboard CPUs.

title: MedForge: Interpretable Medical Deepfake Detection via Forgery-aware Reasoning
summary: Text-guided image editors can now manipulate authentic medical scans with high fidelity, enabling lesion implantation/removal that threatens clinical trust and safety. Existing defenses are inadequate for healthcare. Medical detectors are largely black-box, while MLLM-based explainers are typically post-hoc, lack medical expertise, and may hallucinate evidence on ambiguous cases. We present MedForge, a data-and-method solution for pre-hoc, evidence-grounded medical forgery detection. We introduce MedForge-90K, a large-scale benchmark of realistic lesion edits across 19 pathologies with expert-guided reasoning supervision via doctor inspection guidelines and gold edit locations. Building on it, MedForge-Reasoner performs localize-then-analyze reasoning, predicting suspicious regions before producing a verdict, and is further aligned with Forgery-aware GSPO to strengthen grounding and reduce hallucinations. Experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art detection accuracy and trustworthy, expert-aligned explanations.

AI.tldr: MedForge delivers pre-hoc, evidence-grounded forgery detection for medical scans, introducing MedForge-90K and MedForge-Reasoner with Forgery-aware GSPO to localize and justify detections, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy and expert-aligned explanations.
AI.motivation: Address the risk of authentic medical image editing by text-guided editors, which can implant or remove lesions, undermining clinical trust. Current detectors are mostly black-box and post-hoc explanations may hallucinate; a pre-hoc, explainable, expert-grounded solution is needed.
AI.method: MedForge-90K: a large-scale benchmark of realistic lesion edits across 19 pathologies with expert-guided reasoning via doctor inspection guidelines and gold edit locations. MedForge-Reasoner: a localize-then-analyze reasoning pipeline that first localizes suspicious regions, then produces a verdict; Forgery-aware GSPO to strengthen grounding and reduce hallucinations.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art detection accuracy and provides trustworthy, expert-aligned explanations.
AI.conclusion: MedForge establishes a pre-hoc, evidence-grounded framework for medical forgery detection, along with a comprehensive dataset and reasoning module to improve clinical trust and reduce hallucinations.

title: T-QPM: Enabling Temporal Out-Of-Distribution Detection and Domain Generalization for Vision-Language Models in Open-World
summary: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection remains a critical challenge in open-world learning, where models must adapt to evolving data distributions. While recent vision-language models (VLMS) like CLIP enable multimodal OOD detection through Dual-Pattern Matching (DPM), existing methods typically suffer from two major shortcomings: (1) They rely on fixed fusion rules and assume static environments, failing under temporal drift; and (2) they lack robustness against covariate shifted inputs. In this paper, we propose a novel two-step framework to enhance OOD detection and covariate distribution shift robustness in dynamic settings. We extend the dual-pattern regime into Temporal Quadruple-Pattern Matching (T-QPM). First, by pairing OOD images with text descriptions, we introduce cross-modal consistency patterns between ID and OOD signals, refining the decision boundary through joint image-text reasoning. Second, we address temporal distribution shifts by learning lightweight fusion weights to optimally combine semantic matching and visual typicality. To ensure stability, we enforce explicit regularization based on Average Thresholded Confidence (ATC), preventing performance degradation as distributions evolve. Experiments on temporally partitioned benchmarks demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms static baselines, offering a robust, temporally-consistent framework for multimodal OOD detection in non-stationary environments.

AI.tldr: Temporal Quadruple-Pattern Matching (T-QPM) enhances multimodal OOD detection under temporal drift by enforcing cross-modal OOD-ID consistency and learning adaptive fusion, regularized by Average Thresholded Confidence (ATC).
AI.motivation: Open-world OOD detection under non-stationary distributions suffers from fixed fusion rules and covariate shift; dynamic, multimodal approaches are needed to maintain reliable OOD detection as data distributions evolve.
AI.method: A two-step framework: (1) cross-modal consistency by pairing OOD images with text descriptions to refine the decision boundary through joint image-text reasoning; (2) learn lightweight fusion weights to optimally combine semantic matching and visual typicality, with ATC-based regularization to stabilize performance under distribution shifts.
AI.result: Experiments on temporally partitioned benchmarks show significant improvements over static baselines, demonstrating robustness and temporal consistency for multimodal OOD detection in non-stationary environments.
AI.conclusion: Extends dual-pattern matching to Temporal Quadruple-Pattern Matching (T-QPM) to provide a robust, temporally adaptive multimodal OOD detector. Promising results suggest effectiveness in dynamic open-world settings.

title: Escaping Offline Pessimism: Vector-Field Reward Shaping for Safe Frontier Exploration
summary: While offline reinforcement learning provides reliable policies for real-world deployment, its inherent pessimism severely restricts an agent's ability to explore and collect novel data online. Drawing inspiration from safe reinforcement learning, exploring near the boundary of regions well covered by the offline dataset and reliably modeled by the simulator allows an agent to take manageable risks--venturing into informative but moderate-uncertainty states while remaining close enough to familiar regions for safe recovery. However, naively rewarding this boundary-seeking behavior can lead to a degenerate parking behavior, where the agent simply stops once it reaches the frontier. To solve this, we propose a novel vector-field reward shaping paradigm designed to induce continuous, safe boundary exploration for non-adaptive deployed policies. Operating on an uncertainty oracle trained from offline data, our reward combines two complementary components: a gradient-alignment term that attracts the agent toward a target uncertainty level, and a rotational-flow term that promotes motion along the local tangent plane of the uncertainty manifold. Through theoretical analysis, we show that this reward structure naturally induces sustained exploratory behavior along the boundary while preventing degenerate solutions. Empirically, by integrating our proposed reward shaping with Soft Actor-Critic on a 2D continuous navigation task, we validate that agents successfully traverse uncertainty boundaries while balancing safe, informative data collection with primary task completion.

AI.tldr: A vector-field reward shaping framework guides safe, boundary-focused exploration in offline-to-online RL, mitigating degenerate behavior by combining a gradient-alignment term toward a target uncertainty level with a rotational-flow term along the uncertainty manifold; theoretically guaranteed sustained boundary exploration and validated with SAC on a 2D navigation task.
AI.motivation: Offline RL pessimism limits online exploration; safe, boundary-focused exploration near regions well-covered by offline data and simulators enables informative data collection while preserving safety and recoverability.
AI.method: Train an uncertainty oracle from offline data and define a vector-field reward with two terms: (1) gradient-alignment toward a target uncertainty level, and (2) rotational-flow along the local uncertainty manifold. The policy is updated with this reward (e.g., using Soft Actor-Critic). Theoretical analysis shows sustained boundary exploration without degenerate solutions.
AI.result: Theoretically proven to induce continuous boundary exploration and avoid degenerate parking behavior; empirically demonstrated by integrating the reward with SAC in a 2D continuous navigation task, where agents traverse uncertainty boundaries while balancing safety and task completion.
AI.conclusion: Vector-field reward shaping provides a principled, safe mechanism for boundary exploration in offline-to-online RL, enabling persistent exploration near uncertainty boundaries and improved data collection without sacrificing primary task performance.

title: PRIOR: Perceptive Learning for Humanoid Locomotion with Reference Gait Priors
summary: Training perceptive humanoid locomotion policies that traverse complex terrains with natural gaits remains an open challenge, typically demanding multi-stage training pipelines, adversarial objectives, or extensive real-world calibration. We present PRIOR, an efficient and reproducible framework built on Isaac Lab that achieves robust terrain traversal with human-like gaits through a simple yet effective design: (i) a parametric gait generator that supplies stable reference trajectories derived from motion capture without adversarial training, (ii) a GRU-based state estimator that infers terrain geometry directly from egocentric depth images via self-supervised heightmap reconstruction, and (iii) terrain-adaptive footstep rewards that guide foot placement toward traversable regions. Through systematic analysis of depth image resolution trade-offs, we identify configurations that maximize terrain fidelity under real-time constraints, substantially reducing perceptual overhead without degrading traversal performance. Comprehensive experiments across terrains of varying difficulty-including stairs, boxes, and gaps-demonstrate that each component yields complementary and essential performance gains, with the full framework achieving a 100% traversal success rate. We will open-source the complete PRIOR framework, including the training pipeline, parametric gait generator, and evaluation benchmarks, to serve as a reproducible foundation for humanoid locomotion research on Isaac Lab.

AI.tldr: A modular, reproducible framework (PRIOR) for robust humanoid locomotion on complex terrains using a mocap-derived parametric gait, GRU-based depth-informed terrain estimation, and terrain-adaptive footstep rewards, achieving 100% traversal success with open-source release on Isaac Lab.
AI.motivation: To enable robust, efficient humanoid terrain traversal without multi-stage training, adversarial objectives, or extensive real-world calibration, and to improve reproducibility via an open framework.
AI.method: PRIOR combines (i) a parametric gait generator using motion-capture-derived reference trajectories, (ii) a GRU-based state estimator that infers terrain geometry from egocentric depth images via self-supervised heightmap reconstruction, and (iii) terrain-adaptive footstep rewards that steer foot placement toward traversable regions, along with depth-resolution trade-off analysis for real-time operation and comprehensive terrain tests (stairs, boxes, gaps).
AI.result: The full framework achieves a 100% traversal success rate across tested terrains, with each component contributing complementary gains; depth-visual perceptual overhead is reduced without sacrificing performance; the authors plan to open-source the pipeline, gait generator, and benchmarks on Isaac Lab.
AI.conclusion: PRIOR provides a simple yet effective, reproducible approach to humanoid locomotion on complex terrains, avoiding adversarial training and enabling rapid evaluation and benchmarking within Isaac Lab.

title: ZEBRAARENA: A Diagnostic Simulation Environment for Studying Reasoning-Action Coupling in Tool-Augmented LLMs
summary: Tool-augmented large language models (LLMs) must tightly couple multi-step reasoning with external actions, yet existing benchmarks often confound this interplay with complex environment dynamics, memorized knowledge or dataset contamination. In this paper, we introduce ZebraArena, a procedurally generated diagnostic environment for studying reasoning-action coupling in tool-augmented LLMs, with controllable difficulty and a knowledge-minimal design, which limits gains from memorization or dataset contamination. Each task in ZebraArena requires a set of critical information which is available only through targeted tool use, yielding an interpretable interface between external information acquisition and deductive reasoning. This design provides deterministic evaluation via unique solutions, and a theoretical optimal query count for measuring efficient tool use. We show that ZebraArena requires a combination of in-depth reasoning and accurate external tool calling, which remains a challenge as frontier reasoning models such as GPT-5 and Gemini 2.5 Pro only achieves 60% accuracy on the hard instances. We also observe a persistent gaps between theoretical optimality and practical tool usage. For example, GPT-5 uses 70-270% more tool calls than the theoretical optimum. We highlight the key findings in our evaluation, and hope ZebraArena stimulates further research on the interplay between internal reasoning and external action.

AI.tldr: ZebraArena is a procedurally generated, knowledge-minimal benchmark for studying the coupling between reasoning and tool use in tool-augmented LLMs, with deterministic evaluation and a theoretical optimum for tool queries.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks confound reasoning with environmental dynamics, memorized knowledge, or dataset contamination; ZebraArena isolates reasoning-action coupling and enables controlled, interpretable evaluation of external tool usage.
AI.method: Create tasks with targeted information only accessible via tools; control difficulty; ensure knowledge-minimal design; provide deterministic, unique solutions; compute theoretical optimal query count; evaluate state-of-the-art LLMs on hard instances; analyze efficiency gaps.
AI.result: On hard instances, GPT-5 and Gemini 2.5 Pro reach about 60% accuracy; GPT-5 requires 70–270% more tool calls than the theoretical optimum; reveals persistent gaps between optimal tool use and practical behavior; demonstrates need for integrated reasoning with external actions.
AI.conclusion: ZebraArena offers a principled framework to study reasoning-action coupling and identifies gaps in current models; future work should address refining tool-use efficiency, reducing reliance on memorized strategies, and expanding diagnostic insights into error modes.

title: TexEditor: Structure-Preserving Text-Driven Texture Editing
summary: Text-guided texture editing aims to modify object appearance while preserving the underlying geometric structure. However, our empirical analysis reveals that even SOTA editing models frequently struggle to maintain structural consistency during texture editing, despite the intended changes being purely appearance-related. Motivated by this observation, we jointly enhance structure preservation from both data and training perspectives, and build TexEditor, a dedicated texture editing model based on Qwen-Image-Edit-2509. Firstly, we construct TexBlender, a high-quality SFT dataset generated with Blender, which provides strong structural priors for a cold start. Sec- ondly, we introduce StructureNFT, a RL-based approach that integrates structure-preserving losses to transfer the structural priors learned during SFT to real-world scenes. Moreover, due to the limited realism and evaluation coverage of existing benchmarks, we introduce TexBench, a general-purpose real-world benchmark for text-guided texture editing. Extensive experiments on existing Blender-based texture benchmarks and our TexBench show that TexEditor consistently outperforms strong baselines such as Nano Banana Pro. In addition, we assess TexEditor on the general purpose benchmark ImgEdit to validate its generalization. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/KlingAIResearch/TexEditor.

AI.tldr: TexEditor is a texture-editing model that preserves structure better by leveraging TexBlender SFT data and StructureNFT RL losses, introduces TexBench benchmark, and shows improved performance over baselines, with code at GitHub.
AI.motivation: State-of-the-art texture editing methods often fail to maintain structural consistency during appearance changes. This work seeks to strengthen structure preservation via data priors (TexBlender SFT) and training-time losses (StructureNFT), aiming for robust real-world editing.
AI.method: 1) TexBlender: a high-quality supervised finetuning (SFT) dataset generated from Blender to provide strong structural priors. 2) StructureNFT: RL-based approach that incorporates structure-preserving losses to transfer learned priors from SFT to real scenes. 3) TexBench: a general-purpose real-world benchmark for text-guided texture editing. 4) Evaluation protocol on Blender-based texture benchmarks, TexBench, and ImgEdit for generalization.
AI.result: TexEditor consistently outperforms strong baselines such as Nano Banana Pro on Blender-based texture benchmarks and TexBench. It also demonstrates generalization on the ImgEdit benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The proposed data-plus-training strategy—combining TexBlender priors with StructureNFT losses—and the new TexBench benchmark yield improved structure preservation in text-guided texture editing, with demonstrated gains and available code/data.

title: A Family of Adaptive Activation Functions for Mitigating Failure Modes in Physics-Informed Neural Networks
summary: Physics-Informed Neural Networks(PINNs) are a powerful and flexible learning framework that has gained significant attention in recent years. It has demonstrated strong performance across a wide range of scientific and engineering problems. In parallel, wavelets have been extensively used as efficient computational tools due to their strong approximation capabilities. Motivated by the common failure modes observed in standard PINNs, this work introduces a novel family of adaptive wavelet-based activation functions. The proposed activation functions significantly improve training stability and expressive power by combining trainable wavelet functions with either trainable or fixed hyperbolic tangent and softplus functions. Five distinct activation functions are developed within the PINN framework and systematically evaluated across four representative classes of partial differential equations (PDEs). Comprehensive comparisons using bar plots demonstrate improved robustness and accuracy compared to traditional activation functions. Furthermore, the proposed approach is validated through direct comparisons with baseline PINNs, transformer-based architectures such as PINNsFormer, and other deep learning models, highlighting its effectiveness and generality.

AI.tldr: Introduction of five adaptive wavelet-based activation functions for PINNs that improve training stability, expressivity, and robustness across PDE problems, outperforming standard activations, PINNs, and transformer-based baselines.
AI.motivation: PINNs commonly suffer from training instability and limited expressivity; wavelet-based activations offer adaptable local approximation properties to mitigate failures.
AI.method: Develop five activation functions combining trainable wavelet components with either trainable or fixed tanh/softplus; integrate into PINN framework; evaluate on four PDE classes; compare with baseline PINNs, PINNsFormer, and other DL models.
AI.result: Bar-plot analyses indicate improved robustness and accuracy; Activation functions outperform traditional activations and baseline models in the tested tasks.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive wavelet-based activations provide a general, effective enhancement for PINNs, with potential applicability beyond the tested PDE classes.

title: MERGE: Guided Vision-Language Models for Multi-Actor Event Reasoning and Grounding in Human-Robot Interaction
summary: We introduce MERGE, a system for situational grounding of actors, objects, and events in dynamic human-robot group interactions. Effective collaboration in such settings requires consistent situational awareness, built on persistent representations of people and objects and an episodic abstraction of events. MERGE achieves this by uniquely identifying physical instances of actors (humans or robots) and objects and structuring them into actor-action-object relations, ensuring temporal consistency across interactions. Central to MERGE is the integration of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) guided with a perception pipeline: a lightweight streaming module continuously processes visual input to detect changes and selectively invokes the VLM only when necessary. This decoupled design preserves the reasoning power and zero-shot generalization of VLMs while improving efficiency, avoiding both the high monetary cost and the latency of frame-by-frame captioning that leads to fragmented and delayed outputs. To address the absence of suitable benchmarks for multi-actor collaboration, we introduce the GROUND dataset, which offers fine-grained situational annotations of multi-person and human-robot interactions. On this dataset, our approach improves the average grounding score by a factor of 2 compared to the performance of VLM-only baselines - including GPT-4o, GPT-5 and Gemini 2.5 Flash - while also reducing run-time by a factor of 4. The code and data are available at www.github.com/HRI-EU/merge.

AI.tldr: MERGE presents a hybrid system for persistent actor-object grounding in multi-actor human-robot groups, combining a streaming perception module with selective Vision-Language Model grounding, and introduces the GROUND dataset; claims 2x grounding accuracy and 4x faster runtime over VLM baselines.
AI.motivation: To enable stable situational awareness in dynamic human-robot teams, addressing the need for persistent representations of people/objects and event-level abstractions, and to reduce the cost and latency of VLM-based reasoning in continuous video streams.
AI.method: Identify physical instances of actors (humans/robots) and objects and organize them into actor–action–object relations with temporal consistency. Use a lightweight streaming module to detect changes and selectively invoke a Vision-Language Model (VLM) only when necessary, decoupling perception from reasoning. Introduce the GROUND dataset with fine-grained annotations for multi-person and human–robot interactions. Compare against VLM-only baselines (e.g., GPT-4o, GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Flash). Public release of code and data.
AI.result: On GROUND, MERGE improves the average grounding score by a factor of 2 relative to VLM-only baselines and reduces run-time by a factor of 4. Code and data are publicly available at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: MERGE delivers temporally consistent, efficient situational grounding for multi-actor interactions, with a new benchmark dataset to spur future work, and demonstrates clear gains in both grounding accuracy and processing efficiency.

title: Agentic Flow Steering and Parallel Rollout Search for Spatially Grounded Text-to-Image Generation
summary: Precise Text-to-Image (T2I) generation has achieved great success but is hindered by the limited relational reasoning of static text encoders and the error accumulation in open-loop sampling. Without real-time feedback, initial semantic ambiguities during the Ordinary Differential Equation trajectory inevitably escalate into stochastic deviations from spatial constraints. To bridge this gap, we introduce AFS-Search (Agentic Flow Steering and Parallel Rollout Search), a training-free closed-loop framework built upon FLUX.1-dev.
