AFS-Search incorporates a training-free closed-loop parallel rollout search and flow steering mechanism, which leverages a Vision-Language Model (VLM) as a semantic critic to diagnose intermediate latents and dynamically steer the velocity field via precise spatial grounding. Complementarily, we formulate T2I generation as a sequential decision-making process, exploring multiple trajectories through lookahead simulations and selecting the optimal path based on VLM-guided rewards. Further, we provide AFS-Search-Pro for higher performance and AFS-Search-Fast for quicker generation. Experimental results show that our AFS-Search-Pro greatly boosts the performance of the original FLUX.1-dev, achieving state-of-the-art results across three different benchmarks. Meanwhile, AFS-Search-Fast also significantly enhances performance while maintaining fast generation speed.

AI.tldr: Introduces AFS-Search, a training-free closed-loop framework for text-to-image generation that uses a vision-language model as a semantic critic to steer a velocity-field-based generation process via parallel rollouts, with Pro and Fast variants achieving strong results on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Static text encoders and open-loop sampling in precise T2I generation lead to semantic ambiguities and spatial constraint violations; a real-time, feedback-driven approach is needed to reduce error accumulation and improve relational reasoning.
AI.method: Builds on FLUX.1-dev to create a training-free closed-loop pipeline (AFS-Search) with a parallel rollout search and flow-steering mechanism. A VLM diagnoses intermediate latents and provides VLM-guided rewards to steer the velocity field for better spatial grounding. Treats T2I as sequential decision making with lookahead trajectories selecting the best path. Includes AFS-Search-Pro and AFS-Search-Fast variants.
AI.result: AFS-Search-Pro substantially improves FLUX.1-dev, achieving state-of-the-art performance across three benchmarks; AFS-Search-Fast offers significant gains with faster generation.
AI.conclusion: AFS-Search provides a training-free, feedback-driven method to enhance T2I generation by integrating VLM-based semantic critique and lookahead trajectory optimization, with configurable quality-speed tradeoffs via Pro and Fast variants.

title: FILT3R: Latent State Adaptive Kalman Filter for Streaming 3D Reconstruction
summary: Streaming 3D reconstruction maintains a persistent latent state that is updated online from incoming frames, enabling constant-memory inference. A key failure mode is the state update rule: aggressive overwrites forget useful history, while conservative updates fail to track new evidence, and both behaviors become unstable beyond the training horizon. To address this challenge, we propose FILT3R, a training-free latent filtering layer that casts recurrent state updates as stochastic state estimation in token space. FILT3R maintains a per-token variance and computes a Kalman-style gain that adaptively balances memory retention against new observations. Process noise -- governing how much the latent state is expected to change between frames -- is estimated online from EMA-normalized temporal drift of candidate tokens. Using extensive experiments, we demonstrate that FILT3R yields an interpretable, plug-in update rule that generalizes common overwrite and gating policies as special cases. Specifically, we show that gains shrink in stable regimes as uncertainty contracts with accumulated evidence, and rise when genuine scene change increases process uncertainty, improving long-horizon stability for depth, pose, and 3D reconstruction, compared to the existing methods. Code will be released at https://github.com/jinotter3/FILT3R.

AI.tldr: A training-free Kalman-like latent filter (FILT3R) for streaming 3D reconstruction; maintains per-token uncertainty to adapt updates online, improving long-horizon stability; unifies overwrite/gating behaviors and adapts to scene changes; validated via extensive experiments; code release.
AI.motivation: Streaming 3D reconstruction relies on a persistent latent state updated with incoming frames. Aggressive overwrites lose history; conservative updates lag behind new evidence; both cause instability beyond the training horizon.
AI.method: Cast recurrent updates as stochastic state estimation in token space; maintain per-token variance; Kalman-style gain balances memory and new observations; estimate process noise online from EMA-normalized temporal drift of candidate tokens; plug-in training-free layer; generalizes overwrite and gating policies as special cases.
AI.result: Demonstrates improved long-horizon stability for depth, pose, and 3D reconstruction across experiments; gains contract when evidence accumulates; increase with genuine scene change; interpretable update rule; reported generalization to common policies; code available.
AI.conclusion: FILT3R provides a plug-in, training-free update mechanism that adapts update gains based on uncertainty, stabilizing streaming 3D reconstruction and unifying prior update strategies.

title: Epistemic Generative Adversarial Networks
summary: Generative models, particularly Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), often suffer from a lack of output diversity, frequently generating similar samples rather than a wide range of variations. This paper introduces a novel generalization of the GAN loss function based on Dempster-Shafer theory of evidence, applied to both the generator and discriminator. Additionally, we propose an architectural enhancement to the generator that enables it to predict a mass function for each image pixel. This modification allows the model to quantify uncertainty in its outputs and leverage this uncertainty to produce more diverse and representative generations. Experimental evidence shows that our approach not only improves generation variability but also provides a principled framework for modeling and interpreting uncertainty in generative processes.

AI.tldr: A DS-theory-based generalization of GAN loss with per-pixel mass-function to quantify uncertainty, yielding more diverse generations; empirical evidence shows improved diversity and principled uncertainty modeling.
AI.motivation: GANs often produce limited diversity, indicating a need for uncertainty-aware generative frameworks and a principled way to measure and utilize uncertainty.
AI.method: Introduce a Dempster-Shafer-based generalization of the GAN loss for both generator and discriminator. Extend the generator architecture to predict a mass function for each image pixel, enabling per-pixel uncertainty quantification which is then leveraged to encourage more diverse outputs.
AI.result: Increased generation variability and diversity; the framework provides a principled approach to modeling and interpreting uncertainty in generative processes, with empirical validation supporting the claim.
AI.conclusion: The combination of DS-based loss and per-pixel mass-function prediction yields more diverse, representative generations and offers a rigorous framework for uncertainty in generative modeling.

title: ATG-MoE: Autoregressive trajectory generation with mixture-of-experts for assembly skill learning
summary: Flexible manufacturing requires robot systems that can adapt to constantly changing tasks, objects, and environments. However, traditional robot programming is labor-intensive and inflexible, while existing learning-based assembly methods often suffer from weak positional generalization, complex multi-stage designs, and limited multi-skill integration capability. To address these issues, this paper proposes ATG-MoE, an end-to-end autoregressive trajectory generation method with mixture of experts for assembly skill learning from demonstration. The proposed method establishes a closed-loop mapping from multi-modal inputs, including RGB-D observations, natural language instructions, and robot proprioception to manipulation trajectories. It integrates multi-modal feature fusion for scene and task understanding, autoregressive sequence modeling for temporally coherent trajectory generation, and a mixture-of-experts architecture for unified multi-skill learning. In contrast to conventional methods that separate visual perception and control or train different skills independently, ATG-MoE directly incorporates visual information into trajectory generation and supports efficient multi-skill integration within a single model. We train and evaluate the proposed method on eight representative assembly skills from a pressure-reducing valve assembly task. Experimental results show that ATG-MoE achieves strong overall performance in simulation, with an average grasp success rate of 96.3% and an average overall success rate of 91.8%, while also demonstrating strong generalization and effective multi-skill integration. Real-world experiments further verify its practicality for multi-skill industrial assembly. The project page can be found at https://hwh23.github.io/ATG-MoE

AI.tldr: ATG-MoE is an end-to-end autoregressive trajectory generator with a mixture-of-experts for assembling tasks, fusing RGB-D, language, and proprioception to learn and execute multi-skill trajectories; shows high simulation success and real-world practicality across eight valve-assembly skills.
AI.motivation: Traditional robot programming is labor-intensive and inflexible; existing learning-based assembly methods suffer from weak positional generalization, multi-stage complexity, and poor multi-skill integration. A unified, end-to-end model capable of ingesting multi-modal inputs for trajectory generation is needed.
AI.method: Proposes ATG-MoE: an autoregressive trajectory generator with mixture-of-experts architecture. Uses multi-modal feature fusion (RGB-D, natural language, proprioception) for scene/task understanding; autoregressive sequence modeling for temporally coherent trajectories; MoE to unify multi-skill learning in a single model; closed-loop mapping from observations to manipulation trajectories; trained/evaluated on eight assembly skills in pressure-reducing valve tasks; claims end-to-end integration of perception and control.
AI.result: In simulation, average grasp success 96.3%; overall success 91.8%; demonstrates generalization and multi-skill integration. Real-world experiments validate practicality for multi-skill industrial assembly; project page available.
AI.conclusion: ATG-MoE achieves strong performance with unified multi-skill learning and end-to-end perception-to-action mapping, enabling efficient adaptation to changing tasks and environments in industrial assembly; supports real-world deployment.

title: D-Mem: A Dual-Process Memory System for LLM Agents
summary: Driven by the development of persistent, self-adapting autonomous agents, equipping these systems with high-fidelity memory access for long-horizon reasoning has emerged as a critical requirement. However, prevalent retrieval-based memory frameworks often follow an incremental processing paradigm that continuously extracts and updates conversational memories into vector databases, relying on semantic retrieval when queried. While this approach is fast, it inherently relies on lossy abstraction, frequently missing contextually critical information and struggling to resolve queries that rely on fine-grained contextual understanding. To address this, we introduce D-Mem, a dual-process memory system. It retains lightweight vector retrieval for routine queries while establishing an exhaustive Full Deliberation module as a high-fidelity fallback. To achieve cognitive economy without sacrificing accuracy, D-Mem employs a Multi-dimensional Quality Gating policy to dynamically bridge these two processes. Experiments on the LoCoMo and RealTalk benchmarks using GPT-4o-mini and Qwen3-235B-Instruct demonstrate the efficacy of our approach. Notably, our Multi-dimensional Quality Gating policy achieves an F1 score of 53.5 on LoCoMo with GPT-4o-mini. This outperforms our static retrieval baseline, Mem0$^\ast$ (51.2), and recovers 96.7\% of the Full Deliberation's performance (55.3), while incurring significantly lower computational costs.

AI.tldr: D-Mem introduces a dual-process memory system with fast vector retrieval plus a high-fidelity Full Deliberation fallback, governed by a Multi-dimensional Quality Gating policy to balance speed and accuracy, achieving improved performance with lower cost on LoCoMo/RealTalk benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon reasoning agents require high-fidelity memory access; existing retrieval-based memories are fast but lose critical context due to lossy vector abstractions, hindering fine-grained query resolution.
AI.method: A dual-process architecture: lightweight vector retrieval for routine queries and an exhaustive Full Deliberation module as a high-fidelity fallback. A Multi-dimensional Quality Gating policy dynamically bridges the two processes. Evaluation on LoCoMo and RealTalk using GPT-4o-mini and Qwen3-235B-Instruct.
AI.result: The approach yields an F1 score of 53.5 on LoCoMo with GPT-4o-mini, beating static retrieval baseline Mem0* (51.2) and achieving 96.7% of Full Deliberation’s performance (55.3) at substantially lower computational cost.
AI.conclusion: D-Mem demonstrates that selective, gated escalation to a high-fidelity memory process can significantly improve accuracy while maintaining efficiency, offering a practical path to high-fidelity, long-horizon reasoning in autonomous agents.

title: Mathematical Foundations of Deep Learning
comment: Draft version. Final version is published in "Chapman & Hall/CRC Mathematics and Artificial Intelligence Series" by Taylor & Francis in 2026
summary: This draft book offers a comprehensive and rigorous treatment of the mathematical principles underlying modern deep learning. The book spans core theoretical topics, from the approximation capabilities of deep neural networks, the theory and algorithms of optimal control and reinforcement learning integrated with deep learning techniques, to contemporary generative models that drive today's advances in artificial intelligence.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive theoretical anthology on the mathematics of modern deep learning, unifying approximation theory, optimal control and reinforcement learning integration, and generative modeling.
AI.motivation: Provide a rigorous, unified mathematical framework for deep learning to guide algorithm design, understanding, and future research across subfields.
AI.method: Expository synthesis with rigorous derivations, theorems, and proofs, integrating core theory with algorithmic considerations for DNNs, control/ RL, and generative models.
AI.result: No empirical results are reported; the work is positioned to yield formal theorems, proofs, and a unifying perspective across subspecialties.
AI.conclusion: If successful, it will establish a foundational reference that clarifies concepts and relationships among core theoretical areas underpinning modern AI.

title: Fire as a Service: Augmenting Robot Simulators with Thermally and Visually Accurate Fire Dynamics
summary: Most existing robot simulators prioritize rigid-body dynamics and photorealistic rendering, but largely neglect the thermally and optically complex phenomena that characterize real-world fire environments. For robots envisioned as future firefighters, this limitation hinders both reliable capability evaluation and the generation of representative training data prior to deployment in hazardous scenarios. To address these challenges, we introduce Fire as a Service (FaaS), a novel, asynchronous co-simulation framework that augments existing robot simulators with high-fidelity and computationally efficient fire simulations. Our pipeline enables robots to experience accurate, multi-species thermodynamic heat transfer and visually consistent volumetric smoke without disrupting high-frequency rigid-body control loops. We demonstrate that our framework can be integrated with diverse robot simulators to generate physically accurate fire behavior, benchmark thermal hazards encountered by robotic platforms, and collect realistic multimodal perceptual data. Crucially, its real-time performance supports human-in-the-loop teleoperation, enabling the successful training of reactive, multimodal policies via Behavioral Cloning. By adding fire dynamics to robot simulations, FaaS provides a scalable pathway toward safer, more reliable deployment of robots in fire scenarios.

AI.tldr: A novel asynchronous co-simulation framework, Fire as a Service (FaaS), augments robot simulators with high-fidelity fire dynamics—multi-species heat transfer and volumetric smoke—without hindering rigid-body loops, enabling real-time data generation and training via Behavioral Cloning.
AI.motivation: Current robot simulators emphasize rigid-body physics and photorealism but neglect fire physics, hindering evaluation and data collection in fire scenarios; accurate thermal and optical phenomena are needed for safer deployment.
AI.method: Develops an asynchronous co-simulation pipeline that injects fire physics into existing simulators; supports multi-species thermodynamics, heat transfer, and volumetric smoke; designed to maintain high-frequency control loops; modular to integrate with diverse simulators; enables benchmarking, data collection, and real-time teleoperation; supports training of reactive multimodal policies via Behavioral Cloning.
AI.result: Demonstrates integration with multiple simulators; generates physically accurate fire behavior; benchmarks thermal hazards; collects realistic multimodal data; real-time performance supports human-in-the-loop teleoperation and policy learning.
AI.conclusion: FaaS provides a scalable pathway for adding fire dynamics to robot simulations, improving safety and reliability in fire scenarios, and enabling efficient data-driven policy learning.

title: An Onto-Relational-Sophic Framework for Governing Synthetic Minds
summary: The rapid evolution of artificial intelligence, from task-specific systems to foundation models exhibiting broad, flexible competence across reasoning, creative synthesis, and social interaction, has outpaced the conceptual and governance frameworks designed to manage it. Current regulatory paradigms, anchored in a tool-centric worldview, address algorithmic bias and transparency but leave unanswered foundational questions about what increasingly capable synthetic minds are, how societies should relate to them, and the normative principles that should guide their development. Here we introduce the Onto-Relational-Sophic (ORS) framework, grounded in Cyberism philosophy, which offers integrated answers to these challenges through three pillars: (1) a Cyber-Physical-Social-Thinking (CPST) ontology that defines the mode of being for synthetic minds as irreducibly multi-dimensional rather than purely computational; (2) a graded spectrum of digital personhood providing a pragmatic relational taxonomy beyond binary person-or-tool classifications; and (3) Cybersophy, a wisdom-oriented axiology synthesizing virtue ethics, consequentialism, and relational approaches to guide governance. We apply the framework to emergent scenarios including autonomous research agents, AI-mediated healthcare, and agentic AI ecosystems, demonstrating its capacity to generate proportionate, adaptive governance recommendations. The ORS framework charts a path from narrow technical alignment toward comprehensive philosophical foundations for the synthetic minds already among us.

AI.tldr: The ORS framework provides a Cyberism-based, three-pillar governance structure for synthetic minds: CPST ontology, a graded digital personhood spectrum, and Cybersophy ethics, to guide adaptive governance beyond tool-centric models.
AI.motivation: Regulatory regimes are tool-centric and do not address fundamental questions about what synthetic minds are, how society should relate to them, or the normative principles for development.
AI.method: Propose a three-pillar framework: (1) CPST ontology defining synthetic minds as multi-dimensional; (2) graded digital personhood taxonomy; (3) Cybersophy integrating virtue ethics, consequentialism, and relational ethics to guide governance; apply to autonomous research agents, AI-mediated healthcare, and agentic AI ecosystems; derive governance recommendations.
AI.result: A coherent theoretical framework that links ontological understanding with a relational, ethics-informed governance strategy, enabling proportionate and adaptive regulation across diverse AI contexts.
AI.conclusion: The ORS framework aims to move governance from narrow alignment toward foundational philosophical grounding for synthetic minds already in operation.

title: Efficient Video Diffusion with Sparse Information Transmission for Video Compression
summary: Video compression aims to maximize reconstruction quality with minimal bitrates. Beyond standard distortion metrics, perceptual quality and temporal consistency are also critical. However, at ultra-low bitrates, traditional end-to-end compression models tend to produce blurry images of poor perceptual quality. Besides, existing generative compression methods often treat video frames independently and show limitations in time coherence and efficiency. To address these challenges, we propose the Efficient Video Diffusion with Sparse Information Transmission (Diff-SIT), which comprises the Sparse Temporal Encoding Module (STEM) and the One-Step Video Diffusion with Frame Type Embedder (ODFTE). The STEM sparsely encodes the original frame sequence into an information-rich intermediate sequence, achieving significant bitrate savings. Subsequently, the ODFTE processes this intermediate sequence as a whole, which exploits the temporal correlation. During this process, our proposed Frame Type Embedder (FTE) guides the diffusion model to perform adaptive reconstruction according to different frame types to optimize the overall quality. Extensive experiments on multiple datasets demonstrate that Diff-SIT establishes a new state-of-the-art in perceptual quality and temporal consistency, particularly in the challenging ultra-low-bitrate regime. Code is released at https://github.com/MingdeZhou/Diff-SIT.

AI.tldr: Diff-SIT establishes state-of-the-art perceptual quality and temporal consistency at ultra-low bitrates by sparsely encoding frames and processing with a diffusion-based video model guided by a Frame Type Embedder.
AI.motivation: Address the trade-off between low bitrate, perceptual quality, and temporal coherence; existing end-to-end methods blur at ultra-low bitrates; independent frame processing fails to exploit temporal correlations.
AI.method: Two-module approach: STEM sparsely encodes original frames into an information-rich intermediate sequence; ODFTE performs diffusion-based reconstruction on the sequence; FTE provides frame-type-aware guidance to adaptively reconstruct frames.
AI.result: Empirical results on multiple datasets show state-of-the-art perceptual quality and temporal consistency at ultra-low bitrates; code released.
AI.conclusion: Diff-SIT effectively leverages sparse transmission and conditional diffusion to improve video compression performance in challenging bitrate regimes.

title: RE-SAC: Disentangling aleatoric and epistemic risks in bus fleet control: A stable and robust ensemble DRL approach
summary: Bus holding control is challenging due to stochastic traffic and passenger demand. While deep reinforcement learning (DRL) shows promise, standard actor-critic algorithms suffer from Q-value instability in volatile environments. A key source of this instability is the conflation of two distinct uncertainties: aleatoric uncertainty (irreducible noise) and epistemic uncertainty (data insufficiency). Treating these as a single risk leads to value underestimation in noisy states, causing catastrophic policy collapse. We propose a robust ensemble soft actor-critic (RE-SAC) framework to explicitly disentangle these uncertainties. RE-SAC applies Integral Probability Metric (IPM)-based weight regularization to the critic network to hedge against aleatoric risk, providing a smooth analytical lower bound for the robust Bellman operator without expensive inner-loop perturbations. To address epistemic risk, a diversified Q-ensemble penalizes overconfident value estimates in sparsely covered regions. This dual mechanism prevents the ensemble variance from misidentifying noise as a data gap, a failure mode identified in our ablation study. Experiments in a realistic bidirectional bus corridor simulation demonstrate that RE-SAC achieves the highest cumulative reward (approx. -0.4e6) compared to vanilla SAC (-0.55e6). Mahalanobis rareness analysis confirms that RE-SAC reduces Oracle Q-value estimation error by up to 62% in rare out-of-distribution states (MAE of 1647 vs. 4343), demonstrating superior robustness under high traffic variability.

AI.tldr: Proposes RE-SAC to disentangle aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty in DRL for bus holding control; uses IPM-based critic regularization and a diversified Q-ensemble; achieves superior robustness and performance in simulation.
AI.motivation: In deep RL for stochastic environments (bus traffic and demand), conflation of irreducible noise (aleatoric) and data-coverage gaps (epistemic) destabilizes Q-values, risking policy collapse; need robust mechanisms that selectively hedge each uncertainty.
AI.method: Introduce IPM-based weight regularization on the critic to hedge aleatoric risk, yielding a smooth lower bound for the robust Bellman operator without inner-loop perturbations; employ a diversified Q-ensemble to penalize overconfident estimates in sparsely covered regions; validate via ablation and a realistic bus corridor simulation; perform Mahalanobis-based rarity analysis.
AI.result: RE-SAC achieves the highest cumulative reward (≈ -0.4e6) versus vanilla SAC (≈ -0.55e6); MAE of Oracle Q-value estimation in rare out-of-distribution states reduced by up to 62% (1647 vs 4343); demonstrates improved robustness under high traffic variability.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly disentangling aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty with IPM-regularized critics and diversified ensembles yields improved stability and performance for DRL-based bus-holding control in stochastic environments, mitigating underestimation and misinterpretation of noise.

title: CAMO: A Conditional Neural Solver for the Multi-objective Multiple Traveling Salesman Problem
summary: Robotic systems often require a team of robots to collectively visit multiple targets while optimizing competing objectives, such as total travel cost and makespan. This setting can be formulated as the Multi-Objective Multiple Traveling Salesman Problem (MOMTSP). Although learning-based methods have shown strong performance on the single-agent TSP and multi-objective TSP variants, they rarely address the combined challenges of multi-agent coordination and multi-objective trade-offs, which introduce dual sources of complexity. To bridge this gap, we propose CAMO, a conditional neural solver for MOMTSP that generalizes across varying numbers of targets, agents, and preference vectors, and yields high-quality approximations to the Pareto front (PF). Specifically, CAMO consists of a conditional encoder to fuse preferences into instance representations, enabling explicit control over multi-objective trade-offs, and a collaborative decoder that coordinates all agents by alternating agent selection and node selection to construct multi-agent tours autoregressively. To further improve generalization, we train CAMO with a REINFORCE-based objective over a mixed distribution of problem sizes. Extensive experiments show that CAMO outperforms both neural and conventional heuristics, achieving a closer approximation of PFs. In addition, ablation results validate the contributions of CAMO's key components, and real-world tests on a mobile robot platform demonstrate its practical applicability.

AI.tldr: A conditional neural solver (CAMO) for the Multi-Objective Multi-Traveling Salesman Problem (MOMTSP) with multiple agents and trade-off preferences, generalizing across targets, agents, and preferences; uses a conditional encoder and an autoregressive collaborative decoder; trained with REINFORCE on mixed problem sizes; achieves near Pareto-front performance and validates in simulations and real robots.
AI.motivation: To address the combined challenges of multi-agent coordination and multi-objective trade-offs in MOMTSP, which existing methods for single-agent or single-objective variants fail to handle effectively.
AI.method: Introduce CAMO: a conditional encoder that fuses the preference vector into instance representations, and a collaborative decoder that coordinates all agents by alternating agent selection and node selection to build multi-agent tours autoregressively. Train with a REINFORCE objective over a mixed distribution of problem sizes to improve generalization across varying numbers of targets, agents, and preferences.
AI.result: CAMO outperforms both neural and conventional heuristics, providing a closer approximation to the Pareto front. Ablation studies validate the importance of CAMO’s components. Real-world mobile robot experiments demonstrate practical applicability.
AI.conclusion: CAMO demonstrates strong generalization across problem sizes and preference settings for MOMTSP, delivering high-quality PF approximations and showing promise for real-world multi-robot coordination; future work could explore scalability, dynamic targets, and sample efficiency.

title: Balanced Thinking: Improving Chain of Thought Training in Vision Language Models
summary: Multimodal reasoning in vision-language models (VLMs) typically relies on a two-stage process: supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL). In standard SFT, all tokens contribute equally to the loss, even though reasoning data are inherently token-imbalanced. Long <think> traces overshadow short but task-critical <answer> segments, leading to verbose reasoning and inaccurate answers. We propose SCALe (Scheduled Curriculum Adaptive Loss), which explicitly separates supervision over reasoning and answer segments using dynamic, length-independent weighting. Unlike vanilla SFT, which overweights the <think> segment, SCALe-SFT gradually shifts the focus from <think> to <answer> throughout training via a cosine scheduling policy, encouraging concise and well-grounded reasoning. We evaluate SCALe across diverse benchmarks and architectures. Results show that SCALe consistently improves accuracy over vanilla SFT and matches the performance of the full two-phase SFT + GRPO pipeline while requiring only about one-seventh of the training time, making it a lightweight yet effective alternative. When combined with GRPO, SCALe achieves the best overall performance, highlighting its value both as a standalone method and as a strong foundation for reinforcement refinement.

AI.tldr: SCALe introduces a curriculum-based loss for vision-language model fine-tuning that separates supervision for reasoning and answers, using a cosine schedule to shift focus from think tokens to answer tokens. It achieves accuracy gains over vanilla SFT, matches the full SFT+GRPO pipeline, and requires ~1/7 of training time, serving as a lightweight alternative or foundation for reinforcement refinement.
AI.motivation: Standard SFT weights all tokens equally, causing long reasoning traces to dominate training and produce verbose, potentially incorrect answers. In multimodal reasoning, reasoning and answer segments are inherently imbalanced; explicit, dynamic control over supervision can improve grounding and efficiency.
AI.method: SCALe-SFT uses dynamic, length-independent weighting to separately supervise <think> and <answer> segments. A cosine schedule gradually shifts emphasis from <think> to <answer> during training, reducing reliance on verbose reasoning and promoting concise, grounded responses. The approach can be used standalone or in combination with GRPO for reinforcement refinement.
AI.result: SCALe improves accuracy over vanilla SFT, matches the performance of the full two-phase SFT + GRPO pipeline, and reduces training time to about one-seventh. When combined with GRPO, SCALe yields the best overall performance.
AI.conclusion: SCALe provides a lightweight yet effective alternative to traditional two-stage fine-tuning for multimodal reasoning. It can function as a standalone method or as a strong foundation for reinforcement-based refinement, delivering efficiency without sacrificing accuracy.

title: HOMEY: Heuristic Object Masking with Enhanced YOLO for Property Insurance Risk Detection
summary: Automated property risk detection is a high-impact yet underexplored frontier in computer vision with direct implications for real estate, underwriting, and insurance operations. We introduce HOMEY (Heuristic Object Masking with Enhanced YOLO), a novel detection framework that combines YOLO with a domain-specific masking mechanism and a custom-designed loss function. HOMEY is trained to detect 17 risk-related property classes, including structural damages (e.g., cracked foundations, roof issues), maintenance neglect (e.g., dead yards, overgrown bushes), and liability hazards (e.g., falling gutters, garbage, hazard signs). Our approach introduces heuristic object masking to amplify weak signals in cluttered backgrounds and risk-aware loss calibration to balance class skew and severity weighting. Experiments on real-world property imagery demonstrate that HOMEY achieves superior detection accuracy and reliability compared to baseline YOLO models, while retaining fast inference. Beyond detection, HOMEY enables interpretable and cost-efficient risk analysis, laying the foundation for scalable AI-driven property insurance workflows.

AI.tldr: HOMEY is a YOLO-based property-risk detector using heuristic masking and a risk-aware loss to detect 17 risk classes; it improves accuracy with fast inference and supports interpretable insurance workflows.
AI.motivation: Automated, scalable risk assessment in real estate/insurance is high-impact but underexplored; humans are slow and costly; a CV-based solution can streamline underwriting and risk management.
AI.method: Integrates YOLO with a domain-specific heuristic object masking module to amplify signals in cluttered backgrounds, and a custom loss function that calibrates for class imbalance and severity. Trains on 17 risk-related classes including structural damages, maintenance neglect, and liability hazards.
AI.result: Outperforms baseline YOLO in detection accuracy and reliability on real-world property imagery while maintaining fast inference times."
AI.conclusion: HOMEY provides interpretable, cost-efficient risk analysis, enabling scalable AI-driven workflows for property insurance and related domains.

title: FlowMS: Flow Matching for De Novo Structure Elucidation from Mass Spectra
summary: Mass spectrometry (MS) stands as a cornerstone analytical technique for molecular identification, yet de novo structure elucidation from spectra remains challenging due to the combinatorial complexity of chemical space and the inherent ambiguity of spectral fragmentation patterns. Recent deep learning approaches, including autoregressive sequence models, scaffold-based methods, and graph diffusion models, have made progress. However, diffusion-based generation for this task remains computationally demanding. Meanwhile, discrete flow matching, which has shown strong performance for graph generation, has not yet been explored for spectrum-conditioned structure elucidation. In this work, we introduce FlowMS, the first discrete flow matching framework for spectrum-conditioned de novo molecular generation. FlowMS generates molecular graphs through iterative refinement in probability space, enforcing chemical formula constraints while conditioning on spectral embeddings from a pretrained formula transformer encoder. Notably, it achieves state-of-the-art performance on 5 out of 6 metrics on the NPLIB1 benchmark: 9.15% top-1 accuracy (9.7% relative improvement over DiffMS) and 7.96 top-10 MCES (4.2% improvement over MS-BART). We also visualize the generated molecules, which further demonstrate that FlowMS produces structurally plausible candidates closely resembling ground truth structures. These results establish discrete flow matching as a promising paradigm for mass spectrometry-based structure elucidation in metabolomics and natural product discovery.

AI.tldr: FlowMS introduces discrete flow matching for spectrum-conditioned de novo molecular generation, achieving state-of-the-art on most NPLIB1 metrics and producing plausible candidate structures conditioned on MS spectra.
AI.motivation: De novo structure elucidation from mass spectrometry is challenging due to combinatorial chemical space and fragmentation ambiguity; prior approaches include autoregressive sequence models, scaffold-based methods, and diffusion-based generation; diffusion methods are computationally intensive; discrete flow matching offers a potentially more efficient alternative.
AI.method: FlowMS utilizes a discrete flow matching framework to generate molecular graphs via iterative refinement in probability space, enforcing chemical formula constraints and conditioning on spectral embeddings from a pretrained formula transformer encoder.
AI.result: On the NPLIB1 benchmark FlowMS achieves state-of-the-art on 5 of 6 metrics: top-1 accuracy 9.15% (9.7% relative improvement over DiffMS) and top-10 MCES 7.96 (4.2% improvement over MS-BART); qualitative visualizations show generated molecules closely resemble ground-truth structures.
AI.conclusion: Discrete flow matching is a promising paradigm for MS-based structure elucidation in metabolomics and natural product discovery, with FlowMS demonstrating strong performance and plausible candidate generation.

title: Articulated-Body Dynamics Network: Dynamics-Grounded Prior for Robot Learning
summary: Recent work in reinforcement learning has shown that incorporating structural priors for articulated robots, such as link connectivity, into policy networks improves learning efficiency. However, dynamics properties, despite their fundamental role in determining how forces and motion propagate through the body, remain largely underexplored as an inductive bias for policy learning. To address this gap, we present the Articulated-Body Dynamics Network (ABD-Net), a novel graph neural network architecture grounded in the computational structure of forward dynamics. Specifically, we adapt the inertia propagation mechanism from the Articulated Body Algorithm, systematically aggregating inertial quantities from child to parent links in a tree-structured manner, while replacing physical quantities with learnable parameters. Embedding ABD-NET into the policy actor enables dynamics-informed representations that capture how actions propagate through the body, leading to efficient and robust policy learning. Through experiments with simulated humanoid, quadruped, and hopper robots, our approach demonstrates increased sample efficiency and generalization to dynamics shifts compared to transformer-based and GNN baselines. We further validate the learned policy on real Unitree G1 and Go2 robots, state-of-the-art humanoid and quadruped platforms, generating dynamic, versatile and robust locomotion behaviors through sim-to-real transfer with real-time inference.

AI.tldr: ABD-Net is a dynamics-informed GNN for articulated robots, embedding a forward-dynamics-inspired inertia propagation to improve policy learning; achieves better sample efficiency and sim-to-real transfer across humanoid, quadruped, and hopper, outperforming transformer and generic GNN baselines.
AI.motivation: Dynamics properties govern force/motion propagation in articulated bodies; prior work leveraged structural priors (link topology) but neglected dynamics as an inductive bias; improved policy learning through dynamics-aware representations.
AI.method: Propose ABD-Net: a GNN architecture grounded in forward dynamics via an inertia-propagation mechanism from the Articulated Body Algorithm. Aggregates inertial-like quantities from child to parent in a tree-structured manner; replaces physical quantities with learnable parameters; integrates into policy actor to propagate actions through body.
AI.result: In simulation: humanoid, quadruped, hopper show higher sample efficiency and robustness to dynamics shifts relative to transformer-based and GNN baselines. Real-world: validates sim-to-real on Unitree G1 and Go2, achieving dynamic, versatile locomotion with real-time inference.
AI.conclusion: Dynamics-informed ABD-Net improves learning efficiency and robustness for articulated robots, with successful sim-to-real deployment on multiple platforms.

title: Thinking with Constructions: A Benchmark and Policy Optimization for Visual-Text Interleaved Geometric Reasoning
summary: Geometric reasoning inherently requires "thinking with constructions" -- the dynamic manipulation of visual aids to bridge the gap between problem conditions and solutions. However, existing Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are largely confined to passive inference with static diagrams, lacking the strategic knowledge of when and how to construct effective visual aids. To address this, we present a framework for Visual-Text Interleaved Chain-of-Thought. We first introduce GeoAux-Bench, the first benchmark comprising 4,334 geometry problems that aligns textual construction steps with ground-truth visual updates. Our pilot study reveals two critical insights: (1) interleaved visual-textual aids outperform single-modality counterparts, which cannot losslessly capture geometric synergy; and (2) valid constructions act as entropy reducers, strongly correlating with reduced reasoning perplexity. Building on these findings, we propose Action Applicability Policy Optimization (A2PO), a reinforcement learning paradigm for mastering strategic construction. A2PO employs Adaptive Reward Shaping to regulate the timing and quality of visual aids via counterfactual sampling to distinguish necessary from redundant constructions. Experiments demonstrate our approach enables MLLMs to leverage selective auxiliary constructions, yielding a 3.51% gain over strong baselines. Code and data are available on GitHub.

AI.tldr: A framework for geometric reasoning using interleaved visual-textual chain-of-thought, featuring GeoAux-Bench (4,334 geometry problems) and A2PO (reinforcement learning with adaptive reward shaping) to learn when and how to construct visual aids, achieving gains over baselines.
AI.motivation: MLLMs largely rely on static diagrams and passive inference, lacking strategic knowledge on when and how to construct helpful visual aids for geometry problems.
AI.method: Propose Visual-Text Interleaved Chain-of-Thought. Build GeoAux-Bench aligning textual construction steps with ground-truth visual updates. Introduce A2PO, an RL framework with Adaptive Reward Shaping and counterfactual sampling to control the timing/quality of visual constructions.
AI.result: Interleaved visual-textual aids outperform single-modality approaches; valid constructions act as entropy reducers correlating with lower reasoning perplexity. A2PO yields a 3.51% accuracy/score gain over strong baselines on the benchmark. Code and data available on GitHub.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that strategic, selective visual constructions can enhance geometric reasoning in multimodal LLMs; the GeoAux-Bench and A2PO framework offer a path to generalize dynamic visual reasoning in complex problem domains.

title: From Snapshots to Symphonies: The Evolution of Protein Prediction from Static Structures to Generative Dynamics and Multimodal Interactions
summary: The protein folding problem has been fundamentally transformed by artificial intelligence, evolving from static structure prediction toward the modeling of dynamic conformational ensembles and complex biomolecular interactions. This review systematically examines the paradigm shift in AI driven protein science across five interconnected dimensions: unified multimodal representations that integrate sequences, geometries, and textual knowledge; refinement of static prediction through MSA free architectures and all atom complex modeling; generative frameworks, including diffusion models and flow matching, that capture conformational distributions consistent with thermodynamic ensembles; prediction of heterogeneous interactions spanning protein ligand, protein nucleic acid, and protein protein complexes; and functional inference of fitness landscapes, mutational effects, and text guided property prediction. We critically analyze current bottlenecks, including data distribution biases, limited mechanistic interpretability, and the disconnect between geometric metrics and biophysical reality, while identifying future directions toward physically consistent generative models, multimodal foundation architectures, and experimental closed loop systems. This methodological transformation marks artificial intelligence's transition from a structural analysis tool into a universal simulator capable of understanding and ultimately rewriting the dynamic language of life.

AI.tldr: AI-driven protein science is moving from static structure prediction to modeling dynamic conformational ensembles and interactions, via multimodal representations, generative models, and experimental feedback, while confronting biases and interpretability barriers.
AI.motivation: To address the dynamic, interactive nature of proteins and their biomolecular networks, overcoming data biases and interpretability gaps, and to enable a unified, simulation-capable framework for protein science.
AI.method: Systematic review of AI-driven protein science across five intertwined dimensions (multimodal representations; static prediction refinement with MSAf ree architectures and all-atom modeling; generative frameworks for conformational distributions; heterogeneous interaction prediction; and functional inference). Critical analysis of bottlenecks and future directions.
AI.result: Synthesis identifies five convergent axes of progress, documents persistent bottlenecks (data biases, limited mechanistic interpretability, misalignment between geometry metrics and biophysics), and articulates directions toward physically consistent generative models, multimodal foundations, and closed-loop experimentation.
AI.conclusion: AI is poised to become a universal biomolecular simulator that can understand and potentially rewrite the dynamic language of life, moving beyond structure to function, dynamics, and interactions.

title: Self-Tuning Sparse Attention: Multi-Fidelity Hyperparameter Optimization for Transformer Acceleration
comment: Accepted to the International Conference on Machine Intelligence Theory and Applications (MiTA 2026)
summary: Sparse attention mechanisms promise to break the quadratic bottleneck of long-context transformers, yet production adoption remains limited by a critical usability gap: optimal hyperparameters vary substantially across layers and models, and current methods (e.g., SpargeAttn) rely on manual grid search to identify them. We propose AFBS-BO (Adaptive Fidelity Binary Search with Bayesian Optimization), a fully automated framework that discovers optimal layer- and head-specific hyperparameters without human intervention. Our hybrid algorithm combines Bayesian Optimization for global exploration with binary search for local refinement, leveraging multi-fidelity evaluation across sequence lengths to reduce tuning cost. On Llama-2-7B, AFBS-BO accelerates hyperparameter discovery by 3.4x with 8.8x fewer evaluations than grid search, and identifies high-sparsity configurations that outperform existing sparse attention baselines while closely matching dense attention quality. By transforming sparse attention from a manually tuned heuristic into a self-optimizing primitive, AFBS-BO enables plug-and-play acceleration across diverse transformer architectures and domains.

AI.tldr: AFBS-BO autonomously tunes sparse attention with a hybrid Bayesian optimization and binary search, using multi-fidelity sequence-length evaluations to cut tuning cost; on Llama-2-7B it achieves 3.4x faster hyperparameter discovery with 8.8x fewer evaluations, finding high-sparsity configs that outperform sparse baselines and nearly match dense attention.
AI.motivation: To close the usability gap in sparse attention by eliminating manual, layer- and head-specific hyperparameter tuning.
AI.method: A hybrid search algorithm that couples Bayesian Optimization for global exploration with local binary search for refinement, augmented with multi-fidelity evaluations across different sequence lengths to reduce cost.
AI.result: Significant acceleration in hyperparameter discovery (3.4x) with substantially fewer evaluations (8.8x) and identification of high-sparsity configurations that beat existing sparse baselines while closely matching dense attention quality.
AI.conclusion: Transforms sparse attention from a manually tuned heuristic into a self-optimizing primitive, enabling plug-and-play acceleration across transformer architectures and domains.

title: Tendon-Actuated Robots with a Tapered, Flexible Polymer Backbone: Design, Fabrication, and Modeling
summary: This paper presents the design, modeling, and fabrication of 3D-printed, tendon-actuated continuum robots featuring a flexible, tapered backbone constructed from thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU). Our scalable design incorporates an integrated electronics base housing that enables direct tendon tension control and sensing via actuators and compression load cells. Unlike many continuum robots that are single-purpose and costly, the proposed design prioritizes customizability, rapid assembly, and low cost while enabling high curvature and enhanced distal compliance through geometric tapering, thereby supporting a broad range of compliant robotic inspection and manipulation tasks. We develop a generalized forward kinetostatic model of the tapered backbone based on Cosserat rod theory using a Newtonian approach, extending existing tendon-actuated Cosserat rod formulations to explicitly account for spatially varying backbone cross-sectional geometry. The model captures the graded stiffness profile induced by the tapering and enables systematic exploration of the configuration space as a function of the geometric design parameters. Specifically, we analyze how the backbone taper angle influences the robot's configuration space and manipulability. The model is validated against motion capture data, achieving centimeter-level shape prediction accuracy after calibrating Young's modulus via a line search that minimizes modeling error. We further demonstrate teleoperated grasping using an endoscopic gripper routed along the continuum robot, mounted on a 6-DoF robotic arm. Parameterized iLogic/CAD scripts are provided for rapid geometry generation and scaling. The presented framework establishes a simple, rapid, and reproducible pathway from parametric design to controlled tendon actuation for tapered, tendon-driven continuum robots manufactured using fused deposition modeling 3D printers.

AI.tldr: A scalable, low-cost, 3D-printed tendon-driven continuum robot with a tapered TPU backbone; develops a Cosserat-rod-based forward kinetostatic model for spatially varying cross-sections; validates with motion capture; demonstrates teleoperated grasping, and provides parametric CAD/scripts for rapid design-to-actuation.
AI.motivation: Address the need for customizable, inexpensive continuum robots with high curvature and distal compliance for inspection/manipulation, while accounting for graded stiffness from backbone tapering.
AI.method: Fabricate a TPU-tapered backbone with integrated electronics base for tendon tension control and sensing; extend tendon-actuated Cosserat rod modeling to handle spatially varying cross-sectional geometry; calibrate Young's modulus via line search; validate predictions against motion capture; demonstrate teleoperation with an endoscopic gripper on a 6-DoF arm; supply iLogic/CAD scripts for scalable geometry generation; use FDM 3D printing.
AI.result: Model achieves centimeter-level shape prediction accuracy after calibration; shows high curvature and enhanced distal compliance due to tapering; successful teleoperated grasp; provides a fast, reproducible design-to-actuation workflow.
AI.conclusion: The work offers a simple, rapid, and reproducible framework for actuating tapered, tendon-driven continuum robots manufactured by FDM, emphasizing customizability, low cost, and applicability to compliant robotic inspection/manipulation tasks.

title: MANAR: Memory-augmented Attention with Navigational Abstract Conceptual Representation
summary: MANAR (Memory-augmented Attention with Navigational Abstract Conceptual Representation), contextualization layer generalizes standard multi-head attention (MHA) by instantiating the principles of Global Workspace Theory (GWT). While MHA enables unconstrained all-to-all communication, it lacks the functional bottleneck and global integration mechanisms hypothesized in cognitive models of consciousness. MANAR addresses this by implementing a central workspace through a trainable memory of abstract concepts and an Abstract Conceptual Representation (ACR). The architecture follows a two-stage logic that maps directly to GWT mechanics: (i) an integration phase, where retrieved memory concepts converge to form a collective "mental image" (the ACR) based on input stimuli; and (ii) a broadcasting phase, where this global state navigates and informs the contextualization of individual local tokens. We demonstrate that efficient linear-time scaling is a fundamental architectural byproduct of instantiating GWT functional bottleneck, as routing global information through a constant-sized ACR resolves the quadratic complexity inherent in standard attention. MANAR is a compatible re-parameterization of MHA with identical semantic roles for its projections, enabling knowledge transfer from pretrained transformers via weight-copy and thus overcoming the adoption barriers of structurally incompatible linear-time alternatives. MANAR enables non-convex contextualization, synthesizing representations that provably lie outside the convex hull of input tokens - a mathematical reflection of the creative synthesis described in GWT. Empirical evaluations confirm that MANAR matches or exceeds strong baselines across language (GLUE score of 85.1), vision (83.9% ImageNet-1K), and speech (2.7% WER on LibriSpeech), positioning it as an efficient and expressive alternative to quadratic attention.

AI.tldr: MANAR introduces a memory-augmented, GWT-inspired reparameterization of multi-head attention that uses a central, constant-sized Abstract Conceptual Representation (ACR) to achieve linear-time scaling and non-convex contextualization, matching or surpassing strong baselines across language, vision, and speech tasks.
AI.motivation: Standard MHA enables unrestricted all-to-all communication but lacks a functional bottleneck and global integration mechanism predicted by cognitive models of consciousness; a bottleneck-based, memory-driven integration (GWT) could yield scalable, expressive context with potential for transfer from pretrained transformers.
AI.method: Two-stage process: (i) integration phase retrieves memory concepts to form an ACR from inputs; (ii) broadcasting phase uses the ACR to contextualize local tokens. A central, trainable memory of abstract concepts with an ACR enables a linear-time, quadratic-attention-free mechanism; re-parameterization preserves semantic roles for compatibility with pretrained transformers and supports weight-copy; allows non-convex contextualization.
AI.result: Empirical results show MANAR matches or exceeds strong baselines on language (GLUE 85.1), vision (ImageNet-1K 83.9%), and speech (LibriSpeech 2.7% WER).
AI.conclusion: MANAR provides an efficient, expressive alternative to quadratic attention, with linear-time scaling due to a fixed-size global workspace, enabling non-convex representations and facilitating knowledge transfer from pretrained models.

title: Foundations and Architectures of Artificial Intelligence for Motor Insurance
summary: This handbook presents a systematic treatment of the foundations and architectures of artificial intelligence for motor insurance, grounded in large-scale real-world deployment. It formalizes a vertically integrated AI paradigm that unifies perception, multimodal reasoning, and production infrastructure into a cohesive intelligence stack for automotive risk assessment and claims processing. At its core, the handbook develops domain-adapted transformer architectures for structured visual understanding, relational vehicle representation learning, and multimodal document intelligence, enabling end-to-end automation of vehicle damage analysis, claims evaluation, and underwriting workflows. These components are composed into a scalable pipeline operating under practical constraints observed in nationwide motor insurance systems in Thailand. Beyond model design, the handbook emphasizes the co-evolution of learning algorithms and MLOps practices, establishing a principled framework for translating modern artificial intelligence into reliable, production-grade systems in high-stakes industrial environments.

AI.tldr: A framework for AI-powered motor insurance combining perception, reasoning, and production infrastructure into a cohesive intelligence stack, with domain-adapted transformers for visual and multimodal data, enabling automated damage assessment and claims workflows; demonstrated in Thailand's nationwide system and paired with MLOps for production-grade deployment.
AI.motivation: To address real-world, high-stakes motor insurance tasks—damage analysis, claims processing, underwriting—through end-to-end AI systems that are scalable, reliable, and deployable in nationwide settings; bridging model design with operational practices.
AI.method: Develop domain-adapted transformer architectures for structured visual understanding, relational vehicle representation learning, and multimodal document intelligence; build an end-to-end pipeline integrating perception, reasoning, and production infrastructure; emphasize co-evolution of learning algorithms with MLOps in a production-grade, risk-sensitive environment; apply in the context of Thailand’s motor insurance landscape.
AI.result: A design blueprint and architectural framework for a scalable, end-to-end AI system for motor insurance; claims to improve automation across damage analysis, claims evaluation, and underwriting; deployment-oriented with attention to real-world constraints and lifecycle management (MLOps).
AI.conclusion: Proposes a principled, production-ready AI stack that unifies perception, reasoning, and documentation with domain-specific adaptations to motor insurance, highlighting the importance of integrated MLOps and deployment considerations for high-stakes industry adoption.

title: Towards Noise-Resilient Quantum Multi-Armed and Stochastic Linear Bandits
summary: Quantum multi-armed bandits (MAB) and stochastic linear bandits (SLB) have recently attracted significant attention, as their quantum counterparts can achieve quadratic speedups over classical MAB and SLB. However, most existing quantum MAB algorithms assume ideal quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) procedures on noise-free circuits, overlooking the impact of noise in current noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices. In this paper, we study a noise-robust QMC algorithm that improves estimation accuracy when querying quantum reward oracles. Building on this estimator, we propose noise-robust QMAB and QSLB algorithms that enhance performance in noisy environments while preserving the advantage over classical methods. Experiments show that our noise-robust approach improves QMAB estimation accuracy and reduces regret under several quantum noise models.

AI.tldr: A noise-robust quantum Monte Carlo estimator is developed to mitigate NISQ noise in quantum MAB/SLB; based on this estimator, the authors propose noise-robust QMAB and QSLB algorithms that maintain quantum advantages; experiments show reduced estimation error and regret under quantum noise.
AI.motivation: Quantum MAB/SLB promise quadratic speedups, but noise in NISQ devices undermines performance; most prior work assumes ideal QMC; need robust approaches to exploit quantum advantages in practical settings.
AI.method: Develop a noise-robust QMC estimator for querying quantum reward oracles; integrate it into QMAB and QSLB algorithms; provide theoretical and empirical evaluation under various noise models.
AI.result: Estimation accuracy improved and regret reduced in simulations across multiple noise models; quantum advantage preserved over classical benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Noise-robust QMC-based QMAB/QSLB enable more reliable quantum learning in practical noisy devices, bridging theory to practice.

title: Introducing M: A Modular, Modifiable Social Robot
summary: We present M, an open-source, low-cost social robot platform designed to reduce platform friction that slows social robotics research by making robots easier to reproduce, modify, and deploy in real-world settings. M combines a modular mechanical design, multimodal sensing, and expressive yet mechanically simple actuation architecture with a ROS2-native software package that cleanly separates perception, expression control, and data management. The platform includes a simulation environment with interface equivalence to hardware to support rapid sim-to-real transfer of interaction behaviors. We demonstrate extensibility through additional sensing/actuation modules and provide example interaction templates for storytelling and two-way conversational coaching. Finally, we report real-world use in participatory design and week-long in-home deployments, showing how M can serve as a practical foundation for longitudinal, reproducible social robotics research.

AI.tldr: An open-source, low-cost social robot platform (M) reduces research friction by modular hardware, multimodal sensing, simple actuation, and a ROS2-based software stack with hardware-mapped simulation for rapid sim-to-real transfer; demonstrated through storytelling and conversational coaching templates and real-world in-home deployments, supporting longitudinal, reproducible social robotics research.
AI.motivation: To lower barriers in social robotics research by making robot hardware easily reproducible, modifiable, and deployable in real-world settings; enable rapid sim-to-real workflows and encourage reproducible, longitudinal studies.
AI.method: Modular mechanical design; multimodal sensing; mechanically simple actuation; ROS2-native software architecture separating perception, expression control, and data management; hardware-interface-midelity simulation environment; extensibility via additional sensing/actuation modules; example interaction templates for storytelling and two-way conversational coaching.
AI.result: Demonstrated extensibility and real-world usage through participatory design and week-long in-home deployments; validated M as a practical foundation for longitudinal, reproducible social robotics research.
AI.conclusion: M provides a practical, open-source platform that lowers research friction, supports rapid sim-to-real transfer, and enables reproducible, longitudinal social robotics studies, with a scalable base for future sensing/actuation extensions.

title: Accurate and Efficient Multi-Channel Time Series Forecasting via Sparse Attention Mechanism
summary:
The task of multi-channel time series forecasting is ubiquitous in numerous fields such as finance, supply chain management, and energy planning. It is critical to effectively capture complex dynamic dependencies within and between channels for accurate predictions. However, traditional method paid few attentions on learning the interaction among channels. This paper proposes Linear-Network (Li-Net), a novel architecture designed for multi-channel time series forecasting that captures the linear and non-linear dependencies among channels. Li-Net dynamically compresses representations across sequence and channel dimensions, processes the information through a configurable non-linear module and subsequently reconstructs the forecasts. Moreover, Li-Net integrates a sparse Top-K Softmax attention mechanism within a multi-scale projection framework to address these challenges. A core innovation is its ability to seamlessly incorporate and fuse multi-modal embeddings, guiding the sparse attention process to focus on the most informative time steps and feature channels. Through the experiment results on multiple real-world benchmark datasets demonstrate that Li-Net achieves competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art baseline methods. Furthermore, Li-Net provides a superior balance between prediction accuracy and computational burden, exhibiting significantly lower memory usage and faster inference times. Detailed ablation studies and parameter sensitivity analyses validate the effectiveness of each key component in our proposed architecture.
  Keywords: Multivariate Time Series Forecasting, Sparse Attention Mechanism, Multimodal Information Fusion, Non-linear relationship

AI.tldr: Li-Net introduces a linear–nonlinear hybrid architecture for multivariate time-series forecasting that dynamically compresses sequence and channel representations, uses sparse Top-K Softmax attention within a multi-scale projection, and fuses multimodal embeddings to guide attention. It delivers competitive accuracy with lower memory usage and faster inference, backed by ablation and sensitivity analyses.
AI.motivation: Model inter-channel dependencies in multivariate time series, which traditional methods often underemphasize, while also achieving efficiency suitable for large-scale or real-time forecasting.
AI.method: Li-Net dynamically compresses representations across sequence and channel dimensions, applies a configurable non-linear module, and reconstructs forecasts. It integrates a sparse Top-K Softmax attention mechanism within a multi-scale projection framework, guided by fused multimodal embeddings to focus attention on informative time steps and feature channels.
AI.result: Experimental results on real-world benchmarks show competitive performance against state-of-the-art baselines, with a superior accuracy–computational burden trade-off: lower memory usage and faster inference times. Detailed ablations and parameter sensitivity analyses validate the effectiveness of each key component.
AI.conclusion: Li-Net effectively captures linear and nonlinear dependencies among channels through a sparse, multimodal-guided attention and multi-scale projection framework, achieving competitive accuracy with improved efficiency; the reported ablations support the contribution of each component.

title: OnlinePG: Online Open-Vocabulary Panoptic Mapping with 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: Open-vocabulary scene understanding with online panoptic mapping is essential for embodied applications to perceive and interact with environments. However, existing methods are predominantly offline or lack instance-level understanding, limiting their applicability to real-world robotic tasks. In this paper, we propose OnlinePG, a novel and effective system that integrates geometric reconstruction and open-vocabulary perception using 3D Gaussian Splatting in an online setting. Technically, to achieve online panoptic mapping, we employ an efficient local-to-global paradigm with a sliding window. To build local consistency map, we construct a 3D segment clustering graph that jointly leverages geometric and semantic cues, fusing inconsistent segments within sliding window into complete instances. Subsequently, to update the global map, we construct explicit grids with spatial attributes for the local 3D Gaussian map and fuse them into the global map via robust bidirectional bipartite 3D Gaussian instance matching. Finally, we utilize the fused VLM features inside the 3D spatial attribute grids to achieve open-vocabulary scene understanding. Extensive experiments on widely used datasets demonstrate that our method achieves better performance among online approaches, while maintaining real-time efficiency.

AI.tldr: OnlinePG enables online panoptic mapping with open-vocabulary perception using 3D Gaussian Splatting, combining local-to-global updates with a sliding window, a 3D segment clustering graph for instance fusion, and bipartite matching to fuse local maps into a global map, guided by VLM features for open vocabulary.
AI.motivation: To achieve real-time, instance-level, open-vocabulary scene understanding in embodied robotics, addressing limitations of offline methods that lack online operation and fine-grained semantics.
AI.method: An online pipeline with a local-to-global sliding window. A 3D segment clustering graph jointly leveraging geometric and semantic cues fuses inconsistent segments into complete instances within the window. Global map updates by representing the local 3D Gaussian map with explicit spatial grids and fusing into the global map via robust bidirectional bipartite 3D Gaussian instance matching. Open-vocabulary understanding is enabled by integrating fused VLM features into the 3D spatial grids and performing open-vocabulary perception on the fused map.
AI.result: Experiments on standard datasets show competitive or superior performance for online panoptic mapping with real-time efficiency compared to baseline online methods.
AI.conclusion: OnlinePG delivers effective online panoptic mapping with open-vocabulary perception, suitable for real-world robotic tasks requiring real-time, instance-level, and open-set understanding.

title: MLOW: Interpretable Low-Rank Frequency Magnitude Decomposition of Multiple Effects for Time Series Forecasting
summary: Separating multiple effects in time series is fundamental yet challenging for time-series forecasting (TSF). However, existing TSF models cannot effectively learn interpretable multi-effect decomposition by their smoothing-based temporal techniques. Here, a new interpretable frequency-based decomposition pipeline MLOW captures the insight: a time series can be represented as a magnitude spectrum multiplied by the corresponding phase-aware basis functions, and the magnitude spectrum distribution of a time series always exhibits observable patterns for different effects. MLOW learns a low-rank representation of the magnitude spectrum to capture dominant trending and seasonal effects. We explore low-rank methods, including PCA, NMF, and Semi-NMF, and find that none can simultaneously achieve interpretable, efficient and generalizable decomposition. Thus, we propose hyperplane-nonnegative matrix factorization (Hyperplane-NMF). Further, to address the frequency (spectral) leakage restricting high-quality low-rank decomposition, MLOW enables a flexible selection of input horizons and frequency levels via a mathematical mechanism. Visual analysis demonstrates that MLOW enables interpretable and hierarchical multiple-effect decomposition, robust to noises. It can also enable plug-and-play in existing TSF backbones with remarkable performance improvement but minimal architectural modifications.

AI.tldr: Introduces MLOW, a frequency-based, interpretable multi-effect decomposition for time-series forecasting, using Hyperplane-NMF to learn a low-rank magnitude spectrum and enabling plug-and-play improvements for TSF backbones.
AI.motivation: Time-series forecasting struggles with separating multiple effects (trend, seasonality, noise) in an interpretable, efficient way; smoothing-based methods are limited and existing low-rank approaches (PCA, NMF, Semi-NMF) lack combined interpretability, efficiency, and generalizability.
AI.method: Represent a time series as a magnitude spectrum multiplied by phase-aware basis functions; learn a low-rank magnitude spectrum via Hyperplane-NMF; address spectral leakage with flexible input horizons and frequency levels; provide visual analysis and enable plug-and-play integration into existing TSF backbones.
AI.result: MLOW enables interpretable, hierarchical decomposition of multiple effects, robust to noise; demonstrates significant performance improvements with minimal architectural changes when used as a plug-in in TSF backbones.
AI.conclusion: MLOW offers an interpretable, scalable, frequency-based framework for decomposing multiple effects in time series and can serve as a plug-in to enhance existing forecasting models with minimal modification.

title: Meanings and Measurements: Multi-Agent Probabilistic Grounding for Vision-Language Navigation
comment: Equal contribution: Swagat Padhan and Lakshya Jain, 9 pages, 6 figures, paper website: https://lakshya-asu.github.io/Meanings-Measurements-Multi-Agent-Probabilistic-Grounding/
summary: Robots collaborating with humans must convert natural language goals into actionable, physically grounded decisions. For example, executing a command such as "go two meters to the right of the fridge" requires grounding semantic references, spatial relations, and metric constraints within a 3D scene. While recent vision language models (VLMs) demonstrate strong semantic grounding capabilities, they are not explicitly designed to reason about metric constraints in physically defined spaces. In this work, we empirically demonstrate that state-of-the-art VLM-based grounding approaches struggle with complex metric-semantic language queries. To address this limitation, we propose MAPG (Multi-Agent Probabilistic Grounding), an agentic framework that decomposes language queries into structured subcomponents and queries a VLM to ground each component. MAPG then probabilistically composes these grounded outputs to produce metrically consistent, actionable decisions in 3D space. We evaluate MAPG on the HM-EQA benchmark and show consistent performance improvements over strong baselines. Furthermore, we introduce a new benchmark, MAPG-Bench, specifically designed to evaluate metric-semantic goal grounding, addressing a gap in existing language grounding evaluations. We also present a real-world robot demonstration showing that MAPG transfers beyond simulation when a structured scene representation is available.

AI.tldr: MAPG decomposes natural-language goals into structured subcomponents, grounds each via a vision-language model, and probabilistically fuses the results to produce metrically consistent 3D actions, improving metric-semantic grounding; introduces MAPG-Bench and demonstrates sim-to-real transfer.
AI.motivation: Vision-language grounding excels at semantics but struggles with metric constraints in physically defined spaces; enabling robots to satisfy metric-semantic queries (e.g., 'two meters to the right of the fridge') requires explicit metric reasoning and reliable grounding.
AI.method: Decompose language queries into semantic, spatial, and metric subcomponents; ground each component by querying a VLM; probabilistically combine grounded outputs to enforce metric consistency; evaluate on HM-EQA, and introduce a new MAPG-Bench benchmark; demonstrate real-world transfer with a structured scene representation.
AI.result: MAPG achieves consistent improvements over strong baselines on HM-EQA; MAPG-Bench addresses a gap by targeting metric-semantic grounding; a real-world robot demonstration shows transfer beyond simulation when a structured scene representation is available.
AI.conclusion: MAPG effectively grounds metric-semantic language in 3D space by leveraging component-wise grounding and probabilistic fusion, and provides benchmarks and demonstrations that support real-world applicability.

title: MemMA: Coordinating the Memory Cycle through Multi-Agent Reasoning and In-Situ Self-Evolution
summary: Memory-augmented LLM agents maintain external memory banks to support long-horizon interaction, yet most existing systems treat construction, retrieval, and utilization as isolated subroutines. This creates two coupled challenges: strategic blindness on the forward path of the memory cycle, where construction and retrieval are driven by local heuristics rather than explicit strategic reasoning, and sparse, delayed supervision on the backward path, where downstream failures rarely translate into direct repairs of the memory bank. To address these challenges, we propose MemMA, a plug-and-play multi-agent framework that coordinates the memory cycle along both the forward and backward paths. On the forward path, a Meta-Thinker produces structured guidance that steers a Memory Manager during construction and directs a Query Reasoner during iterative retrieval. On the backward path, MemMA introduces in-situ self-evolving memory construction, which synthesizes probe QA pairs, verifies the current memory, and converts failures into repair actions before the memory is finalized. Extensive experiments on LoCoMo show that MemMA consistently outperforms existing baselines across multiple LLM backbones and improves three different storage backends in a plug-and-play manner. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/ventr1c/memma.

AI.tldr: MemMA is a plug-and-play, multi-agent framework that coordinates the memory cycle in memory-augmented LLM agents by guiding construction and retrieval with a Meta-Thinker and enabling in-situ self-evolving memory repair, achieving state-of-the-art results on LoCoMo across multiple backbones and storage backends.
AI.motivation: To overcome two key limitations in existing memory-augmented LLM systems: (1) forward-path strategic blindness where memory construction and retrieval rely on local heuristics rather than explicit planning, and (2) backward-path sparse supervision where downstream failures seldom trigger memory repairs.
AI.method: MemMA introduces a Meta-Thinker that provides structured guidance to a Memory Manager during memory construction and to a Query Reasoner during iterative retrieval. It also implements in-situ self-evolving memory construction on the backward path, synthesizing probe QA pairs, verifying memory, and turning failures into repair actions before finalization. The framework is designed to be plug-and-play with different LLM backbones and storage backends.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation on the LoCoMo benchmark shows MemMA consistently outperforms existing baselines across multiple LLM backbones and improves three different storage backends in a plug-and-play manner.
AI.conclusion: Coordinating forward and backward memory cycles with a Meta-Thinker-guided memory manager and probe-based memory repair yields robust, scalable memory-augmented LLM agents that better handle long-horizon interactions; code is publicly available.

title: CAFlow: Adaptive-Depth Single-Step Flow Matching for Efficient Histopathology Super-Resolution
summary: In digital pathology, whole-slide images routinely exceed gigapixel resolution, making computationally intensive generative super-resolution (SR) impractical for routine deployment. We introduce CAFlow, an adaptive-depth single-step flow-matching framework that routes each image tile to the shallowest network exit that preserves reconstruction quality.
