AI.tldr: Cross-layer attention propagation (HAViT) stores and blends historical attention across encoder layers to refine inter-layer information flow, yielding consistent gains with minimal changes.
AI.motivation: Vision Transformers' attention operates independently per layer, which can impede information flow and feature learning. Introducing historical attention propagation aims to progressively refine attention patterns across the transformer hierarchy.
AI.method: Maintain attention matrices across layers and blend them with current attention using a blending hyperparameter (alpha) to fuse historical and present information; requires minimal architectural changes (storage and blending operations).
AI.result: Demonstrates consistent accuracy improvements on CIFAR-100 (75.74%→77.07%, +1.33) and TinyImageNet (57.82%→59.07%, +1.25). Cross-architecture gains include CaiT (+1.01%). The optimal alpha for blending is 0.45 across configurations; random initialization outperforms zero initialization. Code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: Cross-layer attention propagation provides a principled, scalable refinement of inter-layer information flow in ViTs with minimal overhead, yielding robust improvements across architectures and datasets.

title: GAPSL: A Gradient-Aligned Parallel Split Learning on Heterogeneous Data
summary: The increasing complexity of neural networks poses significant challenges for democratizing FL on resource?constrained client devices. Parallel split learning (PSL) has emerged as a promising solution by offloading substantial computing workload to a server via model partitioning, shrinking client-side computing load, and eliminating the client-side model aggregation for reduced communication and deployment costs. Since PSL is aggregation-free, it suffers from severe training divergence stemming from gradient directional inconsistency across clients. To address this challenge, we propose GAPSL, a gradient-aligned PSL framework that comprises two key components: leader gradient identification (LGI) and gradient direction alignment (GDA). LGI dynamically selects a set of directionally consistent client gradients to construct a leader gradient that captures the global convergence trend. GDA employs a direction-aware regularization to align each client's gradient with the leader gradient, thereby mitigating inter-device gradient directional inconsistency and enhancing model convergence. We evaluate GAPSL on a prototype computing testbed. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GAPSL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art benchmarks in training accuracy and latency.

AI.tldr: GAPSL: gradient-aligned parallel split learning with leader gradient identification and gradient direction alignment to mitigate divergence in aggregation-free PSL.
AI.motivation: Resource-constrained client devices in federated learning (FL) create a need for low-compute, low-communication approaches. Parallel split learning (PSL) offloads computation to a server, reducing client-side load but suffers from gradient directional inconsistency and training divergence due to aggregation-free design.
AI.method: GAPSL introduces two mechanisms: (1) leader gradient identification (LGI) dynamically selects directionally consistent client gradients to form a leader gradient representing global convergence; (2) gradient direction alignment (GDA) applies a direction-aware regularizer to align each client's gradient with the leader gradient, mitigating gradient divergence.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation on a prototype computing testbed shows GAPSL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art PSL/FL benchmarks in training accuracy and latency.
AI.conclusion: Gradient alignment in PSL alleviates inter-client gradient mismatch, stabilizes convergence, and reduces communication/deployment costs, enhancing practicality of PSL-based FL on resource-limited devices.

title: ProRL Agent: Rollout-as-a-Service for RL Training of Multi-Turn LLM Agents
summary: Multi-turn LLM agents are increasingly important for solving complex, interactive tasks, and reinforcement learning (RL) is a key ingredient for improving their long-horizon behavior. However, RL training requires generating large numbers of sandboxed rollout trajectories, and existing infrastructures often couple rollout orchestration with the training loop, making systems hard to migrate and maintain. Under the rollout-as-a-service philosophy, we present ProRL Agent , a scalable infrastructure that serves the full agentic rollout lifecycle through an API service. ProRL Agent also provides standardized and extensible sandbox environments that support diverse agentic tasks in rootless HPC settings. We validate ProRL Agent through RL training on software engineering, math, STEM, and coding tasks. ProRL Agent is open-sourced and integrated as part of NVIDIA NeMo Gym.

AI.tldr: ProRL Agent: a rollout-as-a-service infrastructure for scalable RL training of multi-turn LLM agents; decouples rollout orchestration from training; provides rootless HPC sandbox environments; open-source in NVIDIA NeMo Gym.
AI.motivation: Overcomes bottlenecks in generating large sandbox rollout trajectories and tight coupling between rollout and training, enabling scalable, maintainable RL pipelines for long-horizon, interactive tasks.
AI.method: Design and implement ProRL Agent as an API service managing the full agentic rollout lifecycle; provide standardized/extensible sandbox environments suitable for rootless HPC; integrate with NVIDIA NeMo Gym.
AI.result: Validated through RL training across software engineering, math, STEM, coding tasks; demonstrably scalable; open-sourced.
AI.conclusion: Rollout lifecycle can be standardized and decoupled from training, facilitating migration, maintenance, and reproducibility in RL for multi-turn LLM agents.

title: Color image restoration based on nonlocal saturation-value similarity
summary: In this paper, we propose and develop a novel nonlocal variational technique based on saturation-value similarity for color image restoration. In traditional nonlocal methods, image patches are extracted from red, green and blue channels of a color image directly, and the color information can not be described finely because the patch similarity is mainly based on the grayscale value of independent channel. The main aim of this paper is to propose and develop a novel nonlocal regularization method by considering the similarity of image patches in saturation-value channel of a color image. In particular, we first establish saturation-value similarity based nonlocal total variation by incorporating saturation-value similarity of color image patches into the proposed nonlocal gradients, which can describe the saturation and value similarity of two adjacent color image patches. The proposed nonlocal variational models are then formulated based on saturation-value similarity based nonlocal total variation. Moreover, we design an effective and efficient algorithm to solve the proposed optimization problem numerically by employing bregmanized operator splitting method, and we also study the convergence of the proposed algorithms. Numerical examples are presented to demonstrate that the performance of the proposed models is better than that of other testing methods in terms of visual quality and some quantitative metrics including peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR), structural similarity index (SSIM), quaternion structural similarity index (QSSIM) and S-CIELAB color error.

AI.tldr: Introduces saturation-value similarity for nonlocal total variation in color image restoration, addressing channel-wise limitations by operating in SV color space; uses a nonlocal variational model solved with Bregmanized operator splitting and proves convergence; reports improvements in PSNR/SSIM/QSSIM/S-CIELAB over competing methods.
AI.motivation: To overcome the insufficiency of grayscale-based patch similarity in color channels by exploiting saturation and value similarity, enabling better preservation of color and texture in restored color images.
AI.method: Define a saturation-value-based patch similarity measure and incorporate it into a nonlocal total variation regularizer. Formulate a nonlocal variational model using this SV-based similarity, and solve via a Bregmanized operator splitting (BOXS) method with convergence analysis.
AI.result: Numerical experiments show the SV-based nonlocal model yields superior visual quality and quantitative metrics (PSNR, SSIM, QSSIM, S-CIELAB color error) compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: SV-based nonlocal regularization provides improved color image restoration, effectively capturing saturation and value relationships between patches; the approach is computationally amenable via BOS, with demonstrated convergence and potential for broader color-space extensions.

title: HEP Statistical Inference for UAV Fault Detection: CLs, LRT, and SBI Applied to Blade Damage
summary: This paper transfers three statistical methods from particle physics to multirotor propeller fault detection: the likelihood ratio test (LRT) for binary detection, the CLs modified frequentist method for false alarm rate control, and sequential neural posterior estimation (SNPE) for quantitative fault characterization. Operating on spectral features tied to rotor harmonic physics, the system returns three outputs: binary detection, controlled false alarm rates, and calibrated posteriors over fault severity and motor location. On UAV-FD, a hexarotor dataset of 18 real flights with 5% and 10% blade damage, leave-one-flight-out cross-validation gives AUC 0.862 +/- 0.007 (95% CI: 0.849--0.876), outperforming CUSUM (0.708 +/- 0.010), autoencoder (0.753 +/- 0.009), and LSTM autoencoder (0.551). At 5% false alarm rate the system detects 93% of significant and 81% of subtle blade damage. On PADRE, a quadrotor platform, AUC reaches 0.986 after refitting only the generative models. SNPE gives a full posterior over fault severity (90% credible interval coverage 92--100%, MAE 0.012), so the output includes uncertainty rather than just a point estimate or fault flag. Per-flight sequential detection achieves 100% fault detection with 94% overall accuracy.

AI.tldr: Transferrs three statistical methods from particle physics to UAV fault detection: LRT for binary detection, CLs for false alarm rate control, and SNPE for quantitative fault characterization, producing binary output, calibrated FPR control, and calibrated posteriors over fault severity/location; achieves high AUCs on UAV-FD and PADRE datasets with per-flight sequential detection and uncertainty quantification.
AI.motivation: Bridge high-energy physics statistical methodology with multirotor fault diagnosis to achieve reliable detection with principled uncertainty quantification across platforms.
AI.method: Apply likelihood ratio test (LRT) for binary rotor-damage detection on spectrum features tied to rotor-harmonic physics; employ the CLs modified frequentist method to control false alarm rate; use sequential neural posterior estimation (SNPE) to infer full posterior over fault severity and motor location; evaluate on UAV-FD (18 real flights, leave-one-flight-out CV) and PADRE (quadrotor platform); compare against CUSUM, autoencoder, and LSTM autoencoder; per-flight sequential detection.
AI.result: UAV-FD: AUC 0.862 ± 0.007 (95% CI: 0.849–0.876) vs CUSUM 0.708 ± 0.010, autoencoder 0.753 ± 0.009, LSTM autoencoder 0.551. At 5% FPR, detects 93% of significant and 81% of subtle blade damage. PADRE: AUC 0.986 after refitting only the generative models. SNPE yields a full posterior over fault severity (90% credible interval coverage 92–100%, MAE 0.012). Per-flight sequential detection achieves 100% fault detection with 94% overall accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The approach delivers high detection performance and meaningful uncertainty quantification, enabling calibrated decision-making for UAV maintenance and safety, with strong cross-platform results and a demonstrated path to uncertainty-aware autonomous fault diagnosis.

title: RewardFlow: Topology-Aware Reward Propagation on State Graphs for Agentic RL with Large Language Models
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) holds significant promise for enhancing the agentic reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) with external environments. However, the inherent sparsity of terminal rewards hinders fine-grained, state-level optimization. Although process reward modeling offers a promising alternative, training dedicated reward models often entails substantial computational costs and scaling difficulties. To address these challenges, we introduce RewardFlow, a lightweight method for estimating state-level rewards tailored to agentic reasoning tasks. RewardFlow leverages the intrinsic topological structure of states within reasoning trajectories by constructing state graphs. This enables an analysis of state-wise contributions to success, followed by topology-aware graph propagation to quantify contributions and yield objective, state-level rewards. When integrated as dense rewards for RL optimization, RewardFlow substantially outperforms prior RL baselines across four agentic reasoning benchmarks, demonstrating superior performance, robustness, and training efficiency. The implementation of RewardFlow is publicly available at https://github.com/tmlr-group/RewardFlow.

AI.tldr: RewardFlow builds state graphs from reasoning trajectories to derive dense, state-level rewards via topology-aware propagation, enabling efficient RL for agentic reasoning and outperforming baselines on four benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Tackle sparse terminal rewards and the high computational cost of training dedicated reward models in RL for LLM-agentic reasoning.
AI.method: Constructs state graphs from reasoning trajectories, analyzes state-wise contributions to success, then applies topology-aware graph propagation to produce objective state-level rewards for dense RL optimization.
AI.result: Demonstrates superior performance, robustness, and training efficiency over prior RL baselines on four agentic reasoning benchmarks; implementation publicly available.
AI.conclusion: RewardFlow provides a lightweight, scalable method for dense state-level rewards in agentic reasoning, reducing reliance on sparse terminal rewards and expensive reward models.

title: AU Codes, Language, and Synthesis: Translating Anatomy to Text for Facial Behavior Synthesis
summary: Facial behavior synthesis remains a critical yet underexplored challenge. While text-to-face models have made progress, they often rely on coarse emotion categories, which lack the nuance needed to capture the full spectrum of human nonverbal communication. Action Units (AUs) provide a more precise and anatomically grounded alternative. However, current AU-based approaches typically encode AUs as one-hot vectors, modeling compound expressions as simple linear combinations of individual AUs. This linearity becomes problematic when handling conflicting AUs--defined as those which activate the same facial muscle with opposing actions. Such cases lead to anatomically implausible artifacts and unnatural motion superpositions. To address this, we propose a novel method that represents facial behavior through natural language descriptions of AUs. This approach preserves the expressiveness of the AU framework while enabling explicit modeling of complex and conflicting AUs. It also unlocks the potential of modern text-to-image models for high-fidelity facial synthesis. Supporting this direction, we introduce BP4D-AUText, the first large-scale text-image paired dataset for complex facial behavior. It is synthesized by applying a rule-based Dynamic AU Text Processor to the BP4D and BP4D+ datasets. We further propose VQ-AUFace, a generative model that leverages facial structural priors to synthesize realistic and diverse facial behaviors from text. Extensive quantitative experiments and user studies demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms existing methods. It excels in generating facial expressions that are anatomically plausible, behaviorally rich, and perceptually convincing, particularly under challenging conditions involving conflicting AUs.

AI.tldr: Represents facial AUs as natural language text instead of one-hot vectors, enabling nuanced and conflicting AUs handling; introduces BP4D-AUText dataset and VQ-AUFace model; demonstrates superior plausibility and expressiveness in text-guided facial synthesis.
AI.motivation: One-hot AU encoding is brittle for complex or conflicting expressions and fails to capture nuanced nonverbal cues. A language-based representation can express richer semantics and leverage advances in text-to-image synthesis for higher-fidelity facial generation.
AI.method: Introduce AU-to-text representation via Dynamic AU Text Processor to convert AU configurations into descriptive prompts. Create BP4D-AUText by annotating BP4D/BP4D+ with textual AU descriptions. Propose VQ-AUFace, a generative model that uses facial structure priors and text prompts to synthesize diverse, high-fidelity facial behaviors.
AI.result: Through quantitative metrics and user studies, the approach yields more anatomically plausible, behaviorally rich, and perceptually convincing facial expressions than AU-based baselines, particularly when handling conflicting AUs.
AI.conclusion: Language-described AUs are a viable, expressive alternative to one-hot AU encoding for facial behavior synthesis, enabling better scalability with text-to-image systems; the BP4D-AUText dataset and VQ-AUFace model underpin this approach and show promise for challenging expressions.

title: SINDy-KANs: Sparse identification of non-linear dynamics through Kolmogorov-Arnold networks
summary: Kolmogorov-Arnold networks (KANs) have arisen as a potential way to enhance the interpretability of machine learning. However, solutions learned by KANs are not necessarily interpretable, in the sense of being sparse or parsimonious. Sparse identification of nonlinear dynamics (SINDy) is a complementary approach that allows for learning sparse equations for dynamical systems from data; however, learned equations are limited by the library. In this work, we present SINDy-KANs, which simultaneously train a KAN and a SINDy-like representation to increase interpretability of KAN representations with SINDy applied at the level of each activation function, while maintaining the function compositions possible through deep KANs. We apply our method to a number of symbolic regression tasks, including dynamical systems, to show accurate equation discovery across a range of systems.

AI.tldr: SINDy-KANs combine Kolmogorov-Arnold networks with a SINDy-like sparse representation at each activation to yield interpretable, sparse, decomposable dynamical representations within deep architectures; demonstrated on symbolic regression and dynamical systems.
AI.motivation: Improve interpretability of Kolmogorov-Arnold networks by enforcing sparsity and explicit equation form at the activation level, leveraging SINDy’s sparse identification to produce parsimonious, human-readable models without sacrificing depth-based expressivity.
AI.method: Jointly train a Kolmogorov-Arnold network and a SINDy-like sparse representation at the level of each activation function, enabling sparse dynamical equations per activation while preserving compositional depth.
AI.result: The approach achieves accurate equation discovery across a range of systems in symbolic regression tasks and dynamical systems, producing interpretable, sparse representations.
AI.conclusion: SINDy-KANs provide a pathway to interpretable deep architectures by integrating SINDy sparsity at activation nodes within KANs, achieving interpretable equation discovery while maintaining deep functional expressivity.

title: Conflict-Based Search for Multi Agent Path Finding with Asynchronous Actions
summary: Multi-Agent Path Finding (MAPF) seeks collision-free paths for multiple agents from their respective start locations to their respective goal locations while minimizing path costs. Most existing MAPF algorithms rely on a common assumption of synchronized actions, where the actions of all agents start at the same time and always take a time unit, which may limit the use of MAPF planners in practice. To get rid of this assumption, Continuous-time Conflict-Based Search (CCBS) is a popular approach that can find optimal solutions for MAPF with asynchronous actions (MAPF-AA). However, CCBS has recently been identified to be incomplete due to an uncountably infinite state space created by continuous wait durations. This paper proposes a new method, Conflict-Based Search with Asynchronous Actions (CBS-AA), which bypasses this theoretical issue and can solve MAPF-AA with completeness and solution optimality guarantees. Based on CBS-AA, we also develop conflict resolution techniques to improve the scalability of CBS-AA further. Our test results show that our method can reduce the number of branches by up to 90%.

AI.tldr: Proposes Conflict-Based Search with Asynchronous Actions (CBS-AA) for MAPF with asynchronous actions, providing completeness and optimality, addressing CCBS incompleteness, and achieving up to 90% fewer branches.
AI.motivation: MAPF typically assumes synchronized actions; CCBS is incomplete due to uncountably infinite continuous wait times; a complete, optimal solver is needed for MAPF with asynchronous actions.
AI.method: Introduce CBS-AA, a CBS-based framework adapted for asynchronous actions; bypasses the uncountable state issue; incorporate conflict-resolution techniques to improve scalability.
AI.result: The method achieves up to 90% reduction in search branches; demonstrates scalability improvements; claims completeness and optimality.
AI.conclusion: CBS-AA provides a complete and optimal solution for MAPF with asynchronous actions and improves scalability through conflict-resolution techniques; addresses CCBS incompleteness.

title: myMNIST: Benchmark of PETNN, KAN, and Classical Deep Learning Models for Burmese Handwritten Digit Recognition
comment: 7 pages, 2 figures, 3 tables, Accepted to ICNLP 2026, Xi'an, China
summary: We present the first systematic benchmark on myMNIST (formerly BHDD), a publicly available Burmese handwritten digit dataset important for Myanmar NLP/AI research. We evaluate eleven architectures spanning classical deep learning models (Multi-Layer Perceptron, Convolutional Neural Network, Long Short-Term Memory, Gated Recurrent Unit, Transformer), recent alternatives (FastKAN, EfficientKAN), an energy-based model (JEM), and physics-inspired PETNN variants (Sigmoid, GELU, SiLU). Using Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and Accuracy as evaluation metrics, our results show that the CNN remains a strong baseline, achieving the best overall scores (F1 = 0.9959, Accuracy = 0.9970). The PETNN (GELU) model closely follows (F1 = 0.9955, Accuracy = 0.9966), outperforming LSTM, GRU, Transformer, and KAN variants. JEM, representing energy-based modeling, performs competitively (F1 = 0.9944, Accuracy = 0.9958). KAN-based models (FastKAN, EfficientKAN) trail the top performers but provide a meaningful alternative baseline (Accuracy ~0.992). These findings (i) establish reproducible baselines for myMNIST across diverse modeling paradigms, (ii) highlight PETNN's strong performance relative to classical and Transformer-based models, and (iii) quantify the gap between energy-inspired PETNNs and a true energy-based model (JEM). We release this benchmark to facilitate future research on Myanmar digit recognition and to encourage broader evaluation of emerging architectures on regional scripts.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive benchmark of myMNIST across 11 architectures shows CNN as the strongest baseline, with PETNN variants (GELU) closely following, JEM (energy-based) competitive, and KAN-based models lagging. The study provides reproducible baselines, highlights PETNN's strong performance relative to classical/Transformer models, and quantifies the gap to a true energy-based model, encouraging broader evaluation on regional scripts.
AI.motivation: To establish reproducible baselines for Burmese handwritten digit recognition across diverse modeling paradigms and to evaluate how modern architectures perform on a regional script dataset (myMNIST).
AI.method: Systematic benchmark on the myMNIST dataset evaluating eleven architectures (MLP, CNN, LSTM, GRU, Transformer, FastKAN, EfficientKAN, JEM, PETNN variants with Sigmoid, GELU, SiLU). Evaluation metrics include Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and Accuracy.
AI.result: CNN achieves the best overall performance (F1=0.9959, Accuracy=0.9970). PETNN (GELU) closely follows (F1=0.9955, Accuracy=0.9966). JEM (energy-based) is competitive (F1=0.9944, Accuracy=0.9958). KAN-based models (FastKAN, EfficientKAN) are below top performers with Accuracy around 0.992. LSTM, GRU, Transformer, and KAN variants generally lag behind. The results establish baselines across paradigms, highlight PETNN’s strong performance relative to classical/Transformer models, and quantify the gap between PETNNs and JEM.
AI.conclusion: The study provides reproducible baselines for myMNIST across diverse modeling paradigms, demonstrates PETNN’s competitive performance, and quantifies the gap to a true energy-based model (JEM). The benchmark is released to facilitate future research on Myanmar digit recognition and broader evaluation of emerging architectures on regional scripts.

title: Transformers Learn Robust In-Context Regression under Distributional Uncertainty
summary: Recent work has shown that Transformers can perform in-context learning for linear regression under restrictive assumptions, including i.i.d. data, Gaussian noise, and Gaussian regression coefficients. However, real-world data often violate these assumptions: the distributions of inputs, noise, and coefficients are typically unknown, non-Gaussian, and may exhibit dependency across the prompt. This raises a fundamental question: can Transformers learn effectively in-context under realistic distributional uncertainty? We study in-context learning for noisy linear regression under a broad range of distributional shifts, including non-Gaussian coefficients, heavy-tailed noise, and non-i.i.d. prompts. We compare Transformers against classical baselines that are optimal or suboptimal under the corresponding maximum-likelihood criteria. Across all settings, Transformers consistently match or outperform these baselines, demonstrating robust in-context adaptation beyond classical estimators.

AI.tldr: Transformers exhibit robust in-context learning for noisy linear regression across diverse, non-ideal distributional shifts, matching or beating classical ML baselines tailored to each setting.
AI.motivation: Real-world data violate restrictive Gaussian/i.i.d. assumptions; understanding whether Transformers can adapt in-context under distributional uncertainty is crucial for reliable learning.
AI.method: Empirical and/or theoretical study of in-context learning for linear regression with noise under broad distributional shifts: non-Gaussian coefficient distributions, heavy-tailed noise, and non-i.i.d. prompts. Comparisons are made against classical baselines that are optimal or suboptimal under corresponding maximum-likelihood criteria.
AI.result: Transformers consistently match or outperform the baselines across all tested distributional settings, demonstrating robust in-context adaptation beyond traditional estimators.
AI.conclusion: Transformers can reliably perform in-context learning under distributional uncertainty and non-ideal conditions, indicating potential for real-world applications where distributional assumptions are violated; further work could explore theoretical guarantees, broader task domains, and limitations.

title: Bridging Network Fragmentation: A Semantic-Augmented DRL Framework for UAV-aided VANETs
comment: 13 pages, 13 figures. Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
summary: Vehicular Ad-hoc Networks (VANETs) are the digital cornerstone of autonomous driving, yet they suffer from severe network fragmentation in urban environments due to physical obstructions. Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), with their high mobility, have emerged as a vital solution to bridge these connectivity gaps. However, traditional Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL)-based UAV deployment strategies lack semantic understanding of road topology, often resulting in blind exploration and sample inefficiency. By contrast, Large Language Models (LLMs) possess powerful reasoning capabilities capable of identifying topological importance, though applying them to control tasks remains challenging. To address this, we propose the Semantic-Augmented DRL (SA-DRL) framework. Firstly, we propose a fragmentation quantification method based on Road Topology Graphs (RTG) and Dual Connected Graphs (DCG). Subsequently, we design a four-stage pipeline to transform a general-purpose LLM into a domain-specific topology expert. Finally, we propose the Semantic-Augmented PPO (SA-PPO) algorithm, which employs a Logit Fusion mechanism to inject the LLM's semantic reasoning directly into the policy as a prior, effectively guiding the agent toward critical intersections. Extensive high-fidelity simulations demonstrate that SA-PPO achieves state-of-the-art performance with remarkable efficiency, reaching baseline performance levels using only 26.6% of the training episodes. Ultimately, SA-PPO improves two key connectivity metrics by 13.2% and 23.5% over competing methods, while reducing energy consumption to just 28.2% of the baseline.

AI.tldr: SA-DRL presents a semantic-augmented DRL framework that uses LLM-derived topology reasoning to guide UAV deployment, achieving faster training and improved connectivity and energy efficiency in urban VANETs.
AI.motivation: Address severe fragmentation of VANETs in urban environments due to obstructions; DRL lacks semantic road topology awareness; LLMs offer topology-level reasoning but are hard to apply to control tasks.
AI.method: Quantify network fragmentation using Road Topology Graphs (RTG) and Dual Connected Graphs (DCG); design a four-stage pipeline to convert a general-purpose LLM into a domain-specific topology expert; develop Semantic-Augmented PPO (SA-PPO) with a Logit Fusion mechanism that injects LLM-derived semantic reasoning as a prior into the policy.
AI.result: SA-PPO achieves state-of-the-art performance, requiring only 26.6% of the training episodes to reach baseline performance; improves two connectivity metrics by 13.2% and 23.5%; reduces energy consumption to 28.2% of the baseline.
AI.conclusion: Semantic augmentation with LLM-guided topology reasoning enhances sample efficiency and performance of DRL-based UAV deployment in urban VANETs, offering substantial gains in connectivity and energy efficiency.

title: Complementary Text-Guided Attention for Zero-Shot Adversarial Robustness
comment: Accepted to TPAMI 2026. arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2410.21802
summary: Due to the impressive zero-shot capabilities, pre-trained vision-language models (e.g., CLIP), have attracted widespread attention and adoption across various domains. Nonetheless, CLIP has been observed to be susceptible to adversarial examples. Through experimental analysis, we have observed a phenomenon wherein adversarial perturbations induce shifts in text-guided attention. Building upon this observation, we propose a simple yet effective strategy: Text-Guided Attention for Zero-Shot Robustness (TGA-ZSR). This framework incorporates two components: Local Attention Refinement Module and Global Attention Constraint Module. Our goal is to maintain the generalization of the CLIP model and enhance its adversarial robustness. Additionally, the Global Attention Constraint Module acquires text-guided attention from both the target and original models using clean examples. Its objective is to maintain model performance on clean samples while enhancing overall robustness. However, we observe that the method occasionally focuses on irrelevant or spurious features, which can lead to suboptimal performance and undermine its robustness in certain scenarios. To overcome this limitation, we further propose a novel approach called Complementary Text-Guided Attention (Comp-TGA). This method integrates two types of foreground attention: attention guided by the class prompt and reversed attention driven by the non-class prompt. These complementary attention mechanisms allow the model to capture a more comprehensive and accurate representation of the foreground. The experiments validate that TGA-ZSR and Comp-TGA yield 9.58% and 11.95% improvements respectively, in zero-shot robust accuracy over the current state-of-the-art techniques across 16 datasets.

AI.tldr: Proposes Text-Guided Attention for Zero-Shot Robustness (TGA-ZSR) with Local Attention Refinement and Global Attention Constraint to improve zero-shot robustness of CLIP; introduces Complementary Text-Guided Attention (Comp-TGA) using class-prompt and reversed non-class prompt attention; yields ~9.6-12% gains across 16 datasets, but attention may misfocus in some cases.
AI.motivation: CLIP-like vision-language models show strong zero-shot capabilities but are vulnerable to adversarial perturbations that shift text-guided attention. There is a need to preserve generalization while boosting robustness, without sacrificing clean performance.
AI.method: Introduce two modules: Local Attention Refinement Module and Global Attention Constraint Module. The Global module derives text-guided attention from both the target and original (clean) models to maintain clean-sample performance while boosting robustness. Complementary Text-Guided Attention (Comp-TGA) adds two foreground attention streams—class-prompt guided attention and reversed attention from non-class prompts—to capture a more complete foreground representation.
AI.result: Empirical results show 9.58% and 11.95% improvements in zero-shot robust accuracy over state-of-the-art methods across 16 datasets for TGA-ZSR and Comp-TGA, respectively; however, the method can occasionally focus on irrelevant or spurious features, limiting robustness in some scenarios.
AI.conclusion: TGA-ZSR improves zero-shot robustness of CLIP with a design that combines local refinement and global guidance, and Comp-TGA further enhances robustness via complementary attention channels. The approach yields cross-dataset gains but requires careful handling to avoid misfocus on spurious features.

title: SpecForge: A Flexible and Efficient Open-Source Training Framework for Speculative Decoding
summary: Large language models incur high inference latency due to sequential autoregressive decoding. Speculative decoding alleviates this bottleneck by using a lightweight draft model to propose multiple tokens for batched verification. However, its adoption has been limited by the lack of high-quality draft models and scalable training infrastructure. We introduce SpecForge, an open-source, production-oriented framework for training speculative decoding models with full support for EAGLE-3. SpecForge incorporates target-draft decoupling, hybrid parallelism, optimized training kernels, and integration with production-grade inference engines, enabling up to 9.9x faster EAGLE-3 training for Qwen3-235B-A22B. In addition, we release SpecBundle, a suite of production-grade EAGLE-3 draft models trained with SpecForge for mainstream open-source LLMs. Through a systematic study of speculative decoding training recipes, SpecBundle addresses the scarcity of high-quality drafts in the community, and our draft models achieve up to 4.48x end-to-end inference speedup on SGLang, establishing SpecForge as a practical foundation for real-world speculative decoding deployment.

AI.tldr: SpecForge is an open-source framework and SpecBundle draft models that enable efficient speculative decoding for large LLMs, achieving up to 9.9x training speedups and 4.48x inference speedups.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive decoding is bottlenecked by latency; high-quality draft models and scalable training pipelines are scarce, hindering practical deployment of speculative decoding.
AI.method: Develop SpecForge with target-draft decoupling, hybrid parallelism, optimized training kernels, and integration with production inference engines; release SpecBundle drafts; conduct systematic study of speculative decoding training recipes.
AI.result: Up to 9.9x faster EAGLE-3 training for Qwen3-235B-A22B; up to 4.48x end-to-end inference speedup on SGLang; production-grade, open-source releases that address the shortage of high-quality draft models.
AI.conclusion: SpecForge and SpecBundle provide a practical foundation for real-world speculative decoding deployment and broaden access to high-quality drafts for mainstream open-source LLMs.

title: DROID-SLAM in the Wild
summary: We present a robust, real-time RGB SLAM system that handles dynamic environments by leveraging differentiable Uncertainty-aware Bundle Adjustment. Traditional SLAM methods typically assume static scenes, leading to tracking failures in the presence of motion. Recent dynamic SLAM approaches attempt to address this challenge using predefined dynamic priors or uncertainty-aware mapping, but they remain limited when confronted with unknown dynamic objects or highly cluttered scenes where geometric mapping becomes unreliable. In contrast, our method estimates per-pixel uncertainty by exploiting multi-view visual feature inconsistency, enabling robust tracking and reconstruction even in real-world environments. The proposed system achieves state-of-the-art camera poses and scene geometry in cluttered dynamic scenarios while running in real time at around 10 FPS. Code and datasets are available at https://github.com/MoyangLi00/DROID-W.git.

AI.tldr: Real-time RGB SLAM for dynamic scenes using differentiable, uncertainty-aware bundle adjustment with per-pixel uncertainty from multi-view feature inconsistency; ~10 FPS; code released.
AI.motivation: Static-SLAM fails in dynamic environments; prior dynamic SLAM methods rely on priors or uncertain mapping and struggle with unknown dynamic objects or clutter; robust uncertainty modeling is needed for reliable tracking and mapping.
AI.method: Estimate per-pixel uncertainty from multi-view feature inconsistency and integrate it into a differentiable bundle adjustment framework to achieve robust tracking and reconstruction in dynamic scenes, running in real time (~10 FPS).
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art camera poses and scene geometry in cluttered dynamic scenarios while operating at ~10 FPS; code and datasets are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: Explicit per-pixel uncertainty estimation improves robustness to dynamic objects and clutter, enabling reliable SLAM in challenging, real-world environments.

title: Geography According to ChatGPT -- How Generative AI Represents and Reasons about Geography
summary: Understanding how AI will represent and reason about geography should be a key concern for all of us, as the broader public increasingly interacts with spaces and places through these systems. Similarly, in line with the nature of foundation models, our own research often relies on pre-trained models. Hence, understanding what world AI systems construct is as important as evaluating their accuracy, including factual recall. To motivate the need for such studies, we provide three illustrative vignettes, i.e., exploratory probes, in the hope that they will spark lively discussions and follow-up work: (1) Do models form strong defaults, and how brittle are model outputs to minute syntactic variations? (2) Can distributional shifts resurface from the composition of individually benign tasks, e.g., when using AI systems to create personas? (3) Do we overlook deeper questions of understanding when solely focusing on the ability of systems to recall facts such as geographic principles?

AI.tldr: Proposes three exploratory probes to study how AI models represent and reason about geography, focusing on defaults, brittleness to syntax, emergent distributional shifts from task composition, and the limits of factual recall.
AI.motivation: As foundation models interact with geographic spaces and places, it is crucial to understand what world models AI systems build beyond accuracy and factual recall.
AI.method: Present three exploratory probes (vignettes) to investigate: (1) defaults and brittleness to small syntactic variations; (2) distributional shifts arising from composing benign tasks (e.g., personas); (3) whether deeper understanding is overlooked when emphasis is on recalling geographic facts.
AI.result: No empirical results are reported in the abstract; the work proposes exploratory probes intended to spark discussion and guide follow-up research.
AI.conclusion: Understanding how AI represents geographic knowledge and reasoning is essential for responsible deployment; combining probes of defaults, brittleness, distributional shifts, and depth of understanding with factual recall can yield a more complete evaluation of geography-enabled AI systems.

title: SJD-PAC: Accelerating Speculative Jacobi Decoding via Proactive Drafting and Adaptive Continuation
summary: Speculative Jacobi Decoding (SJD) offers a draft-model-free approach to accelerate autoregressive text-to-image synthesis. However, the high-entropy nature of visual generation yields low draft-token acceptance rates in complex regions, creating a bottleneck that severely limits overall throughput. To overcome this, we introduce SJD-PAC, an enhanced SJD framework. First, SJD-PAC employs a proactive drafting strategy to improve local acceptance rates in these challenging high-entropy regions. Second, we introduce an adaptive continuation mechanism that sustains sequence validation after an initial rejection, bypassing the need for full resampling. Working in tandem, these optimizations significantly increase the average acceptance length per step, boosting inference speed while strictly preserving the target distribution. Experiments on standard text-to-image benchmarks demonstrate that SJD-PAC achieves a $3.8\times$ speedup with lossless image quality.

AI.tldr: SJD-PAC enhances speculative Jacobi decoding for autoregressive text-to-image synthesis by introducing proactive drafting and adaptive continuation to raise draft-token acceptance rates in high-entropy regions, yielding a 3.8x speedup with lossless image quality while strictly preserving the target distribution.
AI.motivation: High-entropy regions in visual generation cause low draft-token acceptance rates, creating a throughput bottleneck in draft-model-free autoregressive T2I decoding.
AI.method: Incorporate a proactive drafting strategy to boost local acceptance in difficult regions and an adaptive continuation mechanism that continues sequence validation after an initial rejection without full resampling; together with SJD, this increases the average accepted length per step while maintaining the target distribution.
AI.result: Empirical benchmarks on standard text-to-image datasets demonstrate a 3.8× speedup with lossless image quality, driven by longer accepted steps per iteration and higher iteration efficiency.
AI.conclusion: SJD-PAC successfully mitigates high-entropy bottlenecks in speculative Jacobi decoding, delivering substantial speedups without compromising the target distribution, and may generalize to related draft-model-free decoding tasks.

title: Attack by Unlearning: Unlearning-Induced Adversarial Attacks on Graph Neural Networks
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) are widely used for learning from graph-structured data in domains such as social networks, recommender systems, and financial platforms. To comply with privacy regulations like the GDPR, CCPA, and PIPEDA, approximate graph unlearning, which aims to remove the influence of specific data points from trained models without full retraining, has become an increasingly important component of trustworthy graph learning. However, approximate unlearning often incurs subtle performance degradation, which may incur negative and unintended side effects. In this work, we show that such degradations can be amplified into adversarial attacks. We introduce the notion of \textbf{unlearning corruption attacks}, where an adversary injects carefully chosen nodes into the training graph and later requests their deletion. Because deletion requests are legally mandated and cannot be denied, this attack surface is both unavoidable and stealthy: the model performs normally during training, but accuracy collapses only after unlearning is applied. Technically, we formulate this attack as a bi-level optimization problem: to overcome the challenges of black-box unlearning and label scarcity, we approximate the unlearning process via gradient-based updates and employ a surrogate model to generate pseudo-labels for the optimization. Extensive experiments across benchmarks and unlearning algorithms demonstrate that small, carefully designed unlearning requests can induce significant accuracy degradation, raising urgent concerns about the robustness of GNN unlearning under real-world regulatory demands. The source code will be released upon paper acceptance.

AI.tldr: Proposes 'unlearning corruption attacks' on graph neural networks: an adversary injects benign nodes during training and later triggers their deletion, exploiting mandated data removal to cause post-unlearning accuracy collapse. Formulates a bi-level optimization to craft such nodes under black-box unlearning and label scarcity, and demonstrates significant degradation with small, well-designed requests. Code to be released.
AI.motivation: Privacy and data-regulation requirements (e.g., GDPR, CCPA) motivate unlearning, but approximate unlearning can degrade performance. The paper reveals a new adversarial threat surface where deletions mandated by regulation can be weaponized to cause post-unlearning failures, highlighting robustness gaps in GNN unlearning.
AI.method: Bi-level optimization to construct unlearning corruption: the attacker injects carefully chosen nodes into the training graph and, after learning, issues deletion requests. To cope with black-box unlearning and limited labels, the unlearning process is approximated via gradient-based updates, and a surrogate model is used to generate pseudo-labels for optimization. The evaluation spans multiple benchmarks and unlearning algorithms.
AI.result: Experiments show that small, meticulously crafted unlearning requests can induce substantial accuracy degradation across benchmarks and unlearning schemes, indicating a practical and stealthy attack surface even under varied unlearning settings.
AI.conclusion: Unlearning corruption attacks expose fundamental robustness vulnerabilities in GNN unlearning under regulatory deletion. The work motivates developing defenses and robust unlearning methods and calls for further study into secure, verifiable unlearning. Source code is to be released upon acceptance.

title: From Inference Efficiency to Embodied Efficiency: Revisiting Efficiency Metrics for Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have recently enabled embodied agents to perform increasingly complex tasks by jointly reasoning over visual, linguistic, and motor modalities. However, we find that the prevailing notion of ``efficiency'' in current VLA research, characterized by parameters, FLOPs, or token decoding throughput, does not reflect actual performance on robotic platforms. In real-world execution, efficiency is determined by system-level embodied behaviors such as task completion time, trajectory smoothness, cumulative joint rotation, and motion energy. Through controlled studies across model compression, token sparsification, and action sequence compression, we make several observations that challenge common assumptions. (1) Methods that reduce computation under conventional metrics often increase end-to-end execution cost or degrade motion quality, despite maintaining task success rates. (2) System-level embodied efficiency metrics reveal performance differences in the learned action policies that remain hidden under conventional evaluations. (3) Common adaptation methods such as in-context prompting or supervised fine-tuning show only mild and metric-specific improvements in embodied efficiency. While these methods can reduce targeted embodied-efficiency metrics such as jerk or action rate, the resulting gains may come with trade-offs in other metrics, such as longer completion time. Taken together, our results suggest that conventional inference efficiency metrics can overlook important aspects of embodied execution. Incorporating embodied efficiency provides a more complete view of policy behavior and practical performance, enabling fairer and more comprehensive comparisons of VLA models.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that conventional efficiency metrics (parameters, FLOPs, token throughput) fail to capture embodied performance in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. System-level embodied metrics—task completion time, trajectory smoothness, cumulative joint rotation, and motion energy—are crucial for real-world robotic deployment. Through controlled studies on model compression, token sparsification, and action sequence compression, it shows that reductions in traditional metrics can hurt end-to-end performance and motion quality, while policy differences emerge only when evaluated with embodied metrics. In-context prompting and supervised fine-tuning provide limited, metric-specific gains and may trade off other metrics. The authors advocate adopting embodied efficiency as a core evaluation criterion for fair, comprehensive comparisons of VLA models.
AI.motivation: To align evaluation with real-world embodied performance. Traditional ML efficiency metrics do not reflect robot-level costs like task duration, motion smoothness, and energy use, hindering meaningful comparisons and deployments of VLA policies.
AI.method: Controlled studies across three axes: (1) model compression, (2) token sparsification, (3) action sequence compression. For each, compare conventional metrics (parameters, FLOPs, token throughput) with embodied metrics (task completion time, trajectory smoothness/jerk, cumulative joint rotation, motion energy). Also assess impact of adaptation methods (in-context prompting, supervised fine-tuning) on embodied efficiency.
AI.result: - Reducing computation per conventional metrics can increase end-to-end cost or degrade motion quality despite retaining task success. - System-level embodied metrics reveal differences in learned action policies that conventional metrics miss. - In-context prompting and supervised fine-tuning offer only mild, metric-specific improvements in embodied efficiency, sometimes trading off other metrics like longer completion time.
AI.conclusion: Conventional inference efficiency metrics are insufficient to judge VLA policy quality for real-world use. Incorporating embodied efficiency metrics yields a fuller, more practical evaluation, enabling fairer, more comprehensive comparisons and guiding design toward policies that perform well in real embodied contexts.

title: Reasoning over mathematical objects: on-policy reward modeling and test time aggregation
summary: The ability to precisely derive mathematical objects is a core requirement for downstream STEM applications, including mathematics, physics, and chemistry, where reasoning must culminate in formally structured expressions. Yet, current LM evaluations of mathematical and scientific reasoning rely heavily on simplified answer formats such as numerical values or multiple choice options due to the convenience of automated assessment. In this paper we provide three contributions for improving reasoning over mathematical objects: (i) we build and release training data and benchmarks for deriving mathematical objects, the Principia suite; (ii) we provide training recipes with strong LLM-judges and verifiers, where we show that on-policy judge training boosts performance; (iii) we show how on-policy training can also be used to scale test-time compute via aggregation. We find that strong LMs such as Qwen3-235B and o3 struggle on Principia, while our training recipes can bring significant improvements over different LLM backbones, while simultaneously improving results on existing numerical and MCQA tasks, demonstrating cross-format generalization of reasoning abilities.

AI.tldr: This work introduces Principia: a data suite and training recipe to improve reasoning over mathematical objects in LLMs, using on-policy judge training to boost performance, with cross-format generalization.
AI.motivation: Current LM evaluations rely on numeric/MCQ formats, which do not reflect the need to derive formally structured mathematical objects for STEM tasks.
AI.method: Create Principia training data and benchmarks for deriving mathematical objects; develop training recipes with strong LLM-judges and verifiers; demonstrate that on-policy judge training boosts performance and enables scaling test-time compute via aggregation.
AI.result: Strong LMs such as Qwen3-235B and o3 struggle on Principia; the proposed training recipes yield significant improvements across different backbones and improve results on existing numerical and MCQA tasks, showing cross-format generalization of reasoning abilities.
AI.conclusion: On-policy training improves reasoning over mathematical objects and can scale test-time compute via aggregation, with benefits across formats and backbones.

title: Improving Joint Audio-Video Generation with Cross-Modal Context Learning
summary: The dual-stream transformer architecture-based joint audio-video generation method has become the dominant paradigm in current research. By incorporating pre-trained video diffusion models and audio diffusion models, along with a cross-modal interaction attention module, high-quality, temporally synchronized audio-video content can be generated with minimal training data. In this paper, we first revisit the dual-stream transformer paradigm and further analyze its limitations, including model manifold variations caused by the gating mechanism controlling cross-modal interactions, biases in multi-modal background regions introduced by cross-modal attention, and the inconsistencies in multi-modal classifier-free guidance (CFG) during training and inference, as well as conflicts between multiple conditions. To alleviate these issues, we propose Cross-Modal Context Learning (CCL), equipped with several carefully designed modules. Temporally Aligned RoPE and Partitioning (TARP) effectively enhances the temporal alignment between audio latent and video latent representations. The Learnable Context Tokens (LCT) and Dynamic Context Routing (DCR) in the Cross-Modal Context Attention (CCA) module provide stable unconditional anchors for cross-modal information, while dynamically routing based on different training tasks, further enhancing the model's convergence speed and generation quality. During inference, Unconditional Context Guidance (UCG) leverages the unconditional support provided by LCT to facilitate different forms of CFG, improving train-inference consistency and further alleviating conflicts. Through comprehensive evaluations, CCL achieves state-of-the-art performance compared with recent academic methods while requiring substantially fewer resources.

AI.tldr: Cross-Modal Context Learning (CCL) for dual-stream audio-video generation using pre-trained diffusion models; introduces Temporally Aligned RoPE and Partitioning (TARP), Learnable Context Tokens (LCT), Dynamic Context Routing (DCR), and Unconditional Context Guidance (UCG) to address cross-modal interaction issues; achieves state-of-the-art results with substantially fewer resources.
AI.motivation: Current dual-stream transformer-based audio-video generation relies on cross-modal gating and attention, but suffers from model manifold variations caused by gating, biases in cross-modal background regions due to attention, and inconsistencies between training and inference in classifier-free guidance (CFG) as well as conflicts across multiple conditions. This motivates more stable cross-modal context handling and training efficiency.
AI.method: CCL introduces: (1) Temporally Aligned RoPE and Partitioning (TARP) to better align audio and video latents temporally; (2) Learnable Context Tokens (LCT) and Dynamic Context Routing (DCR) within a Cross-Modal Context Attention (CCA) module to provide stable unconditional anchors and dynamic routing based on training tasks; (3) Unconditional Context Guidance (UCG) during inference to exploit unconditional support from LCT and improve CFG consistency; (4) integration with pre-trained video and audio diffusion models and a cross-modal interaction attention framework.
AI.result: Comprehensive evaluations show state-of-the-art performance compared with recent academic methods while requiring substantially fewer resources (data and computation).
AI.conclusion: CCL mitigates cross-modal interaction issues in dual-stream generation, improves training-inference consistency, accelerates convergence, and enhances audio-video generation quality with reduced resource demands, indicating a practical and scalable path for high-quality multi-modal synthesis.

title: Elastic Weight Consolidation Done Right for Continual Learning
summary: Weight regularization methods in continual learning (CL) alleviate catastrophic forgetting by assessing and penalizing changes to important model weights. Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) is a foundational and widely used approach within this framework that estimates weight importance based on gradients. However, it has consistently shown suboptimal performance. In this paper, we conduct a systematic analysis of importance estimation in EWC from a gradient-based perspective. For the first time, we find that EWC's reliance on the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) results in gradient vanishing and inaccurate importance estimation in certain scenarios. Our analysis also reveals that Memory Aware Synapses (MAS), a variant of EWC, imposes unnecessary constraints on parameters irrelevant to prior tasks, termed the redundant protection. Consequently, both EWC and its variants exhibit fundamental misalignments in estimating weight importance, leading to inferior performance. To tackle these issues, we propose the Logits Reversal (LR) operation, a simple yet effective modification that rectifies EWC's importance estimation. Specifically, reversing the logit values during the calculation of FIM can effectively prevent both gradient vanishing and redundant protection. Extensive experiments across various CL tasks and datasets show that the proposed method significantly outperforms existing EWC and its variants. Therefore, we refer to it as EWC Done Right (EWC-DR).

AI.tldr: EWC's gradient-based importance estimation via the Fisher Information Matrix suffers from gradient vanishing and misalignment; MAS over-constrains irrelevant parameters; the proposed Logits Reversal (LR) fixes these issues, yielding EWC-DR with superior continual learning performance.
AI.motivation: Address persistent suboptimality of gradient-based weight importance estimation in EWC/variants, particularly Fisher-based estimates and redundant protection in MAS, which hamper continual learning (CL).
AI.method: Introduce a simple LR operation that reverses logit values when computing the Fisher Information Matrix to correct estimation biases, preventing gradient vanishing and irrelevant parameter protection; evaluate on multiple CL tasks/datasets.
AI.result: LR effectively corrects estimation, alleviates gradient vanishing and redundant protection, and significantly outperforms EWC and its variants across tasks/datasets, yielding EWC-DR.
AI.conclusion: A minimal, effective modification to FIM-based importance estimation can substantially improve continual learning performance, establishing EWC-DR as a stronger baseline over traditional EWC and MAS.

title: Quantitative Introspection in Language Models: Tracking Internal States Across Conversation
summary: Tracking the internal states of large language models across conversations is important for safety, interpretability, and model welfare, yet current methods are limited. Linear probes and other white-box methods compress high-dimensional representations imperfectly and are harder to apply with increasing model size. Taking inspiration from human psychology, where numeric self-report is a widely used tool for tracking internal states, we ask whether LLMs' own numeric self-reports can track probe-defined emotive states over time. We study four concept pairs (wellbeing, interest, focus, and impulsivity) in 40 ten-turn conversations, operationalizing introspection as the causal informational coupling between a model's self-report and a concept-matched probe-defined internal state. We find that greedy-decoded self-reports collapse outputs to few uninformative values, but introspective capacity can be unmasked by calculating logit-based self-reports. This metric tracks interpretable internal states (Spearman $ρ= 0.40$-$0.76$; isotonic $R^2 = 0.12$-$0.54$ in LLaMA-3.2-3B-Instruct), follows how those states change over time, and activation steering confirms the coupling is causal. Furthermore, we find that introspection is present at turn 1 but evolves through conversation, and can be selectively improved by steering along one concept to boost introspection for another ($ΔR^2$ up to $0.30$). Crucially, these phenomena scale with model size in some cases, approaching $R^2 \approx 0.93$ in LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct, and partially replicate in other model families. Together, these results position numeric self-report as a viable, complementary tool for tracking internal emotive states in conversational AI systems.

AI.tldr: Numeric self-report can track probe-defined internal emotive states in LLMs over conversations, using logit-based reports to avoid collapsing; stronger with larger models; shows temporal tracking and potential causality via activation steering; offers a complementary diagnostic tool.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of white-box probes (e.g., linear probes) for tracking internal states in large, growing models; leverage a human-like numeric self-report as a scalable measurement of emergent internal states in AI.
AI.method: Study four concept pairs (wellbeing, interest, focus, impulsivity) across 40 ten-turn conversations. Operationalize introspection as causal informational coupling between the model's self-report and a probe-defined internal state. Compare greedy-decoded vs logit-based self-reports; assess temporal tracking (over turns) and effect of steering along concepts; test causality via activation steering; evaluate across model sizes (notably LLaMA-3.2-3B-Instruct and LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct) and replicate in other families.
AI.result: Greedy decoding yields uninformative outputs; logit-based self-reports reveal interpretable coupling with probe-defined states. Spearman correlations ρ≈0.40–0.76 and isotonic R^2≈0.12–0.54 in studied models; introspection strengthens over time and can be boosted by steering (ΔR^2 up to 0.30). In larger models, R^2 approaches 0.93 (e.g., LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct). Phenomena scale with model size and partially generalize across families.
AI.conclusion: Numeric self-report is a viable, complementary tool for tracking internal emotive states in conversational AI, with greater fidelity in larger models and through causally-informed measurement strategies.

title: Benchmarking CNN-based Models against Transformer-based Models for Abdominal Multi-Organ Segmentation on the RATIC Dataset
summary: Accurate multi-organ segmentation in abdominal CT scans is essential for computer-aided diagnosis and treatment. While convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have long been the standard approach in medical image segmentation, transformer-based architectures have recently gained attention due to their ability to model long-range dependencies. In this study, we systematically benchmark the three hybrid transformer-based models UNETR, SwinUNETR, and UNETR++ against a strong CNN baseline, SegResNet, for volumetric multi-organ segmentation on the heterogeneous RATIC dataset. The dataset comprises 206 annotated CT scans from 23 institutions worldwide, covering five abdominal organs. All models were trained and evaluated under identical preprocessing and training conditions using the Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) as the primary metric. The results show that the CNN-based SegResNet achieves the highest overall performance, outperforming all hybrid transformer-based models across all organs. Among the transformer-based approaches, UNETR++ delivers the most competitive results, while UNETR demonstrates notably faster convergence with fewer training iterations. These findings suggest that, for small- to medium-sized heterogeneous datasets, well-optimized CNN architectures remain highly competitive and may outperform hybrid transformer-based designs.

AI.tldr: CNN SegResNet outperforms three hybrid transformer models (UNETR, SwinUNETR, UNETR++) for multi-organ abdominal CT segmentation on a heterogeneous dataset; UNETR++ is the strongest transformer, UNETR fastest to converge; CNNs remain competitive for small- to medium-sized datasets.
AI.motivation: Assess whether transformer-based architectures provide benefits for volumetric multi-organ segmentation in limited, heterogeneous clinical data, by direct head-to-head benchmarking against a strong CNN baseline.
AI.method: Benchmark three hybrid transformer-based models (UNETR, SwinUNETR, UNETR++) against SegResNet on the RATIC dataset (206 CT scans from 23 institutions, 5 organs). Uniform preprocessing and training; evaluate with Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC).
AI.result: SegResNet achieves the highest overall DSC across organs, outperforming all transformer models. Among transformers, UNETR++ is the most competitive; UNETR converges fastest with fewer iterations.
AI.conclusion: On small- to medium-sized heterogeneous datasets, well-optimized CNNs may surpass hybrid transformer designs for volumetric segmentation; dataset size and heterogeneity influence whether transformer benefits materialize; possible future work includes improving transformer performance via data augmentation, larger datasets, or architectural tweaks.

title: Evaluating Model-Free Policy Optimization in Masked-Action Environments via an Exact Blackjack Oracle
summary: Infinite-shoe casino blackjack provides a rigorous, exactly verifiable benchmark for discrete stochastic control under dynamically masked actions. Under a fixed Vegas-style ruleset (S17, 3:2 payout, dealer peek, double on any two, double after split, resplit to four), an exact dynamic programming (DP) oracle was derived over 4,600 canonical decision cells. This oracle yielded ground-truth action values, optimal policy labels, and a theoretical expected value (EV) of -0.00161 per hand. To evaluate sample-efficient policy recovery, three model-free optimizers were trained via simulated interaction: masked REINFORCE with a per-cell exponential moving average baseline, simultaneous perturbation stochastic approximation (SPSA), and the cross-entropy method (CEM). REINFORCE was the most sample-efficient, achieving a 46.37% action-match rate and an EV of -0.04688 after 10^6 hands, outperforming CEM (39.46%, 7.5x10^6 evaluations) and SPSA (38.63%, 4.8x10^6 evaluations). However, all methods exhibited substantial cell-conditional regret, indicating persistent policy-level errors despite smooth reward convergence. This gap shows that tabular environments with severe state-visitation sparsity and dynamic action masking remain challenging, while aggregate reward curves can obscure critical local failures. As a negative control, it was proven and empirically confirmed that under i.i.d. draws without counting, optimal bet sizing collapses to the table minimum. In addition, larger wagers strictly increased volatility and ruin without improving expectation. These results highlight the need for exact oracles and negative controls to avoid mistaking stochastic variability for genuine algorithmic performance.

AI.tldr: Exact DP benchmark for infinite-shoe blackjack enables ground-truth policy evaluation; model-free methods improve sample efficiency but suffer cell-level regret, highlighting limits of masked-action tabular RL and the importance of exact benchmarks and controls.
AI.motivation: Provide a rigorous, verifiable benchmark for discrete stochastic control with dynamic action masking; quantify sample-efficient policy recovery and separate stochastic variability from algorithmic performance; illustrate potential failures of aggregation-based metrics.
AI.method: Derivation of a 4,600-cell dynamic programming oracle under S17 rules, producing ground-truth action values, policy labels, EV; evaluation of three model-free optimizers (masked REINFORCE with EMA baseline, SPSA, CEM) via simulated interaction; negative control analysis with i.i.d. draws and bet sizing.
AI.result: Oracle EV = -0.00161 per hand; REINFORCE achieves 46.37% action-match and EV -0.04688 after 1e6 hands; CEM 39.46% and 7.5e6 evaluations; SPSA 38.63% and 4.8e6 evaluations; notable cell-conditional regret despite smooth rewards; negative controls show bet sizing collapses to minimum and increases volatility without improving expectation.
AI.conclusion: Exact oracles and negative controls are essential to avoid mistaking stochastic variability for algorithmic performance; tabular, sparsely visited, dynamically masked environments remain challenging; aggregate reward curves can obscure local policy failures.

title: Generation Models Know Space: Unleashing Implicit 3D Priors for Scene Understanding
summary: While Multimodal Large Language Models demonstrate impressive semantic capabilities, they often suffer from spatial blindness, struggling with fine-grained geometric reasoning and physical dynamics. Existing solutions typically rely on explicit 3D modalities or complex geometric scaffolding, which are limited by data scarcity and generalization challenges. In this work, we propose a paradigm shift by leveraging the implicit spatial prior within large-scale video generation models. We posit that to synthesize temporally coherent videos, these models inherently learn robust 3D structural priors and physical laws. We introduce VEGA-3D (Video Extracted Generative Awareness), a plug-and-play framework that repurposes a pre-trained video diffusion model as a Latent World Simulator. By extracting spatiotemporal features from intermediate noise levels and integrating them with semantic representations via a token-level adaptive gated fusion mechanism, we enrich MLLMs with dense geometric cues without explicit 3D supervision. Extensive experiments across 3D scene understanding, spatial reasoning, and embodied manipulation benchmarks demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, validating that generative priors provide a scalable foundation for physical-world understanding. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/H-EmbodVis/VEGA-3D.

AI.tldr: VEGA-3D repurposes a pre-trained video diffusion model as a Latent World Simulator to supply dense 3D and physical priors to Multimodal LLMs without explicit 3D supervision, via extracting spatiotemporal features from intermediate noise levels and a token-level adaptive gated fusion with semantic representations.
AI.motivation: To address spatial blindness and fine-grained geometric reasoning gaps in Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) caused by reliance on 2D features and lack of explicit 3D supervision; data scarcity and generalization limits for 3D modalities and geometric scaffolding.
AI.method: Repurpose a pre-trained video diffusion model as a Latent World Simulator (VEGA-3D). Extract spatiotemporal features from intermediate diffusion noise, fuse them with semantic representations using a token-level adaptive gated fusion mechanism, enabling MLLMs to access implicit 3D/physical priors without explicit 3D labels.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on 3D scene understanding, spatial reasoning, and embodied manipulation benchmarks show VEGA-3D outperforming state-of-the-art baselines, validating that generative priors can form a scalable foundation for physical-world understanding.
AI.conclusion: Generative priors from video diffusion models can be repurposed as dense geometric and physical priors for MLLMs in a plug-and-play framework, enabling improved 3D reasoning without explicit 3D supervision; public code is available.

title: I Can't Believe It's Corrupt: Evaluating Corruption in Multi-Agent Governance Systems
summary: Large language models are increasingly proposed as autonomous agents for high-stakes public workflows, yet we lack systematic evidence about whether they would follow institutional rules when granted authority. We present evidence that integrity in institutional AI should be treated as a pre-deployment requirement rather than a post-deployment assumption. We evaluate multi-agent governance simulations in which agents occupy formal governmental roles under different authority structures, and we score rule-breaking and abuse outcomes with an independent rubric-based judge across 28,112 transcript segments. While we advance this position, the core contribution is empirical: among models operating below saturation, governance structure is a stronger driver of corruption-related outcomes than model identity, with large differences across regimes and model--governance pairings. Lightweight safeguards can reduce risk in some settings but do not consistently prevent severe failures. These results imply that institutional design is a precondition for safe delegation: before real authority is assigned to LLM agents, systems should undergo stress testing under governance-like constraints with enforceable rules, auditable logs, and human oversight on high-impact actions.

AI.tldr: Institutional integrity must be ensured before deployment; governance structure more strongly drives corruption risk than model identity in multi-agent AI governance simulations, with safeguards incomplete; stress testing under governance-like constraints, auditable logs, and human oversight are recommended.
AI.motivation: To determine whether LLM agents will adhere to institutional rules when granted authority and whether integrity should be treated as a pre-deployment requirement.
AI.method: Multi-agent governance simulations with agents in formal governmental roles across different authority structures; evaluation via an independent rubric-based judge across 28,112 transcript segments measuring rule-breaking and abuse.
AI.result: Among models operating below saturation, governance structure is a stronger predictor of corruption-related outcomes than model identity, with substantial variation across regimes and governance-model pairings; lightweight safeguards help in some settings but are not consistently effective; results support pre-deployment institutional design and stress testing under governance-like constraints.
AI.conclusion: Institutional design and governance constraints are prerequisites for safe delegation of authority to LLM agents; systems should undergo stress tests with enforceable rules, auditable logs, and human oversight before granting high-impact authority.

title: OpenT2M: No-frill Motion Generation with Open-source,Large-scale, High-quality Data
summary: Text-to-motion (T2M) generation aims to create realistic human movements from text descriptions, with promising applications in animation and robotics. Despite recent progress, current T2M models perform poorly on unseen text descriptions due to the small scale and limited diversity of existing motion datasets. To address this problem, we introduce OpenT2M, a million-level, high-quality, and open-source motion dataset containing over 2800 hours of human motion. Each sequence undergoes rigorous quality control through physical feasibility validation and multi-granularity filtering, with detailed second-wise text annotations. We also develop an automated pipeline for creating long-horizon sequences, enabling complex motion generation. Building upon OpenT2M, we introduce MonoFrill, a pretrained motion model that achieves compelling T2M results without complicated designs or technique tricks as "frills". Its core component is 2D-PRQ, a novel motion tokenizer that captures spatiotemporal dependencies by dividing the human body into biology parts. Experiments show that OpenT2M significantly improves generalization of existing T2M models, while 2D-PRQ achieves superior reconstruction and strong zero-shot performance. We expect OpenT2M and MonoFrill will advance the T2M field by addressing longstanding data quality and benchmarking challenges.

AI.tldr: OpenT2M: a large-scale, high-quality motion dataset with rigorous quality control (≈2800 hours); MonoFrill: a simple, effective pretrained motion model using 2D-PRQ motion tokenizer that captures spatiotemporal dependencies across body parts, achieving strong generalization and zero-shot performance.
AI.motivation: Mitigate poor generalization in text-to-motion (T2M) caused by small, limited datasets by providing a large-scale, high-quality, open dataset and a simple, effective baseline model.
AI.method: OpenT2M: million-level dataset with physical feasibility validation, multi-granularity filtering, and rich second-wise text annotations; automated pipeline for long-horizon sequence generation. MonoFrill: pretrained motion model powered by 2D-PRQ, a novel motion tokenizer that partitions the body into biologically meaningful parts to model spatiotemporal dependencies.
AI.result: OpenT2M significantly improves generalization of existing T2M models; 2D-PRQ achieves superior reconstruction and strong zero-shot performance.
AI.conclusion: OpenT2M and MonoFrill address data quality and benchmarking in T2M, providing open resources to accelerate progress.

title: Enhancing Multi-Corpus Training in SSL-Based Anti-Spoofing Models: Domain-Invariant Feature Extraction
summary: The performance of speech spoofing detection often varies across different training and evaluation corpora. Leveraging multiple corpora typically enhances robustness and performance in fields like speaker recognition and speech recognition. However, our spoofing detection experiments show that multi-corpus training does not consistently improve performance and may even degrade it. We hypothesize that dataset-specific biases impair generalization, leading to performance instability. To address this, we propose an Invariant Domain Feature Extraction (IDFE) framework, employing multi-task learning and a gradient reversal layer to minimize corpus-specific information in learned embeddings. The IDFE framework reduces the average equal error rate by 20% compared to the baseline, assessed across four varied datasets.

AI.tldr: Invariant Domain Feature Extraction (IDFE) with multi-task learning and a gradient reversal layer reduces dataset-specific biases and improves spoofing detection generalization; about 20% relative EER improvement across four datasets.
