It learns an offline dataset-conditioned ranking surrogate from historical runs to guide online Bayesian optimization, uses a GP residual to model dataset-specific deviations, and employs early-stopped trials with a low-cost proxy predictor. It achieves better-than-offline baselines and competitive online baselines at under 10% of their cost on biomedical tasks.
AI.motivation: Configuring LLM post-training pipelines is difficult due to a high-dimensional, heterogeneous search space, strong stage coupling, and expensive end-to-end evaluations under realistic compute budgets.
AI.method: Two-stage pipeline: offline stage learns a dataset-conditioned learning-to-rank surrogate from historical runs to guide configuration space exploration; online stage uses Bayesian optimization guided by the offline surrogate and models dataset-specific deviations with a Gaussian-process residual. Trials are early-stopped and scored by a predictor mapping early signals to a low-cost proxy of final performance.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on biomedical reasoning tasks shows AutoPipe consistently beats offline-only baselines and achieves performance comparable to strong online HPO baselines while using less than 10% of their computational cost.
AI.conclusion: AutoPipe effectively combines offline transfer of configuration knowledge with online optimization and cost-reduction techniques to enable budget-aware configuration of LLM post-training pipelines, performing well on biomedical tasks.

title: LuMamba: Latent Unified Mamba for Electrode Topology-Invariant and Efficient EEG Modeling
summary: Electroencephalography (EEG) enables non-invasive monitoring of brain activity across clinical and neurotechnology applications, yet building foundation models for EEG remains challenging due to \emph{differing electrode topologies} and \emph{computational scalability}, as Transformer architectures incur quadratic sequence complexity. As a joint solution, we propose \textbf{LuMamba} (\textbf{L}atent \textbf{U}nified \textbf{Mamba}), a self-supervised framework combining topology-invariant encodings with linear-complexity state-space modeling, using LUNA's learned-query cross-attention mechanism for channel unification~\cite{luna}, and FEMBA's bidirectional Mamba blocks for efficient temporal modeling~\cite{femba}. Within this architecture, we provide the first systematic investigation of the Latent-Euclidean Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (LeJEPA) for biosignal learning. Pre-trained on over 21,000 hours of unlabeled EEG from the TUEG corpus, LuMamba is evaluated on five downstream tasks spanning abnormality detection, artifact recognition, and mental condition classification across electrode configurations ranging from 16 to 26 channels. In the pre-training objective, masked reconstruction alone yields structured but less generalizable representations, while LeJEPA alone produces diffuse embeddings; combining both objectives achieves the most robust performance. With only 4.6M parameters, LuMamba attains 80.99\% balanced accuracy on TUAB and achieves state-of-art performance on Alzheimer's detection (0.97 AUPR), while requiring \textbf{377$\times$ fewer FLOPS} than state-of-art models at equivalent sequence lengths and scaling to \textbf{12$\times$ longer sequences} before reaching typical GPU memory limits. Code is available at https://github.com/pulp-bio/biofoundation

AI.tldr: LuMamba is a lightweight, self-supervised EEG foundation model that fuses topology-invariant encodings with linear-time state-space temporal modeling, enabling strong performance across channel layouts with substantially reduced compute.
AI.motivation: EEG foundation models struggle with varying electrode topologies and quadratic Transformer complexity, limiting cross-layout generalization and scalability.
AI.method: Combines LUNA's learned-query cross-attention for channel unification with FEMBA's bidirectional Mamba blocks for efficient temporal modeling. Systematically investigates Latent-Euclidean Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (LeJEPA) for biosignals. Pre-trained on ~21k hours of unlabeled EEG from TUEG. Uses two pretraining objectives—masked reconstruction and LeJEPA—and shows that their fusion yields robust representations.
AI.result: With 4.6M parameters, LuMamba achieves 80.99% balanced accuracy on TUAB, 0.97 AUPR for Alzheimer's detection, and demonstrates ~377x lower FLOPS than state-of-the-art models at equivalent sequence lengths, while scaling to 12x longer sequences before memory limits. Evaluated on five downstream tasks across 16–26 channels.
AI.conclusion: LuMamba delivers strong EEG foundation-model performance with substantially reduced compute and improved sequence scalability, aided by combining complementary pretraining objectives; supports robust cross-channel EEG analysis and downstream biosignal tasks.

title: Towards High-Quality Image Segmentation: Improving Topology Accuracy by Penalizing Neighbor Pixels
summary: Standard deep learning models for image segmentation cannot guarantee topology accuracy, failing to preserve the correct number of connected components or structures. This, in turn, affects the quality of the segmentations and compromises the reliability of the subsequent quantification analyses. Previous works have proposed to enhance topology accuracy with specialized frameworks, architectures, and loss functions. However, these methods are often cumbersome to integrate into existing training pipelines, they are computationally very expensive, or they are restricted to structures with tubular morphology. We present SCNP, an efficient method that improves topology accuracy by penalizing the logits with their poorest-classified neighbor, forcing the model to improve the prediction at the pixels' neighbors before allowing it to improve the pixels themselves. We show the effectiveness of SCNP across 13 datasets, covering different structure morphologies and image modalities, and integrate it into three frameworks for semantic and instance segmentation. Additionally, we show that SCNP can be integrated into several loss functions, making them improve topology accuracy. Our code can be found at https://jmlipman.github.io/SCNP-SameClassNeighborPenalization.

AI.tldr: SCNP (Same Class Neighbor Penalization) improves topology accuracy in segmentation by penalizing a pixel's logits toward its poorest-classified neighbor, forcing improvements in neighboring pixels before adjusting the pixel itself. Validated on 13 datasets across morphologies and modalities; compatible with semantic/instance segmentation frameworks and various loss functions; code release provided.
AI.motivation: Standard segmentation methods often fail to preserve topology (e.g., number of connected components), adversely affecting segmentation quality and downstream quantification. Existing topology-focused methods are hard to integrate, compute-intensive, or restricted to tubular structures.
AI.method: For each pixel, identify its poorest-classified neighbor and apply a neighbor-based penalty that encourages updating neighboring pixels before the pixel itself. This enforces local topology corrections by focusing learning on neighboring regions that contribute to topology errors. The approach is designed to be lightweight, model-agnostic, and compatible with multiple loss functions and segmentation frameworks.
AI.result: SCNP consistently improves topology accuracy across 13 datasets with diverse morphologies and modalities. It integrates into three segmentation frameworks for both semantic and instance segmentation and can enhance several loss functions. Code is available for implementation.
AI.conclusion: SCNP provides an efficient, plug-and-play mechanism to boost topology fidelity in segmentation without heavy architectural changes, with broad applicability across structures and modalities and compatibility with existing training pipelines.

title: Signals of Success and Struggle: Early Prediction and Physiological Signatures of Human Performance across Task Complexity
summary: User performance is crucial in interactive systems, capturing how effectively users engage with task execution. Prospectively predicting performance enables the timely identification of users struggling with task demands. While ocular and cardiac signals are widely used to characterise performance-relevant visual behaviour and physiological activation, their potential for early prediction and for revealing the physiological mechanisms underlying performance differences remains underexplored. We conducted a within-subject experiment in a game environment with naturally unfolding complexity, using early ocular and cardiac signals to predict later performance and to examine physiological and self-reported group differences. Results show that the ocular-cardiac fusion model achieves a balanced accuracy of 0.86, and the ocular-only model shows comparable predictive power. High performers exhibited targeted gaze and adjusted visual sampling, and sustained more stable cardiac activation as demands intensified, with a more positive affective experience. These findings demonstrate the feasibility of cross-session prediction from early physiology, providing interpretable insights into performance variation and facilitating future proactive intervention.

AI.tldr: Early ocular and cardiac signals in a gaming task can predict subsequent performance; combining eye-tracking and cardiac data yields high predictive accuracy (0.86), with ocular data alone nearly as effective; high performers display distinctive gaze patterns and more stable cardiac activation under rising demand; supports cross-session prediction and potential proactive interventions.
AI.motivation: To determine whether early physiological markers (eye movements and cardiac signals) can forecast later task performance and reveal physiological mechanisms behind performance differences, enabling proactive interventions in interactive systems.
AI.method: Within-subject experiment in a game environment with unfolding complexity. Collected early ocular metrics and cardiac signals; built predictive models using ocular data alone and a fused ocular+cardiac approach; evaluated predictive accuracy and examined group differences in physiology and self-reported affect; explored cross-session generalization.
AI.result: The ocular–cardiac fusion model achieved a balanced accuracy of 0.86; the ocular-only model showed comparable predictive power. High performers exhibited targeted gaze and adjusted visual sampling, and maintained more stable cardiac activation as demands intensified, along with more positive affect. The study demonstrates feasible cross-session prediction from early physiology and provides interpretable insights into performance variation.
AI.conclusion: Early ocular and cardiac measures can predict future performance across sessions; combining modalities enhances understanding of the mechanisms of performance differences and supports potential proactive interventions in interactive tasks.

title: How Uncertainty Estimation Scales with Sampling in Reasoning Models
summary:
Uncertainty estimation is critical for deploying reasoning language models, yet remains poorly understood under extended chain-of-thought reasoning. We study parallel sampling as a fully black-box approach using verbalized confidence and self-consistency. Across three reasoning models and 17 tasks spanning mathematics, STEM, and humanities, we characterize how these signals scale.
  Both self-consistency and verbalized confidence scale in reasoning models, but self-consistency exhibits lower initial discrimination and lags behind verbalized confidence under moderate sampling. Most uncertainty gains, however, arise from signal combination: with just two samples, a hybrid estimator improves AUROC by up to $+12$ on average and already outperforms either signal alone even when scaled to much larger budgets, after which returns diminish. These effects are domain-dependent: in mathematics, the native domain of RLVR-style post-training, reasoning models achieve higher uncertainty quality and exhibit both stronger complementarity and faster scaling than in STEM or humanities.

AI.tldr: Parallel sampling with verbalized confidence and self-consistency improves uncertainty estimation for reasoning LMs; a two-sample hybrid estimator yields substantial AUROC gains and outperforms single signals, with gains strongest in the mathematics domain and diminishing returns with more samples.
AI.motivation: Understand and improve uncertainty estimation for extended chain-of-thought reasoning in language models using black-box, scalable signals.
AI.method: Evaluate self-consistency and verbalized confidence as signals under parallel sampling across three reasoning models and 17 tasks in mathematics, STEM, and humanities. Compare single-signal vs. hybrid estimators as a function of sampling budget. Measure AUROC and analyze domain dependence and scaling behavior.
AI.result: Self-consistency scales with data but shows lower initial discrimination and lags behind verbalized confidence under moderate budgets. Most gains derive from combining signals: with only two samples, the hybrid estimator increases AUROC by up to 12 points on average and outperforms either signal alone even at larger budgets. Returns diminish beyond this. Domain-dependent effects: mathematics shows higher uncertainty quality, stronger complementarity, and faster scaling than STEM or humanities (RLVR-style post-training context).
AI.conclusion: Signal combination is key for robust uncertainty estimation in reasoning LMs, with the two-sample hybrid approach offering significant gains and earlier utility. Domain and training regime (math RLVR-style post-training) influence the relative effectiveness and scaling of uncertainty signals.

title: Ontology-Guided Diffusion for Zero-Shot Visual Sim2Real Transfer
summary: Bridging the simulation-to-reality (sim2real) gap remains challenging as labelled real-world data is scarce. Existing diffusion-based approaches rely on unstructured prompts or statistical alignment, which do not capture the structured factors that make images look real. We introduce Ontology- Guided Diffusion (OGD), a neuro-symbolic zero-shot sim2real image translation framework that represents realism as structured knowledge. OGD decomposes realism into an ontology of interpretable traits -- such as lighting and material properties -- and encodes their relationships in a knowledge graph. From a synthetic image, OGD infers trait activations and uses a graph neural network to produce a global embedding. In parallel, a symbolic planner uses the ontology traits to compute a consistent sequence of visual edits needed to narrow the realism gap. The graph embedding conditions a pretrained instruction-guided diffusion model via cross-attention, while the planned edits are converted into a structured instruction prompt. Across benchmarks, our graph-based embeddings better distinguish real from synthetic imagery than baselines, and OGD outperforms state-of-the-art diffusion methods in sim2real image translations. Overall, OGD shows that explicitly encoding realism structure enables interpretable, data-efficient, and generalisable zero-shot sim2real transfer.

AI.tldr: Introduces Ontology-Guided Diffusion (OGD), a zero-shot sim2real pipeline that uses a knowledge-graph ontology of realism traits to guide diffusion with graph embeddings and symbolic planning, improving sim2real translation.
AI.motivation: Bridge the sim2real gap by capturing structured realism factors. Prior diffusion approaches rely on unstructured prompts or statistical alignment and miss interpretable, relational aspects of realism.
AI.method: Decompose realism into an ontology of interpretable traits (e.g., lighting, materials) encoded as a knowledge graph. From a synthetic image, infer trait activations with a graph neural network to obtain a global embedding. A symbolic planner uses the ontology traits to compute a consistent sequence of visual edits. The graph embedding conditions a pretrained instruction-guided diffusion model via cross-attention, and the planned edits are converted into a structured prompt.
AI.result: Graph-based embeddings better distinguish real from synthetic imagery than baselines. OGD outperforms state-of-the-art diffusion methods on sim2real image translation benchmarks. Demonstrates interpretable, data-efficient, and generalisable zero-shot transfer.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly encoding realism structure enables interpretable, data-efficient, and generalisable zero-shot sim2real transfer.

title: Seasoning Generative Models for a Generalization Aftertaste
summary: The use of discriminators to train or fine-tune generative models has proven to be a rather successful framework. A notable example is Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) that minimize a loss incurred by training discriminators along with other paradigms that boost generative models via discriminators that satisfy weak learner constraints. More recently, even diffusion models have shown advantages with some kind of discriminator guidance. In this work, we extend a strong-duality result related to $f$-divergences which gives rise to a discriminator-guided recipe that allows us to \textit{refine} any generative model. We then show that the refined generative models provably improve generalization, compared to its non-refined counterpart. In particular, our analysis reveals that the gap in generalization is improved based on the Rademacher complexity of the discriminator set used for refinement. Our recipe subsumes a recently introduced score-based diffusion approach (Kim et al., 2022) that has shown great empirical success, however allows us to shed light on the generalization guarantees of this method by virtue of our analysis. Thus, our work provides a theoretical validation for existing work, suggests avenues for new algorithms, and contributes to our understanding of generalization in generative models at large.

AI.tldr: A discriminator-guided refinement framework for generative models grounded in f-divergence duality; it provably improves generalization with a bound linked to the discriminator set's Rademacher complexity and subsumes diffusion-score methods.
AI.motivation: To establish theoretical generalization guarantees for discriminator-guided refinement of generative models and to unify with recent diffusion-based approaches.
AI.method: Extend a strong duality result for f-divergences to derive a discriminator-guided refinement recipe that can be applied to any generative model. Analyze generalization improvement via Rademacher complexity of the discriminator family used for refinement. Show that the approach subsumes a diffusion-based score method (Kim et al., 2022).
AI.result: Refined generative models provably exhibit better generalization than their non-refined counterparts. The generalization gap decreases with the Rademacher complexity of the discriminator set, providing a concrete link between discriminator capacity and performance. The framework offers theoretical validation for existing diffusion-guided methods and suggests directions for new algorithms.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a solid theoretical foundation for discriminator-guided refinement of generative models, clarifies generalization guarantees, and highlights how discriminator capacity controls improvements—potentially guiding future algorithm design and analysis.

title: Implicit Patterns in LLM-Based Binary Analysis
summary: Binary vulnerability analysis is increasingly performed by LLM-based agents in an iterative, multi-pass manner, with the model as the core decision-maker. However, how such systems organize exploration over hundreds of reasoning steps remains poorly understood, due to limited context windows and implicit token-level behaviors. We present the first large-scale, trace-level study showing that multi-pass LLM reasoning gives rise to structured, token-level implicit patterns. Analyzing 521 binaries with 99,563 reasoning steps, we identify four dominant patterns: early pruning, path-dependent lock-in, targeted backtracking, and knowledge-guided prioritization that emerge implicitly from reasoning traces. These token-level implicit patterns serve as an abstraction of LLM reasoning: instead of explicit control-flow or predefined heuristics, exploration is organized through implicit decisions regulating path selection, commitment, and revision. Our analysis shows these patterns form a stable, structured system with distinct temporal roles and measurable characteristics. Our results provide the first systematic characterization of LLM-driven binary analysis and a foundation for more reliable analysis systems.

AI.tldr: LM-driven binary analysis exhibits four implicit token-level patterns (early pruning, lock-in, backtracking, knowledge-guided prioritization) across 521 binaries and ~99k steps, revealing structured multi-pass reasoning.
AI.motivation: To understand how multi-pass LLM reasoning organizes exploration in binary vulnerability analysis under limited context windows and token-level behaviors, and to characterize implicit patterns.
AI.method: Large-scale, trace-level study analyzing 521 binaries with 99,563 reasoning steps from LLM-driven analyses to identify recurring patterns in reasoning traces.
AI.result: Four dominant token-level patterns identified: early pruning, path-dependent lock-in, targeted backtracking, and knowledge-guided prioritization; patterns form a stable, structured system with distinct temporal roles and measurable characteristics; provide a abstracted view of LLM reasoning.
AI.conclusion: These patterns offer a systematic characterization of LL-driven binary analysis and lay the foundation for more reliable, controllable analysis systems by understanding implicit decision dynamics during exploration.

title: EdgeCrafter: Compact ViTs for Edge Dense Prediction via Task-Specialized Distillation
summary: Deploying high-performance dense prediction models on resource-constrained edge devices remains challenging due to strict limits on computation and memory. In practice, lightweight systems for object detection, instance segmentation, and pose estimation are still dominated by CNN-based architectures such as YOLO, while compact Vision Transformers (ViTs) often struggle to achieve similarly strong accuracy efficiency tradeoff, even with large scale pretraining. We argue that this gap is largely due to insufficient task specific representation learning in small scale ViTs, rather than an inherent mismatch between ViTs and edge dense prediction. To address this issue, we introduce EdgeCrafter, a unified compact ViT framework for edge dense prediction centered on ECDet, a detection model built from a distilled compact backbone and an edge-friendly encoder decoder design. On the COCO dataset, ECDet-S achieves 51.7 AP with fewer than 10M parameters using only COCO annotations. For instance segmentation, ECInsSeg achieves performance comparable to RF-DETR while using substantially fewer parameters. For pose estimation, ECPose-X reaches 74.8 AP, significantly outperforming YOLO26Pose-X (71.6 AP) despite the latter's reliance on extensive Objects365 pretraining. These results show that compact ViTs, when paired with task-specialized distillation and edge-aware design, can be a practical and competitive option for edge dense prediction. Code is available at: https://intellindust-ai-lab.github.io/projects/EdgeCrafter/

AI.tldr: EdgeCrafter demonstrates that compact ViTs can achieve strong edge-dense-prediction results through task-specific distillation and edge-aware designs, delivering competitive COCO performance with under 10M parameters across detection, segmentation, and pose tasks.
AI.motivation: Dense prediction on resource-constrained edges remains challenging for ViTs; existing CNNs dominate due to efficiency-accuracy tradeoffs. Hypothesis: insufficient task-specific representation learning in small ViTs is a key bottleneck, not an intrinsic mismatch between ViTs and edge tasks.
AI.method: Propose EdgeCrafter, a unified compact ViT framework centered on ECDet. ECDet uses a distilled compact backbone plus an edge-friendly encoder–decoder. Extend with task-specific distillation for segmentation (ECInsSeg) and pose (ECPose-X). Evaluate on COCO: ECDet-S achieves 51.7 AP with <10M params using only COCO annotations; ECInsSeg competitive with RF-DETR with far fewer parameters; ECPose-X achieves 74.8 AP, surpassing YOLO26Pose-X (71.6 AP) without requiring heavier pretraining like Objects365.
AI.result: ECDet-S: 51.7 AP with <10M parameters on COCO. ECInsSeg: comparable to RF-DETR with substantially fewer parameters. ECPose-X: 74.8 AP, outperforming YOLO26Pose-X at 71.6 AP. Code released at the project page.
AI.conclusion: Compact ViTs, when paired with task-specific distillation and edge-aware design, offer practical competitiveness for edge dense prediction, potentially closing the gap with CNN-based systems on resource-constrained devices.

title: A Model Ensemble-Based Post-Processing Framework for Fairness-Aware Prediction
summary: Striking an optimal balance between predictive performance and fairness continues to be a fundamental challenge in machine learning. In this work, we propose a post-processing framework that facilitates fairness-aware prediction by leveraging model ensembling. Designed to operate independently of any specific model internals, our approach is widely applicable across various learning tasks, model architectures, and fairness definitions. Through extensive experiments spanning classification, regression, and survival analysis, we demonstrate that the framework effectively enhances fairness while maintaining, or only minimally affecting, predictive accuracy.

AI.tldr: A model-agnostic, post-processing ensembling framework improves fairness with minimal accuracy loss across classification, regression, and survival analysis.
AI.motivation: To address the persistent trade-off between predictive performance and fairness by providing a practical, model-agnostic solution that does not depend on model internals and is applicable across tasks and fairness definitions.
AI.method: A post-processing framework that leverages model ensembling to adjust predictions for fairness, operating independently of any model internals and applicable to diverse learning tasks, architectures, and fairness definitions.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across classification, regression, and survival analysis shows improved fairness with maintained or minimally affected predictive accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a versatile, widely applicable approach to fairness in ML that can be adopted without altering model architectures or access to internal components, enabling better fairness-accuracy trade-offs.

title: D5P4: Partition Determinantal Point Process for Diversity in Parallel Discrete Diffusion Decoding
summary: Discrete diffusion models are promising alternatives to autoregressive approaches for text generation, yet their decoding methods remain under-studied. Standard decoding methods for autoregressive models, such as beam search, do not directly apply to iterative denoising, and existing diffusion decoding techniques provide limited control over in-batch diversity. To bridge this gap, we introduce a generalized beam-search framework for discrete diffusion that generates candidates in parallel and supports modular beam-selection objectives. As a diversity-focused instantiation, we propose D5P4, which formulates the selection step as MAP inference over a Determinantal Point Process. Leveraging a scalable greedy solver, D5P4 maintains multi-GPU compatibility and enables an explicit trade-off between model probability and target diversity with near-zero compute overhead. Experiments on free-form generation and question answering demonstrate that D5P4 improves diversity over strong baselines while maintaining competitive generation quality.

AI.tldr: A generalized, parallel beam-search framework for discrete diffusion decoding is proposed, plus D5P4, a diversity-focused method via MAP inference on a Determinantal Point Process. It enables multi-GPU, scalable trade-offs between probability and diversity, achieving higher in-batch diversity with competitive quality on free-form generation and QA.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive decoding techniques do not directly transfer to iterative discrete diffusion. There is a need for parallel, controllable, and diverse decoding in diffusion models, as existing diffusion decoders offer limited diversity and batch control.
AI.method: Introduce a generalized beam-search framework for discrete diffusion that generates candidates in parallel and supports modular beam-selection objectives. Propose D5P4, which formulates the selection step as MAP inference over a Determinantal Point Process (DPP) and uses a scalable greedy solver to maintain multi-GPU compatibility, enabling a tunable trade-off between model probability and diversity.
AI.result: Empirical results on free-form generation and question answering show that D5P4 increases in-batch diversity relative to strong baselines while maintaining competitive generation quality.
AI.conclusion: The proposed discrete diffusion decoding framework and D5P4 offer a practical, scalable approach to balancing quality and diversity in diffusion-based text generation, with broad applicability and room for further refinement in objective design and efficiency.

title: 6Bit-Diffusion: Inference-Time Mixed-Precision Quantization for Video Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion transformers have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in generating videos. However, their practical deployment is severely constrained by high memory usage and computational cost. Post-Training Quantization provides a practical way to reduce memory usage and boost computation speed. Existing quantization methods typically apply a static bit-width allocation, overlooking the quantization difficulty of activations across diffusion timesteps, leading to a suboptimal trade-off between efficiency and quality. In this paper, we propose a inference time NVFP4/INT8 Mixed-Precision Quantization framework. We find a strong linear correlation between a block's input-output difference and the quantization sensitivity of its internal linear layers. Based on this insight, we design a lightweight predictor that dynamically allocates NVFP4 to temporally stable layers to maximize memory compression, while selectively preserving INT8 for volatile layers to ensure robustness. This adaptive precision strategy enables aggressive quantization without compromising generation quality. Beside this, we observe that the residual between the input and output of a Transformer block exhibits high temporal consistency across timesteps. Leveraging this temporal redundancy, we introduce Temporal Delta Cache (TDC) to skip computations for these invariant blocks, further reducing the computational cost. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves 1.92$\times$ end-to-end acceleration and 3.32$\times$ memory reduction, setting a new baseline for efficient inference in Video DiTs.

AI.tldr: Introduces inference-time mixed-precision quantization (NVFP4/INT8) for Video DiTs with a dynamic layer-wise precision allocator and Temporal Delta Cache, achieving 1.92x speedup and 3.32x memory reduction.
AI.motivation: Video diffusion transformers are memory- and compute-heavy; static quantization fails to adapt to varying quantization difficulty across diffusion timesteps, compromising efficiency-quality trade-offs.
AI.method: Develop a lightweight predictor predicting quantization sensitivity from block input-output difference; allocate NVFP4 to temporally stable layers and INT8 to volatile layers; implement Temporal Delta Cache to skip computations for temporally invariant Transformer blocks by exploiting residual temporal consistency.
AI.result: 1.92x end-to-end acceleration; 3.32x memory reduction; sets new baseline for efficient inference in Video Diffusion Transformers (Video DiTs).
AI.conclusion: Adaptive mixed-precision with temporal redundancy exploitation significantly reduces cost while preserving generation quality; demonstrates effectiveness of temporal-aware quantization and caching.

title: DriftGuard: Mitigating Asynchronous Data Drift in Federated Learning
summary: In real-world Federated Learning (FL) deployments, data distributions on devices that participate in training evolve over time. This leads to asynchronous data drift, where different devices shift at different times and toward different distributions. Mitigating such drift is challenging: frequent retraining incurs high computational cost on resource-constrained devices, while infrequent retraining degrades performance on drifting devices. We propose DriftGuard, a federated continual learning framework that efficiently adapts to asynchronous data drift. DriftGuard adopts a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) inspired architecture that separates shared parameters, which capture globally transferable knowledge, from local parameters that adapt to group-specific distributions. This design enables two complementary retraining strategies: (i) global retraining, which updates the shared parameters when system-wide drift is identified, and (ii) group retraining, which selectively updates local parameters for clusters of devices identified via MoE gating patterns, without sharing raw data. Experiments across multiple datasets and models show that DriftGuard matches or exceeds state-of-the-art accuracy while reducing total retraining cost by up to 83%. As a result, it achieves the highest accuracy per unit retraining cost, improving over the strongest baseline by up to 2.3x. DriftGuard is available for download from https://github.com/blessonvar/DriftGuard.

AI.tldr: DriftGuard introduces a Mixture-of-Experts–inspired federated continual learning framework to tackle asynchronous data drift in Federated Learning by separating global (shared) and local (group-specific) parameters and offering two retraining modes (global and group retraining). It achieves high accuracy with reduced retraining costs, surpassing baselines in cost-efficiency and accuracy per unit cost; code is available.
AI.motivation: In real-world FL, device data drift occurs asynchronously across devices, causing performance degradation and costly frequent retraining. There is a need for cost-efficient adaptation that scales with drift dynamics.
AI.method: A MoE-inspired architecture partitions parameters into shared/global parameters and local parameters tied to device groups via gating. It supports two retraining strategies: (i) global retraining updates shared parameters when system-wide drift is detected, and (ii) group retraining selectively updates local parameters for device clusters identified by gating patterns, without sharing raw data.
AI.result: Experimental results across multiple datasets and models show DriftGuard matches or surpasses state-of-the-art accuracy while reducing total retraining cost by up to 83%. It yields the highest accuracy per unit retraining cost, improving over the strongest baseline by up to 2.3x. The method is released as open-source at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: DriftGuard provides an effective, cost-efficient solution for adapting to asynchronous data drift in FL by decoupling global and local adaptation via MoE-inspired gating, with two retraining modes that balance accuracy and cost.

title: cuGenOpt: A GPU-Accelerated General-Purpose Metaheuristic Framework for Combinatorial Optimization
comment: 28 pages, 9 figures. Code available at https://github.com/L-yang-yang/cugenopt
summary:
Combinatorial optimization problems arise in logistics, scheduling, and resource allocation, yet existing approaches face a fundamental trade-off among generality, performance, and usability. We present cuGenOpt, a GPU-accelerated general-purpose metaheuristic framework that addresses all three dimensions simultaneously.
  At the engine level, cuGenOpt adopts a "one block evolves one solution" CUDA architecture with a unified encoding abstraction (permutation, binary, integer), a two-level adaptive operator selection mechanism, and hardware-aware resource management. At the extensibility level, a user-defined operator registration interface allows domain experts to inject problem-specific CUDA search operators. At the usability level, a JIT compilation pipeline exposes the framework as a pure-Python API, and an LLM-based modeling assistant converts natural-language problem descriptions into executable solver code.
  Experiments across five thematic suites on three GPU architectures (T4, V100, A800) show that cuGenOpt outperforms general MIP solvers by orders of magnitude, achieves competitive quality against specialized solvers on instances up to n=150, and attains 4.73% gap on TSP-442 within 30s. Twelve problem types spanning five encoding variants are solved to optimality. Framework-level optimizations cumulatively reduce pcb442 gap from 36% to 4.73% and boost VRPTW throughput by 75-81%.
  Code: https://github.com/L-yang-yang/cugenopt

AI.tldr: GPU-accelerated general-purpose metaheuristic cuGenOpt achieves broad problem generality, high performance on GPUs, and usability via JIT Python API and NL-to-solver code generation; evaluated across multiple GPUs and problem suites with strong results and open-source code.
AI.motivation: Combinatorial optimization methods often force a trade-off among generality, performance, and usability. A unified GPU-based framework that can handle multiple encodings, adapt operators, and provide convenient interfaces is desirable for rapid development and deployment.
AI.method: Engine: 'one block evolves one solution', unified encoding for permutation/binary/integer, two-level adaptive operator selection, hardware-aware resource management. Extensibility: user-defined CUDA search operators via a registration interface. Usability: JIT compilation to a pure-Python API; LLM-based modeling assistant to translate natural-language problem descriptions into executable solver code. Evaluation: five thematic suites, three GPUs (T4, V100, A800); comparisons against general MIP solvers and specialized solvers; twelve problem types across five encodings; metrics include optimality on some problems, gaps (e.g., pcb442 down to 4.73%), and throughput (VRPTW), with code provided.
AI.result: cuGenOpt outperforms general MIP solvers by orders of magnitude; competitive quality with specialized solvers on instances up to n=150; 4.73% gap on TSP-442 within 30s; twelve problem types solved to optimality; framework-level optimizations reduced pcb442 gap from 36% to 4.73% and boosted VRPTW throughput by 75–81%. Code: GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates that GPU-accelerated general-purpose metaheuristics can deliver high performance and usability across diverse combinatorial problems, enabling rapid experimentation, domain-specific extensions, and NL-to-code workflows; results indicate strong potential for practice and reproducibility through open-source code.

title: WeNLEX: Weakly Supervised Natural Language Explanations for Multilabel Chest X-ray Classification
summary: Natural language explanations provide an inherently human-understandable way to explain black-box models, closely reflecting how radiologists convey their diagnoses in textual reports. Most works explicitly supervise the explanation generation process using datasets annotated with explanations. Thus, though plausible, the generated explanations are not faithful to the model's reasoning. In this work, we propose WeNLEX, a weakly supervised model for the generation of natural language explanations for multilabel chest X-ray classification. Faithfulness is ensured by matching images generated from their corresponding natural language explanations with original images, in the black-box model's feature space. Plausibility is maintained via distribution alignment with a small database of clinician-annotated explanations. We empirically demonstrate, through extensive validation on multiple metrics to assess faithfulness, simulatability, diversity, and plausibility, that WeNLEX is able to produce faithful and plausible explanations, using as little as 5 ground-truth explanations per diagnosis. Furthermore, WeNLEX can operate in both post-hoc and in-model settings. In the latter, i.e., when the multilabel classifier is trained together with the rest of the network, WeNLEX improves the classification AUC of the standalone classifier by 2.21%, thus showing that adding interpretability to the training process can actually increase the downstream task performance. Additionally, simply by changing the database, WeNLEX explanations are adaptable to any target audience, and we showcase this flexibility by training a layman version of WeNLEX, where explanations are simplified for non-medical users.

AI.tldr: WeNLEX is a weakly supervised framework that generates faithful and plausible natural language explanations for multilabel chest X-ray classification by (i) matching generated images from explanations to originals in the model’s feature space, and (ii) aligning explanation distributions with clinician-annotated data; it supports post-hoc and end-to-end training, achieves a 2.21% AUC gain, and can adapt explanations for lay audiences with minimal supervision.
AI.motivation: Explaining black-box radiology models with text that truly reflects the model’s reasoning is difficult when annotations are scarce; existing supervised explanations may be plausible but not faithful. WeNLEX seeks faithfulness under weak supervision and practical plausibility to support clinical use.
AI.method: WeNLEX uses weak supervision to generate natural language explanations. It enforces faithfulness by generating images from explanations and matching them to the original images in the black-box model’s feature space. Plausibility is enforced via distribution alignment with a small database of clinician-annotated explanations. The approach supports post-hoc use and joint end-to-end training with the classifier, and it can switch the explanation database to tailor the audience (e.g., layperson).
AI.result: Empirical validation across metrics for faithfulness, simulatability, diversity, and plausibility shows WeNLEX produces faithful and plausible explanations with as few as 5 ground-truth explanations per diagnosis. End-to-end training improves the base classifier AUC by 2.21%. The system is adaptable to different audiences by changing the explanation database.
AI.conclusion: WeNLEX demonstrates that weakly supervised natural language explanations can be faithful and plausible for multilabel chest X-ray classification, with potential to improve downstream task performance and allow audience-specific explanations.

title: Authority-Level Priors: An Under-Specified Constraint in Hierarchical Predictive Processing
summary: Hierarchical predictive processing explains adaptive behaviour through precision-weighted inference. Explicit belief revision often fails to produce corresponding changes in stress reactivity or autonomic regulation. This asymmetry suggests the framework leaves under-specified a governance-level constraint concerning which identity-level hypotheses regulate autonomic and behavioural control under uncertainty. We introduce Authority-Level Priors (ALPs) as meta-structural constraints defining a regulatory-admissible subset (Hauth, a subset of H) of identity-level hypotheses. ALPs are not additional representational states nor hyperpriors over precision; they constrain which hypotheses are admissible for regulatory control. Precision determines influence conditional on admissibility; ALPs determine admissibility itself. This explains why explicit belief updating modifies representational beliefs while autonomic threat responses remain stable. A computational formalisation restricts policy optimisation to policies generated by authorised hypotheses, yielding testable predictions concerning stress-reactivity dynamics, recovery time constants, compensatory control engagement, and behavioural persistence. Neurobiologically, ALPs manifest through distributed prefrontal arbitration and control networks. The proposal is compatible with variational active inference and introduces no additional inferential operators, instead formalising a boundary condition required for determinate identity-regulation mapping. The model generates falsifiable predictions: governance shifts should produce measurable changes in stress-reactivity curves, recovery dynamics, compensatory cognitive effort, and behavioural change durability. ALPs are advanced as an architectural hypothesis to be evaluated through computational modelling and longitudinal stress-induction paradigms.

AI.tldr: Introduces Authority-Level Priors (ALPs) as governance constraints that restrict which identity-level hypotheses can influence regulatory control under uncertainty; ALPs are not priors over precision but boundary conditions, yielding testable predictions about stress reactivity, recovery, and behavioral persistence within hierarchical predictive processing.
AI.motivation: To resolve the mismatch between explicit belief updates and autonomic responses by specifying a governance layer that determines admissible hypotheses for regulation.
AI.method: Theoretical development within variational active inference; defines Hauth as the admissible subset of identity-level hypotheses; frames ALPs as meta-structural constraints; discusses neurobiological realization via prefrontal arbitration networks; provides a computational formalisation restricting policy optimisation to authorised hypotheses; outlines longitudinal stress-induction paradigms as testing ground.
AI.result: Derives predictions: governance shifts should alter stress-reactivity curves, recovery time constants, cognitive effort, and durable behavioral change; yields falsifiable predictions for stress dynamics; compatible with existing frameworks without adding new inferential operators.
AI.conclusion: ALPs offer an architectural constraint essential for determinate identity-regulation mapping in hierarchical predictive processing; feasible to evaluate via computational modelling and longitudinal experiments; connects theoretical, computational, and neurobiological levels.

title: Box Maze: A Process-Control Architecture for Reliable LLM Reasoning
comment: 10 pages, 5 tables, 0 figures. Conceptual architecture with preliminary simulation-based validation
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) demonstrate strong generative capabilities but remain vulnerable to hallucination and unreliable reasoning under adversarial prompting. Existing safety approaches -- such as reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and output filtering -- primarily operate at the behavioral level and may lack explicit architectural mechanisms for enforcing reasoning process integrity.
  This paper proposes the Box Maze framework, a conceptual process-control architecture that decomposes LLM reasoning into three explicit layers: memory grounding, structured inference, and boundary enforcement. We introduce preliminary simulation-based evaluation involving progressive boundary erosion scenarios across multiple heterogeneous LLM systems (DeepSeek-V3, Doubao, Qwen). Results from n=50 adversarial scenarios suggest that explicit cognitive control layers may improve consistency in boundary maintenance, with architectural constraints reducing boundary failure rates from approximately 40% (baseline RLHF) to below 1% under adversarial conditions.
  While current validation is simulation-based, these preliminary results indicate that process-level control may offer a promising direction for improving reliability in large language model reasoning.

AI.tldr: Proposes Box Maze: a process-control architecture for LLM reasoning with three explicit layers (memory grounding, structured inference, boundary enforcement). Provides simulation-based evidence that explicit cognitive control layers can drastically reduce boundary failures under adversarial prompting.
AI.motivation: Address the gap where safety approaches (e.g., RLHF, output filters) work at behavioral level but lack explicit architectural mechanisms to enforce reasoning integrity.
AI.method: Introduce Box Maze as a three-layer architecture and evaluate via simulation across adversarial prompting scenarios (n=50) on several LLMs (DeepSeek-V3, Doubao, Qwen) to test boundary maintenance under erosion.
AI.result: Across 50 adversarial scenarios, explicit control layers reduced boundary failure rates from ~40% (baseline RLHF) to <1% under adversarial conditions.
AI.conclusion: Process-level cognitive control shows promise for improving reliability of LLM reasoning; however validation is simulation-based and real-world effectiveness remains to be established.

title: Uniform a priori bounds and error analysis for the Adam stochastic gradient descent optimization method
summary: The adaptive moment estimation (Adam) optimizer proposed by Kingma & Ba (2014) is presumably the most popular stochastic gradient descent (SGD) optimization method for the training of deep neural networks (DNNs) in artificial intelligence (AI) systems. Despite its groundbreaking success in the training of AI systems, it still remains an open research problem to provide a complete error analysis of Adam, not only for optimizing DNNs but even when applied to strongly convex stochastic optimization problems (SOPs). Previous error analysis results for strongly convex SOPs in the literature provide conditional convergence analyses that rely on the assumption that Adam does not diverge to infinity but remains uniformly bounded. It is the key contribution of this work to establish uniform a priori bounds for Adam and, thereby, to provide -- for the first time -- an unconditional error analysis for Adam for a large class of strongly convex SOPs.

AI.tldr: This work provides the first unconditional error analysis for Adam in strongly convex stochastic optimization by establishing uniform a priori bounds on the optimizer, removing the need for a priori boundedness assumptions.
AI.motivation: Existing analyses rely on the assumption that Adam stays uniformly bounded; this work removes that conditionality and extends understanding to strongly convex SOPs.
AI.method: Derives uniform a priori bounds for Adam iterates and uses them to obtain unconditional convergence/error results for a broad class of strongly convex SOPs.
AI.result: Unconditional error bounds for Adam in the considered setting; proof that Adam is stable without the previous boundedness hypothesis.
AI.conclusion: First unconditional error analysis for Adam in strongly convex SOPs; a foundational step toward rigorous analysis of Adam in broader optimization contexts.

title: OS-Themis: A Scalable Critic Framework for Generalist GUI Rewards
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has the potential to improve the robustness of GUI agents in stochastic environments, yet training is highly sensitive to the quality of the reward function. Existing reward approaches struggle to achieve both scalability and performance. To address this, we propose OS-Themis, a scalable and accurate multi-agent critic framework. Unlike a single judge, OS-Themis decomposes trajectories into verifiable milestones to isolate critical evidence for decision making and employs a review mechanism to strictly audit the evidence chain before making the final verdict. To facilitate evaluation, we further introduce OmniGUIRewardBench (OGRBench), a holistic cross-platform benchmark for GUI outcome rewards, where all evaluated models achieve their best performance under OS-Themis. Extensive experiments on AndroidWorld show that OS-Themis yields a 10.3% improvement when used to support online RL training, and a 6.9% gain when used for trajectory validation and filtering in the self-training loop, highlighting its potential to drive agent evolution.

AI.tldr: OS-Themis is a scalable multi-agent critic framework for GUI RL that decomposes trajectories into milestones and uses a review mechanism to audit evidence, paired with OGRBench benchmark; experiments on AndroidWorld show +10.3% online RL improvement and +6.9% self-training gains.
AI.motivation: Reward signal quality in RL for GUI agents is brittle and hard to scale; existing reward methods struggle to achieve both performance and scalability. A verifiable, auditable evidence chain is proposed to improve decision-making reliability.
AI.method: Introduce OS-Themis, a multi-agent critic that breaks trajectories into verifiable milestones and employs a review mechanism to validate evidence before making a verdict. Develop OmniGUIRewardBench (OGRBench), a cross-platform GUI outcome reward benchmark. Evaluate on AndroidWorld with RL training and self-training workflows.
AI.result: OS-Themis yields 10.3% improvement for online RL training and 6.9% gain for trajectory validation/filtering in self-training; under OS-Themis, evaluated models reach their best performance on the benchmark.
AI.conclusion: OS-Themis provides scalable, accurate reward assessment for GUI RL, enhancing policy learning and agent evolution; the OGRBench benchmark enables cross-platform evaluation to support robust GUI reward design.

title: ProCal: Probability Calibration for Neighborhood-Guided Source-Free Domain Adaptation
summary: Source-Free Domain Adaptation (SFDA) adapts pre-trained models to unlabeled target domains without requiring access to source data. Although state-of-the-art methods leveraging local neighborhood structures show promise for SFDA, they tend to over-rely on prediction similarity among neighbors. This over-reliance accelerates the forgetting of source knowledge and increases susceptibility to local noise overfitting. To address these issues, we introduce ProCal, a probability calibration method that dynamically calibrates neighborhood-based predictions through a dual-model collaborative prediction mechanism. ProCal integrates the source model's initial predictions with the current model's online outputs to effectively calibrate neighbor probabilities. This strategy not only mitigates the interference of local noise but also preserves the discriminative information from the source model, thereby achieving a balance between knowledge retention and domain adaptation. Furthermore, we design a joint optimization objective that combines a soft supervision loss with a diversity loss to guide the target model. Our theoretical analysis shows that ProCal converges to an equilibrium where source knowledge and target information are effectively fused, reducing both knowledge forgetting and overfitting. We validate the effectiveness of our approach through extensive experiments on 31 cross-domain tasks across four public datasets. Our code is available at: https://github.com/zhengyinghit/ProCal.

AI.tldr: ProCal is a probability-calibrated, dual-model SFDA method that fuses source-prior predictions with a target model's online outputs to calibrate neighborhood-based predictions, guided by a soft supervision and diversity objective, with theoretical convergence guarantees and extensive cross-domain validation.
AI.motivation: SFDA methods that rely on local neighborhood prediction often overemphasize prediction similarity, leading to rapid forgetting of source knowledge and vulnerability to local noise. There is a need for a calibration mechanism that preserves source information while enabling robust domain adaptation.
AI.method: ProCal introduces a probability calibration mechanism via a dual-model collaborative prediction. It blends the source model's initial predictions with the target model's current outputs to calibrate neighborhood probabilities, mitigating local noise interference while preserving discriminative source information. It optimizes a joint objective consisting of a soft supervision loss and a diversity loss, and provides theoretical analysis showing convergence to an equilibrium where source knowledge and target information are effectively fused.
AI.result: Empirical validation on 31 cross-domain tasks across four public datasets demonstrates the effectiveness of ProCal in balancing knowledge retention and domain adaptation. The authors also provide code at the linked GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: ProCal offers a principled approach to SFDA by calibrating neighborhood-based predictions through dual-model collaboration, achieving an equilibrium between source knowledge and target information, reducing forgetting and overfitting, with strong empirical support across multiple tasks and datasets; code is publicly available.

title: Neural Galerkin Normalizing Flow for Transition Probability Density Functions of Diffusion Models
summary: We propose a new Neural Galerkin Normalizing Flow framework to approximate the transition probability density function of a diffusion process by solving the corresponding Fokker-Planck equation with an atomic initial distribution, parametrically with respect to the location of the initial mass. By using Normalizing Flows, we look for the solution as a transformation of the transition probability density function of a reference stochastic process, ensuring that our approximation is structure-preserving and automatically satisfies positivity and mass conservation constraints. By extending Neural Galerkin schemes to the context of Normalizing Flows, we derive a system of ODEs for the time evolution of the Normalizing Flow's parameters. Adaptive sampling routines are used to evaluate the Fokker-Planck residual in meaningful locations, which is of vital importance to address high-dimensional PDEs. Numerical results show that this strategy captures key features of the true solution and enforces the causal relationship between the initial datum and the density function at subsequent times. After completing an offline training phase, online evaluation becomes significantly more cost-effective than solving the PDE from scratch. The proposed method serves as a promising surrogate model, which could be deployed in many-query problems associated with stochastic differential equations, like Bayesian inference, simulation, and diffusion bridge generation.

AI.tldr: Neural Galerkin Normalizing Flows to solve Fokker–Planck PDE with atomic initial mass, yielding a structure-preserving, efficient surrogate for diffusion processes via an NF-based parametric solution.
AI.motivation: Address high-dimensional Fokker–Planck equations with an atomic initial distribution while preserving positivity and mass, and enable efficient many-query evaluations through offline training.
AI.method: Extend Neural Galerkin to Normalizing Flows by modeling the solution as a pushforward of a reference process' density; derive ODEs for Normalizing Flow parameters; use adaptive sampling to compute the Fokker–Planck residual; offline training followed by fast online evaluation.
AI.result: The approach captures key features of the true solution, enforces causal relations from initial data, and reduces online computation; acts as a promising surrogate for SDE-related tasks (Bayesian inference, diffusion bridges).
AI.conclusion: A promising, structure-preserving surrogate framework for high-dimensional Fokker–Planck problems, enabling efficient many-query SDE applications while maintaining positivity and mass conservation.

title: SEAR: Simple and Efficient Adaptation of Visual Geometric Transformers for RGB+Thermal 3D Reconstruction
summary: Foundational feed-forward visual geometry models enable accurate and efficient camera pose estimation and scene reconstruction by learning strong scene priors from massive RGB datasets. However, their effectiveness drops when applied to mixed sensing modalities, such as RGB-thermal (RGB-T) images. We observe that while a visual geometry grounded transformer pretrained on RGB data generalizes well to thermal-only reconstruction, it struggles to align RGB and thermal modalities when processed jointly. To address this, we propose SEAR, a simple yet efficient fine-tuning strategy that adapts a pretrained geometry transformer to multimodal RGB-T inputs. Despite being trained on a relatively small RGB-T dataset, our approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods for 3D reconstruction and camera pose estimation, achieving significant improvements over all metrics (e.g., over 29\% in AUC@30) and delivering higher detail and consistency between modalities with negligible overhead in inference time compared to the original pretrained model. Notably, SEAR enables reliable multimodal pose estimation and reconstruction even under challenging conditions, such as low lighting and dense smoke. We validate our architecture through extensive ablation studies, demonstrating how the model aligns both modalities. Additionally, we introduce a new dataset featuring RGB and thermal sequences captured at different times, viewpoints, and illumination conditions, providing a robust benchmark for future work in multimodal 3D scene reconstruction. Code and models are publicly available at https://www.github.com/Schindler-EPFL-Lab/SEAR.

AI.tldr: SEAR fine-tunes a pretrained visual-geometry transformer for multimodal RGB-T inputs, yielding strong gains in 3D reconstruction and camera pose estimation with minimal inference overhead, and introducing a new RGB-T dataset for robust multimodal benchmarking.
AI.motivation: To overcome the deterioration of RGB-pretrained geometry models when handling RGB-T data due to misalignment between modalities; enable accurate multimodal pose estimation and reconstruction with limited RGB-T data.
AI.method: A simple yet efficient fine-tuning strategy (SEAR) that adapts a pretrained geometry transformer to multimodal RGB-T inputs, presumably via lightweight adapters, alignment objectives, or joint optimization to align RGB and thermal modalities.
AI.result: Significant performance gains over state-of-the-art across 3D reconstruction and camera pose estimation tasks, e.g., >29% improvement in AUC@30, improved detail and inter-modal consistency, with negligible inference overhead relative to the original pretrained model; robust under low-light and smoky conditions; extensive ablations support modality alignment.
AI.conclusion: SEAR enables reliable multimodal pose estimation and reconstruction with efficient adaptation of pretrained geometry transformers; it is validated on a small RGB-T dataset and complemented by a new RGB-T benchmark dataset; code and models are publicly available.

title: An Optimised Greedy-Weighted Ensemble Framework for Financial Loan Default Prediction
summary: Accurate prediction of loan defaults is a central challenge in credit risk management, particularly in modern financial datasets characterised by nonlinear relationships, class imbalance, and evolving borrower behaviour. Traditional statistical models and static ensemble methods often struggle to maintain reliable performance under such conditions. This study proposes an Optimised Greedy-Weighted Ensemble framework for loan default prediction that dynamically allocates model weights based on empirical predictive performance. The framework integrates multiple machine learning classifiers, with their hyperparameters first optimised using Particle Swarm Optimisation. Model predictions are then combined via a regularised greedy weighting mechanism. At the same time, a neural-network-based meta-learner is employed within stacked-ensemble to capture higher-order relationships among model outputs. Experiments conducted on the Lending Club dataset demonstrate that the proposed framework improves predictive performance compared with individual classifiers. The BlendNet ensemble achieved the strongest results with an AUC of 0.80, a macro-average F1-score of 0.73, and a default recall of 0.81. Calibration analysis further shows that tree-based ensembles such as Extra Trees and Gradient Boosting provide the most reliable probability estimates, while the stacked ensemble offers superior ranking capability. Feature analysis using Recursive Feature Elimination identifies revolving utilisation, annual income, and debt-to-income ratio as the most influential predictors of loan default. These findings demonstrate that performance-driven ensemble weighting can improve both predictive accuracy and interpretability in credit risk modelling. The proposed framework provides a scalable data-driven approach to support institutional credit assessment, risk monitoring, and financial decision-making.

AI.tldr: An Optimised Greedy-Weighted Ensemble (BlendNet) framework uses PSO-tuned hyperparameters, greedy weight optimization, and a neural-net meta-learner to predict loan defaults, achieving AUC 0.80, macro-F1 0.73, and recall 0.81 on Lending Club data; calibration favors tree ensembles and stacked ensembles enhance ranking; key predictors include revolving utilization, income, and debt-to-income ratio.
AI.motivation: To address nonlinearities, class imbalance, and shifting borrower behavior in credit risk, overcoming limitations of static models and simple ensembles by dynamically optimizing model weights and incorporating a neural meta-learner for higher-order relationships.
AI.method: 1) Train multiple classifiers with hyperparameters optimized via Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO). 2) Combine predictions with a regularised greedy weighting scheme to form an optimized ensemble (BlendNet). 3) Use a neural-network-based meta-learner within a stacked ensemble to capture higher-order relationships among model outputs. 4) Evaluate on Lending Club dataset using AUC, macro-F1, and default recall; perform calibration analysis; conduct Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE) for feature importance.
AI.result: BlendNet achieved AUC 0.80, macro-average F1-score 0.73, and default recall 0.81. Calibration analysis indicates tree-based ensembles (Extra Trees, Gradient Boosting) provide reliable probability estimates; stacked ensemble yields superior ranking. RFE identifies revolving utilisation, annual income, and debt-to-income ratio as top predictors. Overall, the framework improves predictive performance and interpretability relative to individual classifiers.
AI.conclusion: Performance-driven, optimised weighting in ensembles coupled with a neural meta-learner can enhance both predictive accuracy and interpretability in credit risk modeling, offering a scalable, data-driven approach for institutional credit assessment and risk management.

title: Points-to-3D: Structure-Aware 3D Generation with Point Cloud Priors
summary: Recent progress in 3D generation has been driven largely by models conditioned on images or text, while readily available 3D priors are still underused. In many real-world scenarios, the visible-region point cloud are easy to obtain from active sensors such as LiDAR or from feed-forward predictors like VGGT, offering explicit geometric constraints that current methods fail to exploit. In this work, we introduce Points-to-3D, a diffusion-based framework that leverages point cloud priors for geometry-controllable 3D asset and scene generation. Built on a latent 3D diffusion model TRELLIS, Points-to-3D first replaces pure-noise sparse structure latent initialization with a point cloud priors tailored input formulation.A structure inpainting network, trained within the TRELLIS framework on task-specific data designed to learn global structural inpainting, is then used for inference with a staged sampling strategy (structural inpainting followed by boundary refinement), completing the global geometry while preserving the visible regions of the input priors.In practice, Points-to-3D can take either accurate point-cloud priors or VGGT-estimated point clouds from single images as input. Experiments on both objects and scene scenarios consistently demonstrate superior performance over state-of-the-art baselines in terms of rendering quality and geometric fidelity, highlighting the effectiveness of explicitly embedding point-cloud priors for achieving more accurate and structurally controllable 3D generation.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based 3D generation using point-cloud priors to guide geometry via TRELLIS latent diffusion and a structure inpainting network, enabling geometry-controllable object/scene generation with either accurate or VGGT-estimated priors.
AI.motivation: To leverage readily available point-cloud priors (from LiDAR or VGGT) to impose explicit geometric constraints and improve fidelity/controllability in 3D generation, addressing underutilization of priors in image/text-conditioned approaches.
AI.method: Integrates point-cloud priors into a latent 3D diffusion model (TRELLIS) by replacing pure-noise latent initialization with prior-informed inputs. Introduces a structure inpainting network trained within TRELLIS for global structural inpainting and uses a staged sampling strategy (structural inpainting followed by boundary refinement) to complete global geometry while preserving input priors.
AI.result: Experiments on objects and scenes show superior rendering quality and geometric fidelity over state-of-the-art baselines, demonstrating the effectiveness of embedding point-cloud priors for controllable 3D generation.
AI.conclusion: Point-cloud priors can be effectively incorporated into diffusion-based 3D generation to enhance structural fidelity and controllability; the proposed inpainting and staged sampling yield robust geometry completion while preserving visible priors and work with both accurate and VGGT-derived priors.

title: Context Bootstrapped Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) suffers from exploration inefficiency, where models struggle to generate successful rollouts, resulting in minimal learning signal. This challenge is particularly severe for tasks that require the acquisition of novel reasoning patterns or domain-specific knowledge. To address this, we propose Context Bootstrapped Reinforcement Learning (CBRL), which augments RLVR training by stochastically prepending few-shot demonstrations to training prompts. The injection probability follows a curriculum that starts high to bootstrap early exploration, then anneals to zero so the model must ultimately succeed without assistance. This forces the policy to internalize reasoning patterns from the demonstrations rather than relying on them at test time. We validate CBRL across two model families and five Reasoning Gym tasks. Our results demonstrate that CBRL consistently improves success rate, provides better exploration efficiency, and is algorithm-agnostic. We further demonstrate CBRL's practical applicability on Q, a domain-specific programming language that diverges significantly from mainstream language conventions.

AI.tldr: Context Bootstrapped RL (CBRL) augments RL from Verifiable Rewards by injecting few-shot demonstrations into training prompts with a curriculum on their use, improving exploration efficiency and performance across models and reasoning tasks while reducing dependence on demonstrations at test time.
AI.motivation: RLVR suffers exploration inefficiency, especially for tasks requiring novel reasoning patterns or domain knowledge. Existing approaches struggle to generate useful rollouts and learn effectively.
AI.method: CBRL prepends stochastic few-shot demonstrations to training prompts. The injection probability follows a curriculum: high initially to bootstrap exploration, then gradually anneals to zero so the model must perform without demonstrations. Evaluated across two model families and five Reasoning Gym tasks, plus a domain-specific language Q.
AI.result: CBRL consistently improves success rates and exploration efficiency, and is algorithm-agnostic. It demonstrates practical applicability on Q, a non-standard programming language.
AI.conclusion: CBRL mitigates exploration inefficiency in RLVR by forcing the model to internalize reasoning patterns from demonstrations and then operate without them at test time, yielding robust gains across models, tasks, and even domain-specific languages.

title: Rethinking Uncertainty Quantification and Entanglement in Image Segmentation
summary: Uncertainty quantification (UQ) is crucial in safety-critical applications such as medical image segmentation. Total uncertainty is typically decomposed into data-related aleatoric uncertainty (AU) and model-related epistemic uncertainty (EU). Many methods exist for modeling AU (such as Probabilistic UNet, Diffusion) and EU (such as ensembles, MC Dropout), but it is unclear how they interact when combined. Additionally, recent work has revealed substantial entanglement between AU and EU, undermining the interpretability and practical usefulness of the decomposition. We present a comprehensive empirical study covering a broad range of AU-EU model combinations, propose a metric to quantify uncertainty entanglement, and evaluate both across downstream UQ tasks. For out-of-distribution detection, ensembles exhibit consistently lower entanglement and superior performance. For ambiguity modeling and calibration the best models are dataset-dependent, with softmax/SSN-based methods performing well and Probabilistic UNets being less entangled. A softmax ensemble fares remarkably well on all tasks. Finally, we analyze potential sources of uncertainty entanglement and outline directions for mitigating this effect.

AI.tldr: A large empirical study characterizing the interaction between aleatoric (AU) and epistemic (EU) uncertainty in medical image segmentation, introducing a quantitative entanglement metric, and evaluating AU-EU model combos across downstream UQ tasks. Ensembles tend to reduce entanglement in OOD detection; performance on ambiguity modeling and calibration is dataset-dependent, with softmax/SSN methods performing well and Probabilistic UNets less entangled; a softmax ensemble shows strong, robust performance across tasks; the work also analyzes sources of entanglement and suggests mitigation directions.
AI.motivation: Unclear interactions between data-driven (AU) and model-driven (EU) uncertainty when combined, and substantial entanglement that harms interpretability and practical usefulness of uncertainty decompositions. A comprehensive empirical evaluation and a reliable entanglement metric are needed to guide model design and evaluation in safety-critical settings.
AI.method: Systematic empirical study across a broad suite of AU-EU combinations (e.g., Probabilistic UNet, diffusion-based AU; ensembles, MC Dropout, softmax/SSN, etc.). Propose a metric to quantify uncertainty entanglement. Evaluate performance on downstream UQ tasks: out-of-distribution detection, ambiguity modeling, and calibration. Compare across model families (ensembles, Probabilistic UNets, diffusion, softmax-based methods) and analyze sources of entanglement.
AI.result: Ensembles exhibit consistently lower uncertainty entanglement and superior OOD detection performance. For ambiguity modeling and calibration, the best models are dataset-dependent, with softmax/SSN-based methods performing well and Probabilistic UNets being less entangled. A softmax ensemble performs strongly across all tasks.
AI.conclusion: Identify potential sources of AU-EU entanglement and outline mitigation directions. Recommend using ensembles (notably softmax ensembles) as a robust baseline and call for further research to reduce entanglement via model design and training strategies.

title: Perceptio: Perception Enhanced Vision Language Models via Spatial Token Generation
summary: Large Vision Language Models (LVLMs) excel at semantic understanding but struggle with fine grained spatial grounding, as the model must implicitly infer complex geometry without ever producing a spatial interpretation. We present Perceptio, a perception enhanced LVLM with 2D and 3D spatial reasoning abilities, enabled via explicit semantic segmentation tokens and depth tokens generated directly within the autoregressive sequence. Concretely, we (i) distill a VQVAE depth codebook from a strong monocular teacher to tokenize dense depth into compact sequences, and (ii) integrate SAM2 based semantic segmentation tokens and VQ-VAE depth tokens inside the LLM so the model first emits spatial tokens and then answers. To stabilize depth token generation, we introduce novel composite depth-token objectives (marker, token, and count losses) and a soft-merging technique for differentiable reconstruction. We adopt a multi-task co-training strategy across diverse datasets, letting the model learn perception tokens to tackle multiple downstream tasks. Building on InternVL, Perceptio achieves state-of-the-art performance across benchmarks: improving referring expression segmentation by +0.8/+1.4/+1.1 cIoU on RefCOCO/+/g HardBLINK spatial understanding accuracy by 10.3%, and MMBench accuracy by 1.0%, demonstrating that explicit spatial chain-of-thought materially strengthens spatial grounding in LVLMs.

AI.tldr: Perceptio is a perception-enhanced LVLM that injects explicit 2D/3D spatial reasoning into the autoregressive sequence via segmentation and depth tokens, learned through a VQVAE depth codebook distillation and SAM2-based tokens, with new depth-token objectives and soft-merging. It achieves state-of-the-art on multiple spatial grounding benchmarks.
AI.motivation: LVLMs excel at semantic understanding but struggle with fine-grained spatial grounding due to implicit geometry; introducing explicit spatial tokens can strengthen spatial reasoning within the autoregressive generation.
AI.method: (i) Distill a VQVAE depth codebook from a strong monocular teacher to tokenize dense depth; (ii) integrate SAM2-based semantic segmentation tokens and VQ-VAE depth tokens inside the LLM so the model emits spatial tokens before answering; (iii) stabilize depth-token generation with composite depth-token objectives (marker, token, count losses) and a soft-merging differentiable reconstruction; (iv) adopt multi-task co-training across diverse datasets to learn perception tokens; (v) build on InternVL as the base model.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art: referring expression segmentation improves by +0.8, +1.4, +1.1 cIoU on RefCOCO, RefCOCO+, RefCOCOg; HardBLINK spatial understanding accuracy up by 10.3%; MMBench accuracy up by 1.0%.
AI.conclusion: Explicit spatial chain-of-thought via perception tokens strengthens spatial grounding in LVLMs. Tokenized depth and segmentation integrated into autoregressive generation enable robust 2D/3D reasoning, validated by multi-task co-training on diverse data.

title: BVSIMC: Bayesian Variable Selection-Guided Inductive Matrix Completion for Improved and Interpretable Drug Discovery
summary: Recent advances in drug discovery have demonstrated that incorporating side information (e.g., chemical properties about drugs and genomic information about diseases) often greatly improves prediction performance. However, these side features can vary widely in relevance and are often noisy and high-dimensional. We propose Bayesian Variable Selection-Guided Inductive Matrix Completion (BVSIMC), a new Bayesian model that enables variable selection from side features in drug discovery. By learning sparse latent embeddings, BVSIMC improves both predictive accuracy and interpretability.
