We validate our method through simulation studies and two drug discovery applications: 1) prediction of drug resistance in Mycobacterium tuberculosis, and 2) prediction of new drug-disease associations in computational drug repositioning. On both synthetic and real data, BVSIMC outperforms several other state-of-the-art methods in terms of prediction. In our two real examples, BVSIMC further reveals the most clinically meaningful side features.

AI.tldr: Bayesian variable selection-guided inductive matrix completion (BVSIMC) learns sparse latent embeddings from side information to improve prediction and interpretability in drug discovery; validated on TB drug resistance and drug–disease repositioning; outperforms state-of-the-art methods and identifies meaningful side features.
AI.motivation: Side information on drugs and diseases is often high-dimensional, noisy, and variably relevant. Effective variable selection is needed to improve predictive performance and interpretability in drug discovery.
AI.method: Introduce BVSIMC, a Bayesian model that performs variable selection on side features while learning sparse latent embeddings via inductive matrix completion. It integrates side information into a matrix factorization framework with a sparsity-inducing prior to select relevant features.
AI.result: BVSIMC outperforms several state-of-the-art methods on both synthetic and real data. It yields improved predictive accuracy and, in real applications, highlights clinically meaningful side features for drug resistance prediction in Mycobacterium tuberculosis and for drug-disease association discovery.
AI.conclusion: BVSIMC provides improved prediction and interpretability by selecting relevant side features and learning sparse latent representations, with demonstrated utility in drug resistance prediction and computational drug repositioning.

title: VesselTok: Tokenizing Vessel-like 3D Biomedical Graph Representations for Reconstruction and Generation
summary: Spatial graphs provide a lightweight and elegant representation of curvilinear anatomical structures such as blood vessels, lung airways, and neuronal networks. Accurately modeling these graphs is crucial in clinical and (bio-)medical research. However, the high spatial resolution of large networks drastically increases their complexity, resulting in significant computational challenges. In this work, we aim to tackle these challenges by proposing VesselTok, a framework that approaches spatially dense graphs from a parametric shape perspective to learn latent representations (tokens). VesselTok leverages centerline points with a pseudo radius to effectively encode tubular geometry. Specifically, we learn a novel latent representation conditioned on centerline points to encode neural implicit representations of vessel-like, tubular structures. We demonstrate VesselTok's performance across diverse anatomies, including lung airways, lung vessels, and brain vessels, highlighting its ability to robustly encode complex topologies. To prove the effectiveness of VesselTok's learnt latent representations, we show that they (i) generalize to unseen anatomies, (ii) support generative modeling of plausible anatomical graphs, and (iii) transfer effectively to downstream inverse problems, such as link prediction.

AI.tldr: VesselTok introduces a centerline-conditioned latent token framework to encode dense vascular graphs via tubular geometry, enabling generalization, generative modeling, and transfer to inverse problems across lung and brain vessels.
AI.motivation: High-resolution curvilinear anatomical graphs are computationally challenging; a compact, geometry-preserving representation is needed to enable analysis tasks like link prediction and graph generation.
AI.method: Learn latent tokens conditioned on centerline points with a pseudo radius to encode tubular geometry as neural implicit representations; apply to lung airways, lung vessels, and brain vessels; assess generalization to unseen anatomies and transfer to inverse problems.
AI.result: Robust latent encoding of complex topologies; tokens generalize to unseen anatomies; enable plausible generative modeling of anatomical graphs; transferable to link prediction and other downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: VesselTok provides a scalable, geometry-aware latent representation for dense tubular graphs, enabling generalization, generation, and downstream task transfer across diverse anatomies.

title: Maximum-Entropy Exploration with Future State-Action Visitation Measures
comment: arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2412.06655
summary: Maximum entropy reinforcement learning motivates agents to explore states and actions to maximize the entropy of some distribution, typically by providing additional intrinsic rewards proportional to that entropy function. In this paper, we study intrinsic rewards proportional to the entropy of the discounted distribution of state-action features visited during future time steps. This approach is motivated by two results. First, we show that the expected sum of these intrinsic rewards is a lower bound on the entropy of the discounted distribution of state-action features visited in trajectories starting from the initial states, which we relate to an alternative maximum entropy objective. Second, we show that the distribution used in the intrinsic reward definition is the fixed point of a contraction operator and can therefore be estimated off-policy. Experiments highlight that the new objective leads to improved visitation of features within individual trajectories, in exchange for slightly reduced visitation of features in expectation over different trajectories, as suggested by the lower bound. It also leads to improved convergence speed for learning exploration-only agents. Control performance remains similar across most methods on the considered benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Entropy-based intrinsic rewards over discounted feature visitation provide an off-policy estimable objective that bounds trajectory entropy, boosting intra-trajectory exploration with comparable control performance.
AI.motivation: To enhance exploration by rewarding the entropy of the discounted distribution of future state-action features, linking to a tractable, off-policy estimable objective and a theoretical lower bound on trajectory entropy.
AI.method: Define intrinsic rewards proportional to the entropy of the discounted distribution of state-action features visited in future steps; prove that the expected sum of these rewards is a lower bound on the trajectory-feature entropy; show that the discounted-feature distribution is the fixed point of a contraction operator enabling off-policy estimation.
AI.result: Empirical results show improved visitation of features within individual trajectories (intra-trajectory coverage) at the expense of reduced feature visitation on average across trajectories; faster convergence for exploration-only agents; control performance remains similar across methods on benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed entropy-based intrinsic reward shapes exploration with a theoretically grounded, off-policy estimable objective, offering faster intra-trajectory exploration and stable performance without degrading control outcomes on tested benchmarks.

title: Statistical Characteristic-Guided Denoising for Rapid High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy Imaging
summary: High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) enables atomic-scale observation of nucleation dynamics, which boosts the studies of advanced solid materials. Nonetheless, due to the millisecond-scale rapid change of nucleation, it requires short-exposure rapid imaging, leading to severe noise that obscures atomic positions. In this work, we propose a statistical characteristic-guided denoising network, which utilizes statistical characteristics to guide the denoising process in both spatial and frequency domains. In the spatial domain, we present spatial deviation-guided weighting to select appropriate convolution operations for each spatial position based on deviation characteristic. In the frequency domain, we present frequency band-guided weighting to enhance signals and suppress noise based on band characteristics. We also develop an HRTEM-specific noise calibration method and generate a dataset with disordered structures and realistic HRTEM image noises. It can ensure the denoising performance of models on real images for nucleation observation. Experiments on synthetic and real data show our method outperforms the state-of-the-art methods in HRTEM image denoising, with effectiveness in the localization downstream task. Code will be available at https://github.com/HeasonLee/SCGN.

AI.tldr: A statistical characteristic-guided denoising network (SCGN) for high-speed HRTEM aims to preserve atomic positions in noisy, short-exposure frames by combining spatial deviation-guided convolution selection with frequency band-guided weighting, plus HRTEM-specific noise calibration and a realistic disordered-structure dataset; it outperforms existing denoisers and benefits downstream localization tasks.
AI.motivation: Mitigate severe noise in short-exposure HRTEM images to observe rapid nucleation dynamics while preserving atomic-scale details essential for localization tasks.
AI.method: SCGN uses two key guiding mechanisms: (1) spatial deviation-guided weighting to select convolution operations per pixel based on local deviation characteristics, and (2) frequency band-guided weighting to emphasize signal-bearing frequency bands and suppress noise. It includes an HRTEM-specific noise calibration procedure and a dataset creation pipeline with disordered structures and realistic HRTEM noise to train/evaluate the model.
AI.result: The approach surpasses state-of-the-art denoisers on both synthetic and real HRTEM data and improves performance on downstream localization tasks related to nucleation observation.
AI.conclusion: Statistical characteristic-guided denoising, leveraging both spatial and frequency-domain guidance, plus hardware-aware noise calibration and realistic data, yields superior HRTEM denoising and supports more reliable nucleation analyses.

title: Best-of-Both-Worlds Multi-Dueling Bandits: Unified Algorithms for Stochastic and Adversarial Preferences under Condorcet and Borda Objectives
summary: Multi-dueling bandits, where a learner selects $m \geq 2$ arms per round and observes only the winner, arise naturally in many applications including ranking and recommendation systems, yet a fundamental question has remained open: can a single algorithm perform optimally in both stochastic and adversarial environments, without knowing which regime it faces? We answer this affirmatively, providing the first best-of-both-worlds algorithms for multi-dueling bandits under both Condorcet and Borda objectives. For the Condorcet setting, we propose \texttt{MetaDueling}, a black-box reduction that converts any dueling bandit algorithm into a multi-dueling bandit algorithm by transforming multi-way winner feedback into an unbiased pairwise signal. Instantiating our reduction with \texttt{Versatile-DB} yields the first best-of-both-worlds algorithm for multi-dueling bandits: it achieves $O(\sqrt{KT})$ pseudo-regret against adversarial preferences and the instance-optimal $O\!\left(\sum_{i \neq a^\star} \frac{\log T}{Δ_i}\right)$ pseudo-regret under stochastic preferences, both simultaneously and without prior knowledge of the regime. For the Borda setting, we propose \AlgBorda, a stochastic-and-adversarial algorithm that achieves $O\left(K^2 \log KT + K \log^2 T + \sum_{i: Δ_i^{\mathrm{B}} > 0} \frac{K\log KT}{(Δ_i^{\mathrm{B}})^2}\right)$ regret in stochastic environments and $O\left(K \sqrt{T \log KT} + K^{1/3} T^{2/3} (\log K)^{1/3}\right)$ regret against adversaries, again without prior knowledge of the regime. We complement our upper bounds with matching lower bounds for the Condorcet setting. For the Borda setting, our upper bounds are near-optimal with respect to the lower bounds (within a factor of $K$) and match the best-known results in the literature.

AI.tldr: Best-of-both-worlds algorithms for multi-dueling bandits under Condorcet and Borda, with regime-free guarantees and matching lower bounds in Condorcet.
AI.motivation: In ranking/recommendation settings, learners select m≥2 arms and observe only the winner; robust algorithms are needed that perform well in both stochastic and adversarial environments without prior regime knowledge.
AI.method: Condorcet: MetaDueling black-box reduction to convert a dueling-bandit algorithm into a multi-dueling-bandit algorithm by turning multi-way winner feedback into unbiased pairwise signals; instantiated with Versatile-DB to obtain a best-of-both-worlds algorithm with O(√(KT)) adversarial regret and instance-optimal stochastic regret O(∑_{i≠a*} log T / Δ_i). Borda: AlgBorda, a stochastic-and-adversarial algorithm achieving specified regret bounds in stochastic and adversarial settings without regime knowledge.
AI.result: Condorcet: matching upper and lower bounds; adversarial regret O(√(KT)) and stochastic regret O(∑ log T / Δ_i) (instance-optimal). Borda: upper bounds O(K^2 log KT + K log^2 T + ∑_{Δ_i^B>0} K log KT /(Δ_i^B)^2) in stochastic, and O(K√(T log KT) + K^{1/3} T^{2/3} (log K)^{1/3}) against adversaries; lower bounds shown for Condorcet; Borda bounds near-optimal (within a factor K).
AI.conclusion: First best-of-both-worlds results for multi-dueling bandits under both Condorcet and Borda; confirms regime-free optimality in Condorcet with matching lower bounds; Borda bounds are near-optimal and competitive with the literature.

title: Towards Interpretable Foundation Models for Retinal Fundus Images
comment: 11 pages, 3 figures, 2 tables, submitted to MICCAI 2026
summary: Foundation models are used to extract transferable representations from large amounts of unlabeled data, typically via self-supervised learning (SSL). However, many of these models rely on architectures that offer limited interpretability, which is a critical issue in high-stakes domains such as medical imaging. We propose Dual-IFM, a foundation model that is interpretable-by-design in two ways: First, it provides local interpretability for individual images through class evidence maps that are faithful to the decision-making process. Second, it provides global interpretability for entire datasets through a 2D projection layer that allows for direct visualization of the model's representation space. We trained our model on over 800,000 color fundus photography from various sources to learn generalizable, interpretable representations for different downstream tasks. Our results show that our model reaches a performance range similar to that of state-of-the-art foundation models with up to $16\times$ the number of parameters, while providing interpretable predictions on out-of-distribution data. Our results suggest that large-scale SSL pretraining paired with inherent interpretability can lead to robust representations for retinal imaging.

AI.tldr: Dual-IFM is an interpretable foundation model for retinal imaging that combines local and global interpretability with competitive performance, trained on ~800k fundus images.
AI.motivation: Interpretability is crucial in high-stakes medical domains; many foundation models offer limited interpretability, hindering trust and deployment.
AI.method: An interpretable-by-design foundation model (Dual-IFM) providing (1) local, faithful class evidence maps for individual images and (2) a global 2D projection layer for visualizing the model's representation space, trained via self-supervised learning on >800k color fundus photographs to learn generalizable representations for downstream tasks.
AI.result: Achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art foundation models with up to 16x more parameters, while offering interpretable predictions on out-of-distribution data.
AI.conclusion: Large-scale SSL pretraining coupled with inherent interpretability yields robust, transferable representations for retinal imaging.

title: Book your room in the Turing Hotel! A symmetric and distributed Turing Test with multiple AIs and humans
summary: In this paper, we report our experience with ``TuringHotel'', a novel extension of the Turing Test based on interactions within mixed communities of Large Language Models (LLMs) and human participants. The classical one-to-one interaction of the Turing Test is reinterpreted in a group setting, where both human and artificial agents engage in time-bounded discussions and, interestingly, are both judges and respondents. This community is instantiated in the novel platform UNaIVERSE (https://unaiverse.io), creating a ``World'' which defines the roles and interaction dynamics, facilitated by the platform's built-in programming tools. All communication occurs over an authenticated peer-to-peer network, ensuring that no third parties can access the exchange. The platform also provides a unified interface for humans, accessible via both mobile devices and laptops, that was a key component of the experience in this paper. Results of our experimentation involving 17 human participants and 19 LLMs revealed that current models are still sometimes confused as humans. Interestingly, there are several unexpected mistakes, suggesting that human fingerprints are still identifiable but not fully unambiguous, despite the high-quality language skills of artificial participants. We argue that this is the first experiment conducted in such a distributed setting, and that similar initiatives could be of national interest to support ongoing experiments and competitions aimed at monitoring the evolution of large language models over time.

AI.tldr: Extends the Turing Test to a group setting with mixed human and LLM participants in a distributed platform, evaluating whether current models can be distinguished from humans in time-bounded, peer-to-peer interactions; reports mixed results and identifies implications for ongoing monitoring of LLM evolution.
AI.motivation: To explore how a collective of humans and LLMs interact in a distributed, interactive arena and to assess the detectability of artificial agents in group discussions, advancing beyond one-to-one Turing tests.
AI.method: Time-bounded discussions within a mixed community of 17 humans and 19 LLMs on the UNaIVERSE platform. The setup defines roles via a World, uses built-in programming tools, and operates over an authenticated peer-to-peer network. Accessible human interface on mobile and laptop.
AI.result: Found that LLMs can still be mistaken for humans in some cases; human fingerprints remain detectable but not universally unambiguous; several unexpected mistakes observed despite high-quality language capabilities.
AI.conclusion: This is the first distributed, group-based Turing Test experiment; results suggest potential value for national-interest initiatives in monitoring LLM evolution and support for future distributed experiments and competitions.

title: HORNet: Task-Guided Frame Selection for Video Question Answering with Vision-Language Models
summary: Video question answering (VQA) with vision-language models (VLMs) depends critically on which frames are selected from the input video, yet most systems rely on uniform or heuristic sampling that cannot be optimized for downstream answering quality. We introduce \textbf{HORNet}, a lightweight frame selection policy trained with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to learn which frames a frozen VLM needs to answer questions correctly. With fewer than 1M trainable parameters, HORNet reduces input frames by up to 99\% and VLM processing time by up to 93\%, while improving answer quality on short-form benchmarks (+1.7\% F1 on MSVD-QA) and achieving strong performance on temporal reasoning tasks (+7.3 points over uniform sampling on NExT-QA). We formalize this as Select Any Frames (SAF), a task that decouples visual input curation from VLM reasoning, and show that GRPO-trained selection generalizes better out-of-distribution than supervised and PPO alternatives. HORNet's policy further transfers across VLM answerers without retraining, yielding an additional 8.5\% relative gain when paired with a stronger model. Evaluated across six benchmarks spanning 341,877 QA pairs and 114.2 hours of video, our results demonstrate that optimizing \emph{what} a VLM sees is a practical and complementary alternative to optimizing what it generates while improving efficiency. Code is available at https://github.com/ostadabbas/HORNet.

AI.tldr: HORNet uses a lightweight frame-selection policy trained with GRPO to optimize which video frames a frozen VLM should see for VQA. It achieves dramatic frame and computation reductions (up to 99% fewer frames, up to 93% faster VLM) while improving QA performance on short-form and temporal-reasoning benchmarks. It introduces the Select Any Frames (SAF) paradigm and shows GRPO generalizes better out-of-distribution than supervised or PPO baselines, with cross-VLM transfer when paired with stronger models (+8.5% relative gain). Code is available.
AI.motivation: Frame sampling greatly affects VQA quality; uniform or heuristic sampling is suboptimal and task-optimized sampling is underexplored. Decoupling visual input curation from reasoning (SAF) and optimizing the input frames can yield efficiency gains without retraining the VLM.
AI.method: Propose HORNet, a lightweight policy (<1M parameters) trained with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to select frames. Operates under the Select Any Frames (SAF) setting, where frame curation is learned separately from VLM reasoning, and the policy transfers across VLM answerers without retraining.
AI.result: Efficiency: up to 99% frame reduction; VLM processing time reduced up to 93%. QA performance: +1.7% F1 on MSVD-QA; temporal reasoning: +7.3 points on NExT-QA. Generalization: GRPO-selected frames outperform supervised and PPO baselines on out-of-distribution data. Cross-model transfer: an 8.5% relative gain when paired with a stronger VLM. Evaluation spans six benchmarks with 341,877 QA pairs and 114.2 hours of video.
AI.conclusion: Optimizing what the VLM sees is a practical, complementary approach to optimizing what it generates. HORNet demonstrates efficient and robust frame curation with strong cross-model transferability, and the SAF/GRPO framework offers a generalizable path for input curation in VQA. Code is available.

title: Foundations of Schrödinger Bridges for Generative Modeling
summary: At the core of modern generative modeling frameworks, including diffusion models, score-based models, and flow matching, is the task of transforming a simple prior distribution into a complex target distribution through stochastic paths in probability space. Schrödinger bridges provide a unifying principle underlying these approaches, framing the problem as determining an optimal stochastic bridge between marginal distribution constraints with minimal-entropy deviations from a pre-defined reference process. This guide develops the mathematical foundations of the Schrödinger bridge problem, drawing on optimal transport, stochastic control, and path-space optimization, and focuses on its dynamic formulation with direct connections to modern generative modeling. We build a comprehensive toolkit for constructing Schrödinger bridges from first principles, and show how these constructions give rise to generalized and task-specific computational methods.

AI.tldr: Unifies modern generative modeling via Schrödinger bridges (entropic OT) to transform simple priors into complex targets along stochastic paths, providing a dynamic, principled framework and a toolkit for constructing bridges and deriving task-specific computational methods.
AI.motivation: Provide a rigorous, unifying theory that connects diffusion models, score-based methods, and flow matching through the Schrödinger bridge problem, enabling principled dynamic formulations and algorithms grounded in optimal transport and stochastic control.
AI.method: Develop the mathematical foundations of the Schrödinger bridge problem, situate it within optimal transport, stochastic control, and path-space optimization, emphasize the dynamic (time-continuous) formulation, and build a practical toolkit for constructing Schrödinger bridges from first principles to derive generalized and task-specific computational methods.
AI.result: A theoretical framework with a comprehensive toolkit for constructing Schrödinger bridges, yielding generalized and task-specific computational methods for generative modeling.
AI.conclusion: Schrödinger bridges offer a unifying dynamic formulation for modern generative modeling (diffusion, score-based, flow-matching) and motivate principled algorithmic developments; the work outlines foundations and tooling, paving the way for empirical validation and application across tasks.

title: Motion-o: Trajectory-Grounded Video Reasoning
summary: Recent research has made substantial progress on video reasoning, with many models leveraging spatio-temporal evidence chains to strengthen their inference capabilities. At the same time, a growing set of datasets and benchmarks now provides structured annotations designed to support and evaluate such reasoning. However, little attention has been paid to reasoning about \emph{how} objects move between observations: no prior work has articulated the motion patterns by connecting successive observations, leaving trajectory understanding implicit and difficult to verify. We formalize this missing capability as Spatial-Temporal-Trajectory (STT) reasoning and introduce \textbf{Motion-o}, a motion-centric video understanding extension to visual language models that makes trajectories explicit and verifiable. To enable motion reasoning, we also introduce a trajectory-grounding dataset artifact that expands sparse keyframe supervision via augmentation to yield denser bounding box tracks and a stronger trajectory-level training signal. Finally, we introduce Motion Chain of Thought (MCoT), a structured reasoning pathway that makes object trajectories through discrete \texttt{<motion/>} tag summarizing per-object direction, speed, and scale (of velocity) change to explicitly connect grounded observations into trajectories. To train Motion-o, we design a reward function that compels the model to reason directly over visual evidence, all while requiring no architectural modifications. Empirical results demonstrate that Motion-o improves spatial-temporal grounding and trajectory prediction while remaining fully compatible with existing frameworks, establishing motion reasoning as a critical extension for evidence-based video understanding. Code is available at https://github.com/ostadabbas/Motion-o.

AI.tldr: Motion-o introduces explicit spatial-temporal trajectory reasoning for video understanding, with a trajectory-grounding augmentation, a Motion Chain of Thought pathway, and a reward-based training scheme that preserves compatibility with existing models.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of explicit motion-trajectory reasoning across observations in video understanding, enabling verifiable trajectories and stronger trajectory-level supervision.
AI.method: Extend visual-language models with a motion-centric extension (Motion-o), augment data with trajectory-grounding annotations to produce denser bounding-box tracks, introduce Motion Chain of Thought with <motion/> tagging to summarize per-object trajectory changes, and train via a reward function that uses visual evidence without changing architecture.
AI.result: Improved spatial-temporal grounding and trajectory prediction; remains compatible with existing frameworks; code released.
AI.conclusion: Explicit motion reasoning is a critical extension for evidence-based video understanding and can be achieved with trajectory-grounding and a structured reasoning pathway without architectural changes.

title: AgentDS Technical Report: Benchmarking the Future of Human-AI Collaboration in Domain-Specific Data Science
summary: Data science plays a critical role in transforming complex data into actionable insights across numerous domains. Recent developments in large language models (LLMs) and artificial intelligence (AI) agents have significantly automated data science workflow. However, it remains unclear to what extent AI agents can match the performance of human experts on domain-specific data science tasks, and in which aspects human expertise continues to provide advantages. We introduce AgentDS, a benchmark and competition designed to evaluate both AI agents and human-AI collaboration performance in domain-specific data science. AgentDS consists of 17 challenges across six industries: commerce, food production, healthcare, insurance, manufacturing, and retail banking. We conducted an open competition involving 29 teams and 80 participants, enabling systematic comparison between human-AI collaborative approaches and AI-only baselines. Our results show that current AI agents struggle with domain-specific reasoning. AI-only baselines perform near or below the median of competition participants, while the strongest solutions arise from human-AI collaboration. These findings challenge the narrative of complete automation by AI and underscore the enduring importance of human expertise in data science, while illuminating directions for the next generation of AI. Visit the AgentDS website here: https://agentds.org/ and open source datasets here: https://huggingface.co/datasets/lainmn/AgentDS .

AI.tldr: AgentDS benchmark shows human-AI collaboration outperforms AI-only approaches in domain-specific data science tasks; AI agents currently struggle with domain-specific reasoning; human expertise remains valuable for achieving top performance.
AI.motivation: To systematically evaluate how AI agents perform on domain-specific data science tasks, identify where human expertise provides advantages, and establish a benchmark/competition for AI-only and human-AI collaboration approaches across industries.
AI.method: 17 challenges spanning commerce, food production, healthcare, insurance, manufacturing, and retail banking. An open competition involved 29 teams and 80 participants, comparing AI-only baselines against human-AI collaborative approaches using the AgentDS dataset and tasks (hosted at agentds.org and HuggingFace).
AI.result: AI agents lag in domain-specific reasoning; AI-only baselines perform near or below the competition median, while the strongest results come from human-AI collaboration.
AI.conclusion: Automation with AI alone is not yet at parity with human experts for domain-specific data science; leveraging human-AI collaboration yields the best performance and highlights directions for next-generation AI systems to better support domain tasks.

title: PromptHub: Enhancing Multi-Prompt Visual In-Context Learning with Locality-Aware Fusion, Concentration and Alignment
comment: Accepted to ICLR 2026. 17 pages, 11 figures, and 9 tables
summary: Visual In-Context Learning (VICL) aims to complete vision tasks by imitating pixel demonstrations. Recent work pioneered prompt fusion that combines the advantages of various demonstrations, which shows a promising way to extend VICL. Unfortunately, the patch-wise fusion framework and model-agnostic supervision hinder the exploitation of informative cues, thereby limiting performance gains. To overcome this deficiency, we introduce PromptHub, a framework that holistically strengthens multi-prompting through locality-aware fusion, concentration and alignment. PromptHub exploits spatial priors to capture richer contextual information, employs complementary concentration, alignment, and prediction objectives to mutually guide training, and incorporates data augmentation to further reinforce supervision. Extensive experiments on three fundamental vision tasks demonstrate the superiority of PromptHub. Moreover, we validate its universality, transferability, and robustness across out-of-distribution settings, and various retrieval scenarios. This work establishes a reliable locality-aware paradigm for prompt fusion, moving beyond prior patch-wise approaches. Code is available at https://github.com/luotc-why/ICLR26-PromptHub.

AI.tldr: PromptHub introduces locality-aware multi-prompt fusion for Visual In-Context Learning to outperform patch-wise fusion, improving accuracy, generalization, and robustness across vision tasks.
AI.motivation: Patch-wise fusion and model-agnostic supervision in VICL fail to fully exploit informative cues and spatial context; there is a need for locality-aware fusion to leverage spatial priors and richer cues.
AI.method: PromptHub implements locality-aware fusion for multi-prompting, leveraging spatial priors to capture richer context; it uses complementary concentration, alignment, and prediction objectives to guide training and incorporates data augmentation.
AI.result: Prompts-based fusion with locality-awareness yields superior performance over patch-wise approaches on three fundamental vision tasks, with demonstrated universality, transferability, and robustness in out-of-distribution and retrieval settings; code released.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a locality-aware paradigm for prompt fusion in VICL, moving beyond patch-wise approaches and enabling more robust, generalizable visual in-context learning.

title: When Differential Privacy Meets Wireless Federated Learning: An Improved Analysis for Privacy and Convergence
summary: Differentially private wireless federated learning (DPWFL) is a promising framework for protecting sensitive user data. However, foundational questions on how to precisely characterize privacy loss remain open, and existing work is further limited by convergence analyses that rely on restrictive convexity assumptions or ignore the effect of gradient clipping. To overcome these issues, we present a comprehensive analysis of privacy and convergence for DPWFL with general smooth non-convex loss objectives. Our analysis explicitly incorporates both device selection and mini-batch sampling, and shows that the privacy loss can converge to a constant rather than diverge with the number of iterations. Moreover, we establish convergence guarantees with gradient clipping and derive an explicit privacy-utility trade-off. Numerical results validate our theoretical findings.

AI.tldr: Provides a comprehensive non-convex DP-WFL analysis accounting for device selection, mini-batch sampling, and gradient clipping; shows privacy loss remains bounded (converges to a constant) and gives convergence guarantees with an explicit privacy-utility trade-off, validated numerically.
AI.motivation: To address open questions on precise privacy loss characterization in DP-WFL for general smooth non-convex objectives and to overcome convexity assumptions and ignoring gradient clipping in prior work.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of DPWFL with general smooth non-convex losses, incorporating device selection and mini-batch sampling, and including gradient clipping; derives bounded privacy loss and convergence guarantees; establishes explicit privacy-utility trade-off; supports findings with numerical experiments.
AI.result: Privacy loss converges to a constant rather than diverging with iterations; convergence guarantees established under gradient clipping; explicit privacy-utility trade-off derived; numerical results validate theory.
AI.conclusion: DPWFL with non-convex objectives can achieve stable privacy loss and provable convergence under realistic settings; results inform design choices on device sampling and clipping to balance privacy and utility; numerical validation corroborates theory.

title: MultihopSpatial: Multi-hop Compositional Spatial Reasoning Benchmark for Vision-Language Model
summary: Spatial reasoning is foundational for Vision-Language Models (VLMs), particularly when deployed as Vision-Language-Action (VLA) agents in physical environments. However, existing benchmarks predominantly focus on elementary, single-hop relations, neglecting the multi-hop compositional reasoning and precise visual grounding essential for real-world scenarios. To address this, we introduce MultihopSpatial, offering three key contributions: (1) A comprehensive benchmark designed for multi-hop and compositional spatial reasoning, featuring 1- to 3-hop complex queries across diverse spatial perspectives. (2) Acc@50IoU, a complementary metric that simultaneously evaluates reasoning and visual grounding by requiring both answer selection and precise bounding box prediction - capabilities vital for robust VLA deployment. (3) MultihopSpatial-Train, a dedicated large-scale training corpus to foster spatial intelligence. Extensive evaluation of 37 state-of-the-art VLMs yields eight key insights, revealing that compositional spatial reasoning remains a formidable challenge. Finally, we demonstrate that reinforcement learning post-training on our corpus enhances both intrinsic VLM spatial reasoning and downstream embodied manipulation performance.

AI.tldr: MultihopSpatial introduces a multi-hop spatial reasoning benchmark with an Acc@50IoU metric and a training corpus to enhance VLM grounding and RL-based performance; evaluation across 37 models reveals persistent challenges in compositional spatial reasoning, with RL post-training improving both intrinsic reasoning and embodied manipulation.
AI.motivation: Current vision-language benchmarks emphasize simple, single-hop spatial relations and lack precise grounding and multi-hop compositional reasoning necessary for real-world vision-language-action (VLA) tasks.
AI.method: 1) MultihopSpatial benchmark with 1–3-hop queries from diverse spatial perspectives; 2) Acc@50IoU metric that enforces both correct answers and accurate bounding boxes for grounding; 3) MultihopSpatial-Train, a large-scale training corpus. Evaluation conducted on 37 state-of-the-art VLMs across eight insights; reinforcement learning post-training on the corpus to boost spatial reasoning and downstream embodied tasks.
AI.result: Findings indicate that compositional spatial reasoning remains a major challenge across current VLMs. The authors report eight key insights and show that RL post-training yields improvements in intrinsic spatial reasoning capabilities and downstream embodied manipulation performance.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark suite, together with the grounding-aware Acc@50IoU metric and the training corpus, provides a foundation for advancing spatial reasoning in VLMs for VLA settings; RL fine-tuning on the corpus is a promising direction for enhancing both perception-grounding and practical manipulation abilities.

title: Communication-Efficient and Robust Multi-Modal Federated Learning via Latent-Space Consensus
comment: Accepted for publication in IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
summary: Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative model training across distributed devices without sharing raw data, but applying FL to multi-modal settings introduces significant challenges. Clients typically possess heterogeneous modalities and model architectures, making it difficult to align feature spaces efficiently while preserving privacy and minimizing communication costs. To address this, we introduce CoMFed, a Communication-Efficient Multi-Modal Federated Learning framework that uses learnable projection matrices to generate compressed latent representations. A latent-space regularizer aligns these representations across clients, improving cross-modal consistency and robustness to outliers. Experiments on human activity recognition benchmarks show that CoMFed achieves competitive accuracy with minimal overhead.

AI.tldr: CoMFed provides a communication-efficient multi-modal FL framework using learnable projection matrices and a latent-space regularizer to align compressed latent representations across clients, achieving competitive HAR accuracy with low overhead.
AI.motivation: Heterogeneous modalities and model architectures in federated learning cause misalignment of feature spaces and increased communication; need privacy-preserving, efficient cross-client alignment.
AI.method: Introduce learnable projection matrices to generate compressed latent representations; apply a latent-space regularizer to align representations across clients, promoting cross-modal consistency and robustness to outliers.
AI.result: On human activity recognition benchmarks, CoMFed achieves competitive accuracy with minimal communication and computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: Latent-space alignment via learnable projections is effective for communication-efficient multi-modal FL and can tolerate outliers, as demonstrated on HAR benchmarks.

title: Translating MRI to PET through Conditional Diffusion Models with Enhanced Pathology Awareness
summary: Positron emission tomography (PET) is a widely recognized technique for diagnosing neurodegenerative diseases, offering critical functional insights. However, its high costs and radiation exposure hinder its widespread use. In contrast, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) does not involve such limitations. While MRI also detects neurodegenerative changes, it is less sensitive for diagnosis compared to PET. To overcome such limitations, one approach is to generate synthetic PET from MRI. Recent advances in generative models have paved the way for cross-modality medical image translation; however, existing methods largely emphasize structural preservation while neglecting the critical need for pathology awareness. To address this gap, we propose PASTA, a novel image translation framework built on conditional diffusion models with enhanced pathology awareness. PASTA surpasses state-of-the-art methods by preserving both structural and pathological details through its highly interactive dual-arm architecture and multi-modal condition integration. Additionally, we introduce a novel cycle exchange consistency and volumetric generation strategy that significantly enhances PASTA's ability to produce high-quality 3D PET images. Our qualitative and quantitative results demonstrate the high quality and pathology awareness of the synthesized PET scans. For Alzheimer's diagnosis, the performance of these synthesized scans improves over MRI by 4%, almost reaching the performance of actual PET. Our code is available at https://github.com/ai-med/PASTA.

AI.tldr: PASTA uses conditional diffusion models to convert MRI into high-quality, pathology-aware synthetic PET, featuring a dual-arm architecture, cycle exchange consistency, and 3D volumetric generation; it outperforms state-of-the-art methods and narrows the gap to real PET in Alzheimer's diagnosis, with open-source code.
AI.motivation: The routine use of PET is limited by cost and radiation exposure, while MRI is safer but less sensitive diagnostically. Cross-modality translation aims to deliver PET-like diagnostic information from MRI, requiring preservation of both anatomy and disease-relevant pathology.
AI.method: PASTA is a conditional diffusion-based translation framework with a dual-arm interactive architecture and multi-modal conditioning, emphasizing pathology awareness. It introduces cycle exchange consistency and a volumetric generation strategy to produce high-quality 3D PET, enabling MRI-to-PET synthesis with preserved structural and pathological details.
AI.result: Qualitative and quantitative evaluations show high-quality PET synthesis with preserved pathology. In Alzheimer's diagnosis, synthetic-PET from MRI improves performance by ~4% over MRI alone, approaching the accuracy of real PET. The authors provide an open-source implementation.
AI.conclusion: PASTA demonstrates that pathology-aware, diffusion-based cross-modality translation can closely approximate PET-level diagnostic signals from MRI, potentially reducing reliance on PET. The approach’s cycle consistency and 3D generation contribute to stronger clinical fidelity, with practical availability via code release.

title: Position: Spectral GNNs Are Neither Spectral Nor Superior for Node Classification
summary: Spectral Graph Neural Networks (Spectral GNNs) for node classification promise frequency-domain filtering on graphs, yet rest on flawed foundations. Recent work shows that graph Laplacian eigenvectors do not in general have the key properties of a true Fourier basis, but leaves the empirical success of Spectral GNNs unexplained. We identify two theoretical glitches: (1) commonly used "graph Fourier bases" are not classical Fourier bases for graph signals; (2) (n-1)-degree polynomials (n = number of nodes) can exactly interpolate any spectral response via a Vandermonde system, so the usual "polynomial approximation" narrative is not theoretically justified. The effectiveness of GCN is commonly attributed to spectral low-pass filtering, yet we prove that low- and high-pass behaviors arise solely from message-passing dynamics rather than Graph Fourier Transform-based spectral formulations. We then analyze two representative directed spectral models, MagNet and HoloNet. Their reported effectiveness is not spectral: it arises from implementation issues that reduce them to powerful MPNNs. When implemented consistently with the claimed spectral algorithms, performance becomes weak. This position paper argues that: for node classification, Spectral GNNs neither meaningfully capture the graph spectrum nor reliably improve performance; competitive results are better explained by their equivalence to MPNNs, sometimes aided by implementations inconsistent with their intended design.

AI.tldr: Spectral GNNs do not properly capture graph spectra; their empirical performance largely derives from MPNN-like dynamics and implementation artifacts.
AI.motivation: Critically assess foundational claims of Spectral GNNs for node classification; explain why spectral interpretations may be invalid and what drives reported performance.
AI.method: Theoretical critique identifying two glitches: (i) graph Fourier bases are not true Fourier bases; (ii) (n-1)-degree Vandermonde interpolation undermines polynomial-approximation narrative; show low/high-pass arise from message passing, not spectrum; examine MagNet and HoloNet and implementation inconsistencies; compare to consistent spectral implementations.
AI.result: Shows that spectral formulations do not meaningfully align with the graph spectrum; in practice, spectral models reduce to standard MPNNs or underperform when implementation adheres to claimed spectral design; empirical success largely explained by dynamics/implementation rather than spectrum.
AI.conclusion: For node classification, Spectral GNNs are not advantageous; focus should be on message-passing frameworks and avoid overreliance on spectral interpretations; reported gains come from MPNN equivalence or experimental artifacts.

title: GHOST: Fast Category-agnostic Hand-Object Interaction Reconstruction from RGB Videos using Gaussian Splatting
summary: Understanding realistic hand-object interactions from monocular RGB videos is essential for AR/VR, robotics, and embodied AI. Existing methods rely on category-specific templates or heavy computation, yet still produce physically inconsistent hand-object alignment in 3D. We introduce GHOST (Gaussian Hand-Object Splatting), a fast, category-agnostic framework for reconstructing dynamic hand-object interactions using 2D Gaussian Splatting. GHOST represents both hands and objects as dense, view-consistent Gaussian discs and introduces three key innovations: (1) a geometric-prior retrieval and consistency loss that completes occluded object regions, (2) a grasp-aware alignment that refines hand translations and object scale to ensure realistic contact, and (3) a hand-aware background loss that prevents penalizing hand-occluded object regions. GHOST achieves complete, physically consistent, and animatable reconstructions from a single RGB video while running an order of magnitude faster than prior category-agnostic methods. Extensive experiments on ARCTIC, HO3D, and in-the-wild datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art accuracy in 3D reconstruction and 2D rendering quality, establishing GHOST as an efficient and robust solution for realistic hand-object interaction modeling. Code is available at https://github.com/ATAboukhadra/GHOST.

AI.tldr: GHOST is a fast, category-agnostic method for reconstructing dynamic hand-object interactions from monocular RGB using 2D Gaussian Splatting, featuring geometric-prior retrieval, grasp-aware alignment, and hand-aware background loss.
AI.motivation: Realistic 3D hand-object reconstruction from monocular RGB is essential for AR/VR, robotics, and embodied AI, but existing methods rely on category-specific templates or heavy computation and often yield physically inconsistent hand-object contact. A fast, category-agnostic approach with robust occlusion handling is needed.
AI.method: Represent hands and objects as dense, view-consistent Gaussian discs via 2D Gaussian Splatting. Introduce three innovations: (1) geometric-prior retrieval with a consistency loss to complete occluded object regions; (2) grasp-aware alignment refining hand translations and object scale to enforce realistic contact; (3) a hand-aware background loss to avoid penalizing hand-occluded object regions. Outputs: complete, physically consistent, animatable 3D reconstructions from a single RGB video, with faster inference than prior category-agnostic methods.
AI.result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art accuracy in 3D reconstruction and 2D rendering on ARCTIC, HO3D, and in-the-wild datasets; achieves an order-of-magnitude faster runtime than prior category-agnostic methods. Code available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: GHOST offers an efficient, robust solution for realistic hand-object interaction modeling, enabling complete, physically plausible, and animatable reconstructions from monocular RGB videos for applications in AR/VR, robotics, and embodied AI.

title: On Optimizing Multimodal Jailbreaks for Spoken Language Models
summary: As Spoken Language Models (SLMs) integrate speech and text modalities, they inherit the safety vulnerabilities of their LLM backbone and an expanded attack surface. SLMs have been previously shown to be susceptible to jailbreaking, where adversarial prompts induce harmful responses. Yet existing attacks largely remain unimodal, optimizing either text or audio in isolation. We explore gradient-based multimodal jailbreaks by introducing JAMA (Joint Audio-text Multimodal Attack), a joint multimodal optimization framework combining Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) for text and Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) for audio, to simultaneously perturb both modalities. Evaluations across four state-of-the-art SLMs and four audio types demonstrate that JAMA surpasses unimodal jailbreak rate by 1.5x to 10x. We analyze the operational dynamics of this joint attack and show that a sequential approximation method makes it 4x to 6x faster. Our findings suggest that unimodal safety is insufficient for robust SLMs. The code and data are available at https://repos.lsv.uni-saarland.de/akrishnan/multimodal-jailbreak-slm

AI.tldr: JAMA introduces a joint gradient-based attack that perturbs both text and audio to jailbreak Spoken Language Models, outperforming unimodal attacks by 1.5x–10x; a sequential approximation speeds it up by 4x–6x; code/data are released.
AI.motivation: SLMs inherit LLM safety vulnerabilities and have an expanded attack surface; existing jailbreaking methods are primarily unimodal (text or audio). There is a need to test robustness against coordinated multimodal perturbations.
AI.method: Joint Audio-text Multimodal Attack (JAMA) combines Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) for text perturbations and Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) for audio perturbations, optimizing both modalities simultaneously; evaluated across four SLMs and four audio types; includes a sequential approximation to speed up optimization.
AI.result: JAMA achieves higher jailbreak success rates than unimodal attacks by 1.5x to 10x across tested models/audio types; the sequential approximation makes the attack 4x–6x faster; four SLMs and four audio types used in evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Unimodal safety is insufficient for robust SLMs; defenses must account for cross-modal vulnerabilities. The work provides open data/code to facilitate multimodal robustness research.

title: Unsupervised Contrastive Learning for Efficient and Robust Spectral Shape Matching
summary: Estimating correspondences between pairs of non-rigid deformable 3D shapes remains a significant challenge in computer vision and graphics. While deep functional map methods have become the go-to solution for addressing this problem, they primarily focus on optimizing pointwise and functional maps either individually or jointly, rather than directly enhancing feature representations in the embedding space, which often results in inadequate feature quality and suboptimal matching performance. Furthermore, these approaches heavily rely on traditional functional map techniques, such as time-consuming functional map solvers, which incur substantial computational costs. In this work, we introduce, for the first time, a novel unsupervised contrastive learning-based approach for efficient and robust 3D shape matching. We begin by presenting an unsupervised contrastive learning framework that promotes feature learning by maximizing consistency within positive similarity pairs and minimizing it within negative similarity pairs, thereby improving both the consistency and discriminability of the learned features.We then design a significantly simplified functional map learning architecture that eliminates the need for computationally expensive functional map solvers and multiple auxiliary functional map losses, greatly enhancing computational efficiency. By integrating these two components into a unified two-branch pipeline, our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in both accuracy and efficiency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach is not only computationally efficient but also outperforms current state-of-the-art methods across various challenging benchmarks, including near-isometric, non-isometric, and topologically inconsistent scenarios, even surpassing supervised techniques.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised contrastive learning for 3D shape matching with a simplified functional map module, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy and efficiency, even surpassing supervised methods, across near-isometric, non-isometric, and topologically inconsistent shapes.
AI.motivation: Current deep functional map approaches either optimize pointwise/functional maps in isolation or jointly, but often produce suboptimal embeddings and incur high computational costs due to conventional functional map solvers. There is a need for robust, efficient embedding learning and a lighter-weight functional map pipeline for non-rigid shape matching.
AI.method: Proposes a two-branch architecture: (1) an unsupervised contrastive learning framework to learn discriminative, consistent embeddings by maximizing similarity within positive pairs and minimizing it within negatives; (2) a simplified functional map learning module that avoids heavy solvers and multiple auxiliary losses; integrates both into an end-to-end pipeline for efficient, robust matching.
AI.result: Empirically achieves state-of-the-art performance in accuracy and efficiency on benchmarks spanning near-isometric, non-isometric, and topologically inconsistent cases; outperforms existing methods, including some supervised approaches.
AI.conclusion: This work introduces the first unsupervised contrastive learning approach for efficient and robust 3D shape matching, coupled with a streamlined functional map component, yielding superior performance and computational efficiency in challenging scenarios.

title: VGGT-360: Geometry-Consistent Zero-Shot Panoramic Depth Estimation
summary: This paper presents VGGT-360, a novel training-free framework for zero-shot, geometry-consistent panoramic depth estimation. Unlike prior view-independent training-free approaches, VGGT-360 reformulates the task as panoramic reprojection over multi-view reconstructed 3D models by leveraging the intrinsic 3D consistency of VGGT-like foundation models, thereby unifying fragmented per-view reasoning into a coherent panoramic understanding. To achieve robust and accurate estimation, VGGT-360 integrates three plug-and-play modules that form a unified panorama-to-3D-to-depth framework: (i) Uncertainty-guided adaptive projection slices panoramas into perspective views to bridge the domain gap between panoramic inputs and VGGT's perspective prior. It estimates gradient-based uncertainty to allocate denser views to geometry-poor regions, yielding geometry-informative inputs for VGGT. (ii) Structure-saliency enhanced attention strengthens VGGT's robustness during 3D reconstruction by injecting structure-aware confidence into its attention layers, guiding focus toward geometrically reliable regions and enhancing cross-view coherence. (iii) Correlation-weighted 3D model correction refines the reconstructed 3D model by reweighting overlapping points using attention-inferred correlation scores, providing a consistent geometric basis for accurate panoramic reprojection. Extensive experiments show that VGGT-360 outperforms both trained and training-free state-of-the-art methods across multiple resolutions and diverse indoor and outdoor datasets.

AI.tldr: VGGT-360 is a training-free, zero-shot panorama depth estimator that unifies fragmented per-view reasoning via panoramic reprojection on multi-view 3D reconstructions, powered by three plug-and-play modules for uncertainty-guided perspective sampling, structure-aware attention, and correlation-weighted 3D correction, achieving state-of-the-art results across diverse datasets.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of training-free, geometry-consistent panoramic depth estimation and to bridge the gap between panoramic inputs and perspective priors used by VGGT-like foundation models, by converting panoramic information into a coherent, cross-view 3D-consistent framework without training.
AI.method: Introduce a panorama-to-3D-to-depth framework with three plug-and-play modules: (i) Uncertainty-guided adaptive projection slices that partition panoramas into perspective views, using gradient-based uncertainty to allocate denser sampling in geometry-poor regions; (ii) Structure-saliency enhanced attention that injects structure-aware confidence into VGGT-like attention during 3D reconstruction to improve cross-view coherence; (iii) Correlation-weighted 3D model correction that reweights overlapping points by attention-derived correlation scores to refine the 3D model for accurate reprojection.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show VGGT-360 outperforming both trained and training-free SOTA methods across multiple resolutions and diverse indoor/outdoor datasets.
AI.conclusion: A robust, training-free panorama depth estimation framework that fuses panoramic reprojection with 3D reconstruction, leveraging three plug-ins to improve uncertainty handling, structural attention, and geometric consistency, yielding superior cross-domain performance.

title: Adaptive Regime-Aware Stock Price Prediction Using Autoencoder-Gated Dual Node Transformers with Reinforcement Learning Control
comment: Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems. 17 pages, 9 figures, 10 tables
summary: Stock markets exhibit regime-dependent behavior where prediction models optimized for stable conditions often fail during volatile periods. Existing approaches typically treat all market states uniformly or require manual regime labeling, which is expensive and quickly becomes stale as market dynamics evolve. This paper introduces an adaptive prediction framework that adaptively identifies deviations from normal market conditions and routes data through specialized prediction pathways. The architecture consists of three components: (1) an autoencoder trained on normal market conditions that identifies anomalous regimes through reconstruction error, (2) dual node transformer networks specialized for stable and event-driven market conditions respectively, and (3) a Soft Actor-Critic reinforcement learning controller that adaptively tunes the regime detection threshold and pathway blending weights based on prediction performance feedback. The reinforcement learning component enables the system to learn adaptive regime boundaries, defining anomalies as market states where standard prediction approaches fail. Experiments on 20 S&P 500 stocks spanning 1982 to 2025 demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves 0.68% MAPE for one-day predictions without the reinforcement controller and 0.59% MAPE with the full adaptive system, compared to 0.80% for the baseline integrated node transformer. Directional accuracy reaches 72% with the complete framework. The system maintains robust performance during high-volatility periods, with MAPE below 0.85% when baseline models exceed 1.5%. Ablation studies confirm that each component contributes meaningfully: autoencoder routing accounts for 36% relative MAPE degradation upon removal, followed by the SAC controller at 15% and the dual-path architecture at 7%.

AI.tldr: An adaptive regime-aware stock market predictor using an autoencoder-based anomaly detector, dual-path transformer networks for stable vs. event-driven regimes, and a Soft Actor-Critic controller to adapt thresholds and routing; shows improved one-day MAPE and directional accuracy over baselines with significant ablations.
AI.motivation: Address regime-dependent prediction performance and the need for manual regime labeling. Develop an automatic, adaptive system that detects deviations from normal market conditions and routes data through specialized predictors to maintain accuracy during volatility.
AI.method: Three-part architecture: (1) an autoencoder trained on normal market data to identify anomalous regimes via reconstruction error, (2) dual-node transformer networks specialized for stable and event-driven conditions, and (3) a Soft Actor-Critic reinforcement learning controller that adaptively tunes the anomaly-detection threshold and pathway blending weights based on prediction performance feedback.
AI.result: On 20 S&P 500 stocks spanning 1982–2025, one-day predictions yield 0.68% MAPE without reinforcement learning, and 0.59% MAPE with the full adaptive system, versus 0.80% for a baseline integrated node transformer. Directional accuracy ~72%. Robustness during high-volatility periods (MAPE < 0.85% when baselines exceed 1.5%). Ablation: autoencoder routing accounts for ~36% relative MAPE degradation when removed, SAC controller ~15%, dual-path ~7%.
AI.conclusion: The proposed adaptive framework improves prediction accuracy and robustness by automatically detecting regime shifts and routing data to specialized predictors, with the RL controller enabling dynamic boundary and routing adaptation. The ablations confirm each component’s contribution and suggest scalability to other markets or asset classes.

title: CRAFT: Aligning Diffusion Models with Fine-Tuning Is Easier Than You Think
summary: Aligning Diffusion models has achieved remarkable breakthroughs in generating high-quality, human preference-aligned images. Existing techniques, such as supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and DPO-style preference optimization, have become principled tools for fine-tuning diffusion models. However, SFT relies on high-quality images that are costly to obtain, while DPO-style methods depend on large-scale preference datasets, which are often inconsistent in quality. Beyond data dependency, these methods are further constrained by computational inefficiency. To address these two challenges, we propose Composite Reward Assisted Fine-Tuning (CRAFT), a lightweight yet powerful fine-tuning paradigm that requires significantly reduced training data while maintaining computational efficiency. It first leverages a Composite Reward Filtering (CRF) technique to construct a high-quality and consistent training dataset and then perform an enhanced variant of SFT. We also theoretically prove that CRAFT actually optimizes the lower bound of group-based reinforcement learning, establishing a principled connection between SFT with selected data and reinforcement learning. Our extensive empirical results demonstrate that CRAFT with only 100 samples can easily outperform recent SOTA preference optimization methods with thousands of preference-paired samples. Moreover, CRAFT can even achieve 11-220$\times$ faster convergences than the baseline preference optimization methods, highlighting its extremely high efficiency.

AI.tldr: CRAFT is a data- and compute-efficient diffusion-model fine-tuning framework using Composite Reward Filtering to curate high-quality data, paired with an enhanced SFT, achieving strong alignment with vastly fewer samples and much faster convergence.
AI.motivation: Address the data bottlenecks of supervised fine-tuning (requirement of costly high-quality images) and the data-demand of DPO-style preference optimization, while also reducing computational overhead in alignment of diffusion models.
AI.method: Introduce Composite Reward Filtering (CRF) to build a high-quality, consistent training dataset, then apply an enhanced variant of supervised fine-tuning (SFT). The authors also prove that CRAFT optimizes a lower bound of group-based reinforcement learning, linking selected-data SFT to RL theory.
AI.result: Empirically, CRAFT with only 100 samples outperforms recent SOTA preference optimization methods that use thousands of preference-paired samples, and achieves 11–220× faster convergence than baseline preference methods.
AI.conclusion: CRAFT provides a principled, highly data- and compute-efficient pathway for aligning diffusion models, connecting data-selected SFT with RL theory and enabling strong performance with minimal data and improved training speed.

title: Hierarchical Latent Structure Learning through Online Inference
summary: Learning systems must balance generalization across experiences with discrimination of task-relevant details. Effective learning therefore requires representations that support both. Online latent-cause models support incremental inference but assume flat partitions, whereas hierarchical Bayesian models capture multilevel structure but typically require offline inference. We introduce the Hierarchical Online Learning of Multiscale Experience Structure (HOLMES) model, a computational framework for hierarchical latent structure learning through online inference. HOLMES combines a variation on the nested Chinese Restaurant Process prior with sequential Monte Carlo inference to perform tractable trial-by-trial inference over hierarchical latent representations without explicit supervision over the latent structure. In simulations, HOLMES matched the predictive performance of flat models while learning more compact representations that supported one-shot transfer to higher-level latent categories. In a context-dependent task with nested temporal structure, HOLMES also improved outcome prediction relative to flat models. These results provide a tractable computational framework for discovering hierarchical structure in sequential data.

AI.tldr: HOLMES presents an online hierarchical latent-structure learner that uses a nested CRP prior with sequential Monte Carlo for tractable trial-by-trial inference, enabling online discovery of multiscale structure in sequential data.
AI.motivation: Current online latent-factor models assume flat partitions and struggle to generalize across tasks, while offline hierarchical Bayesian models capture structure but require batch inference. There is a need for online, scalable learning of hierarchical representations that balance generalization and discriminability.
AI.method: Introduce HOLMES, an online hierarchical model based on a variation of the nested Chinese Restaurant Process priors and sequential Monte Carlo inference to perform trial-by-trial inference over hierarchical latent representations without supervision.
AI.result: Holmes matches the predictive performance of flat models while learning more compact representations that enable one-shot transfer to higher-level latent categories; in a context-dependent nested temporal task, HOLMES improved outcome prediction relative to flat models.
AI.conclusion: HOLMES provides a tractable computational framework for discovering hierarchical structure in sequential data through online inference, enabling efficient learning of multiscale latent representations.

title: Unleashing the Power of Simplicity: A Minimalist Strategy for State-of-the-Art Fingerprint Enhancement
summary: Fingerprint recognition systems, which rely on the unique characteristics of human fingerprints, are essential in modern security and verification applications. Accurate minutiae extraction, a critical step in these systems, depends on the quality of fingerprint images. Despite recent improvements in fingerprint enhancement techniques, state-of-the-art methods often struggle with low-quality fingerprints and can be computationally demanding. This paper presents a minimalist approach to fingerprint enhancement, prioritizing simplicity and effectiveness. Two novel methods are introduced: a contextual filtering method and a learning-based method. These techniques consistently outperform complex state-of-the-art methods, producing clearer, more accurate, and less noisy images. The effectiveness of these methods is validated using a challenging latent fingerprint database. The open-source implementation of these techniques not only fosters reproducibility but also encourages further advancements in the field. The findings underscore the importance of simplicity in achieving high-quality fingerprint enhancement and suggest that future research should balance complexity and practical benefits.

AI.tldr: A minimalist, open-source framework for fingerprint enhancement using contextual filtering and a learning-based method that outperforms complex state-of-the-art methods on latent fingerprints.
AI.motivation: Low-quality fingerprint images hinder reliable minutiae extraction; current enhancement techniques can be computationally heavy. A simple, effective approach can improve performance and practicality.
AI.method: Introduce two methods: (1) a contextual filtering method leveraging local context to reduce noise while preserving ridge structure; (2) a learning-based enhancement method trained on fingerprint data for improved clarity. Both emphasize simplicity and efficiency and are released as open-source.
AI.result: On a challenging latent fingerprint database, the methods consistently outperformed complex state-of-the-art techniques, producing clearer, more accurate, and less noisy images. Open-source implementation promotes reproducibility and further research.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that simplicity can achieve high-quality fingerprint enhancement; future work should balance model complexity with practical benefits and encourage open collaboration.

title: SHAPCA: Consistent and Interpretable Explanations for Machine Learning Models on Spectroscopy Data
summary: In recent years, machine learning models have been increasingly applied to spectroscopic datasets for chemical and biomedical analysis. For their successful adoption, particularly in clinical and safety-critical settings, professionals and researchers must be able to understand and trust the reasoning behind model predictions. However, the inherently high dimensionality and strong collinearity of spectroscopy data pose a fundamental challenge to model explainability. These properties not only complicate model training but also undermine the stability and consistency of explanations, leading to fluctuations in feature importance across repeated training runs. Feature extraction techniques have been used to reduce the input dimensionality; these new features hinder the connection between the prediction and the original signal. This study proposes SHAPCA, an explainable machine learning pipeline that combines Principal Component Analysis (for dimensionality reduction) and Shapely Additive exPlanations (for post hoc explanation) to provide explanations in the original input space, which a practitioner can interpret and link back to the biological components. The proposed framework enables analysis from both global and local perspectives, revealing the spectral bands that drive overall model behaviour as well as the instance-specific features that influence individual predictions. Numerical analysis demonstrated the interpretability of the results and greater consistency across different runs.

AI.tldr: SHAPCA is an explainable ML pipeline that uses PCA for dimensionality reduction and SHAP values for post hoc explanations, returning insights in the original input space for spectroscopic data to support both global and local interpretability and to improve explanation stability across runs.
AI.motivation: Spectroscopy data are high-dimensional and highly collinear, which challenges model explainability and leads to unstable, inconsistent feature importance across repeated trainings; practitioners need explanations that map back to original spectral components.
AI.method: Integrates PCA with SHAP: PCA reduces dimensionality while preserving variance structure; SHAP provides post hoc feature attributions for predictions; explanations are projected back to the original spectral space to enable interpretation in terms of spectral bands; supports global (overall model behavior) and local (instance-specific) explanations.
AI.result: Numerical analysis shows interpretable explanations and greater consistency of attributions across different runs, indicating improved stability and usability of the explanations.
AI.conclusion: SHAPCA enables explainable ML for spectroscopy by grounding PCA-based explanations in the original signal, enhancing interpretability and trust in clinical/safety-critical applications.

title: Generalized Hand-Object Pose Estimation with Occlusion Awareness
summary: Generalized 3D hand-object pose estimation from a single RGB image remains challenging due to the large variations in object appearances and interaction patterns, especially under heavy occlusion. We propose GenHOI, a framework for generalized hand-object pose estimation with occlusion awareness. GenHOI integrates hierarchical semantic knowledge with hand priors to enhance model generalization under challenging occlusion conditions. Specifically, we introduce a hierarchical semantic prompt that encodes object states, hand configurations, and interaction patterns via textual descriptions. This enables the model to learn abstract high-level representations of hand-object interactions for generalization to unseen objects and novel interactions while compensating for missing or ambiguous visual cues. To enable robust occlusion reasoning, we adopt a multi-modal masked modeling strategy over RGB images, predicted point clouds, and textual descriptions. Moreover, we leverage hand priors as stable spatial references to extract implicit interaction constraints. This allows reliable pose inference even under significant variations in object shapes and interaction patterns. Extensive experiments on the challenging DexYCB and HO3Dv2 benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in hand-object pose estimation.

AI.tldr: GenHOI is a generalized hand–object pose estimation framework that uses hierarchical semantic prompts and multi-modal masked modeling to improve occlusion handling and generalization to unseen objects, achieving state-of-the-art results on DexYCB and HO3Dv2.
AI.motivation: Hand–object pose estimation from a single RGB image is challenged by large variations in object appearances, interaction patterns, and heavy occlusions, causing poor generalization to unseen objects and novel interactions.
AI.method: Introduce GenHOI with (1) a hierarchical semantic prompt encoding object states, hand configurations, and interaction patterns via textual descriptions; (2) a multi-modal masked modeling strategy over RGB images, predicted point clouds, and text; (3) hand priors as stable spatial references to impose implicit interaction constraints.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on DexYCB and HO3Dv2 demonstrate state-of-the-art performance in hand–object pose estimation, particularly under occlusion and for unseen objects.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical semantic prompts combined with multi-modal masking and hand priors enable robust occlusion-aware pose estimation and strong generalization to unseen objects and interactions.

title: Rethinking MLLM Itself as a Segmenter with a Single Segmentation Token
summary: Recent segmentation methods leveraging Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown reliable object-level segmentation and enhanced spatial perception. However, almost all previous methods predominantly rely on specialist mask decoders to interpret masks from generated segmentation-related embeddings and visual features, or incorporate multiple additional tokens to assist. This paper aims to investigate whether and how we can unlock segmentation from MLLM itSELF with 1 segmentation Embedding (SELF1E) while achieving competitive results, which eliminates the need for external decoders. To this end, our approach targets the fundamental limitation of resolution reduction in pixel-shuffled image features from MLLMs. First, we retain image features at their original uncompressed resolution, and refill them with residual features extracted from MLLM-processed compressed features, thereby improving feature precision. Subsequently, we integrate pixel-unshuffle operations on image features with and without LLM processing, respectively, to unleash the details of compressed features and amplify the residual features under uncompressed resolution, which further enhances the resolution of refilled features. Moreover, we redesign the attention mask with dual perception pathways, i.e., image-to-image and image-to-segmentation, enabling rich feature interaction between pixels and the segmentation token. Comprehensive experiments across multiple segmentation tasks validate that SELF1E achieves performance competitive with specialist mask decoder-based methods, demonstrating the feasibility of decoder-free segmentation in MLLMs. Project page: https://github.com/ANDYZAQ/SELF1E.

AI.tldr: Decoder-free segmentation for MLLMs using SELF1E: preserves original resolution, refills with residuals from compressed features, uses pixel-unshuffle, and dual-path attention to enable image and segmentation interaction; achieves competitive results without external decoders.
AI.motivation: Eliminate dependence on specialist mask decoders and high-level tokens, address resolution loss in pixel-shuffled MLLM features, and enable end-to-end segmentation within MLLMs.
AI.method: 1) Keep image features at original uncompressed resolution and refill with residuals from MLLM-processed compressed features. 2) Apply pixel-unshuffle to reveal compressed-detail features with/without LLM processing to enhance resolution. 3) Redesign attention mask with dual pathways (image-to-image and image-to-segmentation) for rich pixel–segmentation interaction.
