AI.result: Experiments across multiple segmentation tasks show SELF1E achieves competitive performance compared with decoder-based methods, validating the feasibility of decoder-free segmentation in MLLMs.
AI.conclusion: Decoder-free segmentation within MLLMs is feasible and competitive, potentially simplifying pipelines by removing external decoders; the approach hinges on improved feature resolution and dual-path attention to enable effective segmentation."

title: Rigorous Error Certification for Neural PDE Solvers: From Empirical Residuals to Solution Guarantees
summary:
Uncertainty quantification for partial differential equations is traditionally grounded in discretization theory, where solution error is controlled via mesh/grid refinement. Physics-informed neural networks fundamentally depart from this paradigm: they approximate solutions by minimizing residual losses at collocation points, introducing new sources of error arising from optimization, sampling, representation, and overfitting. As a result, the generalization error in the solution space remains an open problem.
  Our main theoretical contribution establishes generalization bounds that connect residual control to solution-space error. We prove that when neural approximations lie in a compact subset of the solution space, vanishing residual error guarantees convergence to the true solution. We derive deterministic and probabilistic convergence results and provide certified generalization bounds translating residual, boundary, and initial errors into explicit solution error guarantees.

AI.tldr: This work derives deterministic and probabilistic generalization bounds for physics-informed neural networks solving PDEs, linking residual control to the solution-space error. Under a compactness assumption, vanishing residual guarantees convergence to the true solution, with explicit bounds translating residual, boundary, and initial errors into solution error.
AI.motivation: PINNs replace discretization-based error control with residual minimization, introducing new sources of error (optimization, sampling, representation, overfitting) and leaving the resulting generalization error in solution space as an open problem.
AI.method: The authors develop theoretical generalization bounds by connecting residual control to solution-space error. They assume neural approximations lie in a compact subset of the solution space and derive both deterministic and probabilistic convergence results, culminating in certified generalization bounds that map residual, boundary, and initial errors to explicit solution error guarantees.
AI.result: Under the compactness assumption, vanishing residual error implies convergence to the true PDE solution. They provide deterministic and probabilistic convergence statements and explicit generalization bounds that quantify how residual, boundary, and initial errors bound the solution error.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a rigorous link between residual minimization in PINNs and solution accuracy, delivering certified guarantees for PDE UQ tasks and a framework for bounding solution error from residual-based and boundary/initial data.

title: SEM: Sparse Embedding Modulation for Post-Hoc Debiasing of Vision-Language Models
summary: Models that bridge vision and language, such as CLIP, are key components of multimodal AI, yet their large-scale, uncurated training data introduce severe social and spurious biases. Existing post-hoc debiasing methods often operate directly in the dense CLIP embedding space, where bias and task-relevant information are highly entangled. This entanglement limits their ability to remove bias without degrading semantic fidelity. In this work, we propose Sparse Embedding Modulation (SEM), a post-hoc, zero-shot debiasing framework that operates in a Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) latent space. By decomposing CLIP text embeddings into disentangled features, SEM identifies and modulates bias-relevant neurons while preserving query-relevant ones. This enables more precise, non-linear interventions. Across four benchmark datasets and two CLIP backbones, SEM achieves substantial fairness gains in retrieval and zero-shot classification. Our results demonstrate that sparse latent representations provide an effective foundation for post-hoc debiasing of vision-language models.

AI.tldr: Introduces Sparse Embedding Modulation (SEM) for post-hoc debiasing of CLIP by leveraging a Sparse Autoencoder latent space to disentangle bias from semantics, enabling targeted, non-linear edits and yielding fairness gains.
AI.motivation: Address biases in vision-language models trained on large, uncurated data; dense embedding debiasing entangles bias with semantic information, hindering effectiveness.
AI.method: Utilize a Sparse Autoencoder to obtain sparse latent representations of CLIP text embeddings, decompose into bias-relevant vs query-relevant features, identify bias-relevant neurons, and perform zero-shot, post-hoc modulation of those neurons (non-linear) to debias; evaluate on four benchmarks and two CLIP backbones.
AI.result: SEM yields substantial fairness improvements in retrieval and zero-shot classification across four datasets and two CLIP backbones; demonstrates the viability of sparse representations for debiasing.
AI.conclusion: Sparse latent representations provide a robust foundation for post-hoc debiasing of vision-language models; SEM shows that targeted, non-linear interventions in sparse latent space can reduce bias while preserving semantic fidelity.

title: DyMoE: Dynamic Expert Orchestration with Mixed-Precision Quantization for Efficient MoE Inference on Edge
summary: Despite the computational efficiency of MoE models, the excessive memory footprint and I/O overhead inherent in multi-expert architectures pose formidable challenges for real-time inference on resource-constrained edge platforms. While existing static methods struggle with a rigid latency-accuracy trade-off, we observe that expert importance is highly skewed and depth-dependent. Motivated by these insights, we propose DyMoE, a dynamic mixed-precision quantization framework designed for high-performance edge inference. Leveraging insights into expert importance skewness and depth-dependent sensitivity, DyMoE introduces: (1) importance-aware prioritization to dynamically quantize experts at runtime; (2) depth-adaptive scheduling to preserve semantic integrity in critical layers; and (3) look-ahead prefetching to overlap I/O stalls. Experimental results on commercial edge hardware show that DyMoE reduces Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) by 3.44x-22.7x and up to a 14.58x speedup in Time-Per-Output-Token (TPOT) compared to state-of-the-art offloading baselines, enabling real-time, accuracy-preserving MoE inference on resource-constrained edge devices.

AI.tldr: DyMoE is a dynamic mixed-precision quantization framework for MoE models that reduces memory and I/O overhead on edge devices by prioritizing important experts, adapting scheduling by depth, and prefetching to hide I/O stalls, achieving substantial TTFT and TPOT improvements without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.motivation: MoE models incur large memory usage and I/O bandwidth demands due to multiple experts and gating, making real-time edge inference difficult. Existing static quantization cannot flexibly trade latency and accuracy. Expert importance is skewed and depth-dependent, suggesting that dynamic, per-expert, and per-layer handling could improve efficiency.
AI.method: DyMoE introduces three components: (1) importance-aware prioritization that dynamically quantizes experts based on runtime importance; (2) depth-adaptive scheduling to preserve semantic integrity in critical layers by adjusting quantization or execution order with depth; (3) look-ahead prefetching to overlap I/O with computation and hide stalls, enabling smoother data flow for offloading scenarios.
AI.result: On commercial edge hardware, DyMoE achieves 3.44x–22.7x reduction in Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) and up to 14.58x speedup in Time-Per-Output-Token (TPOT) over state-of-the-art offloading baselines, enabling real-time, accuracy-preserving MoE inference on resource-constrained edges.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic mixed-precision via DyMoE effectively mitigates memory and I/O bottlenecks in edge MoE inference. By leveraging expert importance skew and depth sensitivity, it delivers substantial latency reductions and speedups while preserving accuracy, suitable for real-time edge deployment.

title: FUMO: Prior-Modulated Diffusion for Single Image Reflection Removal
summary: Single image reflection removal (SIRR) is challenging in real scenes, where reflection strength varies spatially and reflection patterns are tightly entangled with transmission structures. This paper presents a diffusion model with prior modulation framework (FUMO) that introduces explicit guidance signals to improve spatial controllability and structural faithfulness. Two priors are extracted directly from the mixed image, an intensity prior that estimates spatial reflection severity and a high-frequency prior that captures detail-sensitive responses via multi-scale residual aggregation. We propose a coarse-to-fine training paradigm. In the first stage, these cues are combined to gate the conditional residual injections, focusing the conditioning on regions that are both reflection-dominant and structure-sensitive. In the second stage, a fine-grained refinement network corrects local misalignment and sharpens fine details in the image space. Experiments conducted on both standard benchmarks and challenging images in the wild demonstrate competitive quantitative results and consistently improved perceptual quality. The code is released at https://github.com/Lucious-Desmon/FUMO.

AI.tldr: Proposes FUMO, a diffusion-model-based framework for single image reflection removal with prior modulation, using an intensity prior and a high-frequency prior, plus a coarse-to-fine training (two-stage) to improve spatial controllability and structural fidelity; achieves competitive results and improved perceptual quality; code released.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of spatially varying reflections and entangled reflection-transmission patterns in SIRR by injecting explicit priors into a diffusion model to guide conditioning and refinement.
AI.method: Extracts an intensity prior and a high-frequency prior from the mixed image; uses these priors to gate conditional residual injections in a two-stage training: coarse stage for region-focused conditioning; fine-stage with a refinement network for local misalignment and detail sharpening.
AI.result: Competitive quantitative performance on standard benchmarks and challenging real-world images; consistent improvements in perceptual quality.
AI.conclusion: Explicit guidance signals via priors within a diffusion framework enable better spatial controllability and structural fidelity in SIRR, with practical gains in perceptual quality.

title: SOL-ExecBench: Speed-of-Light Benchmarking for Real-World GPU Kernels Against Hardware Limits
summary: As agentic AI systems become increasingly capable of generating and optimizing GPU kernels, progress is constrained by benchmarks that reward speedup over software baselines rather than proximity to hardware-efficient execution. We present SOL-ExecBench, a benchmark of 235 CUDA kernel optimization problems extracted from 124 production and emerging AI models spanning language, diffusion, vision, audio, video, and hybrid architectures, targeting NVIDIA Blackwell GPUs. The benchmark covers forward and backward workloads across BF16, FP8, and NVFP4, including kernels whose best performance is expected to rely on Blackwell-specific capabilities. Unlike prior benchmarks that evaluate kernels primarily relative to software implementations, SOL-ExecBench measures performance against analytically derived Speed-of-Light (SOL) bounds computed by SOLAR, our pipeline for deriving hardware-grounded SOL bounds, yielding a fixed target for hardware-efficient optimization. We report a SOL Score that quantifies how much of the gap between a release-defined scoring baseline and the hardware SOL bound a candidate kernel closes. To support robust evaluation of agentic optimizers, we additionally provide a sandboxed harness with GPU clock locking, L2 cache clearing, isolated subprocess execution, and static analysis based checks against common reward-hacking strategies. SOL-ExecBench reframes GPU kernel benchmarking from beating a mutable software baseline to closing the remaining gap to hardware Speed-of-Light.

AI.tldr: Introduces SOL-ExecBench, a GPU kernel benchmark of 235 CUDA optimization problems targeting NVIDIA Blackwell, measuring progress against hardware-derived SOL bounds rather than software baselines, with a sandbox harness to prevent reward hacking.
AI.motivation: Addresses misalignment in GPU-optimization benchmarks that reward relative speedups over software baselines; aims to drive hardware-efficient kernel optimization by providing a fixed hardware-grounded bound (SOL) as the target.
AI.method: Assembles 235 CUDA kernel optimization problems from 124 production and emerging AI models across language, diffusion, vision, audio, video, and hybrid architectures, targeting NVIDIA Blackwell GPUs. Evaluates forward and backward workloads across BF16, FP8, NVFP4. Computes SOL bounds using SOLAR, a pipeline for deriving hardware-grounded Speed-of-Light limits. Introduces SOL Score, the metric quantifying closure of the gap to the SOL bound. Provides a sandboxed evaluation harness with GPU clock locking, L2 cache clearing, isolated subprocesses, and static-analysis-based reward-hacking checks.
AI.result: Conceptual framework and dataset with a hardware-grounded performance bound (SOL) and a corresponding SOL Score for benchmarking; includes preventative harness to mitigate reward hacking. The abstract does not report experimental results or numerical performance figures.
AI.conclusion: Shifts GPU kernel benchmarking from chasing a mutable software baseline to closing the remaining gap to hardware Speed-of-Light, fostering hardware-efficient optimization.

title: TerraScope: Pixel-Grounded Visual Reasoning for Earth Observation
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) have shown promise in earth observation (EO), yet they struggle with tasks that require grounding complex spatial reasoning in precise pixel-level visual representations. To address this problem, we introduce TerraScope, a unified VLM that delivers pixel-grounded geospatial reasoning with two key capabilities: (1) modality-flexible reasoning: it handles single-modality inputs (optical or SAR) and adaptively fuses different modalities into the reasoning process when both are available; (2) multi-temporal reasoning: it integrates temporal sequences for change analysis across multiple time points. In addition, we curate Terra-CoT, a large-scale dataset containing 1 million samples with pixel-level masks embedded in reasoning chains across multiple sources. We also propose TerraScope-Bench, the first benchmark for pixel-grounded geospatial reasoning with six sub-tasks that evaluates both answer accuracy and mask quality to ensure authentic pixel-grounded reasoning. Experiments show that TerraScope significantly outperforms existing VLMs on pixel-grounded geospatial reasoning while providing interpretable visual evidence.

AI.tldr: TerraScope offers a pixel-grounded, multimodal geospatial VLM with temporal reasoning, backed by Terra-CoT (1M masked reasoning samples) and TerraScope-Bench (six tasks) showing superior performance and interpretable visual evidence.
AI.motivation: Earth-observation VLMs struggle to ground complex spatial reasoning at pixel level and to integrate multiple modalities (optical/SAR) and time-series data for change analysis. A unified, pixel-grounded model with interpretable reasoning is needed for reliable EO tasks.
AI.method: TerraScope: a unified VLM enabling modality-flexible reasoning that adaptively fuses optical and SAR inputs when available, and integrates temporal sequences for change analysis. Terra-CoT: a large-scale dataset (1M samples) with pixel-level masks embedded in reasoning chains from multiple sources. TerraScope-Bench: a six-subtask benchmark evaluating both answer accuracy and mask quality to ensure authentic pixel-grounded reasoning.
AI.result: Experiments show TerraScope significantly outperforms existing VLMs on pixel-grounded geospatial reasoning and provides interpretable visual evidence.
AI.conclusion: TerraScope advances pixel-grounded geospatial reasoning by enabling pixel-level grounding, multimodal fusion, and temporal analysis, supported by a scalable dataset and a dedicated benchmark. Future work should address generalization, computational efficiency, and broader sensor modalities.

title: MIDST Challenge at SaTML 2025: Membership Inference over Diffusion-models-based Synthetic Tabular data
summary: Synthetic data is often perceived as a silver-bullet solution to data anonymization and privacy-preserving data publishing. Drawn from generative models like diffusion models, synthetic data is expected to preserve the statistical properties of the original dataset while remaining resilient to privacy attacks. Recent developments of diffusion models have been effective on a wide range of data types, but their privacy resilience, particularly for tabular formats, remains largely unexplored. MIDST challenge sought a quantitative evaluation of the privacy gain of synthetic tabular data generated by diffusion models, with a specific focus on its resistance to membership inference attacks (MIAs). Given the heterogeneity and complexity of tabular data, multiple target models were explored for MIAs, including diffusion models for single tables of mixed data types and multi-relational tables with interconnected constraints. MIDST inspired the development of novel black-box and white-box MIAs tailored to these target diffusion models as a key outcome, enabling a comprehensive evaluation of their privacy efficacy. The MIDST GitHub repository is available at https://github.com/VectorInstitute/MIDST

AI.tldr: MIDST evaluates the privacy of diffusion-model–generated synthetic tabular data against membership inference attacks (MIAs), introducing novel black-box and white-box MIAs for single-table and multi-relational tabular data and providing an evaluation framework and repository.
AI.motivation: Addresses the lack of quantitative assessment of privacy resilience for synthetic tabular data produced by diffusion models and the broader need to quantify privacy gains in data publishing.
AI.method: Analyze tabular data with multiple target MIAs tailored to diffusion-model outputs, covering both single-table mixed data types and multi-relational tables; develop black-box and white-box MIA variants; establish an evaluation framework through the MIDST challenge.
AI.result: Introduced novel MIAs tailored to diffusion-model tabular data and an evaluation framework; enabled comprehensive assessment of privacy efficacy; MIDST repository available for replication.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion-based synthetic tabular data’s privacy resilience is not yet settled; MIDST provides a toolkit and protocol for systematic privacy evaluation and highlights the need for continued development of targeted MIAs and privacy defenses.

title: Measuring 3D Spatial Geometric Consistency in Dynamic Generated Videos
summary: Recent generative models can produce high-fidelity videos, yet they often exhibit 3D spatial geometric inconsistencies. Existing evaluation methods fail to accurately characterize these inconsistencies: fidelity-centric metrics like FVD are insensitive to geometric distortions, while consistency-focused benchmarks often penalize valid foreground dynamics. To address this gap, we introduce SGC, a metric for evaluating 3D \textbf{S}patial \textbf{G}eometric \textbf{C}onsistency in dynamically generated videos. We quantify geometric consistency by measuring the divergence among multiple camera poses estimated from distinct local regions. Our approach first separates static from dynamic regions, then partitions the static background into spatially coherent sub-regions. We predict depth for each pixel, estimate a local camera pose for each subregion, and compute the divergence among these poses to quantify geometric consistency. Experiments on real and generative videos demonstrate that SGC robustly quantifies geometric inconsistencies, effectively identifying critical failures missed by existing metrics.

AI.tldr: SGC quantifies 3D spatial geometric consistency in dynamic videos by measuring pose divergence across region-specific depth-driven camera estimates, addressing gaps in fidelity- and consistency-based metrics.
AI.motivation: Current metrics miss 3D geometric distortions: fidelity-based metrics ignore geometry; consistency-based metrics may over-penalize valid foreground motion. A metric focused on 3D geometric consistency is needed for generative video models.
AI.method: Identify static vs dynamic regions, segment static background into spatially coherent sub-regions; predict depth per pixel; estimate local camera pose for each subregion; compute divergence among these poses to quantify geometric consistency.
AI.result: SGC robustly captures geometric inconsistencies in real and generative videos and reveals critical failures that existing metrics overlook.
AI.conclusion: SGC provides a practical, region-aware measure of 3D geometric consistency in dynamically generated videos, complementing existing fidelity and consistency metrics.

title: Improving RCT-Based Treatment Effect Estimation Under Covariate Mismatch via Calibrated Alignment
summary: Randomized controlled trials (RCTs) are the gold standard for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects, yet they are often underpowered for detecting effect heterogeneity. Large observational studies (OS) can supplement RCTs for conditional average treatment effect (CATE) estimation, but a key barrier is covariate mismatch: the two sources measure different, only partially overlapping, covariates. We propose CALM (Calibrated ALignment under covariate Mismatch), which bypasses imputation by learning embeddings that map each source's features into a common representation space. OS outcome models are transferred to the RCT embedding space and calibrated using trial data, preserving causal identification from randomization. Finite-sample risk bounds decompose into alignment error, outcome-model complexity, and calibration complexity terms, identifying when embedding alignment outperforms imputation. Under the calibration-based linear variant, the framework provides protection against negative transfer; the neural variant can be vulnerable under severe distributional shift. Under sparse linear models, the embedding approach strictly generalizes imputation. Simulations across 51 settings confirm that (i) calibration-based methods are equivalent for linear CATEs, and (ii) the neural embedding variant wins all 22 nonlinear-regime settings with large margins.

AI.tldr: CALM learns a shared embedding for covariates from RCT and OS sources to estimate CATE under covariate mismatch, transferring OS outcome models to the RCT space and calibrating with trial data. Finite-sample risk decomposes into alignment, outcome-model, and calibration terms; embedding can outperform imputation, with linear calibration robust to negative transfer and neural embedding vulnerable to distributional shift; under sparse linear models, embedding strictly generalizes imputation. Simulations show neural embedding wins nonlinear regimes; calibration-based methods equivalent in linear CATEs.
AI.motivation: Heterogeneous treatment effect estimation suffers from covariate mismatch between randomized trials (RCTs) and large observational studies (OS). Without overlap, imputing missing covariates is risky. The paper seeks to enable robust CATE estimation by aligning source covariates in a common representation without imputation.
AI.method: Learn source-specific embeddings that map each dataset's covariates into a shared latent space. Transfer OS outcome models into the RCT embedding space and calibrate using trial data to preserve randomization-based causal identification. Provide finite-sample risk bounds decomposed into alignment error, outcome-model complexity, and calibration complexity. Compare a calibration-based linear variant and a neural embedding variant; discuss sparse linear models where embedding generalizes imputation.
AI.result: CALM achieves equivalence with imputation for linear CATEs and outperforms imputational approaches in nonlinear settings, with the neural embedding variant dominating 22 nonlinear-regime simulations. The calibration-based linear variant resists negative transfer, while the neural variant can be sensitive to distributional shift. Under sparse linear models, embedding strictly generalizes imputation.
AI.conclusion: Embedding-based alignment under covariate mismatch, with calibration, provides robust CATE estimation and outperforms traditional imputation in nonlinear regimes. Practically, choose the calibration-based linear variant for stability and negative-transfer protection, or the neural embedding when nonlinear relationships predominate and distributional shift is manageable.

title: SwiftTailor: Efficient 3D Garment Generation with Geometry Image Representation
summary: Realistic and efficient 3D garment generation remains a longstanding challenge in computer vision and digital fashion. Existing methods typically rely on large vision- language models to produce serialized representations of 2D sewing patterns, which are then transformed into simulation-ready 3D meshes using garment modeling framework such as GarmentCode. Although these approaches yield high-quality results, they often suffer from slow inference times, ranging from 30 seconds to a minute. In this work, we introduce SwiftTailor, a novel two-stage framework that unifies sewing-pattern reasoning and geometry-based mesh synthesis through a compact geometry image representation. SwiftTailor comprises two lightweight modules: PatternMaker, an efficient vision-language model that predicts sewing patterns from diverse input modalities, and GarmentSewer, an efficient dense prediction transformer that converts these patterns into a novel Garment Geometry Image, encoding the 3D surface of all garment panels in a unified UV space. The final 3D mesh is reconstructed through an efficient inverse mapping process that incorporates remeshing and dynamic stitching algorithms to directly assemble the garment, thereby amortizing the cost of physical simulation. Extensive experiments on the Multimodal GarmentCodeData demonstrate that SwiftTailor achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and visual fidelity while significantly reducing inference time. This work offers a scalable, interpretable, and high-performance solution for next-generation 3D garment generation.

AI.tldr: SwiftTailor presents a fast, two-stage framework for 3D garment generation that unifies sewing-pattern reasoning with geometry-based mesh synthesis via a Garment Geometry Image, achieving state-of-the-art fidelity with substantially reduced inference time.
AI.motivation: To overcome slow, large-model pipelines that convert 2D sewing patterns to 3D meshes, and to reduce inference time while improving interpretability and scalability.
AI.method: PatternMaker is a lightweight vision-language model predicting sewing patterns from multi-modal inputs. GarmentSewer is a dense prediction transformer translating patterns into a Garment Geometry Image that encodes the 3D garment surface in a unified UV space. Inverse mapping with remeshing and dynamic stitching reconstructs the final 3D mesh, amortizing physics-based simulation costs.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on Multimodal GarmentCodeData demonstrate state-of-the-art accuracy and visual fidelity with significantly reduced inference time.
AI.conclusion: SwiftTailor offers a scalable, interpretable, and high-performance solution for next-generation 3D garment generation, leveraging a unified geometry image to streamline pattern-to-mesh synthesis and fast mesh assembly.

title: Robustness, Cost, and Attack-Surface Concentration in Phishing Detection
summary:
Phishing detectors built on engineered website features attain near-perfect accuracy under i.i.d.\ evaluation, yet deployment security depends on robustness to post-deployment feature manipulation. We study this gap through a cost-aware evasion framework that models discrete, monotone feature edits under explicit attacker budgets. Three diagnostics are introduced: minimal evasion cost (MEC), the evasion survival rate $S(B)$, and the robustness concentration index (RCI).
  On the UCI Phishing Websites benchmark (11\,055 instances, 30 ternary features), Logistic Regression, Random Forests, Gradient Boosted Trees, and XGBoost all achieve $\mathrm{AUC}\ge 0.979$ under static evaluation. Under budgeted sanitization-style evasion, robustness converges across architectures: the median MEC equals 2 with full features, and over 80\% of successful minimal-cost evasions concentrate on three low-cost surface features. Feature restriction improves robustness only when it removes all dominant low-cost transitions. Under strict cost schedules, infrastructure-leaning feature sets exhibit 17-19\% infeasible mass for ensemble models, while the median MEC among evadable instances remains unchanged. We formalize this convergence: if a positive fraction of correctly detected phishing instances admit evasion through a single feature transition of minimal cost $c_{\min}$, no classifier can raise the corresponding MEC quantile above $c_{\min}$ without modifying the feature representation or cost model. Adversarial robustness in phishing detection is governed by feature economics rather than model complexity.

AI.tldr: Budget-aware feature-edit evasion reveals robustness is driven by feature costs; with full features, MEC is 2; majority evasions rely on few cheap surface features; model choice matters less than feature economics.
AI.motivation: There is a gap between i.i.d. performance and deployment robustness due to post-deployment feature manipulation. A cost-aware, discrete, monotone edit model and new diagnostics are proposed.
AI.method: Empirical study on UCI Phishing Websites dataset with 11,055 instances and 30 ternary features; evaluate LR, RF, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost under static and budgeted sanitization evasion; define MEC, S(B), RCI; analyze effect of feature restriction and cost schedules; provide formal convergence result.
AI.result: Under static evaluation AUC ≥ 0.979 for all models; under budgeted evasion, robustness converges across models; median MEC = 2 with full features; >80% evasions target three cheap features; feature restriction helps only if it removes all dominant cheap transitions; strict cost schedules yield 17-19% infeasible mass; MEC for evadable instances unchanged; convergence formalized: if some correctly detected instances are evadable with cost c_min, MEC quantile cannot exceed c_min without changing features or costs.
AI.conclusion: Phishing detector robustness is governed by feature economics rather than model complexity; to improve robustness, adjust feature cost model or representation, not just model choice.

title: Em-Garde: A Propose-Match Framework for Proactive Streaming Video Understanding
summary: Recent advances in Streaming Video Understanding has enabled a new interaction paradigm where models respond proactively to user queries. Current proactive VideoLLMs rely on per-frame triggering decision making, which suffers from an efficiency-accuracy dilemma. We propose Em-Garde, a novel framework that decouples semantic understanding from streaming perception. At query time, the Instruction-Guided Proposal Parser transforms user queries into structured, perceptually grounded visual proposals; during streaming, a Lightweight Proposal Matching Module performs efficient embedding-based matching to trigger responses. Experiments on StreamingBench and OVO-Bench demonstrate consistent improvements over prior models in proactive response accuracy and efficiency, validating an effective solution for proactive video understanding under strict computational constraints.

AI.tldr: Em-Garde decouples semantic understanding from streaming perception to enable efficient proactive video understanding via instruction-guided proposals and lightweight matching.
AI.motivation: Current proactive VideoLLMs rely on per-frame triggering, creating an efficiency-accuracy dilemma in streaming video understanding; a decoupled approach could improve both metrics.
AI.method: At query time, an Instruction-Guided Proposal Parser converts user queries into structured, perceptually grounded visual proposals. During streaming, a Lightweight Proposal Matching Module performs fast embedding-based matching to trigger responses, enabling efficient proactive behavior.
AI.result: Experiments on StreamingBench and OVO-Bench show consistent improvements in proactive response accuracy and efficiency over prior models.
AI.conclusion: Em-Garde provides an effective solution for proactive video understanding under strict computational constraints by decoupling semantic understanding from streaming perception.

title: Online Learning and Equilibrium Computation with Ranking Feedback
summary: Online learning in arbitrary, and possibly adversarial, environments has been extensively studied in sequential decision-making, and it is closely connected to equilibrium computation in game theory. Most existing online learning algorithms rely on \emph{numeric} utility feedback from the environment, which may be unavailable in human-in-the-loop applications and/or may be restricted by privacy concerns. In this paper, we study an online learning model in which the learner only observes a \emph{ranking} over a set of proposed actions at each timestep. We consider two ranking mechanisms: rankings induced by the \emph{instantaneous} utility at the current timestep, and rankings induced by the \emph{time-average} utility up to the current timestep, under both \emph{full-information} and \emph{bandit} feedback settings. Using the standard external-regret metric, we show that sublinear regret is impossible with instantaneous-utility ranking feedback in general. Moreover, when the ranking model is relatively deterministic, \emph{i.e.}, under the Plackett-Luce model with a temperature that is sufficiently small, sublinear regret is also impossible with time-average utility ranking feedback. We then develop new algorithms that achieve sublinear regret under the additional assumption that the utility sequence has sublinear total variation. Notably, for full-information time-average utility ranking feedback, this additional assumption can be removed. As a consequence, when all players in a normal-form game follow our algorithms, repeated play yields an approximate coarse correlated equilibrium. We also demonstrate the effectiveness of our algorithms in an online large-language-model routing task.

AI.tldr: The paper studies online learning with only ranking feedback (instantaneous vs time-average utility) under full-information and bandit settings; shows sublinear regret is impossible in general for instantaneous ranking; under near-deterministic ranking (low-temperature Plackett-Luce), sublinear regret is also impossible for time-average ranking; proposes algorithms achieving sublinear regret when the utility sequence has sublinear total variation; for full-information time-average ranking this extra assumption can be removed; implications include convergence to an approximate coarse correlated equilibrium in repeated normal-form games; empirically validated on online LLM routing.
AI.motivation: Address privacy or practicality constraints where only rankings (not numeric utilities) are observable; understand the learnability and equilibrium outcomes under ranking feedback.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of two ranking feedback mechanisms (instantaneous and time-average) under full-information and bandit feedback; establishes impossibility results; develops new algorithms with sublinear regret given sublinear total variation; shows removal of assumptions in the full-information time-average case; connects to coarse correlated equilibria in repeated games; includes an empirical demonstration in Online LLM routing.
AI.result: Impossibility of sublinear regret for instantaneous ranking; under deterministic Plackett-Luce with low temp, time-average ranking also yields impossibility; novel algorithms attaining sublinear regret under sublinear total variation; in full-information time-average setting the extra assumption is unnecessary; repeated play yields an approximate CCE in normal-form games; practical validation in LLM routing tasks.
AI.conclusion: Ranking-based feedback can support sublinear regret and approximate equilibrium outcomes under reasonable structure; results illuminate when privacy-preserving ranking signals suffice for learning and game-theoretic convergence, with practical empirical support.

title: SignAgent: Agentic LLMs for Linguistically-Grounded Sign Language Annotation and Dataset Curation
summary: This paper introduces SignAgent, a novel agentic framework that utilises Large Language Models (LLMs) for scalable, linguistically-grounded Sign Language (SL) annotation and dataset curation. Traditional computational methods for SLs often operate at the gloss level, overlooking crucial linguistic nuances, while manual linguistic annotation remains a significant bottleneck, proving too slow and expensive for the creation of large-scale, phonologically-aware datasets. SignAgent addresses these challenges through SignAgent Orchestrator, a reasoning LLM that coordinates a suite of linguistic tools, and SignGraph, a knowledge-grounded LLM that provides lexical and linguistic grounding. We evaluate our framework on two downstream annotation tasks. First, on Pseudo-gloss Annotation, where the agent performs constrained assignment, using multi-modal evidence to extract and order suitable gloss labels for signed sequences. Second, on ID Glossing, where the agent detects and refines visual clusters by reasoning over both visual similarity and phonological overlap to correctly identify and group lexical sign variants. Our results demonstrate that our agentic approach achieves strong performance for large-scale, linguistically-aware data annotation and curation.

AI.tldr: Introduces SignAgent, a two-component, LLM-driven framework for scalable, linguistically-grounded Sign Language annotation and dataset curation; demonstrates effectiveness on pseudo-gloss annotation and ID glossing tasks.
AI.motivation: Current SL annotation methods often operate at the gloss level and manual linguistic annotation is slow and expensive, hindering large-scale, phonologically-aware datasets. There is a need to combine linguistic nuance with scalable automation.
AI.method: SignAgent Orchestrator (a reasoning LLM) coordinates a suite of linguistic tools; SignGraph (a knowledge-grounded LLM) provides lexical/linguistic grounding. Evaluation on two tasks: (1) pseudo-gloss annotation using constrained assignment with multi-modal evidence to extract/order gloss labels; (2) ID glossing by reasoning over visual similarity and phonological overlap to cluster lexical sign variants.
AI.result: The agentic framework achieves strong performance on large-scale, linguistically-aware annotation and data curation across the two downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: Agentic, LLM-driven orchestration and grounding enable scalable, linguistically-aware Sign Language annotation and dataset curation.

title: SAVeS: Steering Safety Judgments in Vision-Language Models via Semantic Cues
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly deployed in real-world and embodied settings where safety decisions depend on visual context. However, it remains unclear which visual evidence drives these judgments. We study whether multimodal safety behavior in VLMs can be steered by simple semantic cues. We introduce a semantic steering framework that applies controlled textual, visual, and cognitive interventions without changing the underlying scene content. To evaluate these effects, we propose SAVeS, a benchmark for situational safety under semantic cues, together with an evaluation protocol that separates behavioral refusal, grounded safety reasoning, and false refusals. Experiments across multiple VLMs and an additional state-of-the-art benchmark show that safety decisions are highly sensitive to semantic cues, indicating reliance on learned visual-linguistic associations rather than grounded visual understanding. We further demonstrate that automated steering pipelines can exploit these mechanisms, highlighting a potential vulnerability in multimodal safety systems.

AI.tldr: Semantic cues can steer safety judgments in vision-language models without changing the scene; a dedicated SAVeS benchmark reveals reliance on learned visual–linguistic associations and exposes vulnerability to semantic steering.
AI.motivation: To understand what visual evidence VLMs use for safety judgments, and whether semantic manipulation can bias safety decisions, revealing potential risks in multimodal safety systems.
AI.method: Introduce a semantic steering framework with controlled textual, visual, and cognitive interventions that leave scene content unchanged; propose SAVeS (Situational Safety Under Semantic cues) and a protocol to separate behavioral refusal, grounded safety reasoning, and false refusals; evaluate across multiple VLMs and a state-of-the-art benchmark; also demonstrate automated steering pipelines.
AI.result: Safety decisions are highly sensitive to semantic cues, indicating reliance on learned visual–linguistic associations rather than grounded visual understanding; automated steering can exploit these mechanisms, revealing a vulnerability in multimodal safety systems.
AI.conclusion: Most VLM safety behavior is cue-driven rather than robustly grounded; architectures and evaluation protocols should mitigate cue sensitivity and ensure robust, interpretable grounding in visual content to reduce susceptibility to semantic steering.

title: TAU-R1: Visual Language Model for Traffic Anomaly Understanding
summary: Traffic Anomaly Understanding (TAU) is important for traffic safety in Intelligent Transportation Systems. Recent vision-language models (VLMs) have shown strong capabilities in video understanding. However, progress on TAU remains limited due to the lack of benchmarks and task-specific methodologies. To address this limitation, we introduce Roundabout-TAU, a dataset constructed from real-world roundabout videos collected in collaboration with the City of Carmel, Indiana. The dataset contains 342 clips and is annotated with more than 2,000 question-answer pairs covering multiple aspects of traffic anomaly understanding. Building on this benchmark, we propose TAU-R1, a two-layer vision-language framework for TAU. The first layer is a lightweight anomaly classifier that performs coarse anomaly categorisation, while the second layer is a larger anomaly reasoner that generates detailed event summaries. To improve task-specific reasoning, we introduce a two-stage training strategy consisting of decomposed-QA-enhanced supervised fine-tuning followed by TAU-GRPO, a GRPO-based post-training method with TAU-specific reward functions. Experimental results show that TAU-R1 achieves strong performance on both anomaly classification and reasoning tasks while maintaining deployment efficiency. The dataset and code are available at: https://github.com/siri-rouser/TAU-R1

AI.tldr: TAU-R1 introduces a two-layer vision-language framework for Traffic Anomaly Understanding (TAU) built on the Roundabout-TAU dataset. It combines a lightweight anomaly classifier with a larger anomaly reasoner and uses a two-stage training pipeline (decomposed-QA-enhanced supervised fine-tuning and TAU-GRPO post-training) to achieve strong anomaly classification and reasoning while maintaining deployment efficiency. The dataset and code are released at the provided GitHub link.
AI.motivation: There is a lack of benchmarks and task-specific methodologies for Traffic Anomaly Understanding (TAU). The Roundabout-TAU dataset provides real-world roundabout videos with abundant QA annotations to drive progress and enable evaluation of VLM-based TAU systems.
AI.method: A two-layer architecture: (1) a lightweight anomaly classifier for coarse anomaly categorization and (2) a larger anomaly reasoner for detailed event summaries. Training uses a two-stage approach: decomposed-QA-enhanced supervised fine-tuning to tailor QA capabilities, followed by TAU-GRPO (a GRPO-based post-training method) with TAU-specific reward functions to align reasoning with TAU tasks.
AI.result: Experiments show TAU-R1 achieves strong performance on both anomaly classification and reasoning tasks, while preserving deployment efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The Roundabout-TAU dataset and TAU-R1 framework advance TAU research by providing a realistic benchmark and a practical, effective two-layer VLM-based solution; the authors release the dataset and code at the provided GitHub repository.

title: CustomTex: High-fidelity Indoor Scene Texturing via Multi-Reference Customization
comment: Accepted to CVPR 2026. This version integrates the main paper and supplementary material
summary: The creation of high-fidelity, customizable 3D indoor scene textures remains a significant challenge. While text-driven methods offer flexibility, they lack the precision for fine-grained, instance-level control, and often produce textures with insufficient quality, artifacts, and baked-in shading. To overcome these limitations, we introduce CustomTex, a novel framework for instance-level, high-fidelity scene texturing driven by reference images. CustomTex takes an untextured 3D scene and a set of reference images specifying the desired appearance for each object instance, and generates a unified, high-resolution texture map. The core of our method is a dual-distillation approach that separates semantic control from pixel-level enhancement. We employ semantic-level distillation, equipped with an instance cross-attention, to ensure semantic plausibility and ``reference-instance'' alignment, and pixel-level distillation to enforce high visual fidelity. Both are unified within a Variational Score Distillation (VSD) optimization framework. Experiments demonstrate that CustomTex achieves precise instance-level consistency with reference images and produces textures with superior sharpness, reduced artifacts, and minimal baked-in shading compared to state-of-the-art methods. Our work establishes a more direct and user-friendly path to high-quality, customizable 3D scene appearance editing.

AI.tldr: CustomTex enables high-fidelity, instance-level 3D scene texturing from reference images via a dual-distillation framework (semantic-level with instance cross-attention and pixel-level) within a Variational Score Distillation setup, achieving better alignment, sharpness, and reduced artifacts than baselines.
AI.motivation: Text-driven scene texturing offers flexibility but lacks fine-grained instance control and can produce artifacts or baked-in shading; a method that preserves semantic alignment while delivering high visual fidelity per instance is needed.
AI.method: Input a 3D scene without textures and per-instance reference images. Employ dual-distillation: (1) semantic-level distillation with instance cross-attention to ensure semantic plausibility and reference-instance alignment, and (2) pixel-level distillation to enforce high visual fidelity. Integrate both within a Variational Score Distillation (VSD) optimization to produce a unified, high-resolution texture map.
AI.result: Experiments show precise per-instance consistency with references, textures with superior sharpness, reduced artifacts, and minimal baked-in shading compared with state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that semantic-level and pixel-level distillation under a VSD framework yields improved instance-level control and texture quality, enabling a more direct, user-friendly path for high-quality 3D scene appearance editing.

title: Revisiting Autoregressive Models for Generative Image Classification
summary: Class-conditional generative models have emerged as accurate and robust classifiers, with diffusion models demonstrating clear advantages over other visual generative paradigms, including autoregressive (AR) models. In this work, we revisit visual AR-based generative classifiers and identify an important limitation of prior approaches: their reliance on a fixed token order, which imposes a restrictive inductive bias for image understanding. We observe that single-order predictions rely more on partial discriminative cues, while averaging over multiple token orders provides a more comprehensive signal. Based on this insight, we leverage recent any-order AR models to estimate order-marginalized predictions, unlocking the high classification potential of AR models. Our approach consistently outperforms diffusion-based classifiers across diverse image classification benchmarks, while being up to 25x more efficient. Compared to state-of-the-art self-supervised discriminative models, our method delivers competitive classification performance - a notable achievement for generative classifiers.

AI.tldr: Order-marginalized autoregressive (AR) generative classifiers for vision remove fixed-token-order bias by averaging over multiple token orders; using any-order AR models, they outperform diffusion classifiers and are computationally more efficient, with competitive performance against self-supervised discriminative models.
AI.motivation: Fixed token-order bias in single-order AR visual classifiers limits discriminative effectiveness. Averaging over multiple token orders can capture richer cues and improve accuracy. Any-order AR models enable practical order-marginalization.
AI.method: Apply any-order AR models to estimate order-marginalized predictions by averaging predictions across many token orders. Compare against diffusion-based classifiers and self-supervised discriminative models on standard image classification benchmarks; report efficiency (up to 25x faster) and accuracy.
AI.result: AR-based classifiers with order-marginalization consistently outperform diffusion-based classifiers across diverse benchmarks, achieving up to 25x efficiency. They also achieve competitive accuracy compared with state-of-the-art self-supervised discriminative models.
AI.conclusion: Order-marginalized AR generative classifiers are a strong, efficient alternative for image classification, mitigating fixed-order biases of earlier AR approaches and matching or exceeding diffusion and some discriminative-model performance.

title: ADAPT: Attention Driven Adaptive Prompt Scheduling and InTerpolating Orthogonal Complements for Rare Concepts Generation
comment: Accepted in CVPR 2026 (findings). 10 pages, 4 figures; supplementary material included (8 pages, 10 figures)
summary: Generating rare compositional concepts in text-to-image synthesis remains a challenge for diffusion models, particularly for attributes that are uncommon in the training data. While recent approaches, such as R2F, address this challenge by utilizing LLM for prompt scheduling, they suffer from inherent variance due to the randomness of language models and suboptimal guidance from iterative text embedding switching. To address these problems, we propose the ADAPT framework, a training-free framework that deterministically plans and semantically aligns prompt schedules, providing consistent guidance to enhance the composition of rare concepts. By leveraging attention scores and orthogonal components, ADAPT significantly enhances compositional generation of rare concepts in the RareBench benchmark without additional training or fine-tuning. Through comprehensive experiments, we demonstrate that ADAPT achieves superior performance in RareBench and accurately reflects the semantic information of rare attributes, providing deterministic and precise control over the generation of rare compositions without compromising visual integrity.

AI.tldr: ADAPT is a training-free, deterministic framework for prompting diffusion models to improve rare compositional concepts, using attention-guided planning and orthogonal components to align prompts without additional training, showing strong results on RareBench compared to R2F.
AI.motivation: To overcome stochastic and suboptimal guidance when using LLM-driven prompt scheduling (as in R2F) for rare concept composition in text-to-image synthesis.
AI.method: Deterministic prompt scheduling guided by attention scores and orthogonal components; no training or fine-tuning. The framework plans prompt sequences and semantically aligns them to enhance rare concept composition, leveraging attention-based signals to control semantic alignment.
AI.result: Significantly improves compositional generation of rare concepts on the RareBench benchmark; achieves superior performance while preserving visual fidelity and reducing variance inherent in LLM-driven approaches.
AI.conclusion: ADAPT provides a deterministic, training-free pathway to accurately reflect semantic information of rare attributes and to control rare compositions without compromising image quality, outperforming prior LLM-based prompt scheduling approaches like R2F on RareBench.

title: Adaptive Auxiliary Prompt Blending for Target-Faithful Diffusion Generation
comment: Accepted in CVPR 2026 (main track). 10 pages, 6 figures; supplementary material included (14 pages, 11 figures)
summary: Diffusion-based text-to-image (T2I) models have made remarkable progress in generating photorealistic and semantically rich images. However, when the target concepts lie in low-density regions of the training distribution, these models often produce semantically misaligned or structurally inconsistent results. This limitation arises from the long-tailed nature of text-image datasets, where rare concepts or editing instructions are underrepresented. To address this, we introduce Adaptive Auxiliary Prompt Blending (AAPB) - a unified framework that stabilizes the diffusion process in low-density regions. AAPB leverages auxiliary anchor prompts to provide semantic support in rare concept generation and structural support in image editing, ensuring faithful guidance toward the target prompt. Unlike prior heuristic prompt alternation methods, AAPB derives a closed-form adaptive coefficient that optimally balances the influence between the auxiliary anchor and the target prompt at each diffusion step. Grounded in Tweedie's identity, our formulation provides a principled and training-free framework for adaptive prompt blending, ensuring stable and target-faithful generation. We demonstrate the effectiveness of adaptive interpolation over fixed interpolation through controlled experiments and empirically show consistent improvements on the RareBench and FlowEdit datasets, achieving superior semantic accuracy and structural fidelity compared to prior training-free baselines.

AI.tldr: AAPB is a training-free adaptive prompt blending framework for diffusion-based T2I that uses auxiliary anchor prompts to stabilize generation in low-density regions, with a closed-form adaptive coefficient derived via Tweedie’s identity, achieving better semantic accuracy and structural fidelity on RareBench and FlowEdit.
AI.motivation: Addresses the long-tailed nature of text–image data, where rare concepts or edits are underrepresented, causing semantic misalignment and structural inconsistencies in generated images.
AI.method: Adaptive Auxiliary Prompt Blending (AAPB) blends the target prompt with auxiliary anchor prompts using a closed-form adaptive coefficient per diffusion step, derived from Tweedie’s identity, providing semantic and structural guidance without additional training. The approach is training-free and compared against fixed interpolation and prior baselines.
AI.result: Empirically shows improved semantic accuracy and structural fidelity on RareBench and FlowEdit over prior training-free baselines, demonstrating effective stabilization in low-density regions.
AI.conclusion: AAPB offers a principled, training-free framework for adaptive prompt blending that stabilizes diffusion in low-density regions, yielding target-faithful generation and robust performance in rare-concept editing tasks.

title: ARIADNE: A Perception-Reasoning Synergy Framework for Trustworthy Coronary Angiography Analysis
summary: Conventional pixel-wise loss functions fail to enforce topological constraints in coronary vessel segmentation, producing fragmented vascular trees despite high pixel-level accuracy. We present ARIADNE, a two-stage framework coupling preference-aligned perception with RL-based diagnostic reasoning for topologically coherent stenosis detection. The perception module employs DPO to fine-tune the Sa2VA vision-language foundation model using Betti number constraints as preference signals, aligning the policy toward geometrically complete vessel structures rather than pixel-wise overlap metrics. The reasoning module formulates stenosis localization as a Markov Decision Process with an explicit rejection mechanism that autonomously defers ambiguous anatomical candidates such as bifurcations and vessel crossings, shifting from coverage maximization to reliability optimization. On 1,400 clinical angiograms, ARIADNE achieves state-of-the-art centerline Dice of 0.838, reduces false positives by 41% compared to geometric baselines. External validation on multi-center benchmarks ARCADE and XCAD confirms generalization across acquisition protocols. This represents the first application of DPO for topological alignment in medical imaging, demonstrating that preference-based learning over structural constraints mitigates topological violations while maintaining diagnostic sensitivity in interventional cardiology workflows.

AI.tldr: Two-stage ARIADNE framework combines preference-aligned perception (DPO with Betti constraints) and RL-based reasoning (MDP with rejection) to enforce topological coherence in coronary vessel segmentation, achieving state-of-the-art centerline Dice and strong cross-center generalization.
AI.motivation: Address topological violations (fragmented vessels) common in pixel-wise segmentation by incorporating structural/topological constraints through preference-based learning and explicit reliability-oriented decision making.
AI.method: Perception module: fine-tunes Sa2VA with DPO using Betti number constraints as preference signals to favor complete vessel topology. Reasoning module: formulates stenosis localization as an MDP with an explicit rejection mechanism to defer ambiguous candidates (bifurcations, crossings), optimizing reliability over coverage. Evaluation on 1,400 clinical angiograms; external validation on ARCADE and XCAD.
AI.result: Centerline Dice 0.838; false positives reduced by 41% relative to geometric baselines; demonstrates generalization across acquisition protocols in external datasets.
AI.conclusion: Presents the first use of DPO for topological alignment in medical imaging, showing that preference-based learning over structural constraints mitigates topological violations while preserving diagnostic sensitivity in interventional cardiology workflows.

title: Reconstruction Matters: Learning Geometry-Aligned BEV Representation through 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) perception serves as a cornerstone for autonomous driving, offering a unified spatial representation that fuses surrounding-view images to enable reasoning for various downstream tasks, such as semantic segmentation, 3D object detection, and motion prediction. However, most existing BEV perception frameworks adopt an end-to-end training paradigm, where image features are directly transformed into the BEV space and optimized solely through downstream task supervision. This formulation treats the entire perception process as a black box, often lacking explicit 3D geometric understanding and interpretability, leading to suboptimal performance. In this paper, we claim that an explicit 3D representation matters for accurate BEV perception, and we propose Splat2BEV, a Gaussian Splatting-assisted framework for BEV tasks. Splat2BEV aims to learn BEV feature representations that are both semantically rich and geometrically precise. We first pre-train a Gaussian generator that explicitly reconstructs 3D scenes from multi-view inputs, enabling the generation of geometry-aligned feature representations. These representations are then projected into the BEV space to serve as inputs for downstream tasks. Extensive experiments on nuScenes and argoverse dataset demonstrate that Splat2BEV achieves state-of-the-art performance and validate the effectiveness of incorporating explicit 3D reconstruction into BEV perception.

AI.tldr: Introduces Gaussian splatting-based 3D reconstruction to improve BEV perception; pre-trains a 3D generator to produce geometry-aligned features that are projected to BEV, achieving state-of-the-art results on nuScenes and argoverse.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap in BEV perception where end-to-end image-to-BEV pipelines lack explicit 3D geometry and interpretability, potentially hindering accuracy and reliability.
AI.method: Pre-train a Gaussian generator that reconstructs 3D scenes from multi-view inputs, producing geometry-aligned feature representations; project these 3D features into BEV for downstream tasks.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on nuScenes and argoverse; validates that explicit 3D reconstruction benefits BEV perception.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating explicit 3D geometry through Gaussian splatting enhances BEV representations for semantic segmentation, 3D detection, and motion understanding, and can serve as a blueprint for geometry-aware BEV models.

title: Tinted Frames: Question Framing Blinds Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have been shown to be blind, often underutilizing their visual inputs even on tasks that require visual reasoning. In this work, we demonstrate that VLMs are selectively blind. They modulate the amount of attention applied to visual inputs based on linguistic framing even when alternative framings demand identical visual reasoning. Using visual attention as a probe, we quantify how framing alters both the amount and distribution of attention over the image. Constrained framings, such as multiple choice and yes/no, induce substantially lower attention to image context compared to open-ended, reduce focus on task-relevant regions, and shift attention towards uninformative tokens. We further demonstrate that this attention misallocation is the principal cause of degraded accuracy and cross-framing inconsistency. Building on this mechanistic insight, we introduce a lightweight prompt-tuning method using learnable tokens that encourages the robust, visually grounded attention patterns observed in open-ended settings, improving visual grounding and improving performance across framings.

AI.tldr: VLMs exhibit framing-dependent selective blindness in visual reasoning; open-ended prompts yield better visual grounding than constrained framings. A lightweight prompt-tuning method with learnable tokens mitigates misallocated attention, improving grounding and cross-framing performance.
AI.motivation: To understand why VLMs underperform on tasks requiring visual reasoning when framed with constrained linguistic prompts, and to identify whether attention allocation mediates accuracy gaps across framings.
AI.method: Analyze attention distribution in VLMs under open-ended vs constrained framings (multiple choice, yes/no) using visual attention as a probe. Demonstrate that constrained framings reduce attention to relevant image regions and shift attention to uninformative tokens. Propose a lightweight prompt-tuning approach with learnable tokens to promote robust, visually grounded attention patterns; evaluate impact on visual grounding and task performance across framings.
AI.result: Framings with constraints substantially reduce attention to image context, degrade focus on task-relevant regions, and lower accuracy and cross-framing consistency. The proposed learnable-token prompt-tuning method restores visually grounded attention and improves performance across framings.
AI.conclusion: Framing-induced misallocation of visual attention is a primary driver of degraded performance; targeted prompt-tuning can align attention patterns with open-ended settings, yielding more robust visual grounding and cross-framing reliability.

title: RPiAE: A Representation-Pivoted Autoencoder Enhancing Both Image Generation and Editing
summary: Diffusion models have become the dominant paradigm for image generation and editing, with latent diffusion models shifting denoising to a compact latent space for efficiency and scalability. Recent attempts to leverage pretrained visual representation models as tokenizer priors either align diffusion features to representation features or directly reuse representation encoders as frozen tokenizers. Although such approaches can improve generation metrics, they often suffer from limited reconstruction fidelity due to frozen encoders, which in turn degrades editing quality, as well as overly high-dimensional latents that make diffusion modeling difficult. To address these limitations, We propose Representation-Pivoted AutoEncoder, a representation-based tokenizer that improves both generation and editing. We introduce Representation-Pivot Regularization, a training strategy that enables a representation-initialized encoder to be fine-tuned for reconstruction while preserving the semantic structure of the pretrained representation space, followed by a variational bridge which compress latent space into a compact one for better diffusion modeling. We adopt an objective-decoupled stage-wise training strategy that sequentially optimizes generative tractability and reconstruction-fidelity objectives. Together, these components yield a tokenizer that preserves strong semantics, reconstructs faithfully, and produces latents with reduced diffusion modeling complexity. Experiments demonstrate that RPiAE outperforms other visual tokenizers on text-to-image generation and image editing, while delivering the best reconstruction fidelity among representation-based tokenizers.

AI.tldr: RPiAE is a representation-based tokenizer with Representation-Pivot Regularization and a variational bridge that enables fine-tuning of a pretrained representation-initialized encoder for reconstruction, followed by latent compression for efficient diffusion. It achieves high reconstruction fidelity and strong generation/editing performance, outperforming prior representation-based tokenizers.
AI.motivation: Fix limited reconstruction fidelity and high latent dimensionality when using frozen or representation-based tokenizers in diffusion models, which hurts editing quality and modeling efficiency.
AI.method: Three-stage approach: (1) Representation-Pivot Regularization to fine-tune a representation-initialized encoder while preserving the semantic structure; (2) a variational bridge to compress the latent space into a compact representation for diffusion; (3) objective-decoupled, stage-wise training that first optimizes generative tractability and then reconstruction fidelity, yielding a practical tokenizer for diffusion.
AI.result: Empirical results show that RPiAE outperforms other visual tokenizers on text-to-image generation and image editing, while achieving the best reconstruction fidelity among representation-based tokenizers.
AI.conclusion: RPiAE provides a semantics-preserving, faithfully reconstructing, compact tokenizer that reduces diffusion modeling complexity and enhances generation/editing performance compared to prior representation-based approaches.

title: DreamPartGen: Semantically Grounded Part-Level 3D Generation via Collaborative Latent Denoising
summary: Understanding and generating 3D objects as compositions of meaningful parts is fundamental to human perception and reasoning. However, most text-to-3D methods overlook the semantic and functional structure of parts. While recent part-aware approaches introduce decomposition, they remain largely geometry-focused, lacking semantic grounding and failing to model how parts align with textual descriptions or their inter-part relations. We propose DreamPartGen, a framework for semantically grounded, part-aware text-to-3D generation. DreamPartGen introduces Duplex Part Latents (DPLs) that jointly model each part's geometry and appearance, and Relational Semantic Latents (RSLs) that capture inter-part dependencies derived from language. A synchronized co-denoising process enforces mutual geometric and semantic consistency, enabling coherent, interpretable, and text-aligned 3D synthesis. Across multiple benchmarks, DreamPartGen delivers state-of-the-art performance in geometric fidelity and text-shape alignment.

AI.tldr: DreamPartGen is a semantically grounded, part-aware text-to-3D generation framework that uses Duplex Part Latents (DPLs) and Relational Semantic Latents (RSLs) with synchronized co-denoising to align geometry, appearance, and language, achieving state-of-the-art text-to-3D results.
AI.motivation: Existing text-to-3D methods lack semantic grounding of parts and fail to model inter-part relations and alignment with textual descriptions; current part-aware methods are mostly geometry-focused.
AI.method: Introduce Duplex Part Latents (DPLs) to jointly model each part's geometry and appearance; Relational Semantic Latents (RSLs) to capture inter-part dependencies derived from language; synchronized co-denoising to enforce mutual geometric and semantic consistency.
AI.result: Empirically achieves state-of-the-art performance on geometric fidelity and text-shape alignment across multiple benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: DreamPartGen enables coherent, interpretable, and text-aligned 3D synthesis with improved part-level semantics and relations.

title: LVOmniBench: Pioneering Long Audio-Video Understanding Evaluation for Omnimodal LLMs
summary: Recent advancements in omnimodal large language models (OmniLLMs) have significantly improved the comprehension of audio and video inputs. However, current evaluations primarily focus on short audio and video clips ranging from 10 seconds to 5 minutes, failing to reflect the demands of real-world applications, where videos typically run for tens of minutes. To address this critical gap, we introduce LVOmniBench, a new benchmark designed specifically for the cross-modal comprehension of long-form audio and video. This dataset comprises high-quality videos sourced from open platforms that feature rich audio-visual dynamics. Through rigorous manual selection and annotation, LVOmniBench comprises 275 videos, ranging in duration from 10 to 90 minutes, and 1,014 question-answer (QA) pairs. LVOmniBench aims to rigorously evaluate the capabilities of OmniLLMs across domains, including long-term memory, temporal localization, fine-grained understanding, and multimodal perception. Our extensive evaluation reveals that current OmniLLMs encounter significant challenges when processing extended audio-visual inputs. Open-source models generally achieve accuracies below 35%, whereas the Gemini 3 Pro reaches a peak accuracy of approximately 65%. We anticipate that this dataset, along with our empirical findings, will stimulate further research and the development of advanced models capable of resolving complex cross-modal understanding problems within long-form audio-visual contexts.

AI.tldr: A new benchmark, LVOmniBench, evaluates long-form cross-modal comprehension in OmniLLMs. It comprises 275 open-domain videos (10–90 minutes) with 1,014 QA pairs. Results show current OmniLLMs struggle on long-form AV tasks, with open-source models under 35% accuracy and Gemini 3 Pro reaching ~65%.
AI.motivation: Addresses a gap in evaluating long-form audio-visual understanding relevant to real-world applications, emphasizing long-term memory, temporal localization, fine-grained understanding, and multimodal perception.
AI.method: Curated high-quality open-domain videos with rigorous manual selection and annotation; QA prompts targeted at long-range temporal dependencies, memory, localization, and multimodal integration.
AI.result: Significant performance gaps on long-form AV tasks: open-source models <35% accuracy; Gemini 3 Pro ~65% peak.
AI.conclusion: The dataset will stimulate research and drive development of robust OmniLLMs capable of handling long-form cross-modal content across domains.

title: Rethinking Vector Field Learning for Generative Segmentation
summary: Taming diffusion models for generative segmentation has attracted increasing attention. While existing approaches primarily focus on architectural tweaks or training heuristics, there remains a limited understanding of the intrinsic mismatch between continuous flow matching objectives and discrete perception tasks. In this work, we revisit diffusion segmentation from the perspective of vector field learning. We identify two key limitations of the commonly used flow matching objective: gradient vanishing and trajectory traversing, which result in slow convergence and poor class separation. To tackle these issues, we propose a principled vector field reshaping strategy that augments the learned velocity field with a detached distance-aware correction term. This correction introduces both attractive and repulsive interactions, enhancing gradient magnitudes near centroids while preserving the original diffusion training framework. Furthermore, we design a computationally efficient, quasi-random category encoding scheme inspired by Kronecker sequences, which integrates seamlessly with an end-to-end pixel neural field framework for pixel-level semantic alignment. Extensive experiments consistently demonstrate significant improvements over vanilla flow matching approaches, substantially narrowing the performance gap between generative segmentation and strong discriminative specialists.

AI.tldr: Reframes diffusion-based segmentation as vector-field learning to fix core issues in flow matching; introduces a distance-aware, detached correction to the velocity field to strengthen gradients and class separation, plus a Kronecker-sequence–inspired quasi-random category encoding within an end-to-end pixel neural field, yielding stronger segmentation performance.
AI.motivation: Address the intrinsic mismatch between continuous flow objectives in diffusion models and discrete perception tasks in segmentation, specifically gradient vanishing and poor class separation that slow convergence.
AI.method: Propose a vector-field reshaping strategy by augmenting the learned velocity field with a detached distance-aware correction term that yields attractive/repulsive forces to amplify gradients near centroids; couple with a computationally efficient quasi-random category encoding (Kronecker-sequence–inspired) within an end-to-end pixel neural field framework for pixel-level alignment.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show significant improvements over vanilla flow matching, substantially narrowing the gap between generative segmentation and strong discriminative specialists.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a principled way to align diffusion-based segmentation with discrete perception tasks, offering faster convergence and improved segmentation quality while preserving the diffusion training framework.

title: DriveTok: 3D Driving Scene Tokenization for Unified Multi-View Reconstruction and Understanding
summary: With the growing adoption of vision-language-action models and world models in autonomous driving systems, scalable image tokenization becomes crucial as the interface for the visual modality. However, most existing tokenizers are designed for monocular and 2D scenes, leading to inefficiency and inter-view inconsistency when applied to high-resolution multi-view driving scenes. To address this, we propose DriveTok, an efficient 3D driving scene tokenizer for unified multi-view reconstruction and understanding. DriveTok first obtains semantically rich visual features from vision foundation models and then transforms them into the scene tokens with 3D deformable cross-attention. For decoding, we employ a multi-view transformer to reconstruct multi-view features from the scene tokens and use multiple heads to obtain RGB, depth, and semantic reconstructions. We also add a 3D head directly on the scene tokens for 3D semantic occupancy prediction for better spatial awareness. With the multiple training objectives, DriveTok learns unified scene tokens that integrate semantic, geometric, and textural information for efficient multi-view tokenization. Extensive experiments on the widely used nuScenes dataset demonstrate that the scene tokens from DriveTok perform well on image reconstruction, semantic segmentation, depth prediction, and 3D occupancy prediction tasks.

AI.tldr: DriveTok presents a 3D driving scene tokenizer that unifies semantic, geometric, and texture information into scene tokens via 3D deformable cross-attention. A multi-view transformer decodes RGB, depth, and semantic outputs, with an additional 3D occupancy head, enabling efficient multi-view reconstruction and understanding in nuScenes.
AI.motivation: Existing tokenizers are largely monocular and 2D, causing inefficiency and inter-view inconsistency in high-resolution multi-view driving scenes. A scalable, unified 3D tokenizer is needed to support vision-language-action models and world models for autonomous driving.
AI.method: Extract semantically rich features from vision foundation models, convert them into scene tokens using 3D deformable cross-attention, and decode with a multi-view transformer to reconstruct multi-view RGB, depth, and semantic maps. A 3D head on the tokens predicts 3D semantic occupancy. Training uses multiple objectives to learn unified tokens that encode semantic, geometric, and texture information for efficient cross-view tokenization.
AI.result: Experiments on nuScenes show the DriveTok tokens enable accurate image reconstruction, semantic segmentation, depth prediction, and 3D occupancy prediction, indicating strong cross-view consistency and efficiency in token-based multi-view processing.
AI.conclusion: DriveTok delivers an efficient, unified 3D multi-view scene tokenizer that supports reconstruction and understanding tasks, aligning with the needs of vision-language-action and world models in autonomous driving.

title: Spectrally-Guided Diffusion Noise Schedules
summary: Denoising diffusion models are widely used for high-quality image and video generation. Their performance depends on noise schedules, which define the distribution of noise levels applied during training and the sequence of noise levels traversed during sampling. Noise schedules are typically handcrafted and require manual tuning across different resolutions. In this work, we propose a principled way to design per-instance noise schedules for pixel diffusion, based on the image's spectral properties.
